prior.title: Slovenia Ministry of Environment, Climate and Energy - National Energy and Climate Plan 2030-2040 Preparation Initiated
prior.summary: The Slovenian Ministry of Environment, Climate and Energy announces the start of preparation for the new Comprehensive National Energy and Climate Plan (NEPN) for 2030-2040. The ministry invites public and expert participation in consultation on long-term scenarios. Additional ongoing activities include fuel price regulation updates, international fossil fuel transition initiatives participation, LIFE program 2026 call announcements, sustainable mobility campaigns, and compliance guidance for industrial facilities.
prior.full_brief:
# Slovenia NEPN 2030–2040 Preparation Initiated

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

Slovenia has initiated preparation of its next National Energy and Climate Plan (NEPN) covering the period 2030–2040. FACT: "Slovenia has started preparing its new National Energy and Climate Plan (NEPN) for 2030–2040 and is inviting the public and experts to take part in a consultation on long-term scenarios that will form the basis of future energy and climate policy." *Source: CEE Energy News, "Slovenia begins preparation of NEPN 2030–2040," May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/

The trigger is dual: the EU's own post-2030 NECP governance review cycle and Slovenia's advancing national commitments. FACT: "Member States must submit a draft updated NEPN to the European Commission by 1 January 2028 and adopt the final version by 1 January 2029." *Source: CEE Energy News, "Slovenia begins preparation of NEPN 2030–2040," May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/

The plan's analytical foundation rests on two headline commitments: FACT: "These scenarios are based on two key commitments: the European Union's target of a 90 per cent net emissions reduction by 2040 compared to 1990 levels, and Slovenia's national commitment to achieve climate neutrality by 2045." *Source: CEE Energy News, "Slovenia begins preparation of NEPN 2030–2040," May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/

The prior NECP cycle (2021–2030) has already been assessed by the European Commission. FACT: "On 28 May 2025, the Commission published its EU-wide assessment of the final updated NECPs accompanied by a Staff Working Document with the individual assessment of 23 final updated NECPs submitted and evaluated to that date." *Source: European Commission, National Energy and Climate Plans Hub, https://commission.europa.eu/energy-climate-change-environment/implementation-eu-countries/energy-and-climate-governance-and-reporting/national-energy-and-climate-plans_en

FACT: The updated NECP for 2021–2030 envisages "EUR 57 billion in investment from 2021 until the end of the decade," with "EUR 22 billion for energy and climate targets in particular." *Source: Balkan Green Energy News, "Slovenia adopts updated Integrated National Energy and Climate Plan," published December 27, 2024, updated June 3, 2026. https://balkangreenenergynews.com/slovenia-adopts-updated-integrated-national-energy-and-climate-plan/

The current government reorganisation has split the former Ministry of Environment, Climate and Energy. FACT: "Področje energetike, oskrbe z naftnimi derivati in infrastrukture za alternativna goriva v prometu, trajnostno mobilnost, celostno prometno načrtovanje in javni potniški promet v notranjem in čezmejnem prometu po novem sodi med pristojnosti Ministrstva za infrastrukturo in energetiko." [Translation: The energy portfolio, including infrastructure for alternative transport fuels, sustainable mobility, and integrated transport planning, now falls under the remit of the Ministry of Infrastructure and Energy.] *Source: GOV.SI, Slovenian Ministry of Environment and Spatial Planning, https://gov.si/drzavni-organi/ministrstva/ministrstvo-za-okolje-podnebje-in-energijo

*Analytical inference:* The split of the former MOCE into a new Ministry of Environment and Spatial Planning plus a new Ministry of Infrastructure and Energy means NEPN 2030–2040 will be co-authored across two ministerial domains. This increases inter-agency coordination complexity and creates a period of institutional ambiguity before lines of NEPN responsibility are formally settled — a gap with direct relevance for workspaces engaging in stakeholder consultation.

**Re-check window: Q4 2026** — The analytical groundwork and first public consultation are underway now; the next substantive forward milestone is the draft NEPN submission to the European Commission, due 1 January 2028. Monitor for: (a) appointment of a lead ministry for NEPN coordination post-reorganisation; (b) publication of the long-term scenario results; (c) any EU governance regulation revision that reshapes NECP structure.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

The primary driver is the Slovenian national government, now operating through a newly restructured ministerial architecture following the June 2026 reorganisation. FACT: "Ministrica mag. Rifelj prevzela posle na Ministrstvu za okolje in prostor" — Minister Polona Rifelj assumed the portfolio of the Ministry of Environment and Spatial Planning on 5 June 2026, receiving responsibilities from the prior Ministry of Environment, Climate and Energy (MOCE), the Ministry of Natural Resources and Spatial Planning, and the Ministry of Solidarity Future. *Source: GOV.SI, https://gov.si/drzavni-organi/ministrstva/ministrstvo-za-okolje-podnebje-in-energijo

The European Commission acts as the external governance anchor, establishing the submission timeline and evaluation structure. FACT: The NECPs are "Required under the Regulation (EU) 2018/1999 on the governance of the energy union and climate action (Governance Regulation)." *Source: European Commission, National Energy and Climate Plans Hub. https://commission.europa.eu/energy-climate-change-environment/implementation-eu-countries/energy-and-climate-governance-and-reporting/national-energy-and-climate-plans_en

Civil society organisations have been active participants and critics in prior NECP cycles. FACT: "On the 19th of April, Focus organised a stakeholder meeting with 15 representatives of ministries (Ministry of Infrastructure and Ministry of the Environment and Spatial Planning), civil society (environmental NGOs and Jožef Stefan Institute) and the local municipality of Ljubljana." *Source: CAN Europe / Together for 1.5°C, Slovenia country page. https://1point5.caneurope.org/countries/slovenia/

*Analytical inference:* Civil society has systematically flagged ambition gaps in Slovenia's prior NECP rounds — specifically on renewable energy targets, fossil fuel subsidy phase-out, and transport sector emissions. The NEPN 2030–2040 process is likely to attract the same scrutiny, with civil society using the public consultation phase as a pressure mechanism for higher targets. Workspaces with Slovenian operations, especially in transport and freight, should monitor the transport-sector provisions actively, as this is where the prior NECP's implementation deficit has been most visible.

*Analytical inference:* The Slovenia NEPN 2030–2040 consultation signals an early-stage opportunity for freight operators with Slovenian operations to engage in shaping alternative fuel infrastructure targets and transport decarbonisation policy frameworks before they calcify into the draft submitted to Brussels in January 2028.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Timeline as stated in sources:**

- FACT: "The first step is preparing analytical groundwork, including long-term scenarios for different pathways towards climate neutrality." *Source: CEE Energy News, May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/
- FACT: Draft updated NEPN submission to European Commission: **1 January 2028**. *Source: CEE Energy News, May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/
- FACT: Adoption of final NEPN: **1 January 2029**. *Source: CEE Energy News, May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/

*Analytical inference:* Based on the prior NECP cycle structure — in which the EU Commission published guidance in 2022, draft plans were due June 2023, final updated plans by June 2024, and Commission assessment was published May 2025 — the NEPN 2030–2040 cycle is likely to follow a similar multi-year iterative process, with the analytical scenarios phase in 2026, stakeholder consultations in 2026–2027, draft preparation in 2027, and Commission review from January 2028 onward.

**Conversion triggers** — what converts this from a planning signal to active commercial pressure:

1. Publication of the long-term scenario results, expected from the current analytical phase: this will set the quantitative parameters (GHG reduction pathways, transport fuel mix, infrastructure investment estimates) that feed directly into regulatory and procurement expectations.

2. EU Governance Regulation revision: FACT: "NECPs as a governance tool will be reviewed for the post-2030 period as part of the forthcoming revision of the Governance of the Energy Union and Climate Action Regulation." *Source: European Commission, National Energy and Climate Plans Hub. https://commission.europa.eu/energy-climate-change-environment/implementation-eu-countries/energy-and-climate-governance-and-reporting/national-energy-and-climate-plans_en A revised governance regulation could add new obligations or reporting requirements that raise the compliance bar for operators.

3. Slovenia's decision on nuclear expansion by 2028: FACT: "a quality and transparent decision needs to be reached by 2028 on the proposal to build Slovenia's second conventional nuclear power plant by 2040." *Source: Balkan Green Energy News. https://balkangreenenergynews.com/slovenia-adopts-updated-integrated-national-energy-and-climate-plan/ This decision will shape the power-sector decarbonisation pathway and energy price trajectory across the entire period covered by NEPN 2030–2040.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*No content for this section as of June 2026: quantitative cost figures for Slovenian freight or logistics operators under NEPN 2030–2040 scenarios are not available from primary sources — the analytical groundwork phase is in progress and has not yet published scenario outputs.*

The following are framed as sourced trajectory context, not cost projections:

**Transport sector GHG trajectory:** FACT: The prior NECP targets "a 25.8% share of renewables in transportation" by 2030, rising from 10.8%. *Source: Balkan Green Energy News. https://balkangreenenergynews.com/slovenia-adopts-updated-integrated-national-energy-and-climate-plan/

FACT: Civil society analysis notes "the transport sector is an important component of this surge in emissions" — as of 2022, non-ETS sector emissions surpassed pre-pandemic levels. *Source: CAN Europe / Together for 1.5°C, Slovenia. https://1point5.caneurope.org/countries/slovenia/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating road freight through or within Slovenia, the combination of a rising renewables-in-transport target (from 10.8% to 25.8% by 2030 under the current NECP, with the 2030–2040 plan likely to extend the trajectory further) and acknowledged implementation deficit creates a policy environment where regulatory requirements for alternative fuel use in transport are directionally more stringent over the NEPN 2030–2040 horizon.

*Analytical inference:* The prior NECP's envisaged EUR 57 billion investment envelope, with EUR 223 million specifically for hydrogen infrastructure (pipelines and electrolyzers) and EUR 400 million for BESS, signals that Slovenian energy infrastructure will be materially different by the mid-2030s, with downstream implications for on-site energy procurement and electric-vehicle charging availability for road freight operators.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces handling time-sensitive or high-value cargo on Central European road corridors transiting Slovenia, the NEPN 2030–2040 scenarios will define the regulatory cost environment for diesel, the availability of alternative fuel infrastructure, and the parameters of any Slovenian road transport decarbonisation measures — all relevant planning inputs for multi-year fleet and routing decisions.

Specific [cost figures per vertical] not available from primary sources as of June 2026.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*No content for this section as of June 2026: no sourced reporting is available on named freight or logistics competitors' positioning relative to NEPN 2030–2040 consultation participation or Slovenian transport decarbonisation strategy. This section is omitted per the Integrity Rule rather than filled with speculative competitor attribution.*

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that engage early in Slovenia's NEPN 2030–2040 public consultation — particularly the long-term scenario phase — will have access to analytical scenario data and infrastructure planning assumptions before they become public regulatory commitments. This provides a lead time advantage for transport network planning, fuel transition scheduling, and client communication on Slovenia-corridor sustainability credentials.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Slovenia has formally initiated preparation of its 2030–2040 national climate and energy plan, with a public consultation phase underway as of May 2026. This is the earliest phase of a multi-year process, with a draft due to Brussels by January 2028 and a final plan by January 2029.
- The prior NECP committed Slovenia to a climate neutrality target of 2045, aligned with the EU's 90% net GHG reduction by 2040 target. These are the baseline commitments shaping the scenarios currently under analysis.
- Transport sector emissions in Slovenia have shown an implementation gap relative to prior NECP trajectories — meaning the 2030–2040 plan will face pressure to set more robust transport decarbonisation policies, not weaker ones.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not characterise the NEPN 2030–2040 as a finalised policy document — it is in the analytical/consultation phase only. No binding measures, targets, or compliance dates have been adopted under this plan yet.
- Do not cite specific transport fuel mandates, carbon price trajectories, or infrastructure investment timelines as NEPN 2030–2040 commitments — none have been published.
- Do not extrapolate the prior NECP's EUR 57 billion investment figure as applying to the 2030–2040 period; that figure covers 2021–2030.

**Questions to pose to clients operating in or through Slovenia:**

- What is your planning horizon for fleet decarbonisation on Central European road corridors, and does it align with Slovenia's stated 2045 climate neutrality trajectory?
- Have you mapped your alternative fuel infrastructure requirements against the current public consultation scenarios?

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

The following actions are grounded in the sourced process timeline and are calibrated to the current analytical/consultation phase of NEPN 2030–2040.

**Immediate (now – Q3 2026):**

- **Register as a stakeholder participant in the NEPN 2030–2040 public consultation.** The consultation on long-term scenarios is currently open. FACT: Slovenia "is inviting the public and experts to take part in a consultation on long-term scenarios." *Source: CEE Energy News, May 13, 2026. https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/ Workspaces with Slovenian road transport operations have a direct interest in how the transport sector scenarios are modelled. Participation costs are low; the value is early access to scenario assumptions before they become policy.

- **Monitor the output of the government reorganisation for NEPN lead-ministry clarification.** The former MOCE has been split as of June 5, 2026. Until a lead minister and ministry for NEPN 2030–2040 coordination is publicly confirmed, stakeholder engagement pathways are uncertain. Track GOV.SI announcements. *Source: GOV.SI, https://gov.si/drzavni-organi/ministrstva/ministrstvo-za-okolje-podnebje-in-energijo

**Medium-term (Q4 2026 – Q4 2027):**

- **Pull and review the long-term scenario publication when released.** The analytical groundwork phase will produce scenario documents — these will be the first quantitative signal on transport decarbonisation pathways for 2030–2040. Set a monitor for publication on the GOV.SI energy/climate page and CEE Energy News.

- **Track the EU Governance Regulation revision.** FACT: "NECPs as a governance tool will be reviewed for the post-2030 period as part of the forthcoming revision of the Governance of the Energy Union and Climate Action Regulation." *Source: European Commission NECP Hub. https://commission.europa.eu/energy-climate-change-environment/implementation-eu-countries/energy-and-climate-governance-and-reporting/national-energy-and-climate-plans_en A revised governance regulation may impose new obligations on freight operators or extend the scope of national plans in ways that affect the workspace directly.

**Re-check window:** Set a monitoring trigger for **January 2027** to review scenario publication status, ministerial lead confirmation, and any EU governance regulation consultation launch. Set a second trigger for **July 2027** to assess whether the draft NEPN is on track for the January 2028 submission deadline.

---

# 8. Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Date | Type | URL |
|---|-------|-------------|------|------|-----|
| 1 | Slovenia begins preparation of NEPN 2030–2040 | CEE Energy News (reporting) | May 13, 2026 | Tier 5 — news reporting | https://ceenergynews.com/climate/slovenia-new-nepn-2030-2040/ |
| 2 | National Energy and Climate Plans Hub | European Commission | Ongoing (last updated 2025–2026) | Tier 2 — regulator guidance | https://commission.europa.eu/energy-climate-change-environment/implementation-eu-countries/energy-and-climate-governance-and-reporting/national-energy-and-climate-plans_en |
| 3 | Slovenia adopts updated Integrated National Energy and Climate Plan | Balkan Green Energy News (reporting) | Published December 27, 2024; updated June 3, 2026 | Tier 5 — news reporting | https://balkangreenenergynews.com/slovenia-adopts-updated-integrated-national-energy-and-climate-plan/ |
| 4 | Slovenia \| Together for 1.5°C (NECP tracker) | CAN Europe | Ongoing (2024–2026 data) | Tier 4 — civil society / industry body analysis | https://1point5.caneurope.org/countries/slovenia/ |
| 5 | Ministry of Environment and Spatial Planning — GOV.SI (Slovenian language) | Republic of Slovenia | June 2026 | Tier 1 — official government page | https://gov.si/drzavni-organi/ministrstva/ministrstvo-za-okolje-podnebje-in-energijo |
| 6 | Ministry of Environment and Spatial Planning — GOV.SI (English) | Republic of Slovenia | June 2026 | Tier 1 — official government page | https://www.gov.si/en/state-authorities/ministries/ministry-of-the-environment-climate-and-energy/ |

---

## New Sources Identified

prior.title: Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program - Vehicle Emissions Regulations and Guidance
prior.summary: Maine Department of Environmental Protection's mobile sources program page provides regulatory guidance and resources on vehicle emissions control, including new aftermarket catalytic converter requirements, enhanced vehicle inspection programs, clean diesel initiatives, and participation in regional Transportation and Climate Initiative. Covers light and heavy-duty vehicles, off-road equipment, and emissions data reporting.
prior.full_brief:
# Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program — Vehicle Emissions Regulations and Guidance

**Market Signal Brief | Regulatory and Program Intelligence**
*Prepared for workspaces operating as freight forwarders, road fleet operators, and logistics intermediaries with exposure to U.S. Northeast trade lanes and Maine-connected carrier networks.*

---

## # 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**Program overview.** Maine DEP's Mobile Sources program addresses vehicle emissions from light-duty cars and trucks, medium-duty vehicles, heavy-duty vehicles, and off-road equipment including snowmobiles and ATVs. *Source: Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program Index, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/index.html.

FACT: "Mobile Sources are one of the largest sources of air pollution in Maine." *Source: Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program Index, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/index.html.

FACT: Maine participates in the Transportation and Climate Initiative (TCI), described as "a regional collaboration of 12 Northeast and Mid Atlantic States, including evaluation of clean vehicle options for Maine and increasing the efficiency of freight transportation." *Source: Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program Index, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/index.html.

**Transportation-sector emissions context.** FACT: "Roughly half of Maine's carbon emissions come from the transportation sector." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

FACT: "In 2023, Maine spent over $6 billion on imported fossil fuels including gasoline and oil, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

**Medium/heavy-duty vehicle (MHDV) freight signal.** FACT: "Medium- and heavy-duty vehicles (MHDVs) including trucks and buses account for 27 percent of the greenhouse gas emissions from the state's transportation system, and 10 percent of the state's total emissions." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

*Analytical inference:* These proportions position road-freight operators — including carriers serving Maine-connected supply chains — as a structurally significant compliance and cost exposure group as the state advances decarbonization policy.

**Statutory trigger — ACC II reporting mandate.** FACT: Under PL 2023, c. 624, enacted as an amendment to Maine Revised Statutes Title 38 §585-D: "By January 1, 2025, and annually thereafter, the department shall submit a report to the joint standing committee of the Legislature having jurisdiction over environment and natural resources matters regarding the status of the regulatory program adopted by the California Air Resources Board for new motor vehicle emissions, known as the Advanced Clean Cars II program, and the adoption of that program by other states, as well as any updates regarding federal motor vehicle emissions control requirements." *Source: Maine Legislature – Title 38 §585-D: New Motor Vehicle Emission Standards, Maine Legislature, data extracted 10/20/2025. https://www.mainelegislature.org/legis/statutes/38/title38sec585-D.html.

FACT: Under the same 2023 amendment: "Rules adopted or amended by the board on or after August 1, 2024 pursuant to this section, including, but not limited to, rules to establish zero‑emission requirements, are major substantive rules as defined in Title 5, chapter 375, subchapter 2‑A." *Source: Maine Legislature – Title 5 §585-D, Maine Legislature, data extracted 10/20/2025. https://www.mainelegislature.org/legis/statutes/38/title38sec585-D.html.

*Analytical inference:* The "major substantive rules" classification means any future DEP rulemaking on ZEV mandates for new vehicles — including commercial vehicles — proceeds through a heightened legislative review pathway, slowing adoption but increasing predictability for operators who engage during the consultation period.

---

## # 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Maine DEP Mobile Sources Section** administers the Clean Diesel Grant Program, SmartWay affiliate partnership, Enhanced Vehicle Inspection and Maintenance (I/M) program for Cumberland County, emissions inventory, and the statutory LEV/ZEV rulemaking authority under Title 38 §585-D. *Source: Maine DEP Mobile Sources Program Index, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/index.html.

**Maine Department of Energy Resources (DOER)** led development of the Clean Transportation Roadmap for MHDVs in partnership with the Governor's Office of Policy Innovation and the Future, and the Maine Department of Transportation. FACT: "The roadmap was developed by Cambridge Systematics with support from CALSTART, Cadmus, and Eastern Research Group." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

**U.S. EPA — DERA program.** The federal EPA provides DERA (Diesel Emissions Reduction Act) funding that Maine DEP administers as rolling grants to diesel fleet operators, marine vessel owners, and ground support equipment operators. FACT: The program is "funded by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) through the Diesel Emissions Reduction Act (DERA) to assist States in their efforts to develop diesel emissions reduction programs." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

**Maine Marine Trades Association (MMTA)** serves as the program administrator for Maine's Clean Marine Engine Program, a sub-program under DERA, conducting outreach to boat yards and administering engine repower grants. *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

**Maine Legislature.** The 2023 legislative session (PL 2023, c. 624) amended §585-D to require annual DEP reporting on ACC II adoption and elevated ZEV rulemaking to major substantive rule status, reflecting legislative intent to retain oversight before any ACC II adoption. *Source: Maine Legislature – Title 38 §585-D, Maine Legislature, data extracted 10/20/2025. https://www.mainelegislature.org/legis/statutes/38/title38sec585-D.html.

*Analytical inference:* The Legislature's structural intervention — requiring annual ACC II status reports and making ZEV rules subject to major substantive rule review — signals political caution about mandatory ACC II adoption even as the DEP continues voluntary programs. For logistics operators, this means the regulatory floor for Maine is currently set by the LEV program (California Tier 1 standards under §585-D), not ACC II.

---

## # 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**MONITORING — re-check window: January 2026 (annual ACC II report due)**

The annual DEP report to the Legislature's environment and natural resources committee, due January 1 each year starting 2025, is the primary tracking mechanism for ACC II adoption status in Maine. FACT: "After reviewing the report, the committee may report out legislation relating to the report to the Legislature in the legislative session in which the report is submitted." *Source: Maine Legislature – Title 38 §585-D, Maine Legislature, data extracted 10/20/2025. https://www.mainelegislature.org/legis/statutes/38/title38sec585-D.html.

**What would convert this signal to a compliance obligation:**

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether Maine DEP will formally adopt ACC II standards — which would require 100% of new car sales to be ZEVs by 2035 — and on what timeline, has not been determined as of the date of this brief. FACT: "Maine has not adopted ACC II standards." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

FACT: Maine has adopted the original Advanced Clean Cars (ACC) standards: "Maine, like several other states, has adopted Advanced Clean Cars (ACC) standards originally developed by California to encourage the availability and adoption of cleaner vehicle technologies, including zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs) such as EVs and hydrogen fuel cell vehicles. These ACC standards require automakers to deliver a certain percentage of their annual vehicle sales as ZEVs in each state that has adopted these standards, including Maine." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

*Analytical inference:* ACC II conversion in Maine is the key trigger for freight operators. The 2023 statutory amendments institutionalize annual legislative review, meaning adoption would require at minimum one full legislative cycle of review after DEP tabling. Operators in road freight with vehicle procurement cycles of 5–10 years should track the January annual reports as leading indicators.

**DERA funding trajectory.** FACT: "In November of 2025, DEP received new grant funding from EPA's State DERA program. The 2024 FY State DERA projects will be funded by a combination of DEP's 2024 and 2023 State DERA allocations." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html. FACT: "In January of 2024, DEP received a new year of funding from EPA's State DERA program." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

*Analytical inference:* Successive annual DERA award cycles (2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024) demonstrate a stable federal-state funding pipeline. DERA reauthorization at the federal level is the conversion trigger that could end or restructure this grant stream; tracking EPA DERA reauthorization status is the relevant forward indicator.

---

## # 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

### Transport mode: Road (primary for Maine freight)

**Clean Diesel DERA grants — available now for eligible Class 5–8 truck operators.**

FACT: Eligible heavy-duty trucks are "Class 5 through Class 8: Class 5 (16,001 -19,500 lbs gross vehicle weight rating [GVWR]); Class 6 (19,501 - 26,000 lbs GVWR); Class 7 (26,001 - 33,000 lbs GVWR); Class 8a (33,001 - 60,000 lbs GVWR); Class 8b (60,001 lbs GVWR and over)." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

FACT: "The program can fund up to 45% of the cost of replacing a heavy-duty diesel truck with a new, electric vehicle, including charging infrastructure." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

FACT: Eligible projects include "certified vehicle or equipment replacement with all-electric or alternative fuel; clean alternative fuel conversions; verified emission control technologies such as exhaust controls, cleaner fuels, and engine upgrades; verified idle reduction technologies such as auxiliary power units, fuel operated heaters, shore connection systems, transport refrigeration unit (TRU) electrified parking spaces, and truck stop electrification technologies." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating or contracting Class 5–8 diesel trucks in Maine, the DERA program provides a concrete, immediately accessible cost-offset mechanism for fleet modernization — including EV charging infrastructure costs. Workspaces that arrange ground transport on behalf of clients are eligible to apply directly ("All interested local, State and regional agencies, institutions, as well as businesses, commercial fishermen, and nonprofit organizations operating in the State of Maine").

**MHDV Roadmap cost context.**

FACT: "While many MHD zero-emission vehicles (ZEV) currently have higher upfront costs than conventional vehicles, they can offer long-term financial return through lower fuel and maintenance costs, along with available federal and state incentives." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

*Specific cost differential figures (ZEV vs. conventional MHDV) not available from primary sources as of July 2025.*

### Transport mode: Ocean/marine (secondary for Maine-connected freight)

**Clean Marine Engine Program — ongoing.**

FACT: "As of July 2024, the Maine Clean Marine Engine Program, has awarded $6.6 million from the Diesel Emission Reduction Act to repower 180 commercial vessels." FACT: The program has achieved "45% reduction in NOx of 99.69 tons/year, and a 73% reduction of 7.39 tons/year of PM." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

FACT: Reimbursement for marine engine repower is "up to 40% for a marine engine repower." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html.

*Operational implication:* Workspaces using Maine-based marine carriers for coastal or island freight should assess whether those carriers are eligible for and participating in the Clean Marine program; repowered vessels carry lower fuel costs and may qualify for preferential SmartWay or client-facing sustainability credentials.

### ZEV vehicle supply — forward cost pressure

FACT: ACC standards already in force in Maine "require automakers to deliver a certain percentage of their annual vehicle sales as ZEVs in each state." *Source: Maine Department of Energy Resources – Clean Transportation, Maine DOER, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/energy/initiatives/clean-transportation.

*Analytical inference:* Automaker ZEV delivery obligations in Maine affect fleet composition for new vehicle procurement. Operators in route-planning or carrier qualification roles should factor ZEV availability curves into 3–5 year fleet planning cycles.

---

## # 5. Competitive Implications

**SmartWay as a differentiator for Maine-connected freight.**

FACT: "Maine DEP became an affiliate in EPA's SmartWay Program in May 2015." FACT: "Maine trucking companies, dealers, rail carriers, logistics companies and commercial manufacturers and retailers who ship and receive products are all eligible to join the program." *Source: Maine DEP – EPA SmartWay Affiliate Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/assistance/smartway/index.html.

FACT: "The SmartWay logo sends a message to your customers that you are moving goods in an environmentally responsible manner." *Source: Maine DEP – EPA SmartWay Affiliate Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/assistance/smartway/index.html.

FACT: EPA's SmartWay program "provides a comprehensive and world-recognized system for benchmarking and reporting freight emissions across supply chains" and "helps companies identify and select more efficient freight carriers, service providers, transport modes, equipment, and operational strategies." *Source: EPA SmartWay – Learn About SmartWay, U.S. EPA, last updated August 1, 2025. https://www.epa.gov/smartway/learn-about-smartway.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces qualifying carrier networks used on Maine trade lanes, SmartWay certification status is an immediately actionable filter. Preferring certified carriers lowers Scope 3 emissions exposure on those lanes, reduces audit burden, and supports client-facing sustainability claims — without waiting for ACC II adoption.

**Named Maine SmartWay success cases.** Maine DEP cites Pottle's Transportation (Herman, ME) as having received an EPA Environmental Merit Award on Earth Day 2015 for SmartWay-related performance. Additional success cases cited: Hartt Transportation Systems, Oakhurst Dairy, Maine Biofuels. *Source: Maine DEP – EPA SmartWay Affiliate Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/assistance/smartway/index.html.

*Analytical inference:* Named Maine SmartWay partners are publicly identifiable. Workspaces whose carrier qualification processes include sustainability criteria can use the EPA SmartWay partner list as a verifiable, auditable sourcing tool for Maine-corridor carrier selection — a concrete competitive step that requires no new regulatory action.

**DERA competitive access.** FACT: "Funding is awarded through a rolling application process until all funds have been committed." *Source: Maine DEP – Clean Diesel/DERA Program, Maine DEP, accessed 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/cleandiesel.html. 

*Analytical inference:* Rolling application with limited total funds means early applicants in each funding year capture available grant dollars before late movers. Competitors who identify and apply in the first window of a new DERA award year (typically announced fall/winter) structurally outcompete those who apply later.

---

## # 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Maine DEP has administered the EPA DERA clean diesel grant program continuously since at least 2020, with new funding awarded annually through 2024, supporting truck, marine vessel, and ground support equipment replacements across the state.
- Maine DEP is an EPA SmartWay affiliate since May 2015; the workspace can point clients to the SmartWay partner list as a public, verifiable index of freight carriers benchmarked for emissions performance on Maine lanes.
- Maine's transportation sector accounts for approximately half of the state's carbon emissions, and MHDVs alone account for 27% of transport-sector GHG — figures drawn from Maine DOER's own planning documents.
- Maine has adopted the original ACC standards (requiring ZEV sales percentages from automakers) but has not adopted ACC II as of the date of this brief.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert that Maine has adopted or will adopt ACC II standards — the statutory framework requires annual legislative review, and no adoption decision has been announced.
- Do not characterize DERA grants as guaranteed or as fixed annual amounts — they are a rolling, application-based, federally funded program subject to EPA reauthorization and annual allocation decisions.
- Do not assert specific fleet electrification cost savings for Maine MHDV operators without citing specific studies — the DOER roadmap notes lower long-run costs but specific differential figures are not publicly stated in the source materials reviewed.

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- Are carriers you use on Maine-connected lanes currently SmartWay certified? If not, are they eligible to enroll?
- Do any of your fleet assets or contracted carrier assets in Maine qualify for DERA replacement or repower funding?
- What is your current Scope 3 accounting methodology for Maine road-freight lanes — are you using MOVES-derived emission factors or SmartWay benchmarking tools?

---

## # 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**SEVERITY: MONITORING | Re-check window: January 2026 (ACC II annual DEP report to Legislature)**

**Action 1 — Carrier qualification: SmartWay filter for Maine lanes.**
Review all carriers used on Maine-connected road freight lanes against the EPA SmartWay partner list. Carriers enrolled in SmartWay submit annual performance data; the workspace can use this data to support Scope 3 emissions reporting. Contact: Maine DEP SmartWay contact Julie Churchill, 1-800-789-902, julie.m.churchill@maine.gov. No cost; immediate.
*Why now:* Maine DEP's affiliate status means SmartWay enrollment is locally supported and carrier outreach is facilitated by DEP — a resource not available in all states.

**Action 2 — DERA grant eligibility assessment for Maine-based fleet assets.**
If the workspace operates or contracts Class 5–8 diesel trucks, marine vessels, or airport ground support equipment in Maine, assess eligibility for the 2024 FY DERA grant cycle (funding announced November 2025). Contact: Peter Carleton, Maine DEP Mobile Sources Section, (207) 242-6103.
*Why now:* The 2024 DERA grant is a rolling application until funds are committed — the window closes when the allocation is exhausted, not on a fixed calendar date.

**Action 3 — Emissions inventory data access.**
The workspace can access Maine DEP's Vehicle Emissions and Greenhouse Gas Data files (VMT by county, vehicle population by town, MOVES-derived fuel consumption, and GHG emission factors) to support Scope 3 freight emissions calculations on Maine lanes. *Source: Maine DEP – Vehicle Emission and Greenhouse Gas Data, Maine DEP, last updated July 2025. https://www.maine.gov/dep/air/mobile/vehicle-data.html.
*Why now:* MOVES-derived fuel consumption data was updated July 2025; using current-year data improves Scope 3 accuracy and client-facing reporting credibility.

**Action 4 — ACC II legislative tracking.**
Set a calendar reminder to retrieve the Maine DEP annual ACC II status report when filed (due January 1, 2026 for the second annual report). FACT: "After reviewing the report, the committee may report out legislation relating to the report to the Legislature in the legislative session in which the report is submitted." *Source: Maine Legislature – Title 38 §585-D, Maine Legislature, data extracted 10/20/2025. https://www.mainelegislature.org/legis/statutes/38/title38sec585-D.html. The report is the earliest public signal of Maine's regulatory direction on ZEV mandates for the vehicle supply chain affecting operator fleet procurement.

**Action 5 — MHDV Roadmap: evaluate case study relevance.**
The MHDV Clean Transportation Roadmap includes four fleet case studies (Pine State Trading; Lynch Logistics; two anonymous fleets). For workspaces operating similar Maine-based distribution or trucking operations, these case studies provide peer-context for ZEV total cost of ownership modeling. Available from Maine DOER's clean transportation page.

---

prior.title: Rhode Island Fish Passage Initiative and River Herring Migration Restoration
prior.summary: The Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management is leading collaborative efforts to restore fish passage and migratory fish populations in the Blackstone River. Working with the Narragansett Indian Tribe and Hassanamisco Band of Nipmuc, the initiative focuses on creating fish passage over four lower dams to restore river herring (alewife and blueback herring) migrations, which are critical for spawning cycles.
prior.full_brief:
# Rhode Island Fish Passage Initiative and River Herring Migration Restoration

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**The signal:** Rhode Island's fish passage and river herring restoration programme has reached a measurable inflection point — decades of dam removal, fishway construction, and multi-agency investment are producing documented population rebounds in 2025, while simultaneously exposing the limits of freshwater habitat restoration as a standalone recovery mechanism. The programme is active, multi-river, and federally co-funded. Its trajectory is now being shaped as much by ocean-side bycatch dynamics as by in-river infrastructure work.

**What triggered it:** Two converging events in 2024–2025 crystallised the signal:

1. Preliminary 2025 river count data — described by RIDEM as showing "triple the number of fish compared to the previous year" from early monitoring across two of four surveyed rivers — represents the sharpest single-year rebound in the monitoring dataset. *Source: "Are Rhode Island River Herring's Problems Really Out at Sea?", ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

2. The Atlantic herring commercial quota reduction and a simultaneous mackerel fishery closure reduced oceanic bycatch pressure on river herring from southern New England genetic stocks — identified by Connecticut fisheries biologist Kevin Job as a likely driver of the 2025 in-river rebound. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

**Programme investment documented:** NOAA and its damage-assessment trustees have contributed "more than $260,000 to rebuild the fishways on the Saugatucket River," with the restored fishways enabling river herring to reach "more than 300 acres of upstream spawning and rearing habitat." *Source: "River Restoration Helps Fish Reach Habitat," NOAA DARRP, June 16, 2016. https://darrp.noaa.gov/oil-spills-hazardous-waste/river-restoration-helps-fish-reach-habitat

**Pawcatuck River baseline:** On the Pawcatuck, the USFWS and partners have pursued dam removal and nature-like fishway installation since at least 2012. Bradford Dam — a six-foot barrier blocking the 20-mile migration route from Little Narragansett Bay — was replaced by a nature-like fishway in 2019. *Source: "Restoring the Pawcatuck River," USFWS, https://www.fws.gov/story/restoring-pawcatuck-river

**National context — stock status:** A 2024 stock assessment by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission "concluded that river herring are still depleted on a coastwide basis." The same assessment noted "signs of improvement" in the Gulf of Maine. *Source: "Reviving River Herring," USFWS, https://www.fws.gov/story/reviving-river-herring

*Analytical inference:* The 2025 rebound, combined with the ASMFC depletion finding, positions the Rhode Island programme as a test case for whether targeted local interventions — both in-river and ocean-side — can produce measurable recovery within a nationally depleted stock. The outcome of the current monitoring cycle carries disproportionate weight for federal and state policy decisions about the balance between habitat investment and bycatch management.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Active participants (sourced):**

- **Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management (RIDEM):** Lead state agency for fish passage permitting, in-river monitoring, and dam-safety oversight. RIDEM has monitored herring counts across four rivers and is the source of the 2025 preliminary rebound data. RIDEM also holds authority over dam-safety compliance, relevant to the estimated risk of catastrophic failure at aging structures. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

- **U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (USFWS) — Southern New England Estuary Program:** Operational lead on Pawcatuck River restoration. USFWS biologist Suzanne Paton has worked with conservation partners on the Pawcatuck since 2012. USFWS also coordinates the National Fish Passage Program nationally. *Source: USFWS, "Restoring the Pawcatuck River." https://www.fws.gov/story/restoring-pawcatuck-river

- **NOAA Damage Assessment, Remediation, and Restoration Program (DARRP):** Funded Saugatucket River fishway reconstruction through settlement recoveries from the North Cape oil spill and Rose Hill Landfill contamination. *Source: NOAA DARRP. https://darrp.noaa.gov/oil-spills-hazardous-waste/river-restoration-helps-fish-reach-habitat

- **The Nature Conservancy (TNC) — Rhode Island:** John O'Brien of TNC RI has worked on freshwater habitat restoration and stated publicly that ocean bycatch is now the binding constraint on recovery. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

- **New England Fisheries Management Council (NEFMC):** The body with authority over Atlantic herring quota and bycatch rules. Time-area closure proposals — which would reduce river herring bycatch on southern New England fishing grounds — were under development at NEFMC but were paused. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

- **Wood-Pawcatuck Watershed Association:** Long-term operational partner on Pawcatuck dam removals. Former programme director Denise Poyer has articulated the ecosystem-foundation role of migratory herring across the 300-square-mile watershed. *Source: USFWS, "Restoring the Pawcatuck River." https://www.fws.gov/story/restoring-pawcatuck-river

- **Narragansett Bay Estuary Program (NBEP) and Lower Blackstone consortium:** The corroborating source list identifies NBEP, RIDEM, and TNC as collaborators on the Lower Blackstone River project, targeting passage past four dams. *This item is listed in the corroborating source inventory; the NBEP page at https://www.nbep.org/lower-blackstone-fish-passage was not among the five rendered source blocks and its content is not span-groundable here — carried as a lead under New Sources Identified.*

**What the parties want:**

- RIDEM and USFWS want durable population recovery measured by in-river count trends across multiple seasons, not single-year rebounds.
- NOAA DARRP's role is restitutive — deploying settlement funds to restore fish populations injured by documented spills and contamination.
- TNC and watershed associations want landscape-scale reconnection: not individual fishways but continuous migration corridors from Narragansett Bay to historical spawning grounds.
- *Analytical inference:* The NEFMC pause on time-area closures reflects competing stakeholder interests between the commercial Atlantic herring fishing industry (which benefits from current categorisation of river herring as "incidental catch") and conservation and recreational fishing communities that have invested decades in in-river restoration.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Current phase:** Active, multi-river, ongoing. In-river infrastructure investment (dam removal, fishway construction) is well-established and delivering measurable results on the Pawcatuck and Saugatucket systems. The Blackstone corridor is the active frontier (Lower Blackstone four-dam passage project). The bycatch governance track is stalled at NEFMC following the April 2025 pause.

**Documented trajectory:**

- Herring counts fluctuated dramatically — peaking at "more than 240,000 fish in 2020, dropping the following year to fewer than 100,000" — despite consistent in-river investment. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/
- Preliminary 2025 data show a rebound to "triple the number of fish compared to the previous year" from two of four monitored rivers, coinciding with reduced Atlantic herring quota and mackerel fishery closure. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/
- The 2024 ASMFC coastwide stock assessment found stocks "still depleted on a coastwide basis" while noting Gulf of Maine improvement. *Source: USFWS, "Reviving River Herring." https://www.fws.gov/story/reviving-river-herring

**Conversion triggers — what would shift this from signal to directive:**

1. **NEFMC time-area closure reinstatement:** Resumption of the paused southern New England closure proposals at a future Council meeting would directly reduce bycatch pressure and represent a regulatory commitment to the ocean-side constraint. The pause was explicitly attributed to "cutbacks at [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration] Fisheries and added council workload" from the then-new administration — a workload and staffing condition, not a policy reversal. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025. https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

2. **ASMFC updated stock assessment trigger:** If a subsequent ASMFC assessment upgrades stock status from "depleted" to recovering on a coastwide basis, federal and state harvest management frameworks would update accordingly.

3. **Lower Blackstone four-dam passage completion:** Opening the full Blackstone corridor would represent the largest single expansion of accessible Rhode Island spawning habitat, potentially shifting in-river count baselines.

4. **University of California, Santa Cruz bycatch study adoption:** The study — cited in the ecoRI News coverage — found that "much of the bycatch from the Atlantic herring fishery consisted of river herring from southern New England," and the authors stated that "mitigating bycatch on the southern New England fishing grounds may benefit recovery efforts for alewife and blueback herring genetic stocks that have experienced the greatest declines in spawning adult abundances." If NEFMC formally adopts this finding, it could anchor a time-area closure proposal. *Source: ecoRI News, August 28, 2025 (citing the UC Santa Cruz study). https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/

*Analytical inference:* The NEFMC pause is the single highest-leverage inflection point. Its resumption timeline is driven by NOAA Fisheries staffing and the incoming administration's regulatory posture — neither of which has a published decision date. Re-check window: Q1–Q2 2026 NEFMC Council meeting schedule.

**MONITORING — re-check window: Q1 2026 NEFMC meeting and full-season 2025 RIDEM count publication.**

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*This brief covers a conservation and fisheries-management signal, not a freight or logistics regulatory event. The workspace profile drives the filtering below.*

**For workspaces with supply chain exposure to New England commercial fisheries (perishable seafood verticals, cold-chain, ocean freight on Atlantic coast corridors):**

- *Operational implication:* If NEFMC reinstates time-area closures on southern New England Atlantic herring fishing grounds, vessels currently fishing those grounds in peak season would need to delay or relocate — compressing the Atlantic herring supply window and potentially affecting cold-chain volume and scheduling predictability on inbound New England seafood movements.

- *Analytical inference:* The Atlantic herring commercial quota has already been reduced to levels where, according to Eric Reid (former Rhode Island NEFMC representative), "the fishery and associated bycatch is almost non-existent." If that reduced-quota regime persists or deepens, the directional effect on Atlantic herring supply volumes — and associated reefer freight demand — is further contraction, not recovery.

- *Operational implication:* River herring carry a moratorium on commercial harvest in Rhode Island (in force since the early 2000s), so river herring themselves do not generate commercial freight volume. The freight signal is indirect: Atlantic herring quota changes affect reefer cargo volumes and cold-chain scheduling on the New England coast.

**Specific cost impact:** Specific freight rate or volume impact figures for New England reefer movements tied to Atlantic herring quota changes are not available from primary sources as of the date of this brief.

*Specific [cost/rate figure] not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

**For workspaces with no seafood or New England coastal freight exposure:** No direct operational implication identified from available sourced material.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*No competitor-specific freight or logistics operator positioning has been identified in the sourced material for this signal. The signal operates at the level of fisheries management and ecological restoration, not freight operator strategy.*

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces with established cold-chain capability on New England coastal corridors and relationships with commercial seafood shippers are better positioned to absorb scheduling disruption from Atlantic herring quota fluctuations than operators without those dedicated lane relationships. The 2025 rebound — if confirmed by full-season RIDEM data — may modestly increase stakeholder confidence in the long-term viability of southern New England herring fisheries, but this does not translate to near-term commercial opportunity without a reversal of the moratorium on river herring harvest, which no sourced material indicates is under consideration.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Rhode Island's fish passage programme is an active, federally co-invested initiative with documented results: the Saugatucket fishways are open to more than 300 acres of spawning habitat; Bradford Dam on the Pawcatuck was replaced with a nature-like fishway in 2019; and preliminary 2025 river counts show a significant rebound. These are sourced facts, citable in any sustainability or environmental context.

- The 2024 ASMFC stock assessment confirms river herring remain "depleted on a coastwide basis" — so a single year's rebound does not constitute recovery. Overclaiming recovery would be inaccurate.

- The bycatch question is genuinely unresolved at the governance level: NEFMC paused its time-area closure work in April 2025 explicitly due to NOAA Fisheries staffing reductions and workload. This is a governance gap, not a resolved policy position.

**What to avoid:**

- Do not characterise the 2025 rebound as evidence that the restoration programme has succeeded. The data covers two of four monitored rivers and is preliminary.
- Do not assert that NEFMC will reinstate time-area closures on any specific timeline — no decision date has been published.
- Do not conflate river herring (under moratorium) with Atlantic herring (commercially fished) when discussing supply chain implications.

**Question to pose to clients:**

- For clients in New England seafood supply chains: "Has your current reefer scheduling on Atlantic herring movements accounted for the possibility of further quota reductions or the reinstatement of time-area closures on southern New England grounds?"

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

These are positioning and monitoring actions, not compliance actions.

1. **Monitor NEFMC meeting schedule for Q1–Q2 2026.** The time-area closure proposals were paused, not withdrawn. The next Council meeting that returns to "management flexibility measures" is the trigger event. Source the NEFMC public meeting calendar directly. *Applicable to workspaces with New England seafood cold-chain exposure.*

2. **Track RIDEM's full 2025 herring count publication.** Preliminary data from two rivers shows a triple-year-over-year rebound. Full-season data across all four monitored rivers will confirm or qualify that signal. RIDEM publishes annual fish passage reports; the full 2025 dataset is the re-check document. *Applicable to any workspace seeking to characterise New England fishery health in client conversations.*

3. **Note the ASMFC 2024 coastwide depletion finding in any client sustainability communication.** It is the authoritative national baseline. Citing it alongside the Rhode Island rebound accurately frames the tension between local progress and coastwide status.

4. **For workspaces building ESG narratives around biodiversity or supply chain traceability:** The intersection of NOAA DARRP settlement-funded restoration, USFWS National Fish Passage Program investment, and state-level monitoring creates a documented, multi-agency accountability chain that is citable in biodiversity impact reporting.

5. **Flag the Lower Blackstone four-dam project as a watch item.** No construction completion date is available from the rendered source blocks. *Analytical inference:* Multi-dam fish passage projects of this complexity typically take 3–7 years from permitting to full operational passage, based on the Pawcatuck precedent (2012–2019). Blackstone project status and timeline confirmation should be sought from the RIDEM and NBEP pages listed under New Sources Identified.

---

# 8. Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Type | URL |
|---|-------|-------------|------|-----|
| 1 | Restoring the Pawcatuck River | U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service | Tier 3 — Federal agency narrative | https://www.fws.gov/story/restoring-pawcatuck-river |
| 2 | Reviving River Herring | U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service | Tier 3 — Federal agency narrative | https://www.fws.gov/story/reviving-river-herring |
| 3 | Are Rhode Island River Herring's Problems Really Out at Sea? | ecoRI News | Tier 5 — News reporting | https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/ |
| 4 | River Restoration Helps Fish Reach Habitat | NOAA DARRP | Tier 2 — Federal regulator/damage assessment documentation | https://darrp.noaa.gov/oil-spills-hazardous-waste/river-restoration-helps-fish-reach-habitat |
| 5 | Climate Change | Rhode Island DEM | Tier 2 — State agency site (navigation only; no substantive claims drawn) | https://dem.ri.gov/environmental-protection-bureau/air-resources/climate-change |

*Note: The RIDEM Climate Change page (Source 5) was provided as a source block but contained no substantive content relevant to fish passage or river herring restoration. No claims were drawn from it.*

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------|------------------------|
| RIDEM Lower Blackstone River Fish Passage – Official Initiative Page | https://dem.ri.gov/natural-resources-bureau/initiatives/lower-blackstone-river-fish-passage | 2 | Primary RIDEM page for the active Blackstone dam/fish-ladder construction programme; needed to ground construction phase, timeline, and dam purchase claims that cannot be sourced from rendered blocks. |
| Narragansett Bay Estuary Program – Lower Blackstone Fish Passage Project | https://www.nbep.org/lower-blackstone-fish-passage | 3 | Consortium partner page documenting NBEP/RIDEM/TNC collaboration and the four-dam passage goal; provides participant and scope detail absent from rendered sources. |
| U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service – Reviving River Herring (national context) | https://www.fws.gov/story/reviving-river-herring | 3 | Already rendered and used as Source 2; listed here for completeness per table instructions — this source was in the corroborating list. |
| ecoRI News – Are Rhode Island River Herring's Problems Really Out at Sea? | https://ecori.org/are-rhode-island-river-herrings-problems-really-out-at-sea/ | 5 | September 2025 investigative piece with RIDEM biologist quotes and 2025 preliminary count data; the primary sourced signal driver for bycatch dynamics and the NEFMC pause. |
