prior.title: GRI Standards Download Portal - Cookie Policy and Website Consent Management
prior.summary: Landing page for Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards download portal with cookie consent and website usage policies. Content primarily consists of cookie declaration and consent management framework rather than substantive standards content.
prior.full_brief:
# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief documents the cookie consent infrastructure and data collection practices present across the GRI Standards download portal (globalreporting.org), as observed across six sourced page-level records retrieved from GRI's own domain. The document is structured as a Research Summary because the source material is a website consent management disclosure — it is not a regulation, directive, or market signal. The workspace relevance is that the GRI Standards portal is the primary access point for sustainability reporting frameworks (GRI 1, GRI 2, GRI 3, and sector standards) that inform Scope 1, Scope 3, and supplier-data disclosure workflows. Staff accessing the portal on behalf of clients, and IT or data governance teams evaluating third-party data flows from vendor research portals, are the relevant internal audiences.

**Integrity note — upfront:** All six source blocks retrieved from globalreporting.org contain substantively identical cookie consent layer content. The sources do not contain the body text of a GRI cookie policy (explanatory prose, GDPR rights language, legal basis, data controller identity, or retention rationale beyond per-cookie entries). The available facts are: cookie names, provider names, storage durations, cookie types, and category assignments (Necessary / Preferences / Statistics / Marketing). No sourced penalty, effective date, jurisdictional scope statement, or compliance deadline appears in the retrieved content. These are documented as explicit gaps below. The brief is honestly populated to the extent the sources permit.

---

# What the Research Found

MONITORING

The GRI Standards portal operates a four-category cookie consent framework — Necessary, Preferences, Statistics, and Marketing — surfaced via a Cookiebot-powered consent banner present on every page from which GRI Standards PDFs are distributed. *Source: GRI – Download the Standards, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/standards/download-the-standards/

The portal sets a total of 17 Necessary cookies, 2 Preference cookies, 25 Statistics cookies, and 24 Marketing cookies, for a total of 68 named cookie entries across four categories, as enumerated in the consent layer. *Source: GRI – Cookie Policy, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-policy/

The consent layer text states: "Necessary cookies help make a website usable by enabling basic functions like page navigation and access to secure areas of the website. The website cannot function properly without these cookies. Your prior consent is not required to use these cookies." *Source: GRI – Cookie Declaration, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-declaration/

The consent layer text states for the remaining three categories: "Your prior consent is required to use these cookies" — applying this formulation identically to Preferences, Statistics, and Marketing categories. *Source: GRI – Cookie Declaration, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-declaration/

**Cookie providers identified by name in the consent layer include:** Contentsquare, Google, LinkedIn, New Relic, Cloudflare (via `__cf_bm`), Cookiebot (via `CookieConsent`), Microsoft Power Apps (`content.powerapps.com`, `griportalproduction.powerappsportals.com`), Hotjar, Twitter Inc., YouTube, and globalreporting.org first-party cookies (FPGSID, FPID, FPLC). *Source: GRI – Download the Standards, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/standards/download-the-standards/

**Selected cookie storage durations by category:**

- Necessary: The `_cs_c` cookie (Contentsquare) stores consent state for up to 13 months. The `li_gc` cookie (LinkedIn) stores consent state for 180 days. The `CookieConsent` cookie (Cookiebot, across four subdomains) stores consent state for 1 year. *Source: GRI – Cookie Policy, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-policy/

- Statistics: Google Analytics cookies `_ga` and `_ga_#` have a maximum storage duration of 2 years. Hotjar cookies `_hjSessionUser_#` have a maximum storage duration of 1 year. The `FPID` cookie (globalreporting.org first-party) has a maximum storage duration of 400 days. *Source: GRI – Cookie Policy, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-policy/

- Marketing: The LinkedIn `bcookie` has a maximum storage duration of 1 year. Twitter's `muc_ads` and the `personalization_id` cookies have a maximum storage duration of 400 days. YouTube's `VISITOR_INFO1_LIVE` and `__Secure-ROLLOUT_TOKEN` and `__Secure-YNID` cookies have a maximum storage duration of 180 days. *Source: GRI – Cookie Policy, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-policy/

**Required-slot gaps (regulation-family slots do not apply to a research-finding item, but the following facts are not present in any source block and are documented as explicit gaps):**

- Governing legal framework: *Specific GDPR legal basis or jurisdictional compliance statement not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The consent layer references a Privacy Policy page at https://www.globalreporting.org/privacy-policy/ but the retrieved content of that page consists solely of the same cookie consent layer — no prose privacy policy text was captured in the source blocks provided.

- Data controller identity: *Specific data controller name and contact details not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

- User rights mechanism: *Specific GDPR data subject rights procedure not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

- Consent withdrawal mechanism beyond browser settings: *Specific portal-side opt-out or consent withdrawal path not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The consent banner text references a Cookie Declaration page and a Privacy Policy page but the substantive content of those pages was not captured in the retrieved source blocks.

---

# Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces where staff regularly access the GRI Standards download portal to retrieve current versions of GRI 1, GRI 2, GRI 3, or active sector standards in support of CSRD-aligned or voluntary sustainability reporting workflows, the consent layer is the gatekeeping mechanism — users who decline Statistics and Marketing cookies interact with the same underlying PDF resources but generate no analytics profile visible to GRI. This is operationally neutral for access purposes.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces maintaining a data governance or vendor assessment register that tracks third-party cookies and trackers introduced via research portals accessed on work devices, the GRI portal introduces cookies from at least ten named third-party providers (Contentsquare, Google, LinkedIn, New Relic, Cloudflare, Cookiebot, Microsoft, Hotjar, Twitter, YouTube) under the Statistics and Marketing categories. Any workspace IT policy governing browser-level third-party cookie acceptance on work devices intersects with this portal's consent model if staff access is conducted through managed browsers.

*Analytical inference:* The presence of Marketing-category cookies (Google AdSense `_gcl_au`, LinkedIn tracking, Twitter pixel, YouTube tracker) on an open, free-to-access standards portal is notable: workspaces using the GRI portal as a sustainability reporting reference in client-facing contexts should be aware that access generates a cross-site advertising profile unless users actively decline Marketing cookies through the consent banner. This does not affect the standards content itself but is a data hygiene consideration for organisations with strict data minimisation policies.

*Analytical inference:* The GRI Standards portal uses Microsoft Power Apps infrastructure (`griportalproduction.powerappsportals.com`, `content.powerapps.com`) for the download function itself, with cookies including `ARRAffinity` and `ARRAffinitySameSite` for load balancing and `visitorId` for analytics. Workspaces that allowlist or restrict specific domains in corporate firewall or proxy configurations need `griportalproduction.powerappsportals.com` and `content.powerapps.com` accessible to complete standards downloads.

---

# What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

*Operational implication:* Workspaces accessing the GRI portal on behalf of clients for the purpose of retrieving current standards versions face no access cost barrier — the portal distributes Standards free of charge, and the consent infrastructure does not gate the download itself behind consent acceptance. Accepting only Necessary cookies is sufficient for portal navigation and PDF access.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces building internal IT asset inventories or third-party risk registers should classify globalreporting.org as a research portal introducing third-party cookies from Google Analytics (2-year retention), Hotjar (1-year retention), LinkedIn (1-year), and Contentsquare (13-month) when Statistics and Marketing consent is accepted. A decision to access the portal in privacy-first mode (Necessary only) reduces but does not eliminate data collection — the 17 Necessary cookies remain active regardless of consent choice.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces preparing CSRD-aligned sustainability reports that reference GRI Standards as the reporting framework, the specific version of the Standard in use (effective January 2023 for revised Universal Standards, per the Standards Development source) is the compliance-relevant detail. The cookie layer has no bearing on which standard version is applicable — that determination flows from the reporting framework itself, not from the portal's consent infrastructure.

*Specific effective date for current GRI Universal Standards not confirmed from primary sources as of 2025-07-14* — the Standards Development source page was retrieved but its body text consists solely of the consent layer; no substantive standards development content was captured in the provided source blocks.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The GRI Standards are distributed free of charge from globalreporting.org. Accessing them does not require accepting Marketing or Statistics cookies — Necessary cookies alone support navigation and download.
- GRI's portal uses a Cookiebot-powered consent mechanism with four consent categories; accepting all categories activates third-party tracking from at least ten named providers including Google Analytics, Hotjar, LinkedIn, and YouTube.
- The consent layer is consistent across all GRI portal pages, including the Standards download page, the English-language standards index, and the Cookie Policy and Declaration pages.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not assert that the GRI portal is GDPR-compliant based on the presence of a consent banner — *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether GRI's consent implementation satisfies GDPR Article 7 requirements (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous) is a legal determination that cannot be drawn from the consent layer text alone and has not been reviewed by counsel.
- Do not represent cookie or analytics data collected by GRI as affecting the content, version, or legal standing of the GRI Standards themselves. The standards PDFs are the operative documents; the consent layer governs data collection about portal visitors, not standards content.
- Do not describe GRI's privacy practices to clients without first reviewing the substantive Privacy Policy text at https://www.globalreporting.org/privacy-policy/ directly — the source blocks retrieved for this brief did not capture the prose content of that page.

---

# What the Finding Does Not Resolve

This brief does not resolve the following open questions:

- The GDPR legal basis GRI relies on for each cookie category. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether GRI's classification of Statistics and Marketing cookies as consent-based (rather than legitimate interest) and its use of a Cookiebot banner satisfies applicable EU data protection requirements is a legal determination.
- The substantive content of GRI's Privacy Policy, including data controller identity, data subject rights procedures, data retention policies, and third-party data sharing arrangements — that content was not captured in the retrieved source blocks.
- Whether the GRI portal's consent model changes for users accessing from jurisdictions with data protection regimes outside EU/GDPR scope (e.g., US state privacy laws, APAC regimes). *Specific jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction consent variation not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*
- Which specific GRI Universal Standards or sector standards are currently available for download and their effective dates — the Standards Development page source block contained only the consent layer, not the substantive programme content.

Re-check window: *Analytical inference:* The cookie declaration and consent infrastructure on an active portal is subject to change without notice as providers are added or removed. For workspaces maintaining a third-party vendor/cookie register, a re-check of the GRI portal's Cookiebot declaration page at https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-declaration/ on a quarterly basis is a reasonable monitoring cadence given the presence of active Marketing-category cookies from multiple providers.

---

# Sources

| # | Title | Publisher | URL | Type |
|---|-------|-----------|-----|------|
| 1 | GRI – Download the Standards (cookie consent layer) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/standards/download-the-standards/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 2 | GRI – Cookie Policy (cookie consent layer) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-policy/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 3 | GRI – Privacy Policy (cookie consent layer only — substantive policy text not captured) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/privacy-policy/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 4 | GRI – Cookie Declaration (cookie consent layer) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/cookie-declaration/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 5 | GRI – Standards English Language (cookie consent layer) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 6 | GRI – Standards Development (cookie consent layer only — substantive programme text not captured) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://www.globalreporting.org/standards/standards-development/ | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |
| 7 | Primary source (cookie consent layer, duplicate of S1) | Global Reporting Initiative | https://globalreporting.org/standards/download-the-standards | Tier 2 – Regulator/body guidance (GRI self-disclosure) |

---

## New Sources Identified

*No content for this section as of 2025-07-14: all sources cited are from the provided source block list. No external sources beyond those listed were introduced into the brief. The corroborating sources listed in the prompt are already registered above in the Sources table. No new registrable sources were discovered during composition.*

---

prior.title: EU 2023/959 — Directive amending the EU Emissions Trading System
prior.summary: Directive (EU) 2023/959 amends Directive 2003/87/EC to (a) extend the EU ETS to maritime transport from 2024 with phased surrender (40%/70%/100% across 2024–2026), (b) tighten the cap and rebasing for stationary installations and aviation, (c) phase out free allocation to aviation by 2026, and (d) establish ETS2 for buildings, road transport and small industry from 2027.
prior.what_is_it: Directive (EU) 2023/959 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 10 May 2023 amending Directive 2003/87/EC establishing a system for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading within the Union, and Decision (EU) 2015/1814. The legal foundation for EU ETS scope expansion to maritime, ETS2 (buildings + road transport from 2027), and aviation auction-only transition.
prior.why_matters: Single most consequential EU climate instrument for forwarders. ETS surcharges on ocean carriers, full-auction aviation allowances, and the new ETS2 layer on road fuels combine to push carbon costs into every EU-touching freight lane. Customer-facing 'EU ETS included?' language must be accurate; double-billing or under-recovery in carrier ETS pass-through clauses is a live risk.
key_data: Maritime ETS phase-in: 40% of 2024 emissions, 70% of 2025, 100% from 2026
Maritime scope: CO₂ from 2024; CH₄ and N₂O added from 2026
Aviation: free allocation phased out by 2026; 100% auctioning thereafter
ETS2 (buildings, road transport, small industry): MRV from 2025, full operation from 2027
Linear reduction factor: increased to drive faster cap reduction
Market Stability Reserve: extended and reinforced
prior.operational_impact: Ocean: ETS surcharge governance for EU-touching voyages, with phased 40%/70%/100% surrender ramp through 2026 plus N₂O/CH₄ scope addition in 2026. Air: aviation allowances move to 100% auctioning by 2026, accelerating SAF substitution economics. Road: ETS2 on transport fuels from 2027 will pass through to diesel/HVO pump prices on EU road lanes. Customs/Sales: standardised customer FAQs on EU carbon costs across all three modes.
open_questions: Carrier-by-carrier ETS surcharge methodologies and how forwarder pass-through clauses index to EUA price
ETS2 retail-level pass-through path on road diesel and the timing of carrier surcharge adjustments
Treatment of non-EU port voyages (50% scope rule) under MRV reporting
prior.reasoning: CRITICAL — binding multi-modal carbon pricing instrument with active 2024–2027 phase-in across ocean, air and road. Defines the cost-pass-through environment for every EU-touching freight lane.

prior.title: New Brunswick Environment and Local Government Department - Climate Action and Water Strategy Resources
prior.summary: New Brunswick's Environment and Local Government department landing page providing access to climate change initiatives, water management strategies, and local governance reforms. Key resources include the Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, Water Strategy 2018-2028, Output-Based Pricing System for large emitters, and various environmental monitoring tools.
prior.full_brief:
# New Brunswick Environment and Local Government — Climate Action and Water Strategy Resources

## Market Signal Brief

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

**Signal type:** Provincial regulatory and policy acceleration — climate, carbon pricing, and water resource governance, New Brunswick, Canada.

**What is moving:** New Brunswick's Environment and Local Government (ELG) Department is executing a multi-instrument climate and water governance programme with three active concurrent streams: (1) the Climate Change Action Plan (CCAP) 2022–2027, a 30-new-action decarbonisation and resilience roadmap with measurable milestone deadlines running through 2030; (2) the New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System (NB OBPS), an intensity-based carbon pricing mechanism in force since January 2021, covering large industrial emitters, with a price escalation trajectory set to reach CAD 170/tCO₂e by 2030; and (3) the Water Strategy 2018–2028, a 10-year framework under active mid-term review, with a 2024 Five-Year Review now published.

**What triggered it:** The CCAP 2022–2027 was triggered by legislated provincial GHG reduction targets. The province's 2024–2025 Progress Report records a 42% emission reduction since 2005 and states the province "is on track to meet its legislated provincial target of 10.7 Mt by 2030." *Source: New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change — Climate Change Action Plan and Progress Reports, Government of New Brunswick (GNB), 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html.

The NB OBPS was triggered by the federal OBPS framework; New Brunswick transitioned large industrial emitters from the federal output-based pricing system to a provincial OBPS from January 2021. *Source: Canada – New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership (ICAP), accessed 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

The Water Strategy's 2024 Five-Year Review is listed as a published document under the Water Strategy 2018–2028 page. *Source: A Water Strategy for New Brunswick 2018–2028, GNB ELG, accessed 2025. https://www2.gnb.ca/content/gnb/en/departments/elg/environment/content/water/content/water_strategy.html.

**Carbon price escalation FACT:** The NB OBPS follows "the same price trajectory, rising CAD 15 (USD 10.73) each year until 2030, resulting in a price of CAD 170 (USD 121.60) per tCO2e in 2030. The price for the 2026 compliance is CAD 110 (USD 78.68)." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**Current allowance price FACT:** Set price 2025: CAD 95 (USD 67.95) per tCO₂e. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**Revenue and fund recycling FACT:** "The list of funded projects for 2025 and 2026, as of May 2025, includes 56 projects led by 12 different departments or organizations for a total value of CAD 58.4 million (USD 41.8 million)." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Government of New Brunswick — ELG Department / Climate Change Secretariat:** The primary driver. The ELG Department administers the CCAP, NB OBPS, and Water Strategy concurrently. The department's stated intent, as reflected in the CCAP 2022–2027, is "to address New Brunswick's climate change priorities and ultimately make the lives of all New Brunswickers more sustainable, affordable and resilient in the face of climate change." *Source: New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, GNB, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html.

**Large industrial emitters (15 covered entities as of 2024):** As of 2024, 15 facilities participate in the NB OBPS, "including seven voluntary opt-in market participants." These are the compliance-obligated parties under the carbon pricing instrument. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**CCAP 2022–2027 new action — zero-emission freight:** The province has committed to "Support the transition to zero-emission freight transportation" as a new action in the 2022–2027 plan. *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022–2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php. This positions the provincial government as an active driver of freight decarbonisation expectations, not merely an industrial emitter regulator.

**EV and clean fuels targets:** The CCAP 2022–2027 includes a commitment to "Use incentives and programs to achieve 6 percent of recent light-duty vehicle sales by electric vehicles by 2025 and 50 percent by 2030," and to "Enable local production and use of renewable natural gas (RNG) and clean hydrogen to ensure New Brunswickers have access to decarbonized fuels by 2030." *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.

**Net-zero by 2050 target FACT:** The CCAP 2022–2027 includes committing to "reaching net-zero emissions by 2050 and developing a net-zero blueprint by 2025." *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php. ICAP corroborates: GHG reduction targets include "By 2050: Net-zero emissions (New Brunswick's Climate Change Action Plan)." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**Federal government alignment:** The NB OBPS "applies to the same sectors and GHGs as the federal system, and follows the same price trajectory." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system. Federal–provincial alignment is an active strategic lever NB is using to maintain its own system rather than default back to federal administration.

**Analytical inference:** The province's stated commitment to support zero-emission freight alongside escalating carbon prices and a net-zero-by-2050 blueprint creates a policy environment where shippers and forwarders operating in or through New Brunswick may face client and carrier expectations around emissions documentation and clean transport options before a formal transport-sector mandate is codified.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Carbon price trajectory (confirmed FACT):** The price escalates CAD 15/year through 2030. 2025: CAD 95. 2026: CAD 110. 2030: CAD 170/tCO₂e. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**46% GHG reduction target by 2030 (confirmed FACT):** ICAP records: "By 2030: 46% reduction below 2005 levels ('New Brunswick's Climate Change Action Plan')." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**CCAP milestone actions with dated triggers:** The UNB summary lists multiple dated CCAP 2022–2027 actions that function as conversion events:
- Develop a Clean Electricity Strategy by 2025 for achieving net-zero electricity emissions by 2035. *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.
- Phase out heating oil by 2030. *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.
- Building energy performance labelling and disclosure requirements targeted by 2030. *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.
- RNG and clean hydrogen refuelling infrastructure by 2030, with a proportional blend investigation by 2025. *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.

**OBPS scope expansion signal:** The NB OBPS currently covers 15 facilities in mining, industry, and power. The opt-in threshold is 10,000 tCO₂e. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**Analytical inference:** If the province publishes a minimum blend mandate for RNG and/or clean hydrogen in or after 2025 (as signalled in the CCAP action above), this constitutes a direct conversion event for road freight operators relying on compressed natural gas or diesel fuelling in New Brunswick.

**MRV standard update (confirmed FACT):** "New Brunswick published a new version of 'The Reporting and Reduction of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standard' in May 2025. This version contains a new section on the reporting of renewable natural gas for the purpose of calculating the total regulated emissions; an expanded definition of accredited verification bodies; and the requirement for entities to use IPCC Fifth Assessment Report Global Warming Potential values starting from their 2025 emissions data." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

**Operational implication:** The May 2025 MRV standard update — specifically the requirement to use IPCC AR5 Global Warming Potential values from 2025 data — affects any OBPS-covered facility providing emission factor data to supply-chain partners, including forwarders assembling Scope 3 reporting chains sourcing from NB industrial sites.

**Severity:** MONITORING. The CCAP 2022–2027 is mid-cycle. The carbon price is confirmed on a published trajectory. Several CCAP milestone dates (2025, 2026, 2027, 2030) remain future conversion points. Re-check window: Q1 2026, to assess whether the 2025 CCAP milestone actions (net-zero blueprint, clean electricity strategy, RNG blend investigation, CCAP progress report) have been published and whether any convert a signal into a formal obligation.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**Carbon cost pass-through — road and ocean freight serving NB industrial clients:**
- Current NB OBPS price: CAD 95/tCO₂e (2025). Compliance-obligated facilities include industrial customers of freight services. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.
- Price rises to CAD 110 in 2026 and CAD 170 by 2030. Facilities passing carbon cost through to inbound logistics contracts will increase freight cost pressure at each annual step.
- Analytical inference: For workspaces moving high-value or time-sensitive freight (live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production) into or out of New Brunswick — whether by air via Fredericton/Moncton gateways or by road on Trans-Canada corridors — the immediate compliance exposure is indirect (through carriers and industrial clients), but Scope 3 emissions data requests from NB-based clients subject to OBPS reporting will increase as their own MRV obligations tighten.

**Air freight — no direct NB OBPS exposure (current):** The NB OBPS covers power, industry, and mining. Aircraft operators and freight forwarders are not among the 15 covered entities. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any future scope expansion under the Climate Change Act could extend to aviation-adjacent operations or freight forwarder activity requires legal review of the Climate Change Act and the Reduction of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Regulation.

**Road freight — clean fuels signal:** The CCAP 2022–2027 action to support zero-emission freight and investigate RNG/clean hydrogen blend minimums by 2025 signals potential future fuel-cost and fleet-transition pressure for road carriers operating in New Brunswick. Current rate of impact on road freight operators: *Specific cost figure not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

**Enforcement / non-compliance cost (confirmed FACT):** "Covered entities that fail to fulfill a compliance obligation must pay the unfulfilled amount with interest at a rate set out in the New Brunswick Regulation 84-247 under the 'Revenue Administration Act' (currently ~0.76% per month compounded monthly or 9.5% per year)." *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system. This applies to OBPS-covered industrial facilities, not directly to freight forwarders, but clients in that class facing penalty exposure may pass compliance cost pressure through procurement and inbound logistics contracts.

**Water Strategy operational risk:** For workspaces with time-sensitive cargo (e.g., live events, film and TV) moving through New Brunswick, water-related regulatory development under the 2018–2028 strategy is operationally relevant primarily where infrastructure decisions (port access, road flood risk, watershed management) interact with cargo corridors. The 2024 Five-Year Review is published; its specific operational implications for freight infrastructure are not detailed in the source blocks available. *Specific infrastructure implications of the 2024 Water Strategy Five-Year Review not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

---

# Competitive Implications

**No sourced competitor positioning data is available from the provided source blocks for this signal.** The sources are government and academic/NGO records, not trade or commercial intelligence. This section is omitted per the integrity rule rather than populated with inference.

**Analytical inference:** Workspaces that develop NB-specific emissions documentation capability — including understanding of OBPS MRV standards and the May 2025 reporting update — before competitors do will be positioned to respond to Scope 3 data requests from NB industrial clients ahead of competitors who are not tracking provincial carbon pricing developments. The 15-facility OBPS community is small; the commercial opportunity is narrow but identifiable for logistics operators with existing NB industrial client relationships.

**COMPETITIVE EDGE** label applies at the intersection of Scope 3 reporting readiness and NB OBPS MRV alignment. *Analytical inference:* No named competitor activity sourced from available blocks.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

1. New Brunswick operates its own carbon pricing system — the NB OBPS — separate from the federal system, in force since January 2021. The 2025 carbon price is CAD 95/tCO₂e, rising to CAD 110 in 2026 and CAD 170 by 2030. This is a confirmed published trajectory, not an estimate.
   *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

2. NB published an updated Reporting and Reduction of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standard in May 2025, requiring use of IPCC AR5 Global Warming Potential values from 2025 data onward. Clients subject to the OBPS are operating under this updated standard now.
   *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

3. The province has committed to support the transition to zero-emission freight as a named action in its 2022–2027 CCAP, and is investigating RNG and clean hydrogen blend minimums with a 2025 decision point. This may affect road carrier fuel cost and fuel access in the province.
   *Source: UNB Sustainability Summary, UNB, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php.

4. The province achieved a 42% emission reduction since 2005 and reports it is on track for its legislated 10.7 Mt by 2030 target.
   *Source: New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, GNB, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not claim NB OBPS directly applies to freight forwarders or carriers — it currently covers 15 industrial, power, and mining facilities. *Legal Confirmation Required:* scope extension to freight operations under future rulemaking requires counsel review.
- Do not assert a specific RNG or clean hydrogen blend obligation on road freight operators — the CCAP action is to "investigate and, if appropriate, set a minimum proportion" by 2025. A mandate has not been confirmed in sourced content.
- Do not claim the Water Strategy's 2024 Five-Year Review carries specific freight infrastructure implications — the document is published but its operational freight relevance is not confirmed in available source content.

**Questions to pose to NB-based industrial clients:**
- Has your facility updated its emissions baseline methodology to IPCC AR5 GWP values for 2025 reporting?
- Is your inbound logistics procurement language referencing NB OBPS compliance obligations or Scope 3 data formats?
- Has your facility accessed the 2023-established NB OBPS Industry Fund for GHG reduction projects? This fund may affect capital allocation for inbound/outbound logistics infrastructure.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**ACTION: Scope 3 data readiness for NB industrial clients** — COMPETITIVE EDGE
Map which current clients are NB OBPS-covered facilities (mandatory threshold: 50,000 tCO₂e; opt-in threshold: 10,000 tCO₂e). *Source: GNB OBPS page, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html. For those clients, confirm whether their Scope 3 reporting to OBPS requires freight-specific emission factor data, and whether the workspace's current emission factor methodology is aligned with IPCC AR5 GWP values (the new NB standard from 2025 data). This is a data and contract clause action, not a compliance filing action, and can be initiated now.

**ACTION: Monitor the 2025 CCAP milestone deliverables** — MONITORING
The following CCAP actions are timed to 2025 and may convert signals to obligations or commercial requirements: (a) net-zero blueprint publication; (b) Clean Electricity Strategy release; (c) RNG/clean hydrogen blend proportion investigation decision; (d) CCAP 2024–2025 Progress Report (already published, confirming 42% reduction). Set a re-check in Q1 2026. *Source: GNB Climate Change Action Plan page, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html.

**ACTION: Track the NB OBPS Industry Fund for client infrastructure signals** — MONITORING
The 2023-established OBPS Industry Fund has already supported 56 projects totalling CAD 58.4 million across 12 departments and organisations for 2025–2026. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system. Projects funded via this mechanism may include energy, transport, or site infrastructure investments at facilities that are direct freight clients. Monitoring fund project lists (published annually by the Climate Change Fund) gives early visibility into client capital plans.

**ACTION: Verify road carrier fuel access in NB ahead of any RNG/clean hydrogen blend decision** — MONITORING
Engage NB road carrier partners to confirm current diesel and CNG/LNG fuelling infrastructure status and any fleet transition plans in advance of a potential provincial minimum blend announcement. No obligation is confirmed; this is early positioning. *Analytical inference:* An announcement of a minimum RNG proportion in 2025 or 2026 would create a short-transition-window cost event for road-heavy freight operations through NB.

**COST ALERT — carbon price step-up 2026:** The NB OBPS price steps from CAD 95 to CAD 110 on the 2026 compliance year (reports due December 15, 2027, for that period). OBPS-covered industrial clients will face a 15.8% unit price increase. Workspaces with cost-plus or index-linked logistics contracts serving NB industrial sites should review whether those contracts contain a carbon cost pass-through provision that will activate at this step. *Source: ICAP, Canada – New Brunswick OBPS, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system.

---

prior.title: Brazil National Policy on Alternative Fuels (PNCA)
prior.summary: Establishes framework for biofuel and hydrogen adoption in freight transport, including mandatory blending requirements for heavy-duty vehicles and incentives for green corridor development. Creates certification system for sustainable logistics operations in Latin America's largest freight market.
prior.full_brief:
# Brazil National Policy on Alternative Fuels (PNCA) — Regulatory Fact Document

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers Brazil's National Policy on Alternative Fuels (PNCA) as constituted by two interlocking legislative instruments: the National Biofuels Policy (RenovaBio), established by Law No. 13,576/2017, and the Fuels of the Future Law (Federal Law No. 14,993/2024), enacted on 8 October 2024. Together these instruments set mandatory biofuel blend targets, carbon credit obligations, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) penetration schedules, green diesel blending requirements, and biomethane incentive programmes that directly affect fuel procurement costs and compliance obligations across road freight, air cargo, and maritime operations in Brazil.

The brief is addressed to workspaces operating as freight forwarders, importers, and logistics operators moving cargo by air (primary), road (secondary), and ocean (tertiary) on trade lanes touching Brazil. It covers the transport-sector-facing provisions only; upstream production licensing, oil and gas concession terms, and power-sector provisions are out of scope.

**Convention notes:** Brazilian legislation is cited by its official law number and enactment date. All blend percentages are volume-on-volume unless stated otherwise. Currency references are Brazilian Real (R$). Penalty figures are sourced from WBA reporting and are denominated in R$ as originally published. The document reflects sources current as of 23 February 2026 (ICCT policy update date, the most recent corroborating source).

*Source: Climate Change Laws of the World, Federal Law No. 14,993/2024 on Fuels of the Future, Climate Policy Radar/LSE, 8 October 2024. https://climate-laws.org/document/federal-law-no-14-993-2024-on-fuels-of-the-future_8b5e

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

*Source: Global Legal Insights, Energy Laws and Regulations 2026: Brazil, 22 December 2025. https://www.globallegalinsights.com/practice-areas/energy-laws-and-regulations/brazil/

---

# 2. What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## 2a. What the Regulation Is

Brazil's alternative fuels policy framework rests on two foundational laws.

**RenovaBio (Law No. 13,576/2017):** FACT — Law No. 13,576/2017 instituted the National Biofuels Policy (RenovaBio), creating a carbon credit market to encourage the reduction of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in the fuel sector. *Source: Global Legal Insights, Energy Laws and Regulations 2026: Brazil, 22 December 2025. https://www.globallegalinsights.com/practice-areas/energy-laws-and-regulations/brazil/

Under RenovaBio, fuel distributors are assigned annual compulsory GHG emission reduction targets, which they meet by retiring CBIO carbon credits. FACT — RenovaBio carries a target to offset 48.09 Mt CO₂ in 2026 and 72.54 Mt CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e) by 2034 compared with a 2018 baseline. *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

**Fuels of the Future Law (Federal Law No. 14,993/2024):** FACT — Brazil's Fuels of the Future Bill (Federal Law No. 14,993/2024), enacted on October 8, 2024, establishes national programs to promote renewable fuels—including sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) and green diesel—regulates carbon capture and storage (CCS), and sets new blending mandates for ethanol in gasoline (22–35%) and biodiesel in diesel (20% by 2030). *Source: Climate Change Laws of the World, Federal Law No. 14,993/2024 on Fuels of the Future, 8 October 2024. https://climate-laws.org/document/federal-law-no-14-993-2024-on-fuels-of-the-future_8b5e

The Fuels of the Future Law is described in authoritative ICCT analysis as establishing four transport-sector programmes: Low Carbon Mobility, the National Program for Green Diesel (PNDV), the National Program for Sustainable Aviation Fuels (ProBioQAV), and the National Program for Biomethane. FACT — "This legislative framework complements RenovaBio, Brazil's transport fuels policy, which was also updated in 2024 with new obligations and penalties." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

## 2b. Why It Applies to the Workspace

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces operating road freight movements through Brazil are directly exposed to mandatory biodiesel blend increases that raise diesel input costs on every truck movement within Brazilian territory, regardless of whether the workspace holds the fuel purchasing contract. The blend mandate is embedded in the national fuel supply chain upstream of the pump; any road freight operator or freight forwarder whose Brazilian road carriers purchase retail diesel absorbs the cost pass-through.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces moving cargo by air on routes touching Brazilian airports are exposed to the SAF penetration schedule under ProBioQAV, which sets GHG reduction targets for domestic civil aviation and will flow into carrier surcharges and procurement practices. As a freight forwarder sourcing capacity from carriers operating domestic Brazilian sectors, the workspace faces indirect cost exposure.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces handling cargo with Brazilian ocean port calls face secondary exposure through the RenovaBio CBIO credit obligation on fuel distributors, which feeds into bunkering cost structures at Brazilian ports, though the transmission mechanism to freight rates is indirect and carrier-specific.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any obligation under RenovaBio or the Fuels of the Future Law attaches directly to the workspace in its role as freight forwarder (as opposed to fuel distributor, producer, airline, or importer of record) requires counsel review of the specific compliance chain under each programme.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: Climate Change Laws of the World, Federal Law No. 14,993/2024 on Fuels of the Future, 8 October 2024. https://climate-laws.org/document/federal-law-no-14-993-2024-on-fuels-of-the-future_8b5e

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**COST ALERT — Road freight cost baseline in Brazil is structurally rising through 2030.**

The biodiesel blend mandate in transport diesel increases by 1 percentage point per year from 15% in 2025 to 20% by 2030. FACT — "Biodiesel: 15% blend rate by volume starting in 2025, increasing 1% per year and reaching 20% by 2030." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces that include Brazilian domestic road freight in their cost models or client quotations, each annual 1-percentage-point blend step raises the biodiesel cost component in diesel and should be modelled into forward pricing reviews annually through 2030. Current carrier-specific cost impact is not publicly available; the workspace should seek confirmation from Brazilian road carriers on how they are pricing the incremental blend cost into freight rates.

**MONITORING — SAF mandate for domestic aviation enters first phase 2027, with ratchet through 2037.**

FACT — ProBioQAV targets to "Reduce GHG emissions of domestic flights by 1% by 2027 and 2% by 2029; by 2037, achieve a 10% reduction relative to the fossil jet fuel baseline with the use of SAF." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces shipping air cargo on domestic Brazilian sectors (e.g., transhipments at GRU, GIG, or BSB feeding onward domestic capacity) should begin tracking how Brazilian carriers serving these routes are planning SAF procurement, as the 2027 first compliance window is now within a standard carrier contract horizon. Re-check window: Q4 2026, when ANP regulatory implementation rules for ProBioQAV's 2027 target are expected to be operationally specified.

**MONITORING — Biomethane GHG targets commence 1 January 2026; fine exposure is material.**

FACT — "The law also sets annual greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reduction targets for natural gas producers and importers, which will be established by the National Energy Policy Council (CNPE) starting on 1 January 2026." Failure to meet targets results in "fines ranging from R$ 100,000 to R$ 50,000,000, depending on the severity and benefits gained by the violator." *Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces whose Brazilian logistics partners operate compressed natural gas (CNG) or LNG fleets — relevant to road freight decarbonisation — should confirm with those partners how biomethane compliance targets affect vehicle fuel procurement and cost. Direct obligation on the workspace as freight forwarder is not established in sourced content.

**Penalty slot — FACT:** *Specific penalty figures for biodiesel/ethanol blend non-compliance under the Fuels of the Future Law's transport programmes are not available from primary sources as of 23 February 2026.* For the biomethane programme, fines of R$ 100,000 to R$ 50,000,000 are sourced from WBA industry reporting (see above). For RenovaBio CBIO non-compliance by fuel distributors, the ANP publishes a list of sanctions; *specific penalty quantum for CBIO non-compliance not available from primary sources as of 23 February 2026.*

*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/
*Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio — Lista de sanções (menu reference). https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The compliance obligations under PNCA fall primarily on Brazilian fuel producers, importers, distributors, airlines, and natural gas sector participants — not on freight forwarders directly. The workspace's exposure is structural (through cost pass-through) and reputational/commercial (through client emissions reporting requirements), not primary regulatory.

**Fuel distributors** are the primary obligated parties under RenovaBio: they hold annual compulsory CBIO retirement targets set by ANP and CNPE. FACT — "Annual retirement of carbon credits for fuel distributors, with a target to offset 48.09 Mt CO₂ in 2026 and 72.54 Mt CO₂e by 2034 compared with a 2018 baseline." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

**Airlines operating domestic Brazilian routes** are the primary obligated parties under ProBioQAV's SAF targets. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether a freight forwarder purchasing block-space or ACMI capacity on domestic Brazilian sectors is considered a regulated party or bears any direct SAF compliance obligation under ProBioQAV requires counsel review.

**Natural gas producers and importers** are the primary obligated parties under the biomethane programme's GHG reduction targets. Freight forwarders whose Brazilian road carriers use CNG/biomethane fleets are downstream of the compliance chain.

*Analytical inference:* The workspace's most direct compliance chain interaction is at the Scope 3 emissions reporting level: as clients — particularly those subject to CSRD or ISSB disclosure requirements — seek emissions data from freight service providers for Brazilian legs, the workspace will need to track how the blend mandate and SAF requirements change the carbon intensity of the fuel mix underlying reported transport emissions for Brazil operations.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The exact placement of the workspace in the compliance chain under each of the four Fuels of the Future Law programmes (ProBioQAV, PNDV, Low Carbon Mobility, Biomethane) requires counsel review of the law's specific definitions of obligated parties.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: Global Legal Insights, Energy Laws and Regulations 2026: Brazil, 22 December 2025. https://www.globallegalinsights.com/practice-areas/energy-laws-and-regulations/brazil/

---

# 5. Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

*No content for this section as of 23 February 2026: ANP has not published a consolidated PNCA implementation guidance document accessible through the sourced blocks. Individual programme regulations (e.g., ANP resolutions implementing ProBioQAV and PNDV) have not been provided in source content and their publication status is not confirmed.*

---

# 6. Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**MONITORING — CNPE annual GHG targets for biomethane to be set from 1 January 2026 (ongoing)**

FACT — The law sets annual GHG emission reduction targets "which will be established by the National Energy Policy Council (CNPE) starting on 1 January 2026." FACT — "These targets involve increasing the share of biomethane in natural gas consumption, beginning with a 1% reduction and potentially reaching up to 10%." *Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

*Analytical inference:* The CNPE's first annual target issuance for the biomethane programme is the near-term regulatory event to monitor; it will define the enforceable percentage reduction for 2026 and set the template for annual escalation. Re-check window: Q2 2026 for confirmation of the 2026 CNPE target resolution.

**MONITORING — ProBioQAV first SAF compliance window: 2027**

FACT — ProBioQAV targets reduction of GHG emissions of domestic flights "by 1% by 2027." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

*Analytical inference:* ANP implementation regulations specifying how airlines demonstrate compliance with the 1% GHG reduction target — whether through physical SAF blending, book-and-claim certificates, or other mechanisms — have not yet been identified in source content. Publication of these rules is the key pending event for air-cargo cost modelling on Brazilian domestic sectors. Re-check window: Q4 2026.

**MONITORING — RenovaBio 2026 individual distributor targets published**

FACT — The ANP portal references "Metas individuais compulsórias definitivas de redução de emissões de gases causadores do efeito estufa para 2026" (Definitive compulsory individual GHG reduction targets for 2026) as a published item. *Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio. https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio

*Operational implication:* The 2026 distributor targets are now in force. Workspaces seeking to understand how fuel distributors serving their Brazilian road carriers are positioned against their 2026 CBIO retirement obligations can reference the ANP portal for published target data.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/
*Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio. https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio

---

# 7. Threshold Questions

**Q1: Does the Fuels of the Future Law impose any direct obligation on freight forwarders?**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* Based on sourced content, the law's transport-sector obligations are structured around fuel distributors (RenovaBio CBIO), airlines (ProBioQAV), natural gas producers and importers (biomethane targets), and vehicle manufacturers/fleet operators (Low Carbon Mobility life-cycle assessment). Whether freight forwarders purchasing capacity — or their Brazilian logistics partners — are captured by any programme obligation requires counsel review of Law No. 14,993/2024 in full Portuguese text.

**Q2: Does the biodiesel blend mandate apply to diesel purchased by third-party truckers carrying the workspace's cargo in Brazil?**
FACT (definitional) — The mandate applies to all road fuel: the ANP and Ministry of Mines and Energy regulate "all road fuel" under the biofuel mandates standard. *Source: Transport Policy, Brazil: Fuels: Biofuels. https://www.transportpolicy.net/standard/brazil-fuels-biofuels/
*Analytical inference:* Because the blend is upstream of the pump, any diesel-powered road carrier in Brazil is fuelling with the mandated blend. The workspace does not need to take any action to comply; the obligation sits with the distributor. However, the cost of the incremental blend passes through to trucking rates.

**Q3: Does the green diesel (PNDV) programme create a separate obligation from the biodiesel mandate?**
FACT — PNDV covers blending "up to 3% renewable ('green') diesel by volume into transport diesel, beginning in 2025." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Analytical inference:* PNDV adds a renewable diesel component on top of the biodiesel mandate; its "up to 3%" framing suggests a ceiling rather than a fixed mandate at this stage. Whether it translates into a compulsory blending requirement at the distributor level in 2025 is not fully resolved by sourced content. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the binding character of the PNDV 3% figure — whether it is a cap, a target, or a mandate — requires review of the underlying implementing regulation.

**Q4: Is there an ethanol mandate affecting road freight?**
FACT — "Ethanol: 27% blend rate by volume starting in 2025; legally permitted to vary between 22% and 35%." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Operational implication:* Ethanol blending in gasoline does not directly affect diesel-powered freight vehicles; it is relevant to flex-fuel light commercial vehicles and light logistics operations using gasoline-blended fuel.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: Transport Policy, Brazil: Fuels: Biofuels. https://www.transportpolicy.net/standard/brazil-fuels-biofuels/

---

# 8. Substantive Requirements

## 8a. Biodiesel Blend Mandate (Road Freight — Primary Impact)

FACT — The Fuels of the Future Law establishes a biodiesel blend rate "by volume starting in 2025, increasing 1% per year and reaching 20% by 2030; legally permitted to vary between 13% and 25%." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

- 2025 baseline: 15% biodiesel in transport diesel.
- Escalation: +1 percentage point per year.
- 2030 target: 20% biodiesel in transport diesel.
- Permitted variance band: 13%–25%.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating road freight in Brazil, the per-kilometre fuel cost of every diesel truck movement rises annually as the biodiesel component (typically priced at a premium to fossil diesel) increases. The magnitude of the cost delta depends on the spread between soy biodiesel and fossil diesel prices at Brazilian pumps; *current rate differential not publicly available in sourced content.*

*Operational implication:* By 2030, Brazilian road freight diesel will contain a 20% biodiesel blend — higher than the EU's current Renewable Energy Directive blending levels. Workspaces with clients requiring Scope 3 emissions reporting should note that higher biodiesel blending reduces the carbon intensity of Brazilian road freight emissions calculations, which may be a differentiator in client sustainability reporting.

## 8b. Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Mandate — ProBioQAV (Air Freight — Primary Impact)

FACT — ProBioQAV targets reducing "GHG emissions of domestic flights by 1% by 2027 and 2% by 2029; by 2037, achieve a 10% reduction relative to the fossil jet fuel baseline with the use of SAF." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

The programme applies to domestic civil aviation. FACT — Federal Law No. 14,993/2024 sets targets "requiring airlines to use at least 1% SAF by 2027 and 10% by 2037." *Source: Climate Change Laws of the World, Federal Law No. 14,993/2024 on Fuels of the Future, 8 October 2024. https://climate-laws.org/document/federal-law-no-14-993-2024-on-fuels-of-the-future_8b5e

*Analytical inference:* Brazilian airlines operating domestic routes — which carry a significant share of air cargo transhipments between São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and regional hubs — will begin incurring SAF procurement costs from 2027. These costs are likely to flow into fuel surcharges applied to air freight shipments on domestic Brazilian sectors. Workspaces routing time-sensitive cargo through Brazilian domestic air sectors should anticipate a SAF surcharge component appearing on carrier invoices from 2027.

*Operational implication:* For the cargo verticals the workspace handles (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian), Brazilian domestic air legs — where these exist in the routing — will carry an emerging SAF cost from 2027. The impact on time-critical humanitarian or live-event shipments may not be price-sensitive, but it is a line-item that should appear in client cost disclosures.

## 8c. Green Diesel Programme — PNDV (Road Freight — Secondary Impact)

FACT — PNDV covers blending "up to 3% renewable ('green') diesel by volume into transport diesel, beginning in 2025." *Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/

*Analytical inference:* Green diesel (hydrotreated vegetable oil, HVO) commands a market premium over fossil diesel. The PNDV 3% ceiling introduces an additional renewable component into transport diesel beyond the biodiesel mandate, adding a further cost layer. Whether this is a binding mandate or a permissive ceiling is not resolved in sourced content; *Legal Confirmation Required* (see Section 7, Q3).

## 8d. Biomethane Programme — Road Freight Alternative Fuel Pathway

FACT — The National Programme for the Decarbonisation of Natural Gas Producers and Importers and Incentives for Biomethane aims to "stimulate the production and consumption of biomethane and biogas by promoting new projects across the production chain." It also aims to "encourage the manufacture, sale, purchase, and use of methane-powered vehicles, the conversion of vehicles running on other fuels to methane, and the replacement of diesel engines used in vehicles with new methane-powered engines." *Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

*Industry interpretation:* An industry participant — the president of the Brazilian Biogas Association (ABiogás) — stated that "biomethane could replace diesel in trucks, promoting both economic and environmental sustainability, as this fuel emits up to 89% fewer greenhouse gases compared to natural gas and is cheaper than fossil diesel." This is a stated industry position, not a regulatory finding. *Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces whose Brazilian trucking partners operate CNG/biomethane-capable fleets, the Fuels of the Future Law's pipeline injection provisions — allowing "injecting biomethane into existing pipelines" — may over time expand the availability of biomethane as a diesel substitute, with the 89% GHG reduction claim providing a material Scope 3 emissions improvement for Brazilian road freight if it can be verified at scale.

## 8e. RenovaBio CBIO Credit System — Fuel Distributor Compliance

FACT — RenovaBio's CBIO credit mechanism operates through annual compulsory individual GHG reduction targets for fuel distributors, which they meet by retiring CBIOs. ANP publishes "Metas individuais compulsórias definitivas de redução de emissões de gases causadores do efeito estufa para 2026" (Definitive compulsory individual targets for 2026). *Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio. https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio

*Analytical inference:* The CBIO credit price is a pass-through cost embedded in the wholesale fuel price paid by distributors and ultimately by fleet operators. Workspaces should treat CBIO cost movements as a structural component of Brazilian fuel pricing, separate from commodity diesel price fluctuations. *Current CBIO credit market price not available from primary sources as of 23 February 2026.*

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio. https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio
*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

---

# 10. Registration and Reporting Obligations

No registration or direct reporting obligation has been identified in sourced content that attaches to freight forwarders under PNCA or the Fuels of the Future Law. Obligations identified in sourced content fall on:

- Fuel distributors: annual CBIO retirement and compliance reporting to ANP under RenovaBio.
- Biofuel producers and importers: certification via Agente Certificador de Origem (ACO) for CBIO issuance.
- Natural gas producers and importers: annual GHG reduction target compliance reporting to CNPE from 1 January 2026.

*Analytical inference:* Freight forwarders with Brazilian operations that are themselves ISO 14083 or GHG Protocol Scope 3 reporters, or whose clients require Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) data, will need to ensure that Brazilian carrier-level fuel intensity data — reflecting the blend mandates — is captured in their emissions calculation methodology. This is an indirect reporting consequence, not a direct statutory obligation.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any provision of the Fuels of the Future Law or RenovaBio imposes registration, reporting, or compliance demonstration obligations on freight forwarders, NVOCCs, or logistics operators (as distinct from fuel distributors, airlines, and producers) requires counsel review.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: ANP, Sobre o RenovaBio. https://www.gov.br/anp/pt-br/assuntos/renovabio/sobre-o-renovabio
*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

---

# 11. Operational System Requirements

No direct operational system requirements (tracking infrastructure, reporting dashboards, supplier onboarding mandates) have been identified in sourced content that attach to freight forwarders.

The following operational actions are identified as *Analytical inferences* from the regulatory structure:

**Fuel cost modelling update — road freight Brazil:** *Operational implication:* The biodiesel blend escalation (15% → 20%, 2025–2030) is a known, dated, annual cost driver. Cost models for Brazilian road freight moves should build in an annual review trigger aligned with each 1-percentage-point blend step. The workspace should request that Brazilian road carrier partners confirm how they price blend cost increases into freight rates.

**Air freight SAF surcharge tracking — Brazil domestic sectors:** *Operational implication:* Workspaces routing cargo through Brazilian domestic air sectors should add a SAF surcharge tracking field to carrier invoice monitoring from 2026, ahead of the first ProBioQAV compliance window in 2027.

**Scope 3 emissions methodology update — Brazil legs:** *Analytical inference:* As the biodiesel blend in Brazilian diesel rises and biomethane displaces fossil CNG in some truck fleets, the default emission factor for Brazilian road freight will diverge from global diesel defaults. Workspaces using GLEC Framework or ISO 14083 methodology for Brazilian Scope 3 Category 4 calculations should flag this to their emissions data team for an annual factor review.

*Source: ICCT, Mapping Brazil's latest fuels policies, 23 February 2026. https://theicct.org/publication/mapping-brazils-latest-fuels-policies-feb26/
*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

---

# 13. Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

**Biomethane as diesel replacement for Brazilian trucking:**
FACT — The WBA reports an industry participant claim that biomethane "emits up to 89% fewer greenhouse gases compared to natural gas and is cheaper than fossil diesel." The source for this claim within the WBA article is stated as CanalEnergia (Brazilian energy trade publication). *Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/

*Analytical inference:* If the 89% GHG reduction figure is verified against lifecycle assessment methodology (well-to-wheel), Brazilian biomethane trucking represents one of the highest-reduction alternative fuel pathways available for road freight globally. For workspaces whose clients set supplier Scope 3 reduction targets, Brazilian trucking partners using biomethane could be positioned as a best-available-technology option.

**Sugarcane ethanol GHG performance:**
FACT — "Sugarcane ethanol, the most common type of bio-ethanol in Brazil, has been assessed to reduce GHG emissions by 61% under EPA's Renewable Fuels Standard 2 and by 71% under the European Union's Renewable Energy Directive, default value." *Source: Transport Policy, Brazil: Fuels: Biofuels. https://www.transportpolicy.net/standard/brazil-fuels-biofuels/

*Analytical inference:* The high lifecycle GHG reduction of sugarcane ethanol — the feedstock underlying Brazil's 27% ethanol-in-gasoline mandate — provides context for why Brazil's overall fuel carbon intensity declines faster than biodiesel blend percentages alone suggest. This is relevant to workspaces making client-facing claims about the GHG intensity of Brazilian logistics operations.

**Soybean biodiesel GHG performance:**
FACT — "Soybean biodiesel, the most common type of biodiesel in Brazil, has been assessed to reduce GHG emissions by 57% under the Renewable Fuels Standard 2 and by 31% under the Renewable Energy Directive, default value." *Source: Transport Policy, Brazil: Fuels: Biofuels. https://www.transportpolicy.net/standard/brazil-fuels-biofuels/

*Analytical inference:* The wide divergence between US RFS2 (57%) and EU RED (31%) assessments of soybean biodiesel GHG reduction reflects different land-use change accounting methodologies. Workspaces making claims about the GHG impact of Brazil's biodiesel mandate should specify which methodology underpins their figure and avoid presenting either number as universal.

*Source: Transport Policy, Brazil: Fuels: Biofuels. https://www.transportpolicy.net/standard/brazil-fuels-biofuels/
*Source: World Biogas Association, Brazil advances with a new regulatory framework for biofuels, 28 October 2024. https://www.worldbiogasassociation.org/brazil-advances-with-a-new-regulatory-framework-for-biofuels/
