prior.title: 9th Multi-Stakeholder Forum on Science, Technology and Innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals (STI Forum 2024)
prior.summary: The UN's 9th annual STI Forum will convene May 9-10, 2024, focusing on science, technology, and innovation solutions for eradicating poverty and addressing multiple crises. The forum aligns with the 2024 High-Level Political Forum's in-depth reviews of SDGs 1, 2, 13, 16, and 17, emphasizing sustainable, resilient, and innovative solutions under the theme 'Science, technology and innovation for reinforcing the 2030 Agenda.'
prior.full_brief:
# 9th Multi-Stakeholder Forum on Science, Technology and Innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals (STI Forum 2024)

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

The 9th annual Multi-Stakeholder Forum on Science, Technology and Innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals (STI Forum 2024) was held on 9–10 May 2024 at the Trusteeship Council Chamber, UN Headquarters, New York, with side events running from 8–10 May 2024. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

The forum is convened annually by the President of the Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC). For the 2024 edition, ECOSOC President Her Excellency Paula Narváez appointed two co-chairs: Her Excellency Christina Markus Lassen, Ambassador and Permanent Representative of Denmark to the United Nations, and Her Excellency Inga Rhonda King, Ambassador and Permanent Representative of Saint Vincent and the Grenadines to the United Nations. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

The forum theme for 2024 is: *"Science, technology and innovation for reinforcing the 2030 Agenda and eradicating poverty in times of multiple crises: the effective delivery of sustainable, resilient and innovative solutions."* *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

The trigger for the 2024 edition's specific thematic focus is the alignment between the STI Forum theme and the theme of the 2024 High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development (HLPF), scheduled for 8–17 July 2024. As stated in the event background, the STI Forum theme is "closely aligned with that of the High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development (HLPF) … but will have an exclusive focus on the role and contributions of science, technology and innovation to sustainable development." *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

The 2024 HLPF conducted in-depth reviews of five SDGs: Goal 1 (end poverty), Goal 2 (end hunger), Goal 13 (climate action), Goal 16 (peaceful and inclusive societies), and Goal 17 (means of implementation and global partnership). The STI Forum focused its sessions accordingly. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

The STI Forum is one of four operational components of the UN Technology Facilitation Mechanism (TFM), which was launched under Paragraph 70 of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. The TFM's stated goal is to "facilitate multi-stakeholder collaboration and partnerships through the sharing of information, experiences, best practices and policy advice among Member States, civil society, the private sector, the scientific community, United Nations entities and other stakeholders." *Source: UN Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Institutional conveners and preparatory bodies:**

The forum is prepared by two institutional bodies operating jointly. The UN Interagency Task Team on STI for the SDGs (IATT), convened by UN DESA and UNCTAD, serves as the primary preparatory body. The second is the 10-Member Group of High-Level Representatives appointed by the Secretary-General. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

Together with UN DESA, UNCTAD co-organizes and contributes to the annual STI Forum. UNCTAD co-chairs IATT Work Stream 6 (Capacity Building in STI for SDGs) together with UNESCO. *Source: UNCTAD and the Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UNCTAD, 2024. https://unctad.org/topic/science-technology-and-innovation/technology-facilitation-mechanism.

IATT Work Stream 6 is currently supported by UNCTAD, UNESCO, UNIDO, UNU-MERIT, WIPO, UNDESA, UNEP, World Bank, ITU, UNESCWA, UNECA, UNECLAC, UNECE, and UNESCAP, and the European Commission's Joint Research Centre. *Source: UNCTAD and the Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UNCTAD, 2024. https://unctad.org/topic/science-technology-and-innovation/technology-facilitation-mechanism.

**Named multi-stakeholder participants:**

The International Science Council (ISC) co-coordinates the Science and Technology Major Group and mobilised experts from its membership and broader networks to contribute as speakers, deliver statements, and participate in official side events. The ISC brings together 45 international scientific Unions and Associations, over 140 national and regional scientific organizations including Academies and Research Councils, and 60 international Federations and Societies, as well as Young Academies and Associations. *Source: 9th STI Forum 2024 – International Science Council, ISC, 2024. https://council.science/events/sti-forum-2024/.

The ISC delegation at STI Forum 2024 included Morgan Seag (ISC Liaison to the UN System), Motoko Kotani (ISC Vice-President), Olubukola Oluranti Babalola (ISC Fellow), and Tshiamo Motshegwa (University of Botswana). *Source: 9th STI Forum 2024 – International Science Council, ISC, 2024. https://council.science/events/sti-forum-2024/.

**Sectoral stakeholder constituencies making statements at the pre-forum briefing (5 March 2024):**
- FAO
- ITU
- Business and Industry Major Group (BIMG)
- Major Group for Children and Youth (MGCY)
- Scientific and Technological Community Major Group (STCMC)

*Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

**Side event organising coalitions (selected, named):**

- The Scientific and Technological Community Major Group (co-led by the ISC and the World Federation of Engineering Organizations, WFEO) organised: *"Better leveraging Science, Technology, Innovation and Engineering for accelerating progress on the SDGs"*, 9 May 2024, 13:15–14:30 EST, Trusteeship Council Chamber. *Source: 9th STI Forum 2024 – International Science Council, ISC, 2024. https://council.science/events/sti-forum-2024/.

- Future Africa, in partnership with UNECA, UN DESA, and the Permanent Missions of South Africa, Ghana, Ethiopia, Cameroon, and Morocco, organised: *"Harnessing the STI Coalitions for Africa – a mechanism for stronger partnership to achieve the SDGs"*, 8 May 2024, 10:00–11:15 EST, Conference Room 11, UN HQ. *Source: 9th STI Forum 2024 – International Science Council, ISC, 2024. https://council.science/events/sti-forum-2024/.

- A total of 46 side events were organised by Member States, UN entities, Intergovernmental Organizations, and accredited non-governmental stakeholders, and selected representatives of universities, laboratories, scientific and engineering organizations. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

*Analytical inference:* The breadth of institutional co-convenors (UN DESA, UNCTAD, ECOSOC, 10-Member Group, ISC, WFEO, and 14+ IATT member agencies) and the 46-side-event volume signals a deliberate strategy of widening multi-stakeholder buy-in ahead of the 2030 deadline, rather than a narrow intergovernmental process. The commercial private sector (via BIMG) has a named seat at this table.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Immediate institutional output:**

The STI Forum produces a co-chairs' summary of discussions. Through this summary, the TFM provides "formal mandated input in support of the HLPF's SDG review and its mandated science-policy function." *Source: UN Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm.

*Analytical inference:* The co-chairs' summary of the 2024 STI Forum will feed directly into the 2024 HLPF (8–17 July 2024), and the HLPF's findings in turn inform UN General Assembly sessions and Member State SDG reporting cycles. Operators and freight forwarders appearing in HLPF-aligned national SDG strategies may face downstream reporting asks derived from this process.

**Roadmap Guidebook — second edition (in development, sourced timeline):**

The second edition of the Guidebook for the Development and Implementation of STI for SDGs Roadmaps for the Six Transitions is currently in development, coordinated by UN DESA. Named contributors include the European Commission/Joint Research Centre, Future Africa, the International Science Council, ISC Intelligence in Science, and UNESCO. The draft of the second edition is anticipated to be published on the IATT website in 2026, with a formal launch scheduled for the UN STI Forum in May 2027. *Source: UN Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm.

**STI Forum series continuation (sourced):**

The 11th Multi-Stakeholder Forum on STI for the SDGs will take place on 6–7 May 2026, at UN HQ, New York. *Source: UN Technology Facilitation Mechanism, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm.

*Analytical inference:* The annual cadence of the STI Forum (established through 9 editions as of 2024, with the 11th already scheduled for May 2026) indicates this is a durable institutional mechanism, not a one-off event. Conversion from signal to commercial pressure occurs when Member States incorporate STI Forum recommendations into national regulatory or procurement frameworks — particularly in the SDG 9 (infrastructure and innovation) and SDG 13 (climate action) tracks that directly intersect freight and logistics.

**SDG 9 progress data as context (from parallel UN source):**

Global CO2 emissions from fuel combustion and industrial processes reached a record 37.6 gigatons in 2024, a 0.8 per cent increase from 2023. Global research and development spending grew at 5.1 per cent annually from 2015 to 2022. *Source: SDG 9, UN DESA, 2025. https://sdgs.un.org/goals/goal9. These figures represent the policy backdrop against which the STI Forum's SDG 9 / SDG 13 sessions operate.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*Section partially populated. Concrete cost figures tied directly to STI Forum 2024 outputs are not available from the sourced blocks as of this date. The following chains are grounded in sourced institutional design, not speculative outcomes.*

**For workspaces operating in freight and logistics, the STI Forum's SDG 13 and SDG 9 tracks are the two most operationally relevant:**

- SDG 13 (climate action) sessions at the 2024 forum — specifically Session 2: *"Strengthening scientific cooperation, technology and knowledge sharing and accelerating innovation for integrated climate action"* — produce science-policy inputs that feed HLPF climate-action recommendations. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces moving cargo by air (primary mode), ocean (tertiary mode), and road (secondary mode) across Americas, European, and Asia-Pacific trade lanes, HLPF climate-action recommendations derived from STI Forum outputs may shape future national regulatory cycles on transport emissions. The STI Forum itself sets no binding obligations on private operators; downstream national regulatory action is where cost exposure would materialise.

- SDG 17 (means of implementation) sessions — specifically Session 1: *"More and more effective funding and capacity for SDG related research and innovation in all regions"* — address financing access for technology adoption, which is directly relevant to freight operators evaluating clean-energy infrastructure investment. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces that have sustainability reporting obligations (CSRD-scope entities or clients of same), the STI Forum's science-policy brief outputs become citable context in Scope 3 reporting narratives — but are not themselves a reporting framework. Conflating them would be an overclaim.

*Specific cost figures for STI Forum 2024 outputs' operational translation not available from primary sources as of 2 June 2025.*

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*No competitor-specific sourced reporting identified in the source blocks for this item. Named competitor positioning is not available from the provided sources as of this date. The following is structural analysis from sourced institutional design.*

*Analytical inference:* The STI Forum's Business and Industry Major Group (BIMG) participation channel is a named institutional access point for private-sector operators. Workspaces whose commercial leadership engages BIMG-connected bodies (industry associations, chambers of commerce with ECOSOC consultative status) have earlier visibility into science-policy brief outputs — and thus earlier awareness of which STI trajectories are gaining Member State support — than workspaces that track only published regulatory output.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces competing on sustainability credentials in high-value cargo verticals (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive), the STI Forum's SDG 9 (innovation) and SDG 13 (climate) tracks are increasingly referenced in client tender ESG questionnaires. A workspace that can articulate the institutional pathway from STI Forum → HLPF → national policy is positioned to respond more credibly to client ESG due-diligence asks than one that treats UN forum outputs as background noise.

*Analytical inference:* The STI Forum's Africa-focused sessions (Session 7: *"STI partnerships for accelerating structural transformation in African countries, Least Developed Countries and Landlocked Developing Countries"*) and the Africa STI Coalitions side event signal that Sub-Saharan corridor development is gaining institutional momentum. Workspaces with African trade lane exposure should monitor whether STI roadmap recommendations translate into preferential financing or infrastructure commitments that alter corridor economics. *Source: UN DESA STI Forum 2024 Event Page, UN DESA, 2024. https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024; 9th STI Forum 2024 – International Science Council, ISC, 2024. https://council.science/events/sti-forum-2024/.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The STI Forum is the UN's annual mechanism for connecting science, technology, and innovation communities to the SDG review process. It is institutionally grounded (convened by the ECOSOC President, prepared by IATT and the 10-Member Group), multi-stakeholder (government, private sector, civil society, science), and produces formal mandated input to the HLPF — not advisory opinions.
- The 2024 forum specifically addressed SDG 13 (climate action), which is directly relevant to any client with transport-sector emissions-reduction targets.
- The forum produces co-chairs' summaries that feed into HLPF deliberations, not binding regulatory instruments. Downstream national regulatory adoption is where binding obligations would arise.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Does your sustainability reporting framework reference HLPF SDG review cycles or national SDG voluntary reports? If so, STI Forum science-policy briefs may be citable supporting evidence.
- Are your procurement ESG questionnaires beginning to reference SDG-aligned technology frameworks? If so, the workspace's awareness of HLPF-aligned STI tracks is a differentiator worth surfacing.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert that STI Forum resolutions or co-chairs' summaries create compliance obligations for private operators. They do not. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any specific national regulation implementing STI Forum-aligned recommendations applies to the workspace's operations in a given jurisdiction requires jurisdiction-specific legal review.
- Do not cite STI Forum science-policy briefs as authoritative regulatory guidance. They are analytical inputs to an intergovernmental deliberation process, not binding instruments.
- Do not claim specific technology cost reductions or supply-chain efficiency gains were "endorsed" by the STI Forum without sourcing the specific session output document.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Track the co-chairs' summary output from STI Forum 2024.**
The co-chairs' summary is the formal mandated output of the forum and directly informs the 2024 HLPF (8–17 July 2024). Monitor the HLPF outcome document for SDG 13 and SDG 9 language that may shape national regulatory cycles in the workspace's operating jurisdictions.
- *Who is affected:* Sustainability reporting leads, commercial leadership engaging in client ESG responses.
- *Why now:* The HLPF 2024 deliberations occurred in July 2024; any resulting national follow-on regulatory consultations may already be in early stages.

**Action 2 — Identify whether the workspace's industry association holds ECOSOC consultative status or BIMG affiliation.**
The Business and Industry Major Group is a named institutional channel at the STI Forum. Membership in BIMG-connected bodies provides advance visibility into science-policy brief outputs before they reach HLPF.
- *Who is affected:* Government affairs and sustainability leads.
- *Why now:* The 11th STI Forum is already scheduled for May 2026; positioning in BIMG channels ahead of that cycle requires lead time.

**Action 3 — Monitor the second-edition STI for SDGs Roadmaps Guidebook (anticipated IATT publication 2026, formal launch at STI Forum May 2027).**
The second edition focuses on six key transitions including climate change and digital connectivity — both operationally relevant to freight forwarding. National governments using the guidebook may develop STI roadmaps that include transport-sector technology transition requirements.
- *Who is affected:* Operations leads in jurisdictions where pilot roadmap countries (Ethiopia, Ghana, India, Kenya, Serbia, Ukraine) are active trade partners or where the workspace has ground operations.
- *Re-check window:* IATT website publication anticipated 2026; formal launch May 2027 STI Forum. Set monitoring review for Q1 2026.

**Action 4 — For African corridor exposure, track the STI Coalitions for Africa mechanism.**
The side event "Harnessing the STI Coalitions for Africa," organised by Future Africa, UNECA, UN DESA, and five African member state permanent missions, signals that sub-Saharan infrastructure and innovation financing is gaining coordinated institutional backing. This is the operational precursor to corridor-level investment decisions that affect freight economics.
- *Who is affected:* Trade lane leads for Sub-Saharan or landlocked African routing.
- *Why now:* Coordinated multi-mission backing (South Africa, Ghana, Ethiopia, Cameroon, Morocco) at a senior diplomatic level is an early signal of intent to translate STI recommendations into national investment frameworks.

---

# 8. Sources

| 1 | 9th Multi-stakeholder Forum on Science, Technology and Innovation for the Sustainable Development Goals | UN DESA | 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body event record | https://sdgs.un.org/tfm/STIForum2024 |

| 3 | UNCTAD and the Technology Facilitation Mechanism | UNCTAD | 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body programme page | https://unctad.org/topic/science-technology-and-innovation/technology-facilitation-mechanism |

prior.title: Critical Materials for EV Batteries: IRENA 2024 Report — Research Summary
prior.summary: IRENA publication on critical materials for electric vehicle batteries is currently inaccessible (HTTP 403). Content details cannot be retrieved at this time.
prior.full_brief:
# Critical Materials for EV Batteries: IRENA 2024 Report — Research Summary

## 1. What the Research Found

**Report identity and scope:** IRENA published "Critical Materials: Batteries for Electric Vehicles" on 26 September 2024, a 76-page analysis projecting supply-demand balances for EV battery materials to 2030 under a 1.5°C scenario. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

**Scale of the demand challenge:** The report finds that IRENA's 1.5°C Scenario requires annual global EV sales to rise from 14 million vehicles sold in 2023 to approximately 60 million by 2030. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

Meeting this target requires EV battery production to grow five-fold by 2030, necessitating a proportional rise in raw material supply to avoid supply-demand imbalances. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

The IEA's parallel analysis corroborates this trajectory: battery demand is set to continue growing fast based on current policy settings, increasing four-and-a-half times by 2030 and more than seven times by 2035. *Source: IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, 19 December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

**Per-material headline findings:**

- **Lithium:** Demand is expected to remain high across all battery technology scenarios. Emerging technologies like sodium-ion, which do not rely on lithium, may enter the market later this decade, but their impact on reducing lithium demand will likely be more significant after 2030. The IEA's NZE Scenario projects EVs and battery storage to account for over 90% of total lithium demand by 2030. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials; IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024, IEA. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-critical-minerals-outlook-2024/outlook-for-key-minerals

- **Cobalt:** Its criticality may decrease due to the rise of alternative technologies like LFP and LMFP. However, shortages are possible if cobalt-containing batteries remain prevalent. Cobalt and nickel are no longer used in about half of the passenger EVs sold in 2023. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

- **Graphite:** Natural graphite production is expected to fall short of meeting the total graphite demand by 2030. Synthetic graphite and innovations in emerging anode technologies can bridge the supply-demand gap. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

- **Nickel:** Demand stabilization is aided by the growing adoption of LFP and LMFP batteries, mitigating earlier supply concerns. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

- **Copper, phosphorous, manganese:** The impact of EVs on the demand for copper, phosphorous, and manganese is expected to be minimal compared to other industries. However, the need for high-purity materials presents challenges in scaling up production. *Source: IRENA Press Release, IRENA, 27 September 2024. https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials

**IEA corroboration on supply concentration risk:** Global mineral supply chains are not well diversified, and recent progress on diversifying supply sources has been limited. Analysis of project pipelines indicates that the geographical concentration of mining operations is set to rise further or remain high over the projection period. The market value for refining is notably more concentrated, with China claiming nearly 50% of the market value in 2030. *Source: IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024, IEA. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-critical-minerals-outlook-2024/outlook-for-key-minerals

**Recycling as a demand-reduction pathway:** If recycling is scaled effectively, recycling can reduce lithium and nickel demand by 25%, and cobalt demand by 40% in 2050, in a scenario that meets national climate targets. However, the report notes this impact is more significant after 2035, when the number of end-of-life EV batteries will start growing rapidly. *Source: IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, 19 December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

**Methodology:** The 76-page report covers supply-demand forecasts to 2030, per-material annexes (lithium, cobalt, graphite, nickel, copper, phosphorous, manganese), battery chemistry scenario analysis, and a policy recommendations chapter. RWTH Aachen University is cited as a contributing organisation. *Source: Policy Commons archive of IRENA report, Policy Commons. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/16845709/critical-materials/17730542/

**Scope and limitations:** The report emphasizes the necessity of scaling up production to meet rapidly increasing demand driven by EV adoption; the analysis explores uncertainties stemming from policy changes, technological advancements in battery chemistries (including the rise of LFP and sodium-ion batteries), and potential supply chain disruptions. The report highlights the importance of monitoring market dynamics and considering various scenarios to navigate potential bottlenecks. *Source: Policy Commons archive of IRENA report, Policy Commons. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/16845709/critical-materials/17730542/

---

## 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**For workspaces moving EV-adjacent freight — automotive, manufacturing inputs, high-value components:**

*Operational implication:* A five-fold growth in EV battery production by 2030 translates directly into a structural surge in cross-border freight volume for lithium, graphite, cobalt, and processed battery components — concentrated on lanes connecting Latin America and Africa (mining origin), China (dominant refining), and manufacturing hubs in Europe and North America. For workspaces with automotive or high-value manufacturing cargo verticals, this growth is a lane-level volume signal, not background context.

*Operational implication:* Graphite is the most acute near-term bottleneck identified by IRENA: natural graphite production is expected to fall short of meeting total demand by 2030. Synthetic graphite, predominantly produced in China, would bridge this gap — reinforcing China's refining dominance and concentrating air and ocean freight dependency on that origin. For workspaces moving battery-related inputs, graphite supply tightness is the most actionable pre-2030 constraint flagged by the report.

*Operational implication:* The IEA finding that lithium presents a substantial anticipated gap with climate-driven needs, and that the current downturn in prices may dampen investment appetite for new greenfield projects, signals that upstream supply development is fragile. A price trough that delays investment creates a mid-decade supply shock risk — relevant to workspaces advising clients on supply chain resilience or managing time-sensitive automotive shipments.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating in the humanitarian or film/production verticals, these findings are background intelligence rather than direct operational triggers. The supply-demand dynamics affect those verticals only indirectly through energy transition policy pressure on clients and through general freight capacity competition on lanes carrying battery materials.

---

## 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

*Analytical inference:* The concentration finding — China holding nearly 50% of refining market value in 2030 — is the single claim with the highest strategic consequence for lane planning. Workspaces with automotive cargo on Asia–Europe or Asia–North America corridors should treat this not as a temporary condition but as a structurally confirmed medium-term dependency. Lane diversification or resilience planning premised on rapid supply-chain de-concentration is not supported by this analysis.

*Analytical inference:* The battery chemistry shift — cobalt and nickel no longer used in about half of passenger EVs sold in 2023 — changes which materials are freight-critical. LFP chemistry growth redirects demand toward lithium, phosphorous, and manganese rather than cobalt and nickel, shifting origin geographies for future cargo flows. Workspaces with established cobalt-lane relationships should note this trend and not assume those volumes persist at current levels through 2030.

*Analytical inference:* The recycling pathway identified by the IEA — reducing lithium and nickel demand by 25% and cobalt by 40% by 2050 — is commercially relevant as a client conversation differentiator after 2035, not before. Scaling recycling facilities and increasing collection rates of end-of-life batteries will be essential; this creates a second-decade freight category (battery return logistics, closed-loop collection) that workspaces with logistics design capability can begin positioning for now.

*Analytical inference:* IRENA's call for increased international cooperation in areas such as enhancing data transparency, facilitating investment and technology diffusion, and laying the groundwork for critical material recycling beyond 2030 is a signal that policy frameworks governing battery material trade — origin disclosure, ESG mining standards, traceability — are actively developing. Workspaces operating in regulated automotive or manufacturing supply chains should monitor whether client compliance requirements for material provenance flow down to freight documentation obligations.

---

## 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly state, grounded in the sourced findings:**

- IRENA's 2024 analysis projects five-fold EV battery production growth by 2030 and identifies graphite as the most likely near-term supply bottleneck — natural graphite production is projected to fall short of 2030 demand even in IRENA's modelling.
- Both IRENA and the IEA confirm that China's dominance in battery material refining is not expected to diminish materially by 2030; the market value for refining is nearly 50% China-concentrated in IEA's projections.
- The LFP/LMFP chemistry shift has already changed the material mix: cobalt and nickel were absent from approximately half of passenger EVs sold in 2023, redirecting criticality toward lithium and graphite.
- Recycling becomes a meaningful demand-offset mechanism after 2035 when end-of-life volumes scale — not before.

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not state that supply shortages are certain; the IRENA report explicitly frames its analysis around scenarios and uncertainty ranges. The finding is risk of imbalance, not guaranteed shortage.
- Do not cite specific price forecasts. No price projections appear in the sourced blocks for this item.
- Do not assert that sodium-ion batteries will resolve lithium demand before 2030; the sourced finding is that their impact on reducing lithium demand will likely be more significant after 2030.
- Do not present the IEA and IRENA figures as identical; the IEA projects approximately 45–70 million EV sales by 2030 depending on scenario, while IRENA's 1.5°C Scenario targets approximately 60 million. The directional alignment is strong; the figures are not the same model.

**Questions to pose in client conversations:**

- Is your client's battery-material supply chain exposed to the graphite bottleneck specifically — and is their current sourcing strategy premised on natural or synthetic graphite?
- Does your client's ESG reporting framework already require mineral provenance documentation, and does that flow through to freight documentation requirements?
- Has your client modelled what a lithium price recovery and associated greenfield investment delay would mean for their production timeline?

---

## 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

*Analytical inference:* The IRENA report models to 2030 under a 1.5°C scenario; it does not address what a slower energy transition trajectory (e.g., STEPS or APS) means for per-material demand. The IEA's parallel work provides these alternative scenarios, but the IRENA brief does not integrate them. Workspaces advising clients on long-range procurement should note the scenario dependency.

The report does not address freight-specific consequences: modal capacity, lane congestion, rate dynamics, or customs/documentation requirements for battery material shipments are not within scope. *Specific freight cost or capacity impact figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.*

The recycling pathway's 2050 demand-reduction figures (25% lithium and nickel reduction, 40% cobalt reduction) depend on collection rate assumptions and regulatory frameworks for end-of-life battery management that are currently under development in the EU (Battery Regulation) and other jurisdictions. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the degree to which the EU Battery Regulation's end-of-life and recycled-content obligations will achieve the collection rates assumed in the IEA modelling has not been confirmed by the Commission and requires regulatory tracking.

The IRENA Collaborative Framework on Critical Materials for the Energy Transition — the multilateral platform underpinning this work — had held four meetings as of the October 2024 session, with a 5th meeting listed for 24 November 2025. *Source: IRENA Collaborative Framework on Critical Materials, IRENA. https://www.irena.org/How-we-work/Collaborative-frameworks/Critical-materials Whether that meeting produced updated guidance, revised supply projections, or revised policy recommendations is not available in the sourced blocks. *Specific output of 5th CFCM meeting (November 2025) not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.* MONITORING — re-check IRENA CFCM outputs and any updated IRENA critical materials publications in Q3 2026 for revised projections.

The companion IRENA criticality-ranking report (October 2024) — "Constructing a Ranking of Critical Materials for the Global Energy Transition" — provides a methodological framework that contextualises which materials are prioritised for policy action. Its specific rankings and methodology are not reproduced in the sourced blocks and would require direct access to assess intersection with the EV battery findings. *Specific content of IRENA criticality-ranking methodology not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.*

---

## 6. Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Date | Type | URL |
|---|-------|-------------|------|------|-----|
| 1 | Innovation and Sustainable Mining are Key to Meeting EV Demand for Critical Materials (Press Release) | IRENA | 27 Sep 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body position | https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials |
| 2 | Collaborative Framework on Critical Materials for the Energy Transition | IRENA | Ongoing (last updated Oct 2024) | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body position | https://www.irena.org/How-we-work/Collaborative-frameworks/Critical-materials |
| 3 | EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability | IEA | 19 Dec 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body position | https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability |
| 4 | Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024 — Outlook for Key Minerals | IEA | 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body position | https://www.iea.org/reports/global-critical-minerals-outlook-2024/outlook-for-key-minerals |
| 5 | Critical Materials for Electric Vehicle Batteries: An IRENA Analysis (archive) | Policy Commons / IRENA | 26 Sep 2024 | Tier 5 — Secondary aggregator | https://policycommons.net/artifacts/16845709/critical-materials/17730542/ |

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier Estimate | Why This Source Matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------|------------------------|
| IRENA Press Release: Innovation and Sustainable Mining are Key to Meeting EV Demand for Critical Materials | https://www.irena.org/News/pressreleases/2024/Sep/Innovation-and-Sustainable-Mining-are-Key-to-Meeting-EV-Demand-for-Critical-Materials | 3 | Official IRENA summary of key report findings; the most accessible verbatim source for the 14M–60M EV sales figure, five-fold battery growth target, and per-material findings when the primary PDF is inaccessible |
| IRENA Collaborative Framework on Critical Materials for the Energy Transition | https://www.irena.org/How-we-work/Collaborative-frameworks/Critical-materials | 3 | Multilateral governance platform underpinning the report; tracks CFCM meeting outputs and upcoming sessions including the 5th meeting (Nov 2025); essential for monitoring whether supply projections are revised |
| IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability | https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability | 3 | IEA parallel report providing life-cycle emissions analysis and 2050 recycling demand-reduction projections; corroborates IRENA's battery growth trajectory and extends the analysis to circular economy pathways |
| IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024 — Outlook for Key Minerals | https://www.iea.org/reports/global-critical-minerals-outlook-2024/outlook-for-key-minerals | 3 | Authoritative IEA scenario projections for lithium, cobalt, graphite, nickel, and copper; directly corroborates IRENA's bottleneck analysis and adds supply-concentration and market-value data not in the IRENA press release |
| Policy Commons: Critical Materials — Batteries for Electric Vehicles (IRENA 2024 archive) | https://policycommons.net/artifacts/16845709/critical-materials/17730542/ | 5 | Hosts table of contents, figure list, and per-material annex structure of the full IRENA report; useful as a navigation aid and scope confirmation source when the primary IRENA PDF URL is inaccessible |

prior.title: DP World Sustainability Strategy: Net Zero Pathway and ESG Framework Alignment
prior.summary: DP World has published its comprehensive sustainability strategy 'Our World, Our Future - Where Trade Meets Purpose' with validated Science Based Targets committing to 42% emissions reduction by 2030 (from 2022 baseline, with 13% already achieved). The strategy encompasses four pillars: climate action, community development, education, and water management, with GRI standards alignment and reporting transparency. The company has established partnerships with major organizations including World Food Programme, Barefoot College International, and Jane Goodall Institute.
prior.full_brief:
# DP World Sustainability Strategy: Net Zero Pathway and ESG Framework Alignment

**Format:** Market Signal Brief | **Severity:** MONITORING | **Signal Band:** Corporate

---

## Section 1: What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

DP World, one of the world's largest port and logistics operators with terminals across the Americas, Europe, Middle East, Africa, and Asia, has published a formalised net zero pathway anchored in Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) validation and multi-framework ESG disclosure. The signal is corporate in character: a major port infrastructure operator is converting sustainability commitments into verified, independently assured operational milestones, and extending those milestones into its supply chain relationships with cargo owners.

**Emissions trajectory and SBTi targets:**
DP World reports it has "already reduced emissions by 13 percent since our 2022 baseline" and that its "updated targets, validated by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi), commit us to a 42 percent reduction by 2030." *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025.*

**Southampton terminal — operational net zero 2025:**
The Southampton container terminal "has already cut its Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions by 87% in 2024 compared to 2021, with these reductions independently assured by LRQA." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

The terminal "is on track to become Operational Net Zero for the 2025 Reporting Period," defined as balancing Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions through reduction plus high-quality carbon credit retirement meeting ISO 14068 criteria. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

**HVO fuel switch — Southampton:**
In 2022 the Southampton terminal "became the first in the UK to eliminate fossil diesel from its operations entirely, switching to Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil (HVO), a renewable biodiesel that has slashed net carbon dioxide emissions by over 80% and significantly improved local air quality." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

**Carbon Inset Programme — active from January 2025:**
DP World has launched "an innovative Carbon Inset Programme Trial from 1 January 2025, providing importers into the UK the ability to reduce hard-to-abate Scope 3 carbon emissions from their containerised supply chains." The programme "began issuing certificates in May following the registration of over 150,000 import containers since its January launch" and has been extended through year-end "beyond the original six-month timeline to accommodate growing interest from cargo owners seeking carbon credits." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

**Callao terminal — Scope 1 and 2 target of 90%+ by 2030:**
DP World Callao has committed to "an ambitious target of more than 90% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 carbon emissions by 2030," with a stated investment of "more than US$72 million…for the changeover to electric equipment (replacement and conversion) and renewable energy supply." The strategy is "based on equipment electrification and efficiency, renewable energy, process efficiency and digitalization." *Source: World Port Sustainability Program, sustainableworldports.org/project/dp-world-callao-decarbonization-strategy/, 2024.*

**ESG disclosure framework:**
DP World's sustainability reporting is aligned with Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) standards. *Source: DP World Sustainability Reporting Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability/reporting, accessed 2025.* The corroborating source list indicates alignment with ISSB, SASB, TCFD, and GRI, and the company publishes a GHG Accounting Methodology document and has a Sustainable Finance Framework with a Second Party Opinion, though the full 2024 ESG report PDF was not among the provided source blocks and specific figures from it are not cited here as FACTs.

---

## Section 2: Who's Driving It and What They Want

**DP World (operator and signal originator):**
DP World's stated strategy is titled "Our World, Our Future — Where Trade Meets Purpose," described as ensuring the company "operate[s] as a responsible business, prioritising sustainability impact on people, the environment, and the communities where we operate." *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025.* The company has developed a Sustainable Finance Framework and issued Green Sukuk instruments, signalling that ESG performance is tied to capital-market access and financing terms, not only regulatory compliance.

**LRQA (independent assurance provider):**
The 87% Scope 1 and 2 reduction at Southampton has been "independently assured by LRQA," and the terminal commits to "continue to have its emissions data independently verified annually against ISO 14064-1 standards." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

**Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi):**
The 42% Scope 1 and 2 and the Scope 3 reduction targets are "validated by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi)." *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025; Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.* SBTi validation gives external credibility to targets and creates accountability pressure toward the stated milestones.

**World Port Sustainability Program (WPSP):**
DP World Callao's decarbonisation strategy is documented in the WPSP project database, indicating that DP World is engaging with international port sustainability governance bodies to validate and publicise its corridor-level commitments. *Source: World Port Sustainability Program, sustainableworldports.org/project/dp-world-callao-decarbonization-strategy/, 2024.*

**Cargo owners and importers:**
The Carbon Inset Programme Trial's extension beyond its original timeline and the registration of over 150,000 import containers since January 2025 indicate active demand from cargo owners for port-sourced Scope 3 carbon credits. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

*Analytical inference:* The primary driver for DP World is the convergence of three forces: SBTi target accountability, capital-market ESG requirements tied to its sustainable finance instruments (Green Sukuk, Blue Bond), and growing commercial demand from cargo owners needing credible Scope 3 abatement pathways. The Carbon Inset Programme's uptake rate — 150,000+ containers in four months — suggests the cargo owner demand for port-level Scope 3 credits is not aspirational but already commercially activated.

---

## Section 3: Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Stated schedule (FACTs):**
- Southampton operational net zero: 2025 reporting period. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*
- Group-level 42% Scope 1 and 2 reduction target: by 2030, SBTi-validated. *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025.*
- Callao 90%+ Scope 1 and 2 reduction and US$72M electrification investment: by 2030. *Source: World Port Sustainability Program, sustainableworldports.org/project/dp-world-callao-decarbonization-strategy/, 2024.*
- Group-level net zero across all scopes: 2050. *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025.*
- Carbon Inset Programme extended through end of 2025. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*
- London Gateway all-electric berth expansion: date of completion not stated in provided source blocks.

*Specific completion date for London Gateway expansion not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-10.*

**Conversion triggers — from signal to active commercial pressure:**

*Analytical inference:* The signal converts from observation to commercial pressure for freight-forwarding workspaces when three conditions materialise: (1) cargo owners begin contractually requiring Scope 3 port-level credits as a condition of carrier or forwarder selection; (2) DP World packages the Carbon Inset Programme as a forwarding-channel product rather than a direct importer product; and (3) competing ports or forwarders launch comparable inset programmes, creating a differentiation race. Condition (1) is partially active already, given the 150,000-container inset uptake in the first four months. Conditions (2) and (3) are not yet confirmed in the provided sources.

*Analytical inference:* Southampton achieving independently verified operational net zero in 2025 will set a precedent benchmark against which other UK port calls will be evaluated by cargo owners with CSRD, ISSB, or voluntary GHG Protocol reporting obligations. Workspaces routing containerised cargo through Southampton will face client questions about whether their supply chains can access or reference DP World's verified status.

---

## Section 4: Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**By transport mode and cargo vertical — filtered to workspace profile:**

The workspace operates primarily by air, with road secondary and ocean tertiary. DP World's signal is port-infrastructure-facing and affects ocean-mode legs and road drayage at port gates. Air primary operations are not directly addressed by DP World's port sustainability milestones.

**Ocean — port call emissions and Scope 3 credit access:**
For cargo moving through DP World terminals (Southampton, Callao, London Gateway, and others in the network), the Carbon Inset Programme Trial offers a mechanism to attribute verified Scope 3 port-handling emission reductions to importers' supply chains. The programme "began issuing certificates in May following the registration of over 150,000 import containers since its January launch." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces acting as freight forwarder or importer of record routing containerised cargo through Southampton, the Carbon Inset Programme may offer a credible, independently verified Scope 3 abatement tool for client reporting — provided the programme's certificate structure meets the client's GHG Protocol or CSRD reporting requirements. Whether inset credits issued under ISO 14068 satisfy CSRD or GHG Protocol Scope 3 reporting in a client's specific reporting framework requires independent verification and is not confirmed by the provided sources.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether Carbon Inset Programme certificates issued under ISO 14068 are recognised as a permissible Scope 3 abatement mechanism under a given cargo owner's CSRD or GHG Protocol reporting obligations requires counsel and auditor review; DP World's programme design does not constitute a ruling on third-party reporting eligibility.

**Road — Green Charter and truck emissions charge at Southampton:**
DP World "is the first port in the UK to introduce a 'green' charge on older trucks to pick up or drop off a container." *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*

*Operational implication:* Road carriers and drayage operators using older vehicles at Southampton face a direct cost surcharge from DP World. Workspaces arranging drayage at Southampton (for their ocean-tertiary legs) should confirm whether their contracted road carriers' fleet age triggers this charge and whether it is passed through in drayage invoicing.

*Specific surcharge rate not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-10.*

**Callao (Americas trade lane):**
DP World Callao is investing US$72M in electrification and renewable energy to reach 90%+ Scope 1 and 2 reduction by 2030. *Source: World Port Sustainability Program, sustainableworldports.org/project/dp-world-callao-decarbonization-strategy/, 2024.* For workspaces with Americas ocean legs touching Callao, this terminal's decarbonisation trajectory means port-handling emission intensity is projected to fall materially by 2030, which could improve the Scope 3 profile of Callao-transiting supply chains for clients with long-dated sustainability commitments.

*Analytical inference:* The US$72M capital programme at Callao is a capital expenditure commitment, not yet a completed operational result. Workspaces should not represent Callao's 2030 target as current performance; they should represent it as a stated, WPSP-documented trajectory.

**High-value and time-sensitive cargo verticals (air-primary):**
DP World's port strategy does not directly alter emission profiles for air-primary cargo moves. However, for cargo verticals (e.g., live events, fine art, luxury, film and TV, high-value automotive) that use air as primary mode but involve ocean for secondary moves or equipment repositioning, the port-level inset programme may become relevant to multi-modal supply chain emission reporting.

---

## Section 5: Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* DP World's verified operational net zero milestone at Southampton — if achieved and independently certified in 2025 — will likely become a reference benchmark in tender responses by freight forwarders routing UK container volumes. Forwarders who can demonstrate that their port handling is DP World Southampton-routed, and that they have accessed the Carbon Inset Programme, will have a documentable Scope 3 story; those routing through ports with no comparable programme will face a comparison gap in client sustainability questionnaires.

*Analytical inference:* Named competitor positioning in this space is not confirmed in the provided source blocks. Whether competing major port operators (e.g., Hutchison Ports, PSA International, APM Terminals) operate comparable Scope 3 inset programmes at UK ports is not confirmed by these sources and is not asserted here.

*Analytical inference:* The EcoVadis top-5% ranking referenced in the corroborating source list (DP World Sustainability Reporting Hub) — if confirmed in the full ESG report — would signal that DP World has achieved a procurement-ecosystem credential used by multinational cargo owners to gate supplier selection. Workspaces whose clients use EcoVadis ratings as part of supplier due diligence will face increasing pressure to demonstrate their own sustainability posture beyond their choice of port infrastructure.

*Specific competitor programme details not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-10.*

---

## Section 6: Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- "DP World Southampton has cut its direct port emissions by 87% versus 2021, independently verified by LRQA, and is on track for operational net zero in 2025." This is a sourced FACT and can be used in client discussions about the Scope 3 profile of UK container imports. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*
- "DP World operates a Carbon Inset Programme Trial at Southampton — active since January 2025 — that has already registered over 150,000 import containers. We are monitoring whether this can be accessed to support your Scope 3 reporting." This is a sourced FACT, appropriately qualified. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.*
- "DP World's SBTi-validated group targets commit to 42% Scope 1 and 2 reduction by 2030 and net zero by 2050." This is a sourced FACT appropriate for client-conversation context. *Source: DP World Sustainability Hub, dpworld.com/en/sustainability, accessed 2025.*

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- Does your Scope 3 reporting framework (GHG Protocol, CSRD, voluntary) recognise ISO 14068 carbon inset certificates as a permissible reduction mechanism for Category 4 (upstream transport) or Category 9 (downstream transport)?
- Are you currently routing UK containerised imports through Southampton, or are you open to routing decisions being influenced by terminal-level emission performance?
- What is your timeline for locking your 2025 Scope 3 inventory? If you are interested in the Carbon Inset Programme, the extension through end-2025 creates a registration window now.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not represent Southampton's 2025 operational net zero as a group-wide DP World achievement. The group-level 2050 net zero and 2030 42% targets are separate from the terminal-level milestone.
- Do not represent Carbon Inset Programme certificates as automatically qualifying for any specific regulatory or reporting framework. Their eligibility depends on the client's specific reporting standard and auditor.
- Do not represent Callao's 90% target as current performance — it is a 2030 target with a capital programme underway, not a completed result.
- Do not claim competitor port operators lack comparable programmes without sourced confirmation — this exposes the workspace to a verifiable factual challenge.

---

## Section 7: What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Confirm Carbon Inset Programme access and eligibility (this quarter)**
Contact DP World Southampton directly to confirm whether freight forwarders (as distinct from direct importers) can register containers through the Carbon Inset Programme Trial, and whether the programme's year-end extension creates a registration window for Q3/Q4 2025 shipments. The programme was extended "beyond the original six-month timeline," with the extension running through year-end. *Source: Supply Chain Digital, supplychaindigital.com/sustainability/dp-world-southampton-hitting-operational-net-zero-2025, June 2025.* This is a time-bounded window.

**Action 2 — Add port-level emission data to routing analysis for ocean-tertiary legs**
For containerised cargo on Americas and Europe trade lanes routed through DP World terminals, begin tracking terminal-level Scope 1 and 2 emission intensity as a routing variable. Callao's 2030 trajectory and Southampton's 2025 milestone mean that DP World-terminal routing will carry a progressively lower port-handling Scope 3 footprint for client reporting purposes.

**Action 3 — Review drayage contracts at Southampton for green surcharge exposure**
Confirm with contracted road carriers whether their fleet age triggers DP World Southampton's green charge on older trucks. Determine whether this is a pass-through in existing drayage rate agreements or an unquantified additional cost.

*Specific surcharge rate not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-10.* Request the current charge schedule directly from DP World Southampton operations.

**Action 4 — Position against client Scope 3 questionnaires now, not at renewal**
Cargo owner clients with CSRD or GHG Protocol Scope 3 reporting obligations are increasingly asking forwarders to document port-level emission performance. The workspace should prepare a one-page routing sustainability note for its top ocean-leg accounts that references DP World Southampton's verified status and the Carbon Inset Programme, qualified appropriately per the client conversation guidance above. This positions the workspace ahead of competitors who are not yet monitoring port-level ESG developments.

**Action 5 — Set monitoring window for Southampton 2025 verification**
Southampton's operational net zero certification is expected to be announced following the 2025 reporting period close, likely Q1 2026. The brief should be re-checked at that point to confirm: (a) whether LRQA issues the ISO 14064-1 and ISO 14068 verification, (b) whether the Carbon Inset Programme continues into 2026, and (c) whether DP World packages the programme through forwarding channels. Re-check window: Q1 2026.

---

## Section 8: Sources

| DP World Sustainability Hub | Tier 3 — Operator primary | https://dpworld.com/en/sustainability |
| DP World Sustainability Reporting Hub | Tier 3 — Operator primary | https://www.dpworld.com/en/sustainability/reporting |

---

## New Sources Identified

| DP World – Climate Change & Net Zero (Official) | https://www.dpworld.com/en/sustainability/climate-change | 3 | Primary DP World page detailing net zero pathway, SBTi-validated targets, and renewable electricity milestones; would provide additional span-groundable FACTs on group climate strategy beyond the sustainability hub landing page. |
| DP World Sustainability Report 2024 (Full PDF) | https://www.dpworld.com/angola/-/media/project/dpwg/dpwg-tenant/corporate/global/media-files/sustinability/final-web---dp-world-esg-2024-eng-v3.pdf | 3 | Authoritative 2024 ESG report; cited in the corroborating list as covering GRI/ISSB/SASB/TCFD mapping, SBTi validation, governance structure, and UN Global Compact COP — essential for grounding Scope 3 target detail and EcoVadis ranking as FACTs. |
| Sustainability Magazine – DP World Net Zero & ESG Award Coverage | https://sustainabilitymag.com/news/dp-world-sustainability-net-zero-and-climate-change-action | 5 | Third-party coverage of SBTi targets (42% Scope 1/2 and 28% Scope 3 by 2030) and Global Sustainability Award 2025 win; provides corroborating detail on Scope 3 target and award recognition that is useful for client-conversation grounding. |

prior.title: EU Commission Proposal COM(2023)441 - Digital Markets and Competition Regulatory Framework
prior.summary: European Commission legislative proposal COM(2023)441 addressing digital markets regulation, competition policy, and market regulation framework within the EU jurisdiction
prior.full_brief:

**Critical naming issue flagged:**

The brief request titles this as "EU Commission Proposal COM(2023)441 - Digital Markets and Competition Regulatory Framework." This is **incorrect**. COM(2023)441 is the proposal for a **Regulation on the accounting of greenhouse gas emissions of transport services** — a GHG/emissions reporting regulation directly relevant to freight forwarding workspaces. The "Digital Markets and Competition Regulatory Framework" label appears to be a data entry error.
