Now generating:

---

# Regulatory Fact Document: COM(2023)441 — Proposed EU Regulation on Accounting of Greenhouse Gas Emissions of Transport Services

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers the European Commission's legislative proposal COM(2023)441, published 11 July 2023, titled *Proposal for a Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council on the accounting of greenhouse gas emissions of transport services*. The proposal's CELEX reference is 52023PC0441.

**Naming correction — mandatory notice:** The item record that triggered this brief labels COM(2023)441 as "Digital Markets and Competition Regulatory Framework." This label is incorrect. COM(2023)441 is a GHG emissions accounting regulation for transport services — a directly material regulation for freight forwarding operations. The brief is generated for the actual regulation. The item record label should be corrected in the source registry before this brief is relied upon in client communications or compliance planning.

**Source access status:** The primary source URL provided for this item (https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT?uri=comnat:COM_2023_0441_FIN) returns "Page Not Found" and yields no groundable content. The EUR-Lex CELEX URL (https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441) is accessible and contains the proposal text; all FACT claims are grounded from that source. The EUmonitor source returns Dutch-language marketing copy for a subscription service — no dossier content is accessible. The Umweltbundesamt source returns site navigation only — no substantive claim is groundable from it. Where those sources yield no content, explicit gaps are noted rather than inferred content.

**Convention:** All dates are expressed as day-month-year unless drawn from a direct source quote. The workspace is referenced by operational profile, never by name. Binding-obligation language is sourced verbatim or routed to analysis/legal labels. This is a proposal, not an adopted act — *Legal Confirmation Required* flags apply broadly to enforcement and compliance obligations until the legislative process concludes.

---

# 2. What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## What it is

COM(2023)441 is a Commission proposal for an EU Regulation that would establish a harmonised methodology for calculating and reporting the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of individual transport services — shipments, journeys, and delivery operations — across all transport modes operating in or connected to the EU.

The proposal was published in Strasbourg on 11 July 2023. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

The proposal identifies ISO 14083:2023 — the International Organization for Standardization standard on GHG emissions accounting for transport chains — as the reference methodology. ISO 14083:2023 was published in March 2023, and the proposal notes that CEN subsequently moved to transpose it as a European standard. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

## The problem the regulation addresses

The proposal's explanatory memorandum states that "there is currently no universally accepted framework for greenhouse gas emissions accounting of transport services" and that "transport stakeholders can choose among different standards, methodologies, calculation tools and multiple emissions default values databases and datasets." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

The memorandum identifies this fragmentation as creating "a significant discrepancy in results that compromises the comparability of greenhouse gas emission figures on the market, and provides inaccurate and misleading information on a transport service's performance." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

The memorandum also identifies the risk that entities select "an emissions calculation method and default data based on what is more beneficial for an individual entity," creating "conditions for greenwashing." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

## Why it applies to the workspace

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating as freight forwarders across air, road, and ocean modes on transatlantic and intercontinental lanes, this regulation is directly material in three ways. First, the workspace organises transport services on behalf of clients and would fall within the category of "transport service organisers" that the regulation targets for mandatory GHG data production. Second, clients in verticals with high ESG profile — fine art, luxury goods, live events equipment — are increasingly requesting transport-level (not company-level) emissions data, exactly the data type this regulation addresses. Third, interoperability with CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) Scope 3 reporting by the workspace's shipper clients will be enabled or complicated depending on whether the workspace can produce ISO-14083-aligned data.

*Analytical inference:* The proposal's finding that "only 1.2% of approximately 1.8 million entities performing transport operations on their own" produce disaggregated transport-service-level emissions data signals the baseline problem the regulation is designed to correct — and signals that the workspace, regardless of its current data production capability, will need to demonstrate transport-service-level emissions data to clients under this framework.

**Required slot — jurisdictional scope:** The proposal is an EU-level legislative act proposed under the ordinary legislative procedure (COD procedure, reference 2023/0266(COD)), intended to apply across the European Union and EEA. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441. The precise jurisdictional scope as it applies to non-EU-registered transport service providers operating routes touching the EU is not settled in the available source content. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the regulation's obligations extend to freight forwarders registered outside the EU but operating inbound or outbound EU lanes requires legal review once the final text is adopted.

**Required slot — effective date:** The regulation is a proposal, not an adopted act, as of the date of this brief. No effective date has been set. *Specific effective date not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The legislative process was initiated 11 July 2023 under the COD ordinary legislative procedure. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

**Required slot — primary compliance deadline:** *Specific compliance deadline not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The proposal is pre-adoption; no implementation deadline has been enacted. *Analytical inference:* EU regulations under the ordinary legislative procedure typically enter into force 12–24 months after adoption, with a further transition period. At the pace of the EU legislative calendar, a compliance deadline before 2026 is unlikely, but this estimate is not sourced.

**Required slot — penalty/enforcement:** *Specific penalty provision not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The available source content contains no penalty or enforcement provisions — this is consistent with a Commission proposal document, which typically delegates penalty design to Member States or to a subsequent implementing act.

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — Begin ISO 14083 methodology gap assessment now**

The proposal anchors its methodology to ISO 14083:2023, published March 2023. This standard is currently available. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces that have not mapped their current emissions calculation practice against ISO 14083 are accumulating a methodology gap that will require remediation regardless of whether this specific regulation is adopted — because CSRD Scope 3 client reporting requirements, shipper tender specifications, and voluntary frameworks are converging on ISO 14083 as the reference standard. The cost of a methodology gap assessment is lower now than after mandatory deadlines are set.

Action: Commission an internal gap analysis comparing current GHG data production against ISO 14083:2023 requirements, specifically at transport-service level (not company level). Assign to operations and data leads. Timeline: within the next 90 days.

**MONITORING — Track legislative progress through the COD procedure**

The COD (ordinary legislative procedure) reference 2023/0266(COD) is the dossier identifier for tracking European Parliament committee assignment, Council configuration, and trilogue progress. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* At the time of this brief, no EP first-reading position or Council general approach has been sourced. The legislative dossier should be monitored at 90-day intervals for committee rapporteur appointment, Council general approach adoption, and trilogue opening — each of which represents an escalation point for compliance planning.

Action: Register a dossier alert for 2023/0266(COD) on EUR-Lex or an equivalent legislative tracker. Assign to regulatory affairs or legal. Re-check window: 90 days from the date of this brief.

**MONITORING — Client tender language is changing ahead of the regulation**

The proposal's own consultation evidence notes that 80% of open public consultation respondents recognised the emissions data fragmentation problem as significant or very significant. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441. *Analytical inference:* For workspaces serving clients in high-ESG verticals (luxury, fine art, humanitarian), tender and RFP specifications requesting ISO-14083-aligned emissions data are likely appearing ahead of mandatory dates. The workspace should audit active tender and RFP language for emissions data requirements and assess current ability to respond.

Action: Review current and pipeline tenders for emissions data clauses. Identify gaps between what clients request and what the workspace can currently produce. Assign to commercial leadership.

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The proposal's explanatory memorandum addresses two categories of affected entities: "transport service organisers, including operators" who "do not systematically calculate their greenhouse gas emissions," and "transport service users" who "do not usually obtain accurate information on the performance of transport services." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* As freight forwarders, workspaces sit at the organiser layer of the transport service chain — contracting with carriers, consolidating shipments, and presenting a service to shipper clients. Under the proposed regulation's framework, the workspace would plausibly be a "transport service organiser" responsible for aggregating carrier-level emissions data and producing transport-service-level emissions outputs for clients.

*Analytical inference:* This supply-chain position creates a data dependency: the workspace's ability to produce compliant GHG data for clients depends on receiving accurate, ISO-14083-aligned data from the carriers it subcontracts. Carrier data quality therefore becomes a procurement criterion, not only a sustainability preference.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The precise legal definition of "transport service organiser" in the final regulation, and whether a freight forwarder acting as NVOCC, as customs broker, or as importer of record on specific transactions falls within scope under each role, requires legal review. The proposal text in the source blocks does not contain article-level definitions; the full article text would be required for legal analysis.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether obligations under this regulation attach at the EU entity level, the global group level, or per-transaction on EU-touching lanes is not determinable from the available proposal summary and requires legal review once full article text is assessed.

---

# 6. Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**Legislative procedure milestones — dossier 2023/0266(COD)**

The ordinary legislative procedure (COD) for this proposal was initiated with the Commission publication on 11 July 2023. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Specific EP committee rapporteur assignment, Council general approach date, and trilogue schedule not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The EUmonitor dossier source (https://www.eumonitor.eu/9353000/1/j9vvik7m1c3gyxp/vm4ph56j4zxy) was inaccessible — its content is behind a subscription wall and returned no extractable data. The legislative status therefore cannot be confirmed from available source blocks.

*Analytical inference:* Based on typical EU ordinary legislative procedure durations for technical transport regulations, trilogue negotiations would be expected no earlier than 2024–2025, with final adoption potentially in 2025–2026. This estimate is not sourced and should be replaced with confirmed dossier data as soon as a legislative tracker with live access is registered.

**ISO 14083 CEN transposition timeline**

The proposal notes that CEN moved to transpose ISO 14083:2023 as a European standard following its March 2023 publication. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441. The CEN/CENELEC webinar materials (https://www.cencenelec.eu/media/CEN-CENELEC/Events/Webinars/2024/2024-04-16_webinar_en_iso14083.pdf) published April 2024 are listed as a corroborating source for this process but were not in the four extractable source blocks; their content cannot be used as a span-grounded FACT source. They are listed under New Sources Identified.

*Analytical inference:* The CEN transposition timeline is operationally significant because if the regulation mandates the EN ISO 14083 standard (the European version) rather than the ISO version, implementation timelines will depend on when EN ISO 14083 is formally published by CEN. This is a research gap requiring monitoring.

---

# 7. Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Is this regulation adopted? | No — it is a Commission proposal under procedure 2023/0266(COD). No EP or Council position sourced. |
| Does the regulation apply to non-EU-registered freight forwarders operating EU lanes? | *Legal Confirmation Required* |
| Is the workspace a "transport service organiser" in scope? | *Legal Confirmation Required* — depends on final article-level definitions |
| Does ISO 14083:2023 or EN ISO 14083 apply as the mandated methodology? | Not determinable from current source content — depends on final regulation text |
| Are SME exemptions or phased thresholds included? | The proposal notes low uptake among SMEs but specific exemption provisions are not confirmed in available source content |

---

# 8. Substantive Requirements

The substantive requirements section is based on the proposal's explanatory memorandum as accessible in the EUR-Lex source block. The full article text is not extractable from the available source blocks.

## Harmonised emissions calculation methodology

The proposal is designed to address the current situation in which "transport stakeholders can choose among different standards, methodologies, calculation tools and multiple emissions default values databases and datasets," producing a "significant discrepancy in results." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* The operative effect of the proposed regulation would be to require that transport service-level GHG emissions are calculated using a single harmonised framework — referenced as ISO 14083:2023 in the proposal text — replacing the current multi-methodology environment.

## Transport-service-level data, not company-level data

The proposal makes explicit that only service-level emissions data — calculated per shipment, journey, or transport service — is operationally relevant to client decisions: "only the latter type of information is relevant to effectively support and influence transport service users' decisions." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* For air-primary workspaces, this means that company-level or aircraft-type-level emissions averages will not satisfy the regulation's data requirements. Per-shipment, per-lane data production will be required.

## Scope of affected entities

The proposal references approximately 1.8 million entities performing transport operations in the EU, of which only an estimated 21,660 produce disaggregated transport-service-level data. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* This scale signals that the regulation is designed to pull the entire transport services market toward standardised disclosure — not only large operators. The workspace, regardless of its size, is unlikely to be outside scope if it organises transport services for clients operating in the EU.

## Anti-greenwashing design

The regulation explicitly targets the risk of entities selecting "an emissions calculation method and default data based on what is more beneficial for an individual entity," labelling this "conditions for greenwashing." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441. *Analytical inference:* This design intent signals that the regulation will not permit self-selected methodologies or optimistically chosen default values — the methodology will be locked to the mandated standard.

*Specific article-level obligations (data submission format, reporting frequency, verification requirements, penalty provisions) not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* Full article text review is required for substantive compliance planning.

---

# 10. Registration and Reporting Obligations

*Specific registration requirements, reporting formats, and competent authority designations not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* The available source blocks contain the proposal's explanatory memorandum but not its operative articles, annexes, or implementing acts. Registration and reporting obligations cannot be confirmed without access to the full regulation text. This is an explicit research gap.

*Analytical inference:* Given the regulation's design intent to reach transport service organisers, it is probable that some form of emissions data reporting obligation will attach to entities in the freight forwarder role, but the mechanism (direct reporting to a regulator, disclosure to clients, publication, or a combination) is not determinable from the memorandum alone.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace has a direct reporting obligation to a competent authority, or whether its obligation runs only to its shipper clients, is a legal question that depends on the final article text.

---

# 11. Operational System Requirements

## Data infrastructure gap

The proposal identifies the core operational challenge: the vast majority of EU transport entities "tend to do so at company or vehicle level and are unable to generate data on the greenhouse gas emissions of a transport service." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces not currently producing ISO-14083-aligned, transport-service-level GHG data, the system requirement gap is foundational. It involves: (a) mapping carrier GHG data inputs by mode; (b) implementing a calculation engine aligned to ISO 14083:2023; (c) producing per-shipment outputs attributable to individual client transactions; and (d) creating a data delivery mechanism for client integration.

## Carrier data dependency

*Operational implication:* The workspace cannot produce transport-service-level GHG data unless its air carriers, road hauliers, and ocean carriers supply fuel consumption, load factor, and routing data in a format compatible with ISO 14083 inputs. This creates a supplier onboarding requirement that runs ahead of the workspace's own system build.

*Analytical inference:* Air-mode carrier data quality is the highest-priority gap for workspaces with air as the primary transport mode, given air freight's higher per-unit emissions and the higher ESG scrutiny applied to air-transported cargo in luxury and fine art verticals.

## Timeline for system build

*Specific system build deadline not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.* No adopted compliance deadline exists. *Analytical inference:* ISO-14083-aligned data infrastructure builds for mid-size freight forwarders have been reported in trade press as taking 12–18 months from project initiation to operational output. Beginning assessment now, before a mandatory deadline is set, allows the workspace to avoid compliance-cycle compression. This estimate is not sourced and is provided as a planning orientation only.

---

# 13. Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

## ISO 14083:2023 — the mandated methodology

The proposal states: "ISO published this new standard ISO 14083:2023 in March 2023." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

The ISO 14083:2023 official page is listed as a corroborating source (https://www.iso.org/standard/78864.html) and is identified as a high-priority registry addition. Its content was not among the four extractable source blocks; substantive claims from it are not span-grounded in this brief and are listed under New Sources Identified.

## GLEC Framework — predecessor methodology

The proposal's explanatory memorandum references the "Global Logistics Emissions Council framework" as the industry-led predecessor that the ISO 14083 standard builds upon. *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces already using the GLEC Framework for client emissions reporting, the transition to ISO 14083 is likely less disruptive than for those using proprietary or non-standard methods, given the explicit lineage. However, GLEC-to-ISO alignment is not guaranteed to be one-to-one; a formal mapping exercise is warranted.

The Smart Freight Centre source (https://www.smartfreightcentre.org/en/projects/completed-project-publications/iso-standard-building-on-glec-framework/) is listed as a corroborating source on the GLEC-to-ISO relationship and is recommended for registry addition. Its content was not in the extractable source blocks.

## EU research history: COFRET and LEARN

The proposal notes that between 2011 and 2019, two consecutive EU-funded research projects — the Carbon Footprint of Freight Transport (COFRET) under FP7, and the Logistics Emissions Accounting and Reduction Network (LEARN) under Horizon 2020 — addressed "calculating, reporting and verifying greenhouse gas emissions from transport services, with the aim to develop a global methodology." *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, European Commission, 11 July 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=celex:52023PC0441.

*Analytical inference:* The decade-long research lineage behind this proposal strengthens the likelihood that the regulation will proceed to adoption — it is not a first-generation initiative but the codification of an extended, multi-stakeholder methodology development process.

---

# 14. Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Event | Status |
|---|---|---|
| March 2023 | ISO 14083:2023 published | In force (ISO standard, not EU law) |
| 11 July 2023 | COM(2023)441 published by European Commission; COD procedure 2023/0266 initiated | Confirmed — *Source: EUR-Lex, 52023PC0441, 11 July 2023* |
| TBD | EP committee rapporteur appointment and first-reading position | *Not confirmed — legislative tracker access required* |
| TBD | Council general approach | *Not confirmed* |
| TBD | Trilogue | *Not confirmed* |
| TBD | Final adoption and Official Journal publication | *Not confirmed* |
| TBD | Regulation enters into force | *Not confirmed — Specific effective date not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14* |
| TBD | Compliance deadline for transport service organisers | *Not confirmed — Specific compliance deadline not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14* |

**Re-check window:** This timeline should be reviewed within 90 days of this brief's date. Priority triggers: Council general approach adoption (converts proposal to negotiating text), trilogue opening (signals imminent adoption), and EP rapporteur report publication (first signal of final obligation scope).

---

# 15. Sources

*Note: Sources 2, 3, and 4 are listed for transparency. No FACT claims are grounded from them as their content was inaccessible or non-substantive.*

---

## New Sources Identified

prior.title: Project JOLT Advances eHGV Real-World Trials; Decarbonization Challenges and Digital Innovation in Road Freight
prior.summary: The Centre for Sustainable Road Freight reports significant progress from Project JOLT, a multi-partner initiative conducting real-world trials of electric heavy goods vehicles (eHGVs). As of February 2026, the project has deployed 9 eHGVs across round-robin testing with major logistics partners, collecting high-resolution performance data. Key findings address haulier concerns around capital costs, payload reduction, and range limitations. The research also highlights fundamental decarbonization challenges in long-haul freight, including battery mass penalties, charging time constraints, and grid infrastructure requirements. Additionally, researchers are developing digital twinning HR tools to prepare organizations for technological adoption.
prior.full_brief:
# Project JOLT: eHGV Real-World Trials, Decarbonisation Challenges, and Digital Innovation in Road Freight

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers Project JOLT (Joint Operator Logistics Trial), a UK-based real-world trial programme for battery-electric heavy goods vehicles (eHGVs), synthesised from four source blocks: the Project JOLT website, the University of Cambridge Department of Engineering announcement, the Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero DP World trial report (August 2025), and the Commercial Motor SME trial article (blocked at retrieval; content not groundable from that source). Corroborating context from the Centre for Sustainable Road Freight (SRF) white paper and UK DfT/DESNZ HGV CO₂ consultation is referenced where available from source content; those sources are listed under New Sources Identified as they were not directly retrievable as source blocks with grounded spans.

Convention: FACT claims carry verbatim source spans. ANALYSIS claims open with a closed-set label token. LEGAL items carry *Legal Confirmation Required:*. Severity: MONITORING, with re-check window Q4 2025 (DP World route electrification decision window) and H1 2026 (next published trial phase findings expected).

Workspace relevance: For workspaces operating as freight forwarders or 3PL providers on UK road lanes, and for those advising shipper clients on fleet decarbonisation timelines, Project JOLT is the live evidence base for eHGV operational viability — the findings directly inform procurement decisions, client sustainability conversations, and regulatory readiness under the emerging UK HGV CO₂ framework.

---

## What the Research Found

### Programme Identity and Leadership

FACT: Project JOLT stands for Joint Operator Logistics Trial and is described as "an ambitious initiative aimed at accelerating the electrification of the UK's heavy goods vehicles."
*Source: Project JOLT website, jolt.eco, 2024. https://jolt.eco/

FACT: The programme "is led by The Centre for Sustainable Road Freight (SRF) and involves partners including John Lewis Partnership, Volvo Trucks UK, and Flexible Power Systems, a software company specialising in fleet management optimisation software for electric vehicles."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "SRF was founded to help industry and government minimise carbon emissions from the road freight sector and is a collaboration between Cambridge University, Heriot-Watt University in Edinburgh, the University of Westminster, and industry and government partners."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: Professor David Cebon is identified as "Professor of Mechanical Engineering at Cambridge University and director of The Centre for Sustainable Road Freight" and established Project JOLT.
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

### Consortium Scale and Structure (as of August 2025)

FACT: As of the DP World trial report (August 2025), Project JOLT "is made up of 15 fleet operators, four manufacturers, a charge point operator and three enablers."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: Evyve is named as "the charge point operator" providing "high powered charging infrastructure and energy solutions for the initiative and subsequent roll outs."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

### Data Methodology and Sharing Architecture

FACT: The trial "will pool operations and technical data from a fleet of rigid and articulated eHGVs being used across a wide range of 3PL, retailer and manufacturer use cases."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "High resolution operational data will be collected during each trial period and then analysed, anonymised, and shared with all partners as part of a managed collective learning process. All shared data will be subject to the provisions of a Data Confidentiality Agreement which will be agreed between all partners before the work starts."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "The objective is to develop the knowledge and models needed to de-risk electric freight operations and inform investment decisions."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "Specialists at Cambridge University and Heriot-Watt University will analyse and model data including vehicle and charger performance, operational efficiency, and costs across as many industry uses as possible."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: The trial "is open to all HGV operators becoming involved by accessing a shared vehicle fleet supplied by OEMs or sharing data from their own vehicles."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

### DP World Trial: Vehicles, Routes, and Results

FACT: "DP World has successfully trialled three electric vehicles under the Project Jolt initiative, which it joined three months ago." The vehicles were "the Scania G230E truck and the Mercedes e-Actros 300, which was upgraded to the e-Actros 600 in the final 3 weeks."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: "DP World trial saw over 170 runs made, amounting to 24,000 kilometres."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: The trucks "made daily shuttles of 115 kilometres from Swindon to Oxford and back."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: DP World's transport controller "devised some simple metrics on battery charging to eliminate range anxiety" and was "delighted" with truck performance.
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: "Project Jolt has provided us with the intelligence, knowledge and confidence in our 12 week trial to be comfortable in operating an electric fleet" — DP World transport operations manager.
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: "Following the trial, DP World and its customer have carried out a strategic review on the commercial opportunity to permanently electrify this route."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: "The company said that, although the cost of the electric truck is still high, the likely cost of electricity will create a favourable total cost of ownership compared to diesel."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

FACT: Professor David Cebon stated: "Our research on battery performance is crucial to the ultimate findings on electrification and will help determine the residual value of electric trucks."
*Source: Motor Transport / Freight Carbon Zero, 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial', 12 August 2025. https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article

### Why This Finding Matters for the UK Road Freight Sector

FACT: "HGVs also account for 20% of CO₂ emissions from domestic transport."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "Electrifying the UK's HGV fleet would be the same as removing 13 million cars from our roads."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: "Freight is hugely important; it contributed £13.6 billion to the economy in 2022. 98% of our food and agricultural products are carried by road, mostly on HGVs."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

FACT: Professor Cebon stated: "The urgency of the climate crisis is driving adoption of electric heavy goods vehicles at a rate that few in the industry would have expected five years ago. Operators are purchasing and running these vehicles today in fleets of all sizes. But there's still a long way to go to understand how whole fleets and industries can transition to electric heavy goods vehicles in a technically feasible way."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

### Commercial Motor / EVITA SME Extension — Source Status

The Commercial Motor article (May 2026) covering SJG Haulage and Port Express running Volvo FH Electric and Mercedes eActros 600 under a JOLT/DP World EVITA scheme was blocked by Cloudflare at retrieval. No verbatim content was accessible. Its stated details — SME participants, vehicle models, EVITA scheme name — are not groundable from the retrieved content and are therefore omitted as FACT claims. They are listed under New Sources Identified as a priority retrieval target.

---

## Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating road-primary freight on UK domestic lanes — particularly fixed-route shuttle operations comparable to DP World's Swindon-Oxford corridor — Project JOLT's 24,000 km / 170-run dataset is the first publicly reported real-world evidence base at this operational scale for routes under 120 km round-trip. The DP World trial demonstrates operational confidence at the 12-week horizon; the strategic review of permanent route electrification signals that at least one major port logistics operator is now evaluating conversion, not just observation.

*Analytical inference:* The total-cost-of-ownership framing in the DP World trial report — high capital cost offset by expected electricity cost advantage over diesel — directly shapes the commercial conversation freight forwarders and 3PL operators must have with shipper clients. Workspaces advising clients on fleet transition or tendering road freight contracts should anticipate TCO comparisons becoming a standard client request.

*Analytical inference:* The battery-residual-value research thread (Professor Cebon's explicit statement on battery performance and residual value) matters commercially because residual value uncertainty is a primary barrier to eHGV financing and leasing decisions. When JOLT publishes battery degradation data, it will directly affect fleet financing terms available to operators — a change that propagates into contract pricing and asset management.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces offering road freight on short-to-medium UK corridors (sub-120 km), the Swindon-Oxford trial result is the most operationally comparable public evidence currently available. Longer corridors — cross-border European road lanes — are not addressed by JOLT's current trial data and remain a separate research gap.

**No-Vacuum Link — Regulatory Context:** The UK Government's January 2026 consultation on a new HGV CO₂ emissions regulatory framework (DfT/DESNZ) sets ZE HGV sales targets for 2035 and 2040. Project JOLT's trial findings are explicitly the evidence base underpinning whether those targets are operationally achievable. *Specific regulatory penalty figures and target percentages from the DfT/DESNZ consultation are not groundable from the source blocks provided; that document is listed under New Sources Identified.*

---

## What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**MONITORING — Re-check window: Q4 2025 for DP World route electrification decision; H1 2026 for next JOLT findings publication.**

### Decision 1: Whether to Participate in JOLT

FACT: The trial "is open to all HGV operators becoming involved by accessing a shared vehicle fleet supplied by OEMs or sharing data from their own vehicles."
*Source: University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial', eng.cam.ac.uk. https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating own-account or 3PL road fleets on UK domestic lanes, the JOLT data-sharing structure offers access to real-world battery performance, charging infrastructure, and cost data without requiring the capital outlay of a unilateral trial. The pre-competitive data-sharing model — governed by a Data Confidentiality Agreement — lowers the intelligence cost of fleet electrification planning. Workspaces that have not yet evaluated whether to apply for JOLT participation should do so before the consortium's trial roster closes.

### Decision 2: Client-Facing Sustainability Commitments on Road Lanes

*Analytical inference:* Shipper clients increasingly request credible decarbonisation timelines from their road freight providers. Project JOLT's published findings — specifically DP World's 24,000 km operational record and subsequent strategic electrification review — provide a citable third-party evidence base that workspaces can reference when advising clients, without overstating their own fleet readiness.

### Decision 3: Charging Infrastructure Planning

*Analytical inference:* Evyve's role as the JOLT charge point operator providing "high powered charging infrastructure and energy solutions for the initiative and subsequent roll outs" signals a commercial pathway — not just a research one — for depot charging deployment. Workspaces evaluating depot infrastructure investment should track Evyve's post-JOLT commercial offering as a potential procurement avenue.

### Decision 4: EU HGV CO₂ Regulation Applicability

*Analytical inference:* JOLT is a UK programme. For workspaces operating cross-border road freight into EU member states, EU-level HGV CO₂ regulation (Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, as amended) sets separate and independently enforced ZE targets. The JOLT evidence base informs, but does not satisfy, EU compliance planning. *Legal Confirmation Required:* how EU amended HGV CO₂ targets apply to non-EU operators running vehicles into EU territory requires counsel and carrier confirmation.

---

## Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

### What the Workspace Can Credibly Say

- Project JOLT is the UK's largest multi-operator eHGV real-world trial programme, led by the University of Cambridge's Centre for Sustainable Road Freight, with 15 fleet operators, four vehicle manufacturers, and a charge point operator as of August 2025.
- The DP World element of the trial completed 170+ runs across 24,000 km on a real commercial shuttle route, with the operator subsequently initiating a strategic review to permanently electrify that route — this is not a lab result, it is operational evidence.
- Battery performance research within JOLT is designed to resolve residual value uncertainty, which is currently the principal barrier to eHGV financing.
- Freight clients asking about electrification timelines can be pointed to JOLT's framework: the data-sharing model is designed precisely to "de-risk electric freight operations and inform investment decisions."

### Pitfalls to Avoid

- Do not cite specific cost figures, electricity price projections, or TCO numbers from JOLT: none have been published in the source material as specific quantified values. The DP World assessment describes a "favourable total cost of ownership compared to diesel" directionally; no figures are sourced.
- Do not claim JOLT covers long-haul or cross-border routes: the DP World trial operated a 115 km daily shuttle. Long-haul electrification evidence is not yet in the public JOLT record.
- Do not claim JOLT results apply to non-UK regulatory environments without separate verification.
- The Commercial Motor / EVITA SME extension (May 2026) cannot be cited from retrieved content — the page was blocked. Avoid referencing EVITA scheme details until that source is retrieved.

### Questions to Pose to Clients

- What proportion of your UK road fleet operates on fixed, sub-120 km daily routes? That is where current eHGV evidence is strongest.
- Have you modelled electricity tariff exposure versus diesel exposure across your depot locations?
- Has your current road carrier confirmed their timeline for fleet electrification, and do they have access to JOLT data or equivalent real-world evidence?

---

## What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**The following material gaps remain open as of the date of this brief:**

1. **Long-haul eHGV viability:** JOLT's published trial data covers a sub-120 km daily shuttle corridor. Whether eHGVs are operationally and commercially viable on longer domestic or cross-border routes is not resolved by current JOLT findings. *Specific distance thresholds, battery range data, and megawatt charging infrastructure requirements are not available from the source blocks provided.*

2. **Battery degradation and residual value:** Professor Cebon explicitly flags battery performance research as "crucial to the ultimate findings on electrification." Those findings are not yet published. Residual value figures are not available from any grounded source in this brief.

3. **EVITA scheme / SME extension:** The Commercial Motor article covering SJG Haulage and Port Express under the JOLT/DP World EVITA scheme was inaccessible at retrieval. The EVITA scheme's design, eligibility criteria, and results are not groundable.

4. **UK HGV CO₂ regulatory targets:** The DfT/DESNZ January 2026 consultation document is listed as a corroborating source but was not among the retrievable source blocks. ZE HGV sales targets for 2035 and 2040 are referred to directionally but specific target percentages and penalty structures are not grounded in this brief. *These are critical to assessing compliance timelines. Legal Confirmation Required:* penalty and enforcement provisions require direct review of the DfT/DESNZ consultation document and any final regulatory text.

5. **EU-side regulatory obligations:** Project JOLT operates under UK policy context. EU amended HGV CO₂ regulation (Regulation (EU) 2019/1242) applies independently to EU-operating vehicles. The interaction between UK and EU HGV decarbonisation obligations for workspaces operating cross-border is not resolved here.

6. **Charging infrastructure costs and grid constraints:** The Faraday Institution Faraday Insight 23 (March 2026) on eHGV battery technology and charging constraints is listed as a corroborating source but was not a retrievable source block. Charging cost figures, grid upgrade requirements, and infrastructure lead times are not grounded in this brief.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| University of Cambridge – Department of Engineering: 'Operators unite in electric HGVs trial' | https://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/electric-hgv-trial | 2 (Academic institution primary page) | Official Cambridge Engineering announcement of Project JOLT; carries consortium partner list, digital-twin/data-sharing methodology, and founding rationale — used as a FACT source in this brief |
| Motor Transport – Freight Carbon Zero: 'DP World hails success of Project Jolt electric truck trial' | https://motortransport.co.uk/freightcarbonzero/dp-world-hails-success-of-project-jolt-electric-truck-trial/26783.article | 4 (Trade press, specialist freight) | Most detailed published operational results from JOLT to date: 170+ runs, 24,000 km, vehicle models, route data, TCO direction, and battery research framing — used as a FACT source in this brief |
| Commercial Motor: 'SMEs trial electric HGVs in DP World and Project JOLT partnership' | https://www.commercialmotor.com/news/article/smes-trial-electric-hgvs-in-dp-world-and-project-jolt-partnership | 4 (Trade press, specialist road transport) | May 2026 update covering SME operator extension under EVITA scheme with Volvo FH Electric and Mercedes eActros 600; blocked by Cloudflare at retrieval — priority for re-retrieval to ground EVITA scheme details and SME participant data |
| Centre for Sustainable Road Freight – White Paper: 'Decarbonising the UK's Long-Haul Road Freight at Minimum Economic Cost' | https://www.csrf.ac.uk/2020/07/white-paper-long-haul-freight-electrification/ | 2 (University research centre primary publication) | SRF's foundational research underpinning JOLT's design rationale; reportedly shows >60% emission cuts require long-haul electrification and 80–90% reduction feasible by 2050 — not yet retrieved as a groundable source block; would anchor the long-haul viability gap |
| UK Government – DfT/DESNZ: 'Consultation on a New Heavy Goods Vehicle CO₂ Emissions Regulatory Framework for the UK' (January 2026) | https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/6983430d13622473b51ca9db/newhgv_co2_emissions_regulatory_framework_for_UK.pdf | 1 (Government primary regulatory document) | Sets ZE HGV sales targets for 2035 and 2040 — the policy context that makes JOLT's urgency legible; not yet retrieved as a groundable source block; critical for effective_date, primary_deadline, and penalty_summary slots if a regulatory brief is generated |
| Faraday Institution – Faraday Insight 23: 'Electrifying Freight: Battery-Powered Heavy Goods Vehicles' (March 2026) | https://www.faraday.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/FaradayInsights-23_FINAL_23March2026.pdf | 2 (Specialist research institution, government-backed) | March 2026 authoritative analysis of eHGV battery technology, charging constraints, and global market trajectory; directly addresses the battery-residual-value and infrastructure gaps this brief cannot resolve from current source blocks |
