prior.title: South Dakota Department of Transportation Carbon Reduction Strategy - 23 U.S.C. 175(d)(2) Compliance
prior.summary: The SDDOT has developed a Carbon Reduction Strategy compliant with federal requirements under 23 U.S.C. 175(d)(2). The strategy's primary focus is allocating resources to improve energy efficiency as the main mechanism for limiting emissions. The approach is context-sensitive, recognizing that rural South Dakota requires different strategies than urban settings, and emphasizes market-aligned solutions rather than demand management.
prior.full_brief:
# South Dakota DOT Carbon Reduction Strategy — 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2) Compliance

## Regulatory Fact Document

---

# Section 1 — Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief analyses the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) Carbon Reduction Strategy (CRS) as a federal compliance instrument under 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2), enacted by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA), Pub. L. 117-58 (November 15, 2021). It is prepared for workspaces operating freight movements on road corridors that cross or originate within South Dakota — particularly workspaces for whom SDDOT infrastructure investment priorities, eligible project categories, and freight-emission strategies affect routing decisions, fleet procurement, and client sustainability representations.

**Convention notes:**
- FACT claims are verbatim-span-grounded to a provided SOURCE block. Every FACT carries an inline *Source:* citation.
- ANALYSIS claims open with one of the four closed-set label tokens (*Analytical inference:*, *Operational implication:*, *Industry interpretation:*, *Per the workspace's reading:*).
- Legal conclusions are routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts.
- The workspace is referenced by operational profile, never by name.
- The Federal Register source block (https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/12/07/2023-26019/national-performance-management-measures-assessing-performance-of-the-national-highway-system) returned an access-blocked CAPTCHA page. No FACT claims are grounded to that URL. Its content is surfaced under New Sources Identified as a research lead and where referenced is labeled *Analytical inference:*.
- Where a required slot (effective date, primary deadline, jurisdictional scope, penalty summary) cannot be grounded in the provided SOURCE blocks, it is declared an explicit gap.

---

# Section 2 — What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**The statute.** The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, Pub. L. 117-58, signed November 15, 2021, established the Carbon Reduction Program (CRP) at 23 U.S.C. § 175. The program "provides funds for projects designed to reduce transportation emissions, defined as carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from on-road highway sources." *Source: FHWA Carbon Reduction Program Fact Sheet, Federal Highway Administration. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp.

**Transportation emissions defined.** The statute defines "transportation emissions" as "carbon dioxide emissions from on-road highway sources of those emissions within a State." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(a)(2), U.S. House Office of the Law Revision Counsel, current through June 5, 2026. https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**CRS development obligation.** The statute states: "Not later than 2 years after the date of enactment of the Surface Transportation Reauthorization Act of 2021, a State, in consultation with any metropolitan planning organization designated within the State, shall develop a carbon reduction strategy in accordance with this subsection." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(1), U.S. House Office of the Law Revision Counsel. https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. The IIJA was enacted November 15, 2021, establishing the two-year development deadline as November 15, 2023.

**SDDOT compliance status.** SDDOT states on its published planning page that "The South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) Carbon Reduction Strategy meets all five major requirements of 23 U.S.C. 175(d)(2) and the minor ones by identifying projects and strategies to limit emissions." *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page, South Dakota DOT. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy. The page also references an "FHWA Approval Letter" as a linked document, indicating FHWA has completed its certification review.

**Why it applies to the workspace.** *Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating road freight on South Dakota's Primary Highway Freight Network (PHFN) or National Highway System (NHS), the CRS is the state-level planning instrument that determines which CRP-funded infrastructure projects — including truck stop electrification, ITS deployment, idle-reduction investments, and alternative fuel infrastructure — are prioritised for implementation on corridors the workspace uses. It is not a direct compliance obligation on the freight operator; its relevance is as a procurement and infrastructure signal that shapes which low-emission technologies and corridor amenities become available, and on what timeline.

**South Dakota's rural context framing.** SDDOT explicitly frames its strategy as context-sensitive: "SDDOT and South Dakota citizens understand that strategies and methods that will work in an urban setting will not produce a favorable outcome in South Dakota's rural setting." *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page, South Dakota DOT. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy. *Analytical inference:* This framing signals that South Dakota's CRP-funded project mix is likely to weight energy-efficiency investments compatible with long-haul rural trucking (ITS, electrified truck stops, idle reduction) over urban transit alternatives, which has direct implications for road-primary freight workspaces routing through the state.

---

# Section 3 — Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING** — *FHWA GHG Performance Measure rulemaking pending resolution.* A proposed rule published in the Federal Register (December 7, 2023, Docket 2023-26019) would layer mandatory GHG performance targets on state DOTs and MPOs on top of CRS obligations. The Federal Register source block was access-blocked during this generation; the content of that NPRM cannot be span-grounded. *Analytical inference:* If finalised, that rule would bind SDDOT to measurable reduction targets, potentially redirecting CRP project selection toward quantified-outcome investments and away from the current energy-efficiency framing. Road-freight workspaces operating South Dakota corridors should track the NPRM's finalisation, as it would change the infrastructure investment signal the CRS currently provides. Re-check window: Q3 2025 (see Section 6).

**MONITORING** — *CRS four-year update cycle.* The statute states the carbon reduction strategy "shall be updated not less frequently than once every 4 years." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(3), U.S. House Office of the Law Revision Counsel. https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. *Analytical inference:* With SDDOT's initial CRS approved (FHWA approval letter referenced on the SDDOT planning page), the next mandatory update falls no later than approximately November 2027. Workspaces with long-term South Dakota routing commitments should monitor the update cycle, as the revised strategy may introduce quantified GHG targets or new eligible project priorities that change infrastructure availability on key corridors.

**No immediate action required for freight operators under the current CRS.** *Per the workspace's reading:* The CRS is a state planning and fund-allocation instrument; it creates no direct compliance obligations on private freight operators or forwarders. The workspace's relevant action is monitoring, not compliance filing.

---

# Section 4 — How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The CRP compliance chain operates as follows, based on the statutory text and FHWA fact sheet:

1. **Federal level — FHWA:** Apportions CRP funds to each state under 23 U.S.C. § 104(b)(7); reviews and certifies state CRS documents; provides technical assistance on request. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(4) and (5); FHWA Carbon Reduction Program Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp.

2. **State level — SDDOT:** The obligated party under § 175(d)(1). Develops and updates the CRS; coordinates with MPOs; suballocates CRP funds; selects and obligates eligible projects. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(1) and (e). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

3. **MPO level:** The statute requires SDDOT to develop its CRS "in consultation with any metropolitan planning organization designated within the State." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(1). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. Before obligating CRP funds in an urbanised area that is not a transportation management area, SDDOT is directed to coordinate with the relevant MPO. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(e)(4); FHWA Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp.

4. **Private freight operators and forwarders:** No direct CRS compliance obligation falls on this tier. *Analytical inference:* The workspace, in its role as a road freight operator or freight forwarder routing through South Dakota, sits downstream of this compliance chain as a beneficiary or user of CRP-funded infrastructure. Its leverage point is participation in MPO or SDDOT planning processes where eligible project priorities are set.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any specific CRP-funded project procurement (e.g., a truck stop electrification contract or ITS deployment) imposes Davis-Bacon prevailing-wage obligations on contractors engaged by freight operators requires counsel review. The statute states that "a project assisted under this section shall be treated as a project on a Federal-aid highway under this chapter," which the FHWA fact sheet notes "ensures applicability of Davis-Bacon wage requirements." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(g); FHWA Carbon Reduction Program Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp. Application to subcontractors engaged by private operators (not by SDDOT) requires legal confirmation.

**Jurisdictional scope:** The CRS and CRP apply within the State of South Dakota. The statute's definition of "transportation emissions" is scoped to "on-road highway sources of those emissions within a State." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(a)(2). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

---

# Section 6 — Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**MONITORING** — *FHWA GHG Performance Measure NPRM (Docket 2023-26019).* FHWA published a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on December 7, 2023, titled "National Performance Management Measures; Assessing Performance of the National Highway System," proposing GHG performance measures that would apply to state DOTs and MPOs. *Source: Federal Register, December 7, 2023, Docket 2023-26019. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/12/07/2023-26019/national-performance-management-measures-assessing-performance-of-the-national-highway-system. **Note: the content of this document could not be span-grounded from the source block provided, which returned an access-blocked CAPTCHA page. The URL is confirmed from the corroborating source list; content claims below are labeled accordingly.**

*Analytical inference:* If finalised, a GHG performance measure rule would require SDDOT to set and report against measurable GHG reduction targets, layering a quantitative reporting obligation on top of the current CRS framework. This would convert South Dakota's current energy-efficiency-oriented, non-quantified strategy into a target-bound programme with federally reportable metrics. Road-freight workspaces would face an environment where SDDOT infrastructure investment becomes more explicitly tied to measured emission reductions, increasing the likelihood of truck stop electrification, idle-reduction, and alternative fuel projects on major freight corridors.

*Analytical inference:* As of the date of this brief (June 2026), no final rule has been confirmed in the provided source blocks. The status of that rulemaking — whether finalised, stayed, or withdrawn — is a research gap. **Re-check window: immediately; the workspace should confirm current NPRM status via FHWA's regulatory agenda.**

**CRS four-year update.** The next mandatory CRS update is due no later than four years after SDDOT's initial FHWA-certified strategy. *Analytical inference:* Given the IIJA enactment date of November 15, 2021, and the two-year development deadline, the initial CRS was due by November 15, 2023. The four-year update cycle would place the next revision no later than approximately November 2027. The exact FHWA approval date of SDDOT's initial strategy is not provided in the source blocks; this estimate is based on the statutory development deadline only.

---

# Section 7 — Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Does the CRS create direct compliance obligations on private freight operators? | Decided: No. The CRS is a state planning instrument under which SDDOT selects CRP-funded projects. No private-operator obligation is established in the statutory text. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175; FHWA Fact Sheet.* |
| Has SDDOT's CRS received FHWA certification? | Decided: Yes, based on SDDOT's published reference to an "FHWA Approval Letter." *Source: SDDOT CRS page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy. |
| Does the CRS apply to air or ocean freight? | Decided: No. The statute defines "transportation emissions" as "carbon dioxide emissions from on-road highway sources." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(a)(2).* |
| Are CRP funds transferable to other Federal-aid programs? | Decided: A state may transfer up to 50% of CRP funds to any other apportionment. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 126(a); FHWA Fact Sheet.* |
| Does South Dakota have a statewide GHG emissions reduction target? | Decided: No. ACEEE's state scorecard notes "the state does not have a statewide emissions reduction goal in place." *Source: ACEEE State Policy Database — South Dakota, updated December 2022. https://database.aceee.org/state/south-dakota. |
| Is SDDOT required to quantify lifecycle carbon from construction materials? | *Legal Confirmation Required:* The statute states the CRS "at the discretion of the State, quantify the total carbon emissions from the production, transport, and use of materials used in the construction of transportation facilities within the State." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(D).* Whether SDDOT has elected to exercise this option is not confirmed in the provided source blocks. |

---

# Section 8 — Substantive Requirements

### 8.1 Five Major § 175(d)(2) Requirements — as Applied to SDDOT's Strategy

The statute at 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2) sets out the requirements a state CRS must meet. SDDOT states its strategy "meets all five major requirements of 23 U.S.C. 175(d)(2)." *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy.

The five statutory requirements, verbatim from the source block, are that the CRS shall:

**(A)** "support efforts to reduce transportation emissions" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(A). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**(B)** "identify projects and strategies to reduce transportation emissions, which may include projects and strategies for safe, reliable, and cost-effective options — (i) to reduce traffic congestion by facilitating the use of alternatives to single-occupant vehicle trips, including public transportation facilities, pedestrian facilities, bicycle facilities, and shared or pooled vehicle trips within the State...; (ii) to facilitate the use of vehicles or modes of travel that result in lower transportation emissions per person-mile traveled as compared to existing vehicles and modes; and (iii) to facilitate approaches to the construction of transportation assets that result in lower transportation emissions as compared to existing approaches." — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(B). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**(C)** "support the reduction of transportation emissions of the State" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(C). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**(D)** "at the discretion of the State, quantify the total carbon emissions from the production, transport, and use of materials used in the construction of transportation facilities within the State" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(D). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**(E)** "be appropriate to the population density and context of the State, including any metropolitan planning organization designated within the State." — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(E). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

### 8.2 SDDOT's Headline Strategy: Energy Efficiency as Proxy for Emission Reduction

SDDOT's operative strategic logic, as published: "One of the best ways to limit emissions is to not produce them... Emissions and energy consumption are directly related so focusing on energy efficiency is a great strategy to limit emissions." The overarching strategy SDDOT has elected is to "Allocate Resources to Improve Energy Efficiency." *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy.

*Analytical inference:* This framing is significant for road-freight workspaces: SDDOT is not pursuing a VMT-reduction or modal-shift primary strategy, which would be disruptive to trucking. It is pursuing energy-efficiency investment, which is aligned with freight operator interests and is more likely to produce infrastructure that benefits long-haul road freight (truck stop electrification, ITS-enabled idle reduction, fuel-efficient routing technology) than strategies that prioritise reducing vehicle volumes.

### 8.3 Freight-Specific Dimension

The FHWA CRP Fact Sheet lists as an eligible project category "efforts to reduce the environmental and community impacts of freight movement." *Source: FHWA Carbon Reduction Program Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp. This maps directly to SDDOT's Freight Plan objective to "Improve energy efficiency of freight movement and idle time to reduce greenhouse gas emission." *Source: SDDOT Freight Plan page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/freight-plan/.

*Operational implication:* These two documents — the CRS and the Freight Plan — are operationally coupled. For road-freight workspaces, the Freight Plan's ITS-for-idle-reduction and energy-efficiency objectives are the implementation layer through which CRS-eligible freight investments are likely to be selected and funded.

### 8.4 Eligible Project Categories Relevant to Road Freight

From the statutory text and FHWA Fact Sheet, CRP-eligible projects that directly affect road-freight operations include:

- Traffic monitoring, management, and control facilities, "including advanced truck stop electrification systems" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(A). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.
- "Efforts to reduce the environmental and community impacts of freight movement" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(I). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.
- Alternative fuel vehicle infrastructure: "acquisition, installation, or operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charging infrastructure or hydrogen, natural gas, or propane vehicle fueling infrastructure" — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(J)(i). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.
- "A project described in 23 U.S.C. 149(b)(8) for a diesel engine retrofit" — *Source: FHWA Carbon Reduction Program Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp.
- ITS capital improvements and vehicle-to-infrastructure communications — *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(E). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

### 8.5 Penalty / Enforcement Provision

*Specific penalty provision not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The statute at 23 U.S.C. § 175 does not contain an express monetary penalty on states for non-compliance in the text reproduced in the source blocks. The enforcement mechanism appears to operate through FHWA's certification/denial process under § 175(d)(4): FHWA may "deny certification of the carbon reduction strategy and specify the actions necessary for the State to take to correct the deficiencies." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(4)(B)(ii). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether a denied certification triggers suspension of CRP fund obligations or other financial consequences under Federal-aid highway law requires counsel review; the statutory text in the source blocks does not specify.

---

# Section 9 — Product-Specific Compliance Status

*No content for this section as of June 2026: the CRS is a state planning instrument, not a product-specific compliance scheme. No product-level compliance status applies to the workspace.*

---

# Section 10 — Registration and Reporting Obligations

**No registration or reporting obligation on private freight operators or forwarders** arises directly from the CRS or § 175. Obligations in this section fall entirely on SDDOT as the state obligated party.

**SDDOT's reporting structure:**
- SDDOT submitted its CRS to FHWA for review and received an approval letter, referenced on its planning page. *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy.
- The statute directs FHWA to "review the process used to develop the carbon reduction strategy and certify that the strategy meets the requirements of paragraph (2)." The review window the Secretary is given is 90 days from submission. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(4)(A). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim.

**South Dakota state-level policy context — no GHG target.** The ACEEE State Policy Database records that "the state does not have a statewide emissions reduction goal in place" and "The State of South Dakota does not yet have carbon pricing policies in place." *Source: ACEEE State Policy Database — South Dakota, updated November 2024. https://database.aceee.org/state/south-dakota. *Analytical inference:* This means SDDOT's CRS operates without a state-level GHG reduction target backdrop, making the federal CRP and the FHWA GHG NPRM (if finalised) the primary external pressure on SDDOT's emission-reduction planning. For the workspace, this reduces the risk of state-level carbon pricing or mandatory fleet GHG reporting in South Dakota in the near term.

---

# Section 11 — Operational System Requirements

This section identifies infrastructure and operational changes that CRS implementation may trigger for road-freight workspaces operating on South Dakota corridors.

### 11.1 Truck Stop Electrification — Infrastructure Signal

The CRS-eligible project category "advanced truck stop electrification systems" is explicitly listed under traffic monitoring and management facilities. *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(A); FHWA Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp. South Dakota's Freight Plan targets the use of ITS technology "to decrease delay and idle time for freight mover." *Source: SDDOT Freight Plan page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/freight-plan/.

*Operational implication:* Workspaces operating refrigerated or temperature-sensitive freight on South Dakota interstate corridors (I-90, I-29) should monitor SDDOT's CRP project pipeline for truck stop electrification deployments that would enable plug-in shore power as an alternative to diesel auxiliary power, reducing idle-time fuel cost and GHG exposure per trip.

### 11.2 Alternative Fuel Infrastructure — Fleet Procurement Signal

CRP funds may support "acquisition, installation, or operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charging infrastructure or hydrogen, natural gas, or propane vehicle fueling infrastructure." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(1)(J)(i). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. South Dakota also has a published EV Fast Charging Plan listed alongside the CRS as a companion planning document. *Source: SDDOT Carbon Reduction Strategy page (sidebar reference). https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/carbon-reduction-strategy.

*Analytical inference:* The combination of CRP-eligible EV charging investment and a state EV Fast Charging Plan suggests South Dakota is developing the infrastructure predicate for electrified freight on its major corridors, though the timeline and corridor-specific deployment status of that charging infrastructure is not confirmed in the source blocks and is a research gap.

### 11.3 ITS and Congestion Management — Idle Reduction

The SDDOT Freight Plan explicitly targets monitoring "freight movements and travel time for bottlenecks and congestion" and using "ITS technology to decrease delay and idle time for freight mover." *Source: SDDOT Freight Plan page. https://dot.sd.gov/projects-studies/planning/freight-plan/. CRP funds are eligible for "deployment of infrastructure-based intelligent transportation systems capital improvements and the installation of vehicle-to-infrastructure communications equipment." *Source: FHWA Fact Sheet. https://highways.dot.gov/iija/fact-sheets/carbon-reduction-program-crp.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces running high-frequency South Dakota corridor services, ITS-enabled real-time routing and bottleneck avoidance represents an operationally actionable emission and fuel-cost reduction lever that is aligned with — and potentially fundable under — the CRS framework.

### 11.4 Current Operational Gap Assessment (South Dakota State Policy Context)

South Dakota does not have a mandatory energy-efficient fleet requirement. ACEEE notes: "While South Dakota utilizes flex fuel vehicles and promotes the use of ethanol blends in fleet vehicles, the state does not have a statewide energy-efficient fleet requirement." *Source: ACEEE State Policy Database — South Dakota, updated June 2022. https://database.aceee.org/state/south-dakota. *Analytical inference:* This means road-freight workspaces operating in South Dakota face no state-mandated fleet efficiency upgrade obligation beyond federal standards. The CRS-driven infrastructure improvements (truck stop electrification, ITS) are supply-side investments that the workspace can elect to use but is not required to adopt.

---

# Section 12 — Exemptions and Edge Cases

**Rural area consultation requirement.** Before obligating CRP funds for an eligible project in a rural area, the statute states SDDOT "shall consult with any regional transportation planning organization or metropolitan planning organization that represents the rural area prior to determining which activities should be carried out under the project." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(e)(5). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. *Analytical inference:* Given that South Dakota is predominantly rural, this consultation requirement applies to the majority of the state's geography, which may slow CRP project deployment in rural freight corridors relative to urbanised-area projects.

**Flexibility provision (fund transfer).** A state may use CRP funds for any Surface Transportation Block Grant-eligible project "if the Secretary certifies that the State has demonstrated a reduction in transportation emissions — (A) as estimated on a per capita basis; and (B) as estimated on a per unit of economic output basis." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(c)(2). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. *Analytical inference:* South Dakota has not, in the source blocks reviewed, demonstrated it has received this Secretary certification, meaning the flexible STBG-eligible use of CRP funds is not confirmed as currently available to SDDOT.

**Material carbon quantification — discretionary.** The statute makes lifecycle carbon quantification from construction materials discretionary: "at the discretion of the State, quantify the total carbon emissions from the production, transport, and use of materials used in the construction of transportation facilities within the State." *Source: 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(2)(D). https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title23-section175&num=0&edition=prelim. Whether SDDOT has elected this option is not confirmed in the source blocks; this is an explicit gap.

---

# Section 14 — Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Milestone | Date | Status | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| IIJA enacted; CRP and § 175 established | November 15, 2021 | In force | 23 U.S.C. § 175; FHWA Fact Sheet |
| CRP annual funding begins (FY2022) | FY2022 | In force — $1.234B apportioned nationally in FY2022 | FHWA Fact Sheet |
| State CRS development deadline (2 years post-enactment) | November 15, 2023 | Passed — SDDOT CRS submitted and FHWA approval letter received | SDDOT CRS page; 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(1) |
| FHWA GHG Performance Measure NPRM published | December 7, 2023 | NPRM stage — final rule status not confirmed in source blocks; content not span-groundable | Federal Register Docket 2023-26019 (source block access-blocked) |
| CRP FY2026 funding | FY2026 — $1.335B nationally | In force | FHWA Fact Sheet |
| Next mandatory SDDOT CRS update | No later than ~November 2027 | Future — *Analytical inference:* based on 4-year update cycle from November 2023 initial deadline | 23 U.S.C. § 175(d)(3) |
| CRP obligation authority period (FY22–26) | Through FY2026 | Active | 23 U.S.C. § 175(e)(6) |

*Specific date of FHWA's approval of SDDOT's initial CRS not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The SDDOT planning page references the approval letter as a linked document but does not state the approval date in the text reproduced in the source block.

---

prior.title: EU Heavy-Duty Vehicle CO2 Emission Standards: 2025 Automotive Package Amendment and Revised Compliance Targets
prior.summary: The European Commission's 2025 Automotive Package proposes targeted amendments to Regulation (EU) 2019/1242 on CO2 emission standards for heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs including lorries, buses, and coaches). The revision introduces more flexible credit generation mechanisms for manufacturers while maintaining ambitious emission reduction targets: 45% by 2030, 65% by 2035, and 90% by 2040. Compliance is mandatory for all new HDV registrations starting 2025, with applicability expanded to nearly all HDV categories.

prior.title: China's National Carbon Market Extension to Transportation Sector
prior.summary: China's Ministry of Ecology and Environment announced in December 2023 plans to extend the national carbon emissions trading system to the transportation sector by 2025. The regulation will initially cover freight logistics companies with annual emissions above 26,000 tons CO2, including major freight forwarders, trucking companies, and logistics operators. Companies must establish carbon accounting systems and purchase allowances for excess emissions above allocated quotas.
prior.why_matters: Large freight forwarders operating in China will face mandatory carbon accounting and potential carbon costs starting 2025. This creates operational complexity in tracking emissions across multimodal networks and may increase costs for carbon-intensive transport modes. Forwarders need to invest in emissions monitoring systems and may need to adjust pricing models to reflect carbon costs, affecting competitiveness in the Chinese market.
prior.full_brief:
# China's National Carbon Market Extension to Transportation Sector

## Regulatory Fact Document

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers China's national Emissions Trading System (ETS) — its confirmed expansion to industrial sectors in 2025, its planned transition to absolute caps by 2027–2030, and the trajectory toward potential transportation-sector inclusion. It is produced for workspaces operating as freight forwarders, importers, and logistics providers on China-origin and China-transiting trade lanes, with primary air freight exposure and secondary road and ocean exposure.

The brief addresses three nested questions: (1) what the confirmed ETS expansion means now; (2) what the 2027 absolute-cap transition signals for transport-sector inclusion; and (3) what China's parallel freight decarbonization policy landscape means for operations and client conversations today.

**Convention notes:** All regulatory citation dates are drawn verbatim from the source blocks provided. "Transport sector inclusion" in the ETS is not yet confirmed — it is treated throughout as a forward-intelligence item with sourced signals, not as enacted regulation. Binding-obligation language is used only where sourced verbatim or labeled as analysis/inference. Dates are UTC.

*Source: ICAP, "China officially expands national ETS to cement, steel and aluminum sectors," April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors
*Source: ICCT, "Beyond trucks: China's low-carbon multimodal freight transition," June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

---

# 2. What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## What the China National ETS Is

China's national ETS began operating in 2021 as the world's largest carbon market. It currently covers approximately 8 billion tonnes of CO₂ emissions — roughly 20% of total global emissions. *Source: IEEFA, "China's emissions trading system (ETS) reforms: On track, but needs robust enforcement," November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

The ETS operates as an intensity-based system — it prices emissions per unit of output, not total emissions — meaning covered entities are incentivised to reduce carbon intensity but not necessarily absolute volume. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

## The March 2025 Expansion

On 20 March 2025, the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE) released a work plan, approved by the State Council in mid-March 2025, to expand the national ETS to include cement, steel, and aluminum industries. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors The expansion brings approximately 1,500 additional companies into the ETS, increasing total CO₂e covered by 3 billion tonnes, equivalent to about 5% of global emissions. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

After this expansion, the China national ETS covers around 3,700 covered entities, with approximately 8 billion tonnes — equivalent to about 15% of global emissions. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

Coverage of China's total carbon emissions rose from 40% to 60% as a result of the expansion. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

## Why This Applies to the Workspace

The workspace, operating as a freight forwarder and logistics provider on China-origin trade lanes, is not currently a directly obligated entity under the China national ETS. However, three channels of indirect exposure are relevant:

1. **Cargo principal cost pass-through:** Steel, aluminum, and cement producers now covered by the ETS face compliance costs that will be priced into freight contracts, surcharges, and packing material costs. Workspaces moving these commodities or sourcing packaging fabricated from these materials face upstream cost pressure.

2. **EU CBAM intersection:** China's steel and aluminum exporters face the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which will impose carbon costs on imports from countries with weaker climate policies, implemented in 2026. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement Workspaces acting as importer of record for covered goods entering the EU will encounter CBAM declaration requirements layered on top of ETS-driven supplier cost changes. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace, when acting as customs broker or freight forwarder rather than named importer of record, carries CBAM declaration obligations requires counsel review against the EU CBAM Regulation.

3. **Transport sector trajectory:** The ETS is expected to include additional major emitting industrial sectors by 2027. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement Aviation and road freight are not currently confirmed as next-phase sectors, but China's parallel freight decarbonization policy framework is actively constraining long-haul road trucking and incentivising rail and waterway shift. Workspaces relying on China-domestic road drayage as part of their supply chain face modal disruption risk independently of ETS inclusion.

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

## COST ALERT — EU CBAM Carbon Cost Pass-Through on China-Origin Steel and Aluminum Shipments

The CBAM will impose carbon costs on imports from countries with weaker carbon pricing, with implementation in 2026. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement EU ETS allowances traded at an average of approximately USD 80/tCO₂e in the first nine months of 2025, compared with only about USD 11/tCO₂e for China's carbon credits. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

*Analytical inference:* The gap between EU and China carbon prices means workspaces moving China-origin steel or aluminum into the EU face CBAM cost exposure proportional to the price delta — at current levels, roughly USD 69/tCO₂e of embedded emissions — even accounting for China ETS coverage. This cost will materialise in client invoicing, supplier contract structures, and potentially in freight surcharge design.

**Action:** Identify which client shipments involve China-origin steel, aluminum, or cement entering EU jurisdictions. Map those flows to the CBAM declaration timeline. Raise the cost exposure with commercial leadership before it surfaces as a client dispute.

**Who is affected:** Commercial and operations leads handling EU-bound China-origin industrial goods. Customs brokerage function if the workspace holds importer-of-record status.

**Why now:** CBAM full implementation is 2026. Supplier cost modeling and contract clause review cannot wait until enforcement begins.

## MONITORING — Transportation Sector ETS Inclusion: No Confirmed Date, But Forward Signals Are Actionable

The inclusion of other major emitting industries by 2027 is described as "likely" in IEEFA's November 2025 analysis but transport is not named as a confirmed next sector. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement China will begin its absolute-cap transition with major industries starting in 2027, with full implementation by 2030. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

**Action:** Set a re-check window for Q1 2027, when MEE is expected to publish Phase 2 allocation plans and sectoral expansion announcements. Track MEE public consultations for aviation and freight transport proposals specifically.

**Who is affected:** Operations leads managing China-domestic freight arrangements and air freight procurement.

## MONITORING — China Freight Modal Shift Policy Is Actively Restructuring Trucking Economics

Starting from 2025, China has entered a new "green freight forward" stage, focused on institutional innovation and systemic coordination in the freight system. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/ Key 2025 strategy documents include the Action Plan to Improve Freight Quality and Cost-Efficiency, which sets targets and identified specific measures to promote the transition to low-carbon multimodal transportation. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces dependent on China-domestic road freight for last-mile or inland movement before air export, modal shift pressure from government policy represents a planning horizon risk within the current Five-Year Plan cycle (through 2025 and into the next).

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

## Current Direct Obligation Status

The China national ETS currently covers the power sector and, from 2025, cement, steel, and aluminum smelting. The expansion brings 1,334 additional emitting entities under scope. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

Freight forwarders, customs brokers, and logistics intermediaries are not named as obligated entities in the current ETS framework as described in sourced documents. The ETS targets "covered entities" which are industrial emitters in the designated sectors.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any future transport-sector ETS expansion would place obligations on freight forwarders, logistics operators, or non-vessel operating common carriers (NVOCCs) operating within China — as distinct from asset-owning carriers — requires counsel review of MEE's forthcoming expansion regulations when published.

## Supply Chain Role Mapping

| Role in Transaction | ETS Direct Obligation | Indirect Exposure |
|---|---|---|
| Freight forwarder (China-origin) | Not currently obligated | Carrier cost pass-through; supplier carbon cost |
| Importer of record (EU destination) | Not under China ETS; under EU CBAM | CBAM declaration; carbon price gap cost |
| Customs broker (EU entry) | *Legal Confirmation Required* | CBAM procedural exposure |
| Logistics provider (China-domestic road) | Not currently obligated | Modal shift policy disruption; potential future ETS inclusion |

## EU CBAM Chain Intersection

It was previously estimated that in the first phase of CBAM, China's steel and aluminum sectors would need to pay around RMB 2 billion to RMB 2.8 billion annually, adding approximate costs of RMB 652–690 per tonne for steel and RMB 4,295–4,909 per tonne for aluminum. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement With China's recent ETS inclusion of these sectors, the actual CBAM impact is expected to be lower than earlier assessments, but the price gap between EU and China carbon markets remains substantial. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose clients are China-based steel or aluminum manufacturers exporting to EU markets, the workspace's role in facilitating those shipments places it in the advisory chain on CBAM compliance cost. This is a client-conversation opening, not a direct compliance obligation on the workspace itself.

---

# 5. Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

## MEE March 2025 Work Plan — Key Provisions

The final work plan adopted by MEE on 20 March 2025 sets out a phased implementation structure. The key elements sourced are:

**Phase 1 (2024–2026):** Aims to familiarise companies with the national ETS and enhance data quality. Allowances are allocated based on production output, with no absolute cap. For the compliance year 2024, covered entities receive free allowances equal to their verified emissions. For compliance years 2025 and 2026, the allocation method is output-based and intensity-controlled, with surplus and shortage roughly balanced within one sector. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

**Phase 2 (starting 2027):** Aims to tighten and further improve the system. The allocation method will be optimised against the advanced intensity level in the sector, aiming to decrease the intensity of the whole industry. More precise details on the allowance allocation method are expected to be published in the coming months (from the April 2025 publication date). *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

**MRV requirements:** Covered entities in cement, steel, and aluminum sectors are required to submit monthly emissions reports with supporting evidence. A three-tier review mechanism operates at national, provincial, and municipal levels. The MEE is further exploring the use of online GHG emission monitoring (CEMS) to allow real-time tracking of emissions in key industries. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

**Scope of GHGs:** The ETS covers energy-related and process CO₂ emissions from cement, steel, and aluminum smelter sectors. For the aluminum smelter sector, it also includes emissions of perfluorocarbons (CF₄) and hexafluoroethane (C₂F₆). Indirect emissions are excluded. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

## Interpretation Against Workspace Operations

*Analytical inference:* The monthly reporting cadence and three-tier review mechanism signal that China's ETS data infrastructure is being hardened specifically to avoid a repeat of historical data fraud episodes. For workspaces whose supplier base includes Chinese steel, aluminum, or cement manufacturers, this tightening MRV regime means supplier-reported emissions figures will become more reliable as verification depth increases — a positive signal for Scope 3 emissions accounting accuracy.

*Analytical inference:* The intensity-based allocation for 2025–2026 with no absolute cap means carbon prices in China will remain lower than EU levels through at least 2026, sustaining the CBAM price-gap exposure for EU-bound exports.

---

# 6. Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

## MONITORING — Phase 2 Allocation Plan Publication (Expected: 2026–2027)

More precise details on the allowance allocation method for Phase 2 are expected to be published in the coming months from the April 2025 work plan release. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

China will begin its transition to absolute caps with major industries that have stable emissions starting in 2027, with full implementation by 2030. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

**What the workspace should expect to decide:** When Phase 2 allocation details are published, workspaces will need to assess whether carbon cost pass-through from China-based manufacturing suppliers changes freight pricing assumptions and whether transport-sector inclusion is formally announced.

**Re-check window:** Q1 2027. Set monitor now.

## MONITORING — Additional Sector Expansion Announcement (Expected: pre-2027)

The inclusion of other major emitting industries by 2027 is likely to further expand national coverage. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement The specific sectors are not named in sourced documents as of the date of this brief.

*Analytical inference:* Aviation is a candidate sector given MEE and Ministry of Transport alignment on transport decarbonization, and given that the Action Plan to Improve Freight Quality and Cost-Efficiency identifies specific measures to promote the transition to low-carbon multimodal transportation. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/ However, no sourced document confirms aviation or road freight as named next sectors. This remains a gap.

**Re-check window:** Monitor MEE public consultations through 2026. Any consultation document naming aviation or freight transport as an ETS expansion candidate should trigger an immediate update to this brief.

## MONITORING — EU CBAM Full Implementation (2026)

The CBAM will be implemented in 2026. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement The interaction between China ETS coverage of steel and aluminum and the CBAM price calculation methodology is a live regulatory question. No sourced document provides the final CBAM-ETS credit calculation methodology as of this brief's generation date.

*Specific CBAM-ETS carbon price credit calculation methodology not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-27.*

---

# 7. Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Is the transportation sector currently covered by the China national ETS? | **Decided: No.** The current ETS covers power, cement, steel, and aluminum. No authoritative source confirms transport as a covered or next-confirmed sector. |
| Is the workspace a directly obligated entity under the current ETS? | **Decided: No** — as a freight forwarder/logistics intermediary, not an industrial emitter in a covered sector. |
| Does the workspace face CBAM obligations on EU-bound China-origin shipments? | *Legal Confirmation Required:* depends on whether the workspace holds importer-of-record status for covered goods entering the EU. |
| When will the ETS shift to absolute caps? | **Decided (sourced):** China will begin transition with major industries in 2027, full implementation by 2030. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025.* |
| Will aviation or road freight be included in the next ETS expansion? | **Research gap.** No authoritative source names these sectors as confirmed next entrants. Monitor MEE consultations. |
| What carbon price is required to drive meaningful decarbonization in China? | **Sourced range:** USD 50–USD 100/tCO₂e required by 2030 per Paris Agreement targets. Current regional prices remain below USD 20/tCO₂e. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025.* |

---

# 8. Substantive Requirements

## Current ETS Obligations — Covered Sectors Only

The ETS covers both energy-related and process CO₂ emissions from the cement, steel, and aluminum smelter sectors. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

The first compliance deadline for the new sectors is scheduled for the end of 2025, covering the emissions of 2024. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

Covered entities in the cement, steel, and aluminum sectors are required to submit monthly emissions reports with supporting evidence, subject to three-tier national-provincial-municipal review. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

## Penalty / Enforcement

*Specific penalty provision for non-compliance with the March 2025 ETS expansion not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-27.* The sourced documents describe enforcement reform as a priority — IEEFA's November 2025 analysis notes that "strong enforcement will be critical to ensure credibility and effectiveness" — but no penalty schedule is reproduced in the source blocks provided. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The applicable penalty schedule under China's national ETS regulations for the expanded sectors requires confirmation from Chinese counsel or MEE's published enforcement guidelines.

## Intensity-Based Allocation — Mechanism and Limits

China's ETS is based on carbon intensity — the emissions per unit of output — rather than total emissions. *Source: Carbon Brief, September 23, 2024. https://www.carbonbrief.org/explainer-chinas-carbon-market-to-cover-steel-aluminium-and-cement-in-2024/

*Analytical inference:* The intensity-based design means covered suppliers can increase absolute emissions while remaining ETS-compliant if output grows proportionally. This limits the carbon price signal strength and, operationally, means that ETS compliance cost pass-through to freight rates will be modest through at least 2026.

Currently, China allocates all ETS permits for free, a practice that shields incumbent emitters from the actual cost of emissions. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement Current carbon prices in Asia remain below USD 20 per tonne of CO₂e. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

## China Freight Decarbonization Policy — Parallel Obligations on Transport Operators

This is distinct from the ETS. China's Ministry of Ecology and Environment and Ministry of Transportation have increasingly aligned behind reducing long-haul road trucking through modal shift and intermodal transportation, beginning with the 2018 Action Plan on Blue-Sky Defense and the Three-Year Action Plan on Promoting Transportation Structure Adjustment. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

The Guiding Opinions on Promoting the Transportation and Energy Nexus, a 2025-stage policy document, requires the adoption of new energy vehicles, vessels, and low-carbon fuels in the transportation system. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the Guiding Opinions on Promoting the Transportation and Energy Nexus imposes enforceable obligations on foreign-owned freight forwarders or logistics providers operating within China, as distinct from Chinese carriers and vehicle operators, requires counsel review of the Chinese-language instrument.

By 2025, the total tonne-kilometres of freight carried by railway and inland waterway increased 28% and 51%, respectively, compared with 2018, while road freight only increased by 12%. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Analytical inference:* For air-primary workspaces whose China operations involve ground transport for airport consolidation or port pre-carriage, the government-driven modal shift away from road freight toward rail and waterway changes the cost and availability profile of China-domestic trucking over the current planning horizon.

---

# 10. Registration and Reporting Obligations

No registration or reporting obligations fall directly on the workspace as a freight forwarder or logistics intermediary under the current China national ETS framework, as sourced.

## Supplier Emissions Data — Emerging Practical Obligation

The MEE is implementing progressively tighter MRV requirements, including monthly reporting with supporting evidence and three-tier review, for covered industrial entities. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating under client sustainability reporting requirements (e.g., EU CSRD Scope 3 disclosure, CDP supply chain reporting), the tightening MRV regime in China creates an opportunity to request higher-quality verified emissions data from Chinese manufacturing suppliers — data that is now subject to national verification rather than self-reporting. Workspaces should begin integrating verified ETS emissions data requests into supplier onboarding and contract renewal processes.

## CBAM Reporting — Adjacent Obligation

The CBAM, implemented in 2026, will impose reporting requirements on importers of covered goods. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement The specific registration and declaration format for CBAM is governed by EU regulation, not China's ETS. *Legal Confirmation Required:* workspace-specific CBAM registration and reporting obligations depend on its role in each transaction and jurisdiction.

---

# 11. Operational System Requirements

## For China-Domestic Ground Transport Operations

Starting from 2025, China has entered a new "green freight forward" stage, focused on institutional innovation and systemic coordination in the freight system. Key documents include the Action Plan to Improve Freight Quality and Cost-Efficiency, which sets targets and identified specific measures to promote the transition to low-carbon multimodal transportation. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces using China-domestic road trucking for airport pre-carriage or port drayage should evaluate whether carrier partners are subject to new energy vehicle or modal shift requirements under the 2025-stage policy documents, and whether capacity or cost changes are expected as a result.

**Gap from current operational baseline:** If the workspace manages China-domestic ground transport through spot or informal carrier arrangements without contract-level visibility into carrier compliance status, the transition policy creates a procurement gap. Carrier assessment criteria should include compliance with applicable Chinese transport decarbonization requirements.

**Deadline:** No single hard deadline identified from sourced documents for foreign-operated logistics providers. The policy framework is active from 2025. Monitor annually.

## For Supplier Emissions Data Collection

**Scope:** All suppliers in China's cement, steel, and aluminum sectors who are covered ETS entities.

**Gap from current operational baseline:** If the workspace does not currently collect verified ETS compliance data from China-based manufacturing clients or packaging suppliers, this is a Scope 3 data gap that will become commercially visible as client sustainability disclosure requirements tighten.

**Deadline:** The first compliance deadline for covered entities is the end of 2025. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors Post-2025, verified emissions data will exist in the MEE registry for covered entities. Workspaces should request access from supplier counterparts.

---

# 12. Exemptions and Edge Cases

## Intensity-Based System Creates Cost Exemption Equivalent for High-Output Suppliers

*Analytical inference:* Under the intensity-based allocation through 2026, covered suppliers that are growing output and improving emissions intensity simultaneously may face near-zero net ETS compliance costs. The provision of generous allowances has led to oversupply in the market previously — a 2021 report estimated power companies received on average 17% more allowances than needed in the 2019–2020 compliance cycle. *Source: Carbon Brief, September 23, 2024. https://www.carbonbrief.org/explainer-chinas-carbon-market-to-cover-steel-aluminium-and-cement-in-2024/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces should not assume that ETS coverage of a supplier automatically creates meaningful carbon cost pass-through in the 2024–2026 compliance period. The cost signal is weak under current design.

## Transition from Pilot ETSs

Companies in the three new sectors currently part of regional pilot ETS programs will transition to the national ETS. *Source: ICAP, April 10, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors This may affect suppliers in pilot cities (Beijing, Shanghai, Guangdong, Shenzhen, Tianjin, Chongqing, Hubei, Fujian) who previously reported under different pilot rules.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with China-based supplier relationships in pilot cities, the transition to national ETS may require suppliers to adopt new MRV processes, potentially causing short-term disruption to their reporting capacity. Workspaces requesting supplier emissions data should allow for a transition adjustment period in 2025.

---

# 13. Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

## China's Freight Modal Shift — Quantified Trajectory

In China, trucks carry about 30% of all tonne-kilometres of goods, far above the global average of 19%. Long-haul trucks traveling over 500 km carry about 60% of trucking tonne-kilometres. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

ICCT internal estimates suggest that modal shift will play a leading role in the decarbonization of transportation through 2030 and will continue to contribute 10%–20% of annual transportation CO₂ emission reductions until 2050. *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces advising clients on China supply chain design, the 10%–20% annual CO₂ reduction contribution from modal shift is a credible, sourced figure that can be used in client sustainability conversations — provided it is attributed as an ICCT estimate and not represented as a regulatory requirement or guaranteed outcome.

## The Barrier to China's Green Freight Ambitions

ICCT identifies the barrier to China's green freight ambitions as "the absence of a holistic, system-level strategy to shift long-haul flows to rail and waterway while electrifying shorter-haul segments." *Source: ICCT, June 1, 2026. https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/

*Analytical inference:* This gap between policy ambition and systemic integration creates execution risk for workspaces attempting to commit to China green freight routing today. The infrastructure exists (China has the world's largest electrified rail network and vast inland waterways per ICCT), but the integrated modal planning framework is still developing.

## CBAM as ETS Acceleration Driver

The implementation of the EU's CBAM in 2026 provides a strong incentive for China to strengthen its national carbon pricing system. *Source: IEEFA, November 3, 2025. https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces positioning themselves as sustainability-informed logistics advisors, the CBAM-ETS linkage is the strongest near-term commercial hook: clients exporting from China to the EU have a direct financial incentive to understand and reduce their embedded carbon costs, and the workspace is positioned to facilitate that conversation.

---

# 14. Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Event | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 2021 | China national ETS became operational, covering power sector | In force |
| September 2024 | MEE published draft policy for ETS expansion to cement, steel, aluminum; public consultation opened | Completed |
| November 2024 | Allocation plan excluded indirect emissions in the power sector | In force |
| February 2024 | MEE released new regulations to tackle emissions data fraud | In force |
| Mid-March 2025 | State Council approved ETS expansion work plan | In force |
| 20 March 2025 | MEE released official work plan for ETS expansion | In force |
| End of 2025 | First compliance deadline for new sectors (cement, steel, aluminum), covering emissions of 2024 | Upcoming |
| 2025–2026 (Phase 1) | Output-based, intensity-controlled allocation; companies familiarise with ETS; data quality enhancement | Active |
| 2026 | EU CBAM full implementation — carbon costs imposed on imports from countries with weaker carbon pricing | Upcoming |
| 2026 | Phase 2 allocation plan details expected from MEE (pending publication as of April 2025) | Research gap — monitor MEE announcements |
| 2027 (Phase 2 start) | ETS tightening and improvement; absolute cap transition begins with major industries | Anticipated per sourced plan |
| 2030 | Full implementation of absolute-cap ETS across covered sectors | Anticipated per sourced plan |
| 2030 | China aims to peak carbon emissions | Stated national target |
| 2060 | China aims to achieve carbon neutrality | Stated national target |

*Sources: ICAP, April 10, 2025; IEEFA, November 3, 2025.*

---

# 15. Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Date | Type | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | China officially expands national ETS to cement, steel and aluminum sectors | International Carbon Action Partnership (ICAP) | April 10, 2025 | Intergovernmental body tracking / Industry body (Tier 3) | https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/news/china-officially-expands-national-ets-cement-steel-and-aluminum-sectors |
| 2 | Explainer: China's carbon market to cover steel, aluminium and cement in 2024 | Carbon Brief | September 23, 2024 | Expert analysis / News (Tier 5–6) | https://www.carbonbrief.org/explainer-chinas-carbon-market-to-cover-steel-aluminium-and-cement-in-2024/ |
| 3 | China's emissions trading system (ETS) reforms: On track, but needs robust enforcement | Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA) | November 3, 2025 | Analysis / Think tank (Tier 6) | https://ieefa.org/resources/chinas-emissions-trading-system-ets-reforms-track-needs-robust-enforcement |
| 4 | Beyond trucks: China's low-carbon multimodal freight transition | International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) | June 1, 2026 | Industry body analysis (Tier 4) | https://theicct.org/beyond-trucks-chinas-low-carbon-multimodal-freight-transition-jun26/ |

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| China National Carbon Market Progress Report 2024 – MEE (Official PDF) | https://www.mee.gov.cn/ywdt/xwfb/202407/W020240722528850763859.pdf | 2 | MEE's own 2024 progress report on ETS scope, trading volumes, MRV framework, and sectoral expansion roadmap; as a regulator-issued primary document it would ground FACT claims about ETS design with higher authority than ICAP or Carbon Brief. |
| China Policies and Actions for Addressing Climate Change 2025 Annual Report – MEE (English) | https://english.mee.gov.cn/Resources/Reports/reports/202511/P020251105496730575050.pdf | 2 | MEE English-language annual report referencing green/low-carbon transportation progress alongside ETS expansion; would allow direct regulator-sourced claims about transport-sector policy alignment without relying on secondary analysis. |
