prior.title: OECD ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative
prior.summary: Source URL points to OECD International Transport Forum content on decarbonising transport, but the actual content excerpt contains only a security verification page placeholder rather than substantive policy information.
prior.full_brief:
# OECD ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative

## Research Summary

---

# 1. What the Research Found

**Initiative identity and scope.** The International Transport Forum (ITF), an intergovernmental organisation affiliated with the OECD, launched the Decarbonising Transport (DT) initiative in 2016 with core funding from the ITF's Corporate Partnership Board (CPB). The initiative provides decision-makers with tools to select CO₂ mitigation measures that deliver on their climate commitments.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**The transport emissions problem.** The initiative is grounded in three headline findings from ITF modelling:

- Transport emits around 23% of energy-related CO₂ that feeds global warming. Without immediate action, its share could reach 40% by 2030.
- Transport emissions have grown faster than those of any other sector over the past 50 years.
- CO₂ emissions from transport activity could increase by 60% by 2050, in part because transport relies on oil for 92% of its energy.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**Five work streams.** The DT initiative is organised into five work streams: (1) Tracking progress — evaluating how current mitigation measures contribute to reducing transport CO₂; (2) In-depth sectoral studies — identifying effective policies for decarbonising urban passenger transport, road freight transport, maritime transport, aviation, and inter-urban transport; (3) Focus studies — analysing specific decarbonisation issues; (4) National pathways — assessing policy levers from a country perspective, including regional or sub-national levels; and (5) Policy Dialogue — organising global dialogue through high-level roundtables, policy briefings, and technical workshops.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**Scope and methodology.** The initiative does not advocate specific measures or policies. Building on an evidence-based assessment of mitigation impacts, it identifies options for decision-makers to achieve their targets — for instance, the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) submitted by countries under the Paris Agreement, as well as targets set by sectors, companies, or cities. Assessments are grounded in data analysis and advanced modelling; the ITF modelling framework derives projections of transport activity from analysing the drivers of demand and then models how changes in mobility patterns affect CO₂ emissions.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**Partnership scale.** The initiative currently unites more than 70 governments, organisations, institutions, foundations, and companies. Partners contribute in different roles including as funders and knowledge partners. An annual meeting of project partners is held each May back-to-back with the Summit of transport ministers organised by the ITF, discussing findings and reviewing progress against the work plan. Partners are drawn from eight categories: Corporate Partnership Board member companies, knowledge partners, intergovernmental organisations, multilateral development banks, professional and sectoral associations, cities and regions, non-governmental organisations, and foundations.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport; ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Partners, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport-project-partners

**Named partners of direct relevance to freight and logistics.** The partner roster includes organisations with direct relevance to freight operations and logistics:

- *Knowledge partners:* International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT), Smart Freight Centre, Transport & Environment (T&E), Institute for Transport and Development Policy (ITDP), Global Fuel Economy Initiative (GFEI), World Resources Institute (WRI).
- *Intergovernmental:* European Commission, OECD, UNECE, UNEP, UNFCCC.
- *Multilateral development banks:* World Bank, Asian Development Bank (ADB), European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD), European Investment Bank (EIB), Inter-American Development Bank Group (IADB).
- *Professional and sectoral associations:* International Federation of Freight Forwarders Associations (FIATA), European Association for Forwarding, Transport, Logistics and Customs Services (CLECAT), International Road Transport Union (IRU), Air Transport Action Group (ATAG), International Union of Railways (UIC), Swedish Shipowners' Association.
- *Corporate:* Shell, BP, TotalEnergies, ExxonMobil, Aramco, Volvo Group, Amazon, Rolls Royce, Siemens AG, Alstom, Airbus.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Partners, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport-project-partners

**Funding base.** Funding partners include the governments of France, Korea, Ireland, and the Netherlands; the World Bank; the European Commission; the ClimateWorks Foundation; the Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA); the FIA Foundation; the International Road Transport Union (IRU); and the Swedish Shipowners' Association.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**UNFCCC focal-point status.** In recognition of the work of its Decarbonising Transport initiative, the UN Climate Change Secretariat (UNFCCC) named the International Transport Forum a focal point for transport under its Marrakech Partnership. In this role, the ITF acts as a conduit for the exchange of information between the transport sector and the UNFCCC, as well as providing inputs to the UNFCCC process.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport

**Emerging economies sub-project (DTEE).** A dedicated sub-project — Decarbonising Transport in Emerging Economies (DTEE) — supports transport decarbonisation in Argentina, Azerbaijan, India, and Morocco. It is carried out by the ITF in collaboration with the Wuppertal Institute (WI) and supported by the International Climate Initiative (IKI) of the German Federal Ministry for the Environment, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMWK). The project is designing a common assessment framework for transport emissions covering several transport sub-sectors and transport modes. Country-specific modelling tools and policy scenarios will help the participating governments to implement ambitious CO₂-reduction initiatives. The four participating countries share the characteristic that all have lower transport CO₂ emissions per capita than the OECD average, but higher growth rates of transport activity.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport in Emerging Economies (DTEE), International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://www.itf-oecd.org/dtee

**DTEE baseline data.** Transport accounted for 30% of CO₂ emissions in OECD countries in 2016. In non-OECD countries it contributed only 16%. But transport emissions in the OECD are expected to decrease by 1% annually through to 2030, according to ITF estimates. In non-OECD countries they will grow by 2%. Transport volumes in non-OECD countries are projected to grow more than twice as fast as in the OECD, with 4% annual growth for passenger and 5% for freight transport.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport in Emerging Economies (DTEE), International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://www.itf-oecd.org/dtee

**2024 evolution to Sustainable Transport Systems Initiative (STSI).** The DT initiative has evolved into a broader initiative focused on implementing sustainable transport policies: the Sustainable Transport Systems Initiative, launched during the ITF 2024 Summit. The STSI is described as an evolution of ITF's Decarbonising Transport initiative. Active projects under STSI include East Africa Freight Transport: Building Regional Connectivity and West Africa Freight Transport: Enhancing Regional Connectivity (published December 2025), among others. The STSI follows a four-step process: identify challenges; propose options by mapping systemic relationships; mobilise actors across government, industry, and NGOs; and implement through dialogue.

*Source: ITF Sustainable Transport Systems Initiative, International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://www.itf-oecd.org/sustainable-transport-systems-initiative

**Transport Climate Action Directory (TCAD).** *This item is listed under the corroborating sources as a key deliverable — an UNFCCC-endorsed database of 80 CO₂ mitigation measures, funded by EC Horizon 2020 and the German IKI. The TCAD URL (https://www.itf-oecd.org/tcad) was provided as a corroborating source lead but no SOURCE block content was provided for it.* Specific content from the TCAD page is not available from the provided source blocks as of May 2026. The TCAD is referenced by name in the primary source as the Transport Climate Action Directory and described as a "catalogue of effective CO₂ mitigation measures."

*Specific TCAD database scope, measure count, and funding details not available from primary source blocks as of May 2026.* See New Sources Identified.

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**Mechanism for freight and logistics operators.** The ITF/DT initiative does not regulate operators directly. *Analytical inference:* Its operational significance is structural — as the UNFCCC's designated transport focal point under the Marrakech Partnership, and with FIATA and CLECAT as named partners, the initiative functions as the channel through which freight-sector decarbonisation positions are translated into UNFCCC negotiating inputs and, downstream, into nationally determined contributions. Policy outputs that emerge from this process — road freight decarbonisation studies, aviation sector work, maritime in-depth studies — feed directly into the regulatory pipeline that produces binding measures such as the EU ETS extension to maritime, CORSIA, and national road freight CO₂ standards.

**Air freight.** The initiative's in-depth sectoral work explicitly covers aviation. *Analytical inference:* For workspaces where air is the primary mode, the DT/STSI sectoral analysis on aviation decarbonisation represents an upstream input to CORSIA Phase 2 design and any future SAF mandate tightening. The ITF's UNFCCC focal-point role means its aviation findings reach ICAO-level discussions before they reach commercial operators.

**Road freight.** The initiative covers road freight transport as a dedicated sectoral study stream. *Analytical inference:* For workspaces with road as a secondary mode, ITF road freight pathway work — and its absorption into NDC frameworks — is a leading indicator for future fleet CO₂ standards and zero-emission vehicle mandates across trade lanes in Europe, Latin America (Argentina is a DTEE country), and Asia.

**Emerging-economy trade lanes.** The DTEE sub-project covers Argentina, India, and Morocco — three of the four participating countries — which represent active freight corridors for Americas, South Asia, and North Africa trade lanes. *Analytical inference:* The 5% annual freight transport volume growth projected for non-OECD countries is the demand environment in which these corridor-specific policy scenarios are being built; operators active on these corridors face a regulatory shaping process that is currently in modelling and scenario development, not yet in enforcement.

**Partner network as intelligence channel.** *Analytical inference:* The presence of FIATA and CLECAT as named sectoral partners means that the forwarder community has formal representation in the DT/STSI process. Workspaces engaged with FIATA or CLECAT — whether as members or through national association membership — are already within the information boundary of the initiative's annual partner meetings held each May.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Overview; ITF Partners page; ITF DTEE page; ITF STSI page — all International Transport Forum / OECD, [retrieved May 2026]. https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport, https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport-project-partners, https://www.itf-oecd.org/dtee, https://www.itf-oecd.org/sustainable-transport-systems-initiative

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**NDC pipeline monitoring.** *Analytical inference:* The DT initiative's national pathways work stream, combined with the DTEE country-modelling for Argentina, India, and Morocco, is producing the policy scenarios that will shape future NDC revisions in those jurisdictions. For workspaces operating freight on those corridors, this is the upstream signal — not the downstream regulation. The decision that changes is whether to track ITF sectoral outputs as part of regulatory horizon-scanning, rather than waiting for binding measures to appear in domestic legislation.

**CORSIA and SAF exposure.** *Analytical inference:* The aviation in-depth sectoral study stream represents ITF's formal input channel to ICAO processes. Workspaces with significant air-freight volumes should treat ITF aviation publications as early indicators of CORSIA Phase 2 parameters and future SAF blending requirement trajectories, not as binding rules. The distinction matters for client conversations: citing ITF analysis in a client meeting requires clearly labeling it as policy analysis, not regulation.

**Scope 3 reporting positioning.** *Analytical inference:* With the ICCT, Smart Freight Centre, and WRI as named knowledge partners — all of whom contribute to emissions accounting methodology development — the DT initiative is a production site for the methodologies that downstream flow into frameworks such as ISO 14083, the GHG Protocol freight module, and CSRD-aligned Scope 3 reporting. Workspaces making methodology choices for Scope 3 freight emissions accounting should understand that ITF-endorsed approaches carry institutional weight in regulatory consultations.

**East and West Africa freight corridor projects.** The STSI already has active freight connectivity projects for East Africa and West Africa (West Africa Freight Transport: Enhancing Regional Connectivity, published December 2025). *Analytical inference:* For workspaces with active or prospective freight lanes through West or East Africa, these projects are the most operationally proximate outputs of the STSI — they will shape corridor-specific policy recommendations with direct routing and cost implications before those recommendations reach formal regulatory status.

**Annual partner meeting as forward-intelligence moment.** The annual meeting of project partners is held each May back-to-back with the ITF Summit of transport ministers. *Analytical inference:* The May 2026 Summit has already occurred (ITF news references May 2026 events); the next annual partner meeting will be back-to-back with the ITF 2027 Summit scheduled for 12–14 May 2027 in Leipzig, Germany. That meeting is the next formal moment at which the STSI's work plan and findings are reviewed with 70+ partners including FIATA and CLECAT.

*Source: ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Partners, International Transport Forum / OECD. https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport-project-partners; ITF Sustainable Transport Systems Initiative. https://www.itf-oecd.org/sustainable-transport-systems-initiative

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The ITF — the OECD's transport research body and the UNFCCC's designated transport focal point — projects that without immediate action, transport's share of energy-related CO₂ could reach 40% by 2030, up from around 23% today. This is the institutional baseline that national regulators and NDC authors are working from.
- The ITF's freight-specific sectoral studies on road freight, aviation, and maritime are the upstream inputs that feed into regulatory pipelines including EU road CO₂ standards, CORSIA design, and IMO GHG strategy reviews. When clients ask "where are the freight rules heading?", the ITF's sectoral output is one of the most authoritative leading indicators available.
- Freight transport volumes in non-OECD countries are projected to grow at 5% annually — faster than any OECD market. Clients sourcing or distributing through emerging-economy corridors (Argentina, India, Morocco, and other non-OECD markets) face a regulatory environment being actively modelled right now, with country-specific decarbonisation pathways under development.
- The forwarder community — through FIATA and CLECAT — has formal partner status in the ITF/STSI process. Workspaces can credibly say their sector has a seat at the table.

**What to avoid:**

- Do not present ITF findings as regulations or binding requirements. The initiative explicitly does not advocate specific measures or policies; its outputs are options for decision-makers, not mandates.
- Do not cite specific ITF study conclusions without reading the source document. The initiative produces a large volume of reports; a vague attribution to "the ITF" without the specific study title and date will undermine credibility with sophisticated counterparties.
- Do not present the DTEE country modelling for Argentina, India, and Morocco as finalized regulatory pathways; these are scenario-building exercises, and the policy response in each country is a separate decision for national governments.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Which of your origin or destination corridors pass through non-OECD markets projected for 5% annual freight volume growth? The regulatory environment in those corridors is being shaped now.
- Has your sustainability reporting methodology been benchmarked against the methodologies endorsed by the ITF's knowledge partners — including the Smart Freight Centre and ICCT — which feed into CSRD and ISO 14083 alignment?

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

*Specific Transport Climate Action Directory (TCAD) measure-level content — including which of the 80 CO₂ mitigation measures are classified as applicable to air freight, road freight, or maritime freight specifically — is not available from the provided source blocks as of May 2026.* The TCAD URL (https://www.itf-oecd.org/tcad) was identified as a corroborating source but no source block content was retrieved from it. The TCAD's measure-level database is the most operationally specific output of the DT initiative, and its contents would materially sharpen the analysis of which interventions are closest to regulatory adoption. See New Sources Identified.

*Specific publication schedule and output titles for the Sustainable Transport Systems Initiative's active projects — including the East Africa and West Africa freight connectivity projects — are not confirmed beyond the December 2025 publication date for the West Africa report.* Full report content is not available from the provided source blocks as of May 2026.

*The ITF Submission to the UNFCCC First Global Stocktake (PDF) was listed as a corroborating source (https://unfccc.int/sites/default/files/resource/202203011748_ITF_Submission_to_the_First_Global_Stocktake.pdf) and is described as detailing the DT Implement project, a 31-country coalition, and a hard-to-abate freight/aviation focus.* No SOURCE block content was provided for this document; the claim details above are from the corroborator description only, not from a retrieved source block. *Specific content from that submission is not available from primary source blocks as of May 2026.* The document is listed under New Sources Identified.

This brief does not resolve whether any specific ITF sectoral output has been formally cited or adopted by name in any binding regulatory instrument (EU, IMO, ICAO, or national). That linkage — between ITF analysis and specific regulatory text — requires regulation-specific research beyond the scope of this brief.

**Re-check window:** The ITF 2027 Summit (Leipzig, 12–14 May 2027) is the next major moment at which STSI work-plan progress will be reviewed with partners including FIATA and CLECAT. *Severity: MONITORING.* Re-check this item in Q1 2027 for pre-summit publication releases and partner meeting agenda.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| ITF Transport Climate Action Directory (TCAD) | https://www.itf-oecd.org/tcad | 2 | UNFCCC-endorsed database of 80 CO₂ mitigation measures; the most operationally specific output of the DT initiative; essential for identifying which measures apply to air, road, and maritime freight and which are closest to regulatory adoption. |
| ITF Submission to the UNFCCC First Global Stocktake (PDF) | https://unfccc.int/sites/default/files/resource/202203011748_ITF_Submission_to_the_First_Global_Stocktake.pdf | 2 | Official UNFCCC-filed document detailing a 31-country coalition and hard-to-abate freight and aviation focus; primary-source evidence of the ITF's formal input to the global climate stocktake process, directly relevant to how freight-sector positions reach UNFCCC negotiations. |
| ITF Decarbonising Transport Initiative – Main Page (Cloudflare-blocked mirror) | https://www.itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport | 2 | The canonical ITF DT initiative page; the version retrieved via the non-www URL (https://itf-oecd.org/decarbonising-transport) yielded content but the www-prefixed URL returned a Cloudflare security block — both should be tracked to ensure full content retrieval on future pulls. |

---

prior.title: Washington Administrative Code (WAC) - State Regulatory Framework Access and Navigation
prior.summary: The Washington Administrative Code (WAC) is the official compilation of Washington State executive branch agency regulations issued by statutory authority. The online WAC is updated twice monthly, with historical versions available from 2004 forward. The content provides navigation and search access to WAC titles organized by subject and agency, covering regulatory areas from accountancy to corrections and numerous departments and commissions.
prior.full_brief:
# Washington Administrative Code (WAC) — State Regulatory Framework Access and Navigation

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document describes the structure, publication cycle, institutional maintenance, and navigation mechanics of the Washington Administrative Code (WAC) — the codified body of executive branch agency regulations in Washington State. It is produced for a global freight forwarding operation whose trade lanes include the Americas and whose cargo verticals span live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian freight. The workspace's supply chain roles include freight forwarder, importer, and customs broker on US domestic and cross-border movements touching Washington State.

The WAC is not itself a single regulation; it is the regulatory container for all Washington State agency rules. This brief maps the container — how it is structured, updated, and navigated — so that compliance and operations personnel can locate and track the specific agency rules that govern freight, transport, hazardous materials, labor, and environmental standards affecting Washington State operations.

**Convention note:** All regulatory text is cited verbatim from source blocks. All interpretive statements are labeled. No binding legal conclusions are drawn. Items requiring legal determination are flagged explicitly.

---

## What the WAC Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**Regulatory status and authority.** The WAC is Washington State primary law. "Regulations of executive branch agencies are issued by authority of statutes. Like legislation and the Constitution, regulations are a source of primary law in Washington State." *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

"The WAC codifies the regulations and arranges them by subject or agency." *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

**Institutional definition.** "The Washington Administrative Code (WAC) is a list of all current state rules. State agencies create rules based on the authority they receive from state laws." *Source: State rules (WAC), Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/state-rules-wac/

The Office of the Code Reviser (CRO) maintains the WAC. "The Washington State Code Reviser's Office supports the legislative process by drafting bills, revising state laws, and overseeing administrative rules. They write and edit laws to ensure they comply with legal standards and are easy to understand. The office maintains and updates the Revised Code of Washington (RCW) and the Washington Administrative Code (WAC), which contain the state's laws and agency rules." *Source: Office of the Code Reviser, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/about-the-legislature/legislative-agencies/cro/

**Why it applies to the workspace.** Washington State is a major Pacific gateway for air and ocean freight, a hub for high-value cargo transiting Seattle-Tacoma International Airport and the Port of Seattle, and a state with active agency rulemaking across transportation (WAC Title 12), labor and industries (WAC Title 296), and ecology/environmental regulation (WAC Title 173). Workspaces forwarding freight through or originating in Washington State encounter WAC-sourced rules on hazardous materials classification, driver and labor standards, and environmental compliance. *Analytical inference:* The workspace's air-primary mode through Seattle-Tacoma and road-secondary drayage within Washington State means WAC Title 296 (Labor and Industries) and WAC Title 12 (Transportation/Aeronautics) are the highest-frequency compliance surfaces; WAC Title 173 (Ecology) is relevant to any hazmat or sensitive-cargo movements.

**Jurisdictional scope.** The WAC applies within Washington State. "Washington administrative law consists of the rules, regulations, decisions, and other documents issued by executive branch agencies, boards, commissions, and offices, in accordance with Washington's Administrative Procedure Act (RCW 34.05)." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777

*Specific effective date and penalty summary not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-06.* (The WAC is a regulatory container, not a single regulation with a single effective date or unified penalty schedule; these vary by agency and specific rule. See Section 8 for penalty and enforcement structure.)

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING** — Biweekly WAC update cycle creates ongoing compliance drift risk. The WAC is updated twice per month. Rules affecting Washington State freight operations (labor standards, transport safety, environmental requirements) can change without a consolidated notification system unless the workspace is actively subscribed. *Action:* Assign a compliance designee to monitor the Washington State Register (WSR) on its biweekly publication schedule. The WSR is the authoritative pre-publication notice vehicle. *Source: State rules (WAC), Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/state-rules-wac/

**MONITORING** — Agency-specific rulemaking agendas published twice yearly. "Twice a year, the agency must notify the public about its plans for rulemaking in the coming 6 months." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777. *Action:* Pull rulemaking agendas for WAC Title 296 (Labor and Industries — affecting driver/labor compliance on road drayage) and WAC Title 173 (Ecology — affecting hazmat/environmental compliance) at each semi-annual publication. The CRO's Rule Making Activity Report, updated quarterly, provides a consolidated tracking surface. *Source: Washington State Register, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/

**MONITORING** — Emergency rules can take effect immediately and remain in force for 120 days. "If there are extenuating circumstances, an agency can by-pass the notice and comment process and create an emergency rule. The rule can only remain in effect for 120 days." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777. *Operational implication:* Emergency rules affecting freight operations (e.g., hazmat handling, driver hours) can be in force before the workspace becomes aware. The WSR is the only advance signal; a monitoring subscription is the only mitigation.

---

## How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The WAC is a container; individual agency titles impose obligations on specific regulated parties. The workspace's compliance exposure varies by agency title and role:

- **As freight forwarder and customs broker** operating through Washington State: WAC Title 12 (Transportation/Aeronautics) and WAC Title 296 (Labor and Industries) govern carrier and labor-side obligations that touch the workspace's contracted drayage and ground handling partners.
- **As importer of record** on shipments entering Washington State: WAC Title 173 (Ecology) governs environmental and hazardous materials requirements that attach to cargo classification and documentation.
- **As a party commissioning warehouse or terminal handling** in Washington State: WAC Title 296 labor safety rules govern workplace conditions at facilities the workspace uses.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in its capacity as a non-asset freight forwarder rather than a carrier or employer, is a directly obligated party under specific WAC titles (as opposed to its contracted carriers and facility operators) depends on the specific rule language in each relevant title. Counsel review against the workspace's specific contracts and operational roles is required before treating any WAC title as directly binding on the workspace.

"Authority for agency regulations must be delegated from the legislature or the Governor through organic acts, enabling acts, and executive orders." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

"Judicial review is available for state agency decisions after the exhaustion of administrative remedies." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

---

## Substantive Requirements — WAC Structure, Update Mechanics, and Rulemaking Process

### WAC Structure

The WAC is organized by Title, with each Title corresponding to a specific agency or subject area. "In the WAC, each agency's regulations are grouped together." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

Titles directly relevant to the workspace's Washington State operations include:

- **Title 12** — Transportation, Department of (Aeronautics Commission): *Analytical inference:* Governs aeronautics-related transport regulation relevant to air cargo operations through Washington State airports, including Seattle-Tacoma.
- **Title 173** — Ecology, Department of: Environmental regulation, including water pollution, environmental policy, and marine safety (see also Titles 197, 317, 372, 508). Relevant to hazmat cargo verticals and any environmental compliance attached to ocean/port movements at Washington State ports. *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac
- **Title 226** — Freight Mobility Strategic Investment Board: *Analytical inference:* Relevant to Washington State freight infrastructure policy, potentially affecting routing and infrastructure access decisions.
- **Title 296** — Labor and Industries, Department of: The dominant labor, safety, and workers' compensation rulemaking body. *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac
- **Title 317** — Ecology, Department of (Marine Safety, Office of): Relevant to ocean and port operations at Washington State marine terminals. *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

### Publication Cycle and Currency

"The online version of the WAC is updated twice a month." *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

"The Washington Administrative Code contains the current and permanent rules and regulations of state agencies. It is arranged by agency. New editions are published every two years, with a supplement in the off-year. It is updated by the Washington State Register." *Source: Washington Administrative Code, University of Washington Law Library Research Guide, updated Apr 14, 2026. https://lib.law.uw.edu/c.php?g=1238333&p=9062292

Archive coverage: "The online version of the WAC is updated twice a month. Copies of the WAC as they existed each year since 2004 are available in the WAC archive." *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

Official publication status: "The Statute Law Committee declares that the certified PDF publication documents in the WAC Archive area on the Office of the Code Reviser's website constitute the official publication of the Washington Administrative Code." *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

### The Washington State Register (WSR) as the Live Rulemaking Feed

"The Washington State Register is an official publication for rule-making activity by state agencies in Washington. It includes notices of proposed and final rules, public meetings, and other legal information." *Source: Office of the Code Reviser, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/about-the-legislature/legislative-agencies/cro/

"The Washington State Register (WSR) is published every 2 weeks, and includes: Rulemaking Agendas, Preproposal Inquiries, Proposed Rules, Notice of Hearings, Permanent Rules, Emergency Rules, and Executive Orders." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

The WSR is current through May 20, 2026, Register issue 26-11. *Source: Washington State Register, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/

"The Statute Law Committee declares that the certified PDF Register documents on the Code Reviser's website constitute the official publication of the Washington State Register." *Source: Washington State Register, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces tracking Washington State freight, labor, and environmental rules in real time, the WSR is the only authoritative pre-enforcement notice vehicle. The two-week publication gap means a rule can be filed and take effect (31 days after filing for permanent rules, immediately for emergency rules) within a window that requires active monitoring rather than periodic review.

### Permanent Rulemaking Process — Three Steps

The APA (RCW 34.05) governs all permanent rulemaking. "Rules to carry out those laws — which are sometimes called regulations and sometimes called WACs, for the Washington Administrative Code — are adopted by agencies through a process mandated in law by Washington's Administrative Procedure Act (APA). The APA sets out exactly what steps an agency has to follow to adopt rules." *Source: Rulemaking Process and Terms, WA Department of Labor & Industries, accessed June 2026. https://lni.wa.gov/rulemaking-activity/rulemaking-process-terms

The three formal steps, as described by the L&I process page:

1. **Pre-proposal Statement of Inquiry (CR-101):** "An agency files a notice (Pre-proposal Statement of Inquiry) with the Office of the Code Reviser explaining that it is considering a rule adoption or amendment. The Code Reviser then publishes the notice in the Washington State Register (Register), which is published twice a month." *Source: Rulemaking Process and Terms, WA Department of Labor & Industries, accessed June 2026. https://lni.wa.gov/rulemaking-activity/rulemaking-process-terms

2. **Proposed Rule:** "A minimum of 30 days must pass after the publication of the preproposal inquiry before the agency can publish the text of the proposed rule." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

3. **Final Rule (CR-103):** "After consideration of the submitted comments and hearing testimony, the agency will publish the final rule and it will have legal effect on the effective date." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

**Effective date for permanent rules:** "For permanent rules, the effective date is 31 days after filing." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

**Emergency rules:** "An emergency rule is effective immediately for the duration of 120 days." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

*Operational implication:* The minimum elapsed time from first public notice (CR-101) to a permanent rule taking effect is approximately 61+ days (30-day preproposal window plus 31-day post-filing window), but the workspace's practical window to influence or prepare is concentrated in the comment period following the proposed rule publication. Emergency rules compress this to zero notice.

### Rulemaking Trigger: Federal Law

"Note on the left side of the chart, one of the triggers for rulemaking is federal law or regulation, which can require a state agency to create a regulation. If the state fails to implement the regulation, the federal agency will take over (this is common in environmental law)." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace's cargo verticals — particularly hazardous materials (humanitarian) and environmental compliance (ocean port calls at Washington State marine terminals) — federal regulatory changes (EPA, DOT, PHMSA) can directly trigger Washington State WAC amendments. Tracking federal rulemakings in these domains is therefore a leading indicator for WAC changes affecting Washington State operations.

### Penalty and Enforcement

*Specific unified penalty framework not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-06.* The WAC does not carry a single penalty schedule; penalties are defined within individual agency title rules and the underlying authorizing statutes (RCW). *Legal Confirmation Required:* The penalty exposure for non-compliance with any specific WAC title (e.g., WAC 296 for labor violations, WAC 173 for environmental violations) requires review of the specific rule text and the authorizing RCW, not the WAC container documentation.

---

## Registration and Reporting Obligations

No unified WAC registration or reporting obligation applies to the workspace solely by virtue of freight forwarding or customs brokerage. *Analytical inference:* Registration and reporting obligations, where they exist, are embedded in specific agency titles (e.g., WAC 296 for workers' compensation coverage for Washington-based employees, WAC 173 for environmental reporting for regulated emissions or discharges). These are vertical-specific and role-specific and are not triggered by WAC existence alone.

The CRO provides a Rule Drafting Service (RDS) for agencies, not for private parties. *Source: Office of the Code Reviser, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/about-the-legislature/legislative-agencies/cro/

The CRO contact for WAC editorial questions: Emery Miller, Editor/WAC, Emery.Miller@leg.wa.gov. *Source: State rules (WAC), Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/state-rules-wac/

---

## Operational System Requirements — Navigation and Monitoring Infrastructure

For workspaces with freight operations touching Washington State, the following operational system capabilities are indicated by the WAC's structure and update cadence:

**1. Citation-based search access (no-cost official source).** The primary WAC search interface is available at https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac. Search accepts full or partial citation numbers (e.g., 4.04.010). *Source: Washington Administrative Code (WAC), Washington State Legislature, May 6, 2026. https://apps.leg.wa.gov/wac

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces without subscription legal database access, the official WAC at apps.leg.wa.gov and the WSR at leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/ provide sufficient current-rule access at no cost. Subscription databases (Westlaw, Lexis, Bloomberg Law) add historical coverage and citator functions (KeyCite, Shepard's, BCite) for tracking whether a specific rule has been amended — a meaningful operational upgrade for high-frequency compliance monitoring.

**2. WSR biweekly monitoring.** The WSR is the only source for proposed rules and emergency rules before they take effect. *Source: Washington State Register, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces without a designated WAC/WSR monitor face a latent compliance drift risk on Washington State operations. The two-week publication cadence and 31-day permanent-rule effective-date window mean that an unmonitored rule can reach enforcement before internal compliance systems are updated.

**3. Agency filing link per rule section.** "The legislature's website includes a link to 'Agency Filings Affecting this Section' for each regulation. You can also use the WAC to WSR table to view the most recent agency filings." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

*Operational implication:* For specific rules the workspace has identified as operationally material (e.g., a specific WAC 296 safety rule governing a Washington State warehouse partner), the per-section Agency Filings link provides a direct amendment-tracking path without requiring full WSR review.

**4. Quarterly CRO reports on rule changes.** "The Washington State Code Reviser's Office publishes quarterly reports on rule changes. These reports let you track updates from state agencies and view rule-making actions. You can find recent reports or explore past ones dating back to 1999." *Source: Washington State Register, Washington State Legislature, 2026. https://leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/

*Analytical inference:* Quarterly CRO reports provide a consolidated summary of rule changes across all agencies, suitable for a periodic compliance review cycle. For workspaces that cannot commit to biweekly WSR monitoring, quarterly CRO reports are a lower-frequency alternative, with the acknowledged gap that emergency rules and 31-day permanent rules will have already taken effect before the quarterly review.

**5. Navigation discipline for WAC searching.** "Regulations, just like statutes, function as a unit. It is important to look at the entire chapter so that you don't miss definitions, penalties, exceptions, or exemptions. You will also notice that regulatory sections are often interrelated and interdependent." *Source: Washington Administrative Law, Seattle University School of Law Library, updated Oct 7, 2025. https://lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777.

*Operational implication:* Workspaces conducting ad hoc WAC research (e.g., looking up a specific hazmat requirement) should navigate to the full chapter, not the single section retrieved by keyword search, to avoid missing definitions, scope limitations, or exemptions that would change the compliance picture.

---

## Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Milestone | Status | Source |
|---|---|---|
| WAC established as primary law of Washington State | In force; no single effective date — a living codification | apps.leg.wa.gov/wac |
| WAC online update frequency: twice per month | Ongoing, continuously in force | apps.leg.wa.gov/wac |
| WSR publication frequency: every 2 weeks | Ongoing; current through 26-11 (May 20, 2026) | leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/ |
| Permanent rule effective date: 31 days after filing | Ongoing procedural rule under APA (RCW 34.05) | lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777 |
| Emergency rule duration: 120 days, effective immediately | Ongoing procedural rule under APA (RCW 34.05) | lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777 |
| Rulemaking agenda published: twice yearly per agency | Ongoing; each agency publishes semi-annually | lawlibguides.seattleu.edu/c.php?g=713342&p=5074777 |
| CRO quarterly rule-change reports: back to 1999 | Ongoing; most recent available at CRO website | leg.wa.gov/state-laws-and-rules/washington-state-register/ |
| WAC Archive coverage (online): 2004 to present (annual snapshots) | In force; hosted at CRO website | apps.leg.wa.gov/wac |

---

prior.title: Clark County Environment and Sustainability Department - Air Quality and Dust Control Services
prior.summary: Clark County's Environment and Sustainability Department provides air quality management services including dust control permitting, dust monitoring classes, air quality complaint processing, and compliance enforcement. The department offers online portals for permit applications, forms, and dust class registrations.
prior.full_brief:
# Operations Profile: Clark County, Nevada — Air Quality and Dust Control Regulatory Environment

**Format:** Operations Profile | **Item Type:** regional_data
**Region:** Clark County, Nevada (Las Vegas Valley metropolitan area)
**Regulatory Body:** Clark County Department of Environment and Sustainability (DES), Division of Air Quality (DAQ)
**Document Date:** 2026-06-10

---

## Context Before Content

This brief covers the air quality and dust control regulatory environment administered by Clark County DES/DAQ as it affects freight, logistics, construction, and commercial operations within Clark County, Nevada. The workspace profile driving this brief encompasses operations where ground-disturbing activities, construction logistics, fleet movements, and stationary source emissions intersect with a county-level air pollution control regime that operates independently of the Nevada state air agency. Every section is grounded in sourced content; sections where no grounded data exists are omitted with an explanatory note.

---

# Section 1: Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

**MONITORING**

The Clark County DES/DAQ administers a permit fee schedule for air quality services within the region. A current fee schedule (Section 18) is listed as effective 2/12/2026 in the DAQ rules index.

*Source: Clark County DES – Current AQ Rules, Clark County DES/DAQ, amended index as of 2025–2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

However, the specific fee amounts for Dust Control Operating Permits (DCOPs), stationary source permits, and annual emissions inventory filings are not reproduced in any of the source blocks provided. *Specific fee rates not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-10.*

Operational implications for cost baseline:

- Analytical inference: Any commercial operation in Clark County that triggers DCOP thresholds (soil disturbance ≥0.25 acres, mechanized trenching ≥100 linear feet, mechanical demolition ≥1,000 sq ft, or temporary commercial activities ≥0.25 acres) will incur permit application and processing fees payable to DAQ. The magnitude is not publicly confirmed in the provided source content.
- Analytical inference: Stationary source permit fees (major and minor source) are governed by AQR Section 18, effective 2/12/2026, but dollar amounts are not available in the provided source blocks. Workspaces operating fuel-burning equipment, diesel generators, or process emission sources at fixed facilities within Clark County should obtain the current Section 18 fee schedule directly from DAQ (Air Quality Fees & Payments portal, 702-455-5942, AirQuality@ClarkCountyNV.gov).

Small Business Assistance Program (SBAP): DAQ operates a no-cost permitting and compliance assistance program for facilities with 100 or fewer employees that are not major stationary sources. Contact: 702-455-1660 or AQSBAP@ClarkCountyNV.gov.

*Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNR BEP (industry body summary), 2024–2025. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

---

# Section 2: Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

**MONITORING**

## 2.1 Dust Control Operating Permit (DCOP) Triggering Thresholds

A Dust Control Operating Permit (DCOP) is listed in the DAQ permitting portal as the instrument governing soil-disturbing and construction activities at the following thresholds:

- Soil disturbance or construction activities of 0.25 acres or greater in overall area
- Mechanized trenching of 100 feet or greater in length
- Mechanical demolition of any structure 1,000 square feet or larger
- Temporary commercial activities of 0.25 acres or greater in overall area

*Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County DES/DAQ, current as of 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Analytical inference: For workspaces involved in freight terminal development, warehouse construction, staging area establishment, or event setup on unpaved or disturbed ground of 0.25 acres or more, DCOP obtainment is the operative first step before ground disturbance commences. The AQ Map Assistant tool allows pre-application verification of whether a target parcel is already covered by an active DCOP.

## 2.2 Stationary Source Permit Types

DAQ issues major and minor stationary source permits under AQRs 12.0–12.5 and 12.11, and authorities to operate under a general permit program for gasoline dispensing facilities and soil/groundwater remediation operations.

*Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNR BEP, 2024–2025. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

- Minor source permitting thresholds: found in AQR 12.1.1(e).
- Significant thresholds triggering a controls analysis (RACT): found in AQR 12.1.1(k).
- DCOP thresholds for construction or temporary commercial activities: found in AQR 94.4.1(b).

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether specific freight forwarding, logistics staging, or event-freight operations trigger minor stationary source permitting (as opposed to only DCOP requirements) depends on actual emission source types and volumes at a given facility. Counsel or DAQ direct consultation is required to confirm permit type applicability to a specific operational configuration.

## 2.3 Fugitive Dust Control Sections in Force

The following Clark County Air Quality Regulation sections governing fugitive dust are currently adopted and in force:

- Section 41 – Fugitive Dust (amended 1/21/2020; EPA-approved 5/19/2022)
- Section 90 – Fugitive Dust from Open Areas and Vacant Lots (amended 1/21/2020)
- Section 91 – Fugitive Dust from Unpaved Roads, Unpaved Alleys, and Unpaved Easement Roads (amended 4/15/2014)
- Section 92 – Fugitive Dust Control Requirements for Unpaved Parking Lots and Storage Areas (amended 12/17/2024; effective 1/1/2025, Ordinance 5205)
- Section 93 – Fugitive Dust from Paved Roads and Street Sweeping Equipment (amended 1/21/2020)
- Section 94 – Permitting and Dust Control for Construction and Temporary Commercial Activities (amended 12/17/2024; effective 1/1/2025, Ordinance 5205)

*Source: Clark County DES – Current AQ Rules, Clark County DES/DAQ, index amended through 2025–2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

Operational implication: Sections 92 and 94 both carry a 1/1/2025 effective date under Ordinance 5205 — these are the most recently refreshed dust control standards and are now operative. Workspaces with unpaved parking lots, storage yards, or active construction activities in Clark County are operating under the 2025-effective versions of these rules.

## 2.4 Alternative Asphalt Paving for Unpaved Storage Areas

The DAQ permitting portal identifies Alternative Asphalt Paving as one stabilization option for new unpaved storage areas under Section 92. DAQ written pre-approval is noted as the precondition before implementation.

*Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County DES/DAQ, current as of 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Analytical inference: Workspaces operating unpaved freight staging yards or storage areas in Clark County that wish to use alternative surface treatments in lieu of traditional paving should initiate the DAQ written-approval process before any surface treatment is applied. Proceeding without approval risks a non-compliant stabilization measure finding under Section 92.

# Section 4: Cross-Regional Strategic Implications

**MONITORING**

## 4.1 Dual-Regulator Jurisdiction: DAQ vs. NDEP

Clark County DES/DAQ operates as an independent regional air district within Nevada. The Nevada Division of Environmental Protection (NDEP) Bureau of Air Quality Planning (BAPC) handles air permitting for all other Nevada counties, but Clark County has its own separate regulatory authority and its own adopted Air Quality Regulations.

*Source: NDEP – Air Permit Guidance, Nevada Division of Environmental Protection, current. https://ndep.nv.gov/air/permitting/permit-guidance

*Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNR BEP, 2024–2025. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

Operational implication: Workspaces operating in both Clark County and other Nevada locations face two separate regulatory regimes — Clark County DAQ for Las Vegas Valley operations, and NDEP BAPC for all other Nevada facilities. A permit or compliance clearance obtained from NDEP does not satisfy Clark County DAQ requirements, and vice versa.

## 4.2 Federal SIP Overlay: EPA Region 9

Clark County's air quality regulations form part of the Nevada State Implementation Plan (SIP), approved and overseen by U.S. EPA Pacific Southwest (Region 9). EPA has approved the majority of Clark County's core rules into the federal SIP, meaning federal enforcement authority overlays the county-level program.

*Source: U.S. EPA Region 9 – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County (Nevada SIP), U.S. EPA, last updated July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

Analytical inference: Because Clark County regulations are embedded in the federal SIP, major violations of DAQ rules carry the potential for federal enforcement escalation beyond the county level. Workspaces with operations that generate significant particulate matter (PM) or ozone precursor emissions should treat Clark County DAQ compliance as carrying federal-tier regulatory consequence, not merely local administrative exposure.

## 4.3 2025–2026 Regulatory Update Cycle: Active Rulemaking

The Clark County AQR index reflects a notably active 2025–2026 amendment cycle. Key recent and forthcoming changes:

- Section 12.0 (General Permitting Applicability): amended 3/17/2026, effective 4/1/2026 (Ordinance 5353)
- Sections 12.2, 12.3, 12.4, 12.5 (Major Source Permitting): amended 5/19/2026 (Ordinance 5375), effective 6/3/2026; Section 12.5 under EPA review
- Section 12.1 (Minor Source Permitting): amended 12/2/2025, effective 12/17/2025 (Ordinance 5319)
- Section 13 (National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants): amended 10/7/2025, effective 10/22/2025 (Ordinance 5298)
- Section 14 (New Source Performance Standards): amended 10/7/2025, effective 10/22/2025 (Ordinance 5298)
- Section 18 (Permit and Technical Service Fees): effective 2/12/2026
- Section 92 and Section 94 (Fugitive Dust): amended 12/17/2024, effective 1/1/2025 (Ordinance 5205)

*Source: Clark County DES – Current AQ Rules, Clark County DES/DAQ, index amended through 2025–2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

Analytical inference: The concurrent amendment of Sections 12.2–12.5 (all major source permitting sections) under a single ordinance (5375) effective 6/3/2026, with Section 12.5 still under EPA review, represents the most consequential near-term change for workspaces operating or planning major stationary sources in Clark County. Operations scheduled to commence or expand after 6/3/2026 will be subject to the revised major source permitting framework; the EPA review status of Section 12.5 means the Title V operating permit requirements may continue to evolve through 2026–2027.

# Section 6: Client Conversation Talking Points

The following points are grounded in sourced facts and can be raised credibly in client discussions. Each is labeled by claim type.

**1. DCOP as a project-entry requirement for construction and logistics development**

For any client or project partner planning ground disturbance of 0.25 acres or more, mechanized trenching of 100+ feet, or commercial activities on 0.25+ acres of disturbed ground in Clark County, a Dust Control Operating Permit from DAQ is the operational prerequisite. The AQ Map Assistant allows verification of existing permit coverage before application.

*Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Clark County DES/DAQ, current 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**2. Separate regulatory authority — not NDEP**

Clients accustomed to operating elsewhere in Nevada should be advised that Clark County DAQ is a separate regulatory body from NDEP. NDEP permits do not transfer or substitute for Clark County DAQ permits.

*Source: UNR BEP – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, 2024–2025. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

**3. 2025–2026 rule amendments: permitting rules are freshly updated**

The minor source, major source, NESHAP, NSPS, and fugitive dust rules have all been amended within the past 12–18 months. Any permit or compliance analysis based on pre-2025 versions of Clark County AQRs may be out of date. Clients should confirm they are working from the current adopted text.

*Source: Clark County DES – Current AQ Rules, Clark County DES/DAQ, 2025–2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

**Pitfall to avoid:** Do not represent specific penalty amounts, fee levels, or permit processing timelines without confirming current figures directly with DAQ. These are not publicly confirmed in the available source content, and citing invented figures in a client context creates liability.

---

# Section 7: Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus

**MONITORING — re-check window: Q3 2026 (EPA review of Section 12.5)**

## 7.1 Section 12.5 (Title V / Part 70 Operating Permits) — Under EPA Review

Section 12.5, amended 5/19/2026 under Ordinance 5375, is listed as "under EPA review" rather than carrying a confirmed EPA approval date. This is the Title V operating permit requirements section, which governs the most significant stationary sources in Clark County.

*Source: Clark County DES – Current AQ Rules, Clark County DES/DAQ, index as of 2025–2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

Analytical inference: Until EPA completes its review and either approves or requires revision of Section 12.5, workspaces operating Title V (major) sources in Clark County face regulatory uncertainty about which version of operating permit requirements will ultimately be federally enforceable. The re-check window for this item is Q3–Q4 2026, when EPA review outcomes for recently submitted SIP revisions are typically processed. *Specific EPA review timeline not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-10.*

## 7.2 All-In Clark County Climate Action Initiative

DES leads the All-In Clark County initiative described as addressing "climate impacts in Southern Nevada," including "home and vehicle electrification."

*Source: Clark County DES – About the Department of Environment and Sustainability, Clark County DES, current 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/about-us

Analytical inference: The All-In Clark County initiative is framed as a climate action program encompassing vehicle electrification. Analytical inference: For workspaces operating road freight or fleet-based logistics in the Las Vegas Valley, the All-In Clark County initiative represents a policy signal that county-level pressure toward fleet electrification and reduced vehicle emissions is part of DES's stated mandate — even if no specific fleet mandate regulation has been confirmed in the source content provided. The initiative's regulatory translation into enforceable fleet or facility requirements is a research gap; no specific regulatory output from All-In Clark County has been confirmed in the available sources. Monitoring is appropriate; re-check window: as new ordinances are proposed by Clark County Board of County Commissioners.

## 7.3 Ozone Attainment Plan — Active Planning

The DES portal lists an "Ozone Attainment Plan" and "Smog-Free Clark County" as active programs under the Planning division.

*Source: Clark County DES – Environment and Sustainability (main portal), Clark County DES, current 2026. https://clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/index.php

Analytical inference: Clark County's active ozone attainment planning indicates the Las Vegas Valley remains under regulatory pressure to meet federal ozone NAAQS standards. Analytical inference: If the region is reclassified or faces an ozone nonattainment SIP revision, Section 12.3 (Nonattainment Area Major Source permitting, itself freshly amended under Ordinance 5375, effective 6/3/2026) becomes the operative permitting framework for sources above major-source thresholds. Workspaces planning new or expanded stationary emission sources should confirm current attainment status directly with DAQ before initiating any pre-construction review. *Current ozone attainment classification status not confirmed from available source blocks as of 2026-06-10.*
