prior.title: Wyoming DEQ Receives EPA Partial Approval for Coal Combustion Residuals Permit Program
prior.summary: Wyoming Department of Environmental Quality has received partial approval from the EPA to implement its own Coal Combustion Residuals (CCR) permit program, effective March 30, 2026. Wyoming becomes the fifth state (alongside Oklahoma, Georgia, Texas, and North Dakota) to assume regulatory authority for coal ash management. This marks a significant regulatory transition, with the DEQ now responsible for state-level oversight of CCR standards and disposal, though full permit program approval awaits conclusion of outstanding federal rulemakings.

prior.title: Smart Freight Centre Releases Updated GLEC Framework Version 3.0
prior.summary: Smart Freight Centre announced an update to the GLEC (Global Logistics Emissions Council) framework to further accelerate freight decarbonization efforts. Content unavailable due to server access error.
prior.full_brief:
# GLEC Framework Version 3.0 — Research Summary

**Smart Freight Centre | Released September 2023**

---

# What the Research Found

**GLEC Framework v3.0: Headline Finding and Scope**

The Smart Freight Centre (SFC) released version 3.0 of the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC) Framework in the week of 22 September 2023. *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

The GLEC Framework is described as "the first globally recognised methodology to calculate logistics GHG emissions and offer multinationals and their suppliers a harmonised, efficient and transparent way to calculate and report logistics emissions." *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

The framework was originally launched in 2016 and covers all transport modes — ocean, road, rail, and air freight — ensuring comprehensive emissions tracking across multi-modal supply chains. *Source: Searoutes, "GLEC Framework v3.2 for ISO 14083 Compliance (2025 update)," Searoutes, 11 November 2025. https://searoutes.com/2025/11/11/glec-framework-v3-2-2025.

**Core methodological change in v3.0:** Version 3.0 "incorporates the methodologies of the ISO standard to support companies implementing transparency on the efficiency of their supply chains and logistics. It offers an easy-to-use approach to an ISO 14083-compliant calculation of GHG emissions from transport, covering the transport itself as well as logistics hubs and the emissions from the energy supply to them both." *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

**ISO 14083 alignment:** The GLEC Framework v3.0 is described as being "in full alignment with the new ISO 14083 standard," the standard on the quantification and reporting of greenhouse gas emissions of transport operations, which was published in March 2023. *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

**Development relationship:** "The GLEC Framework was a core element used to develop the content for the new ISO 14083 standard on the quantification and reporting of greenhouse gas emissions of transport operations, published in March 2023." *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

**Methodology scope — what v3.0 covers:** The framework covers GHG emissions from transport operations and logistics hubs, and includes the emissions from the energy supply to both. *Source: CLECAT Newsletter, CLECAT, 22 September 2023. https://www.clecat.org/en/news/newsletters/smart-freight-centre-publishes-glec-framework-30.

**Ongoing evolution — v3.2 (October 2025):** The framework has continued to evolve beyond v3.0. "Version 3.2, published on October 23rd, 2025, represents a minor but important revision primarily focused on updating default values and introducing critical new capabilities," including a new Module 6 covering Air Pollutant Emissions Methodology. *Source: Searoutes, "GLEC Framework v3.2 for ISO 14083 Compliance (2025 update)," Searoutes, 11 November 2025. https://searoutes.com/2025/11/11/glec-framework-v3-2-2025.

**v3.2 fuel and emission factor updates:** v3.2 updates include revised emission factors for LNG, CNG, and electricity in Europe; ethanol, biodiesel, HVO, CNG, and electricity in North America following GREET 2024 standards; updated marine fuel factors for LPG, ammonia, methanol, bio-methanol, and bio-ethanol following IMO MEPC81 guidelines; and newly added India-specific emission factors for road, rail, and other transport modes. *Source: Searoutes, "GLEC Framework v3.2 for ISO 14083 Compliance (2025 update)," Searoutes, 11 November 2025. https://searoutes.com/2025/11/11/glec-framework-v3-2-2025.

**v3.2 adoption timeline:** "For 2025 Inventory: Application of v3.2 default values is encouraged but not mandatory. Companies can continue using previous versions for 2025 reporting. For 2026 Inventory: Implementation of v3.2 default values is expected and recommended." *Source: Searoutes, "GLEC Framework v3.2 for ISO 14083 Compliance (2025 update)," Searoutes, 11 November 2025. https://searoutes.com/2025/11/11/glec-framework-v3-2-2025.

**Methodology limitations — what is not confirmed from grounded source content:**

*Specific [penalty / enforcement provision] not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-09.* The GLEC Framework is an industry methodology standard, not a binding regulation. No penalty clause applies directly to the framework itself; enforcement exposure arises indirectly through linked regulatory schemes (CSRD, EU ETS) that reference or expect alignment with standards such as ISO 14083. The connection to those regulatory schemes is addressed in Section 3 below.

*Specific [version 3.0 release date beyond "the week of 22 September 2023"] not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-09.* The CLECAT source confirms the week but not the exact calendar date.

---

# Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**For workspaces operating as freight forwarders across air, road, and ocean lanes:**

*Operational implication:* The GLEC Framework v3.0 / ISO 14083 alignment means that any multimodal emissions calculation a workspace presents to a client — across air, road, or ocean legs — can now be assessed against a single internationally recognised standard. Forwarders who calculate using pre-v3.0 methodology risk presenting figures that are methodologically inconsistent with what shipper clients, auditors, or downstream regulatory disclosures now expect.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces handling cargo verticals with high sustainability scrutiny — including live events (large, complex supply chains assembled on tight timelines), luxury goods (brands with publicly stated net-zero commitments), and humanitarian freight (donor-facing emissions accountability) — the existence of ISO 14083-aligned calculation is now a credibility threshold, not a differentiator. Clients operating under CSRD or with investor ESG expectations will ask about methodology; "GLEC v3.0 / ISO 14083 aligned" is a defensible answer; "we use our own internal model" invites scrutiny.

*Analytical inference:* The Searoutes source notes that GLEC accreditation "helps businesses meet regulatory requirements such as CSRD and EU ETS." This is an industry service-provider's characterisation, not a regulatory determination. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether ISO 14083 / GLEC alignment satisfies a specific disclosure obligation under CSRD or EU ETS reporting rules for a forwarder in its exact supply-chain role requires legal and technical counsel review.

**Air freight — primary mode:**

*Operational implication:* The framework covers air freight as a transport mode, meaning air legs on multimodal shipments — a primary mode for the workspace across high-value, time-sensitive verticals — carry a calculable GHG figure under GLEC methodology. Clients routing film and TV equipment, luxury goods, or live event freight by air will increasingly expect air-leg emissions to be quantified and reported in a format consistent with their own Scope 3 emissions accounting.

**CORSIA and SAF intersection:**

*Analytical inference:* CORSIA (the ICAO Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation) and emerging SAF blend mandates in the EU (ReFuelEU Aviation) place emissions quantification requirements on the aviation supply chain. Freight forwarders moving cargo by air are not directly regulated operators under CORSIA, but shipper clients whose Scope 3 emissions include air freight may apply GLEC-compatible factors to their own reporting. A workspace using ISO 14083-aligned calculation for air legs is positioned to supply data that is consistent with what clients need for their own disclosure — though *Legal Confirmation Required:* the precise data specification a shipper client needs for CORSIA-adjacent Scope 3 reporting is a technical and legal question the workspace cannot answer without client-side guidance.

**Road and ocean legs:**

*Operational implication:* The framework's coverage of logistics hubs and hub-to-hub energy supply means that for workspaces handling bonded warehouse dwell, transshipment, or intermodal transfer — common in complex live events or humanitarian supply chains — hub emissions are within scope and calculable under v3.0, not just in-transit emissions.

**Forward trajectory — HBEFA 5.1 and CLEVER database (2026):**

"HBEFA 5.1 was published on October 21, 2025. European Road Freight emission intensity values will be updated in 2026 to reflect HBEFA 5.1. CLEVER's harmonised emission factor database expected to be completed in 2026/2027. Integration of CLEVER database into GLEC Framework planned for future versions." *Source: Searoutes, "GLEC Framework v3.2 for ISO 14083 Compliance (2025 update)," Searoutes, 11 November 2025. https://searoutes.com/2025/11/11/glec-framework-v3-2-2025.

*Analytical inference:* European road freight default values in the workspace's current calculation tools — if based on HBEFA data earlier than version 5.1 — will diverge from the updated GLEC Framework values when the 2026 update lands. This is a forward recalibration risk for workspaces quoting road-leg emissions on European lanes.

severity label: MONITORING — Re-check window: when SFC publishes the HBEFA 5.1-updated GLEC Framework (stated as 2026; expected H1–H2 2026 based on HBEFA 5.1's October 2025 publication). *Analytical inference:* publication typically follows the underlying reference document by 6–12 months; re-check by Q2 2026.

---

# What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**1. Emissions calculation methodology selection**

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that have not yet adopted an ISO 14083-aligned (GLEC v3.0+) calculation methodology face a growing gap between their reported figures and what clients, auditors, and downstream regulatory frameworks will treat as credible. Adopting or commissioning GLEC-accredited tooling is the decision this finding triggers, not a background option.

**2. Client emissions data delivery**

*Analytical inference:* For logistics service providers to shippers whose own CSRD sustainability reports include Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation), the quality of the emissions data the workspace supplies directly affects the quality of the client's regulatory disclosure. This creates a commercial incentive — and, depending on client contract terms, potentially a contractual obligation — to supply ISO 14083-aligned data. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any specific client contract creates a binding data-quality obligation of this kind is a legal and commercial review question, not a regulatory determination.

**3. Air pollutant reporting — forward decision point**

The v3.2 Module 6 air pollutant methodology is new as of October 2025. *Operational implication:* Workspaces managing cargo verticals in urban environments or under clean air zone pressure — including road legs feeding city-centre event venues or film sets — may find the air pollutant module relevant earlier than the general freight sector, as local authority or venue-level reporting requirements for NOx, PM₁₀, and PM₂.₅ may arrive before broad regulatory mandates.

**4. Tool and supplier audit**

*Operational implication:* Any third-party emissions calculation tool currently in use by the workspace should be assessed against GLEC v3.0 (and v3.2 for 2026 inventories) compliance. The Searoutes source frames GLEC accreditation as "a requirement for credible reporting" from a tool-provider perspective. This is an industry service-provider's characterisation, not a binding legal standard — but it represents the direction of commercial expectation.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly claim:**

- "Our emissions calculations for multimodal shipments follow the GLEC Framework, which is the only globally recognised methodology for logistics GHG calculation and is aligned with ISO 14083, the international standard published in March 2023." — This is supported by sourced content and is factually defensible.
- "We can provide emissions data for your air, road, and ocean legs, as well as logistics hub emissions, in a format consistent with ISO 14083." — Defensible if the workspace uses a GLEC-accredited tool.
- "The GLEC Framework was the foundational input for ISO 14083, so alignment between the two is structural, not approximate." — Supported by CLECAT sourcing.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- What emissions calculation standard does your Scope 3 reporting use, and does your auditor specify a methodology?
- Do you need air-leg, road-leg, and hub emissions broken out separately, or as a consolidated figure per shipment?
- For 2026 inventory reporting, are you planning to transition to v3.2 default values?

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not claim that using GLEC methodology satisfies CSRD or EU ETS obligations specifically — that is a legal determination the workspace cannot make without counsel review.
- Do not claim GLEC accreditation for the workspace itself unless accreditation has been formally obtained from SFC for the calculation tool in use. Accreditation belongs to the tool, not to the forwarder.
- Do not present v3.0 figures as the current standard for 2026 inventories without noting that v3.2 is the recommended version for 2026 reporting.
- Do not cite the BigMile or FEPORT sources directly in client materials — both source pages returned 404 errors; their content is not confirmable from the provided source blocks.

---

# What the Finding Does Not Resolve

The GLEC Framework v3.0 / ISO 14083 alignment does not resolve:

- Whether ISO 14083-aligned calculation data supplied by a freight forwarder satisfies the specific data-quality requirements a shipper client faces under CSRD Delegated Regulation disclosure standards — that depends on CSRD implementing rules and client-specific auditor guidance.
- What the precise Scope 3 accounting treatment is for freight forwarders acting as intermediaries (non-asset-based) versus asset-owning carriers — the framework addresses calculation methodology, not organisational boundary definitions.
- Whether the GLEC Framework or ISO 14083 will be explicitly named as a required or accepted methodology under any specific EU regulatory instrument — as of the sourced content available, this remains an industry-adopted standard rather than a mandated regulatory tool.
- How HBEFA 5.1 changes will alter European road freight intensity values quantitatively — the update is confirmed as in progress but the numerical changes are not published in the sourced content.
- The primary source at the SFC domain (smartfreightcentre.org) was inaccessible (403 error) during this generation; v3.0 release detail therefore relies entirely on CLECAT and Searoutes corroborating sources. If the SFC primary page becomes accessible, a re-pull may surface additional v3.0 technical detail not captured here.

*Specific [version 3.0 release date, exact day] not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-09* — see SFC primary source when accessible for the precise publication date.

*Does NOT resolve whether GLEC/ISO 14083 alignment satisfies CSRD Scope 3 data-quality requirements for a forwarder's clients — see CSRD Delegated Regulation and client auditor guidance for binding answer.*

---

# Sources

prior.title: IDB Group Transport Sector Framework — Latin America & Caribbean
prior.summary: Content from IADB transport sector framework page is currently inaccessible due to Cloudflare security blocking. The page appears to contain guidance or framework information on transport infrastructure but cannot be retrieved due to access restrictions.
prior.full_brief:
# IDB Group Transport Sector Framework — Latin America & Caribbean

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

The IDB Group's Transport Sector Framework establishes the strategic architecture governing how the IDB Group deploys capital and technical assistance across the Latin America and Caribbean (LAC) transport sector. The Framework "guides the work carried out by the IDB Group with the countries and governments of Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) in the transportation sector." *Source: IDB Sector Framework Transport page, IDB, [undated]. https://iadb.org/en/who-we-are/topics/transport/sector-framework-transport.

**Access status:** The primary Sector Framework Document itself is blocked behind a download gate on the IDB's sector page. The document is listed for download but source content returned navigation and cookie-consent scaffolding only — no substantive Framework text was retrievable from that URL. This brief is therefore synthesised from the six corroborating sources that did return groundable content. The Framework's internal provisions (article structure, financial targets, specific mandate language) are not available from the source pool and are not asserted here.

**What triggered current momentum:** A Regional Policy Dialogue convened in Santo Domingo, jointly organized by the IDB and the International Transport Forum (ITF), produced two concrete institutional launches in December 2025. "The IDB and ITF launched the Regional Transport Network, a first-of-its-kind permanent platform connecting key stakeholders across the transport sector," and separately "the launch of the Transport Policy Lab, a partnership with eight universities across the region." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development for Resilience and Competitiveness, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

**Scale signal:** "25 delegations gathered in Santo Domingo for the Regional Transport Dialogue." Participating country delegations included Brazil, Chile, Costa Rica, Guatemala, and Spain; participating organisations included the OECD and FIA Foundation. *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

**Demand pressure driving investment:** "Demand for passenger and freight transport is expected to nearly double by 2050," while LAC population is projected to grow "from 663 million to more than 730 million by 2050." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

**Investment gap quantification:** "According to IDB calculations, the region needs to double its infrastructure investments over the next 30 years to increase average growth from 2.4% to 5.0%." *Source: IDB Invest Transport Sector Page, IDB Invest, [undated]. https://www.idbinvest.org/en/sectors/transport.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**IDB (public-sector arm):** The IDB's stated objective across the transport sector is "to contribute to achieving efficient, inclusive, and sustainable transportation systems, addressing key areas of action such as: access to quality transportation services, seeking to improve the funding and financing for the sector, the improvement of logistics for competitiveness and regional integration, the sustainability of the transport system, its digital transformation, and the improvement of its institutional framework." *Source: IDB Sector Framework Transport page, IDB. https://iadb.org/en/who-we-are/topics/transport/sector-framework-transport.

**IDB Invest (private-sector arm):** IDB Invest "support[s] the construction and modernization of transport infrastructure and services such as ports, roads, airports, subways, and railroads" and "relies on our longstanding and extensive track record financing the expansion of transport infrastructure in the region." Projects "are usually implemented through public-private partnerships (PPPs) with private companies bearing the initial costs and sharing the risks, management responsibility, and potential future profits." *Source: IDB Invest Transport Sector Page, IDB Invest. https://www.idbinvest.org/en/sectors/transport.

Analytical inference: IDB Invest's PPP-financing model means that the Framework's investment flows reach the workspace's LAC trade-lane operations not through direct IDB loans but through privately-financed infrastructure that the workspace uses as a customer — ports, road corridors, airports. The workspace's operational exposure to Framework outcomes is therefore indirect but real: corridor upgrades and border modernisation change transit times, handling costs, and routing options on lanes the workspace already operates.

**International Transport Forum (ITF):** The ITF co-convened the Santo Domingo dialogue and co-launched the Regional Transport Network. *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

**South American member states (South Connection):** 11 countries have committed support to South Connection, described as "a joint commitment by South American countries to develop strategic corridors integrating transport, energy, and digital infrastructure" with goals including "regulatory modernization, stronger institutions, and system coordination to facilitate the cross-border flow of goods, services, and data." *Source: IDB South Connection page, IDB. https://www.iadb.org/en/who-we-are/how-we-are-organized/departments-offices-and-sectors/vice-presidency-countries-and-regional-integration/south-connection.

**Recent financing decisions signal active deployment:**
- $200 million IDB loan approved February 2026 for a section of the Bi-Oceanic Corridor in Paraguay. *Source: IDB Transport Topic Hub, IDB. https://www.iadb.org/en/who-we-are/topics/transport.
- $530 million approved November 2025 for the First Line of the Metro of Bogota. *Source: IDB Transport Topic Hub, IDB. https://www.iadb.org/en/who-we-are/topics/transport.
- $200 million IDB Invest financing for La Dorada–Chiriguaná Railway Corridor in Colombia. *Source: IDB Invest Transport Sector Page, IDB Invest. https://www.idbinvest.org/en/sectors/transport.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Regional Transport Network — active, annual cadence:**
The Regional Transport Network "will host one annual high-level policy dialogue and promote year-round strategic exchange among policymakers, experts, and academia." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

Analytical inference: The annual dialogue cycle means Framework-aligned policy signals will refresh on approximately a 12-month cadence from December 2025 onward. The workspace should anticipate the next high-level dialogue in approximately Q4 2026, at which corridor investment decisions and regulatory harmonisation progress are likely to be announced.

**Transport Policy Lab — expanded from pilot:**
The Policy Lab launched at the Santo Domingo dialogue as an expansion of "a successful two-year pilot," now formalised as "a partnership with eight universities across the region." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

Analytical inference: The Moviliblog source listed as a corroborator for the Transport Policy Lab was access-denied at retrieval time. Specific university partners, research agenda details, and 2025-launch date cited in the corroborator summary could not be span-grounded from that source. The December 2025 blog post confirms the Lab's launch at the Santo Domingo event; the 2023 pilot start date cited in the corroborator summary is unconfirmed from available source content.

**Decarbonisation pathway — 2050 horizon:**
The IDB's Transportation 2050 report identifies the transport sector as "the largest source of CO2 emissions in the LAC region, accounting for 40% of total emissions," with road transport accounting for "92% of total emissions, domestic aviation for 4%, domestic navigation for 2%, and rail transportation for 1%." *Source: Transportation 2050: Pathways to Decarbonization & Climate Resilience in LAC, IDB / Green Finance LAC, October 2023. https://greenfinancelac.org/resources/publications/transportation-2050-pathways-to-decarbonization-and-climate-resilience-in-latin-america-and-the-caribbean/.

The report notes that "transportation is mentioned in 19 of the 26 Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) of LAC countries, only two set emission reduction targets." *Source: Transportation 2050, IDB / Green Finance LAC, October 2023. https://greenfinancelac.org/resources/publications/transportation-2050-pathways-to-decarbonization-and-climate-resilience-in-latin-america-and-the-caribbean/.

Analytical inference: The gap between NDC mention (19 of 26 countries) and NDC target-setting (2 of 26) indicates the decarbonisation trajectory is aspirational rather than binding at the national regulatory level across most of the region as of the report date. Conversion of Framework aspiration to enforceable national mandates depends on NDC revision cycles (next formal UNFCCC NDC submission round expected 2025) and domestic legislation — neither of which is settled from available sources.

**Supply chain resilience as a conversion trigger:**
Six priorities identified at the Santo Domingo dialogue included "enabling global supply chains," with participants noting that "the reconfiguration of global supply chains driven by geopolitical shifts presents an opportunity for the region." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness.

Analytical inference: Nearshoring demand from North American importers is the most plausible commercial trigger that would accelerate Framework investment conversion from announced loans to operational infrastructure — specifically bi-oceanic road and rail corridors and border modernisation — on a 3–5 year horizon.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**Air (primary mode for workspaces with air-priority operations):**
The Transportation 2050 report records domestic aviation at 4% of LAC transport CO2 emissions. *Source: Transportation 2050, IDB / Green Finance LAC, October 2023. https://greenfinancelac.org/resources/publications/transportation-2050-pathways-to-decarbonization-and-climate-resilience-in-latin-america-and-the-caribbean/. Analytical inference: For workspaces moving high-value, time-sensitive cargo by air across LAC lanes, the immediate Framework consequence is not regulatory compliance pressure but longer-term infrastructure development at secondary airports and improved surface-side access. Specific cost effects on air cargo handling charges require carrier- and airport-specific data not available in current sources.

**Road (secondary mode):**
South Connection specifically targets "roads, ports, waterways, power grids, and digital networks—particularly along key bi-oceanic corridors that unlock regional and global trade potential," with work to "improve logistics services, modernize border crossings, and streamline customs operations." *Source: IDB South Connection page, IDB. https://www.iadb.org/en/who-we-are/how-we-are-organized/departments-offices-and-sectors/vice-presidency-countries-and-regional-integration/south-connection. Analytical inference: Border modernisation and customs streamlining on bi-oceanic corridors would directly reduce drayage dwell times and improve transit predictability for road-mode cargo on South American cross-border lanes. Quantified time or cost savings are not available from current sources.

**Ocean (tertiary mode):**
IDB Invest has active port financing, including a $125 million financing for the expansion of Terminal Internacional del Sur in Peru and a $14 million financing for Puerto Austral in Chile. *Source: IDB Invest Transport Sector Page, IDB Invest. https://www.idbinvest.org/en/sectors/transport. Analytical inference: Port capacity expansion on the Pacific South American coast is relevant to ocean-mode cargo serving Asia–Americas trade lanes. The direction of effect is positive for throughput and handling reliability, but specific timeline, capacity additions, and cost-per-TEU implications are not available from current sources.

**Public transport systems financial sustainability:**
The Santo Domingo dialogue identified that "public transport systems in Latin America and the Caribbean face a growing financial gap due to their reliance on fares and subsidies, declining demand, and rising operating costs," with three recommended lines of action including "improving operational efficiency through better planning and service integration; better targeting subsidies to benefit those who need them most; and diversifying funding sources through instruments such as value capture, congestion and parking charges." *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025. https://www.iadb.org/en/blog/transport/transport-driver-development-resilience-and-competitiveness. Analytical inference: For workspaces whose last-mile delivery or client event logistics in LAC cities depend on urban transport access, congestion and parking charging mechanisms — if adopted nationally — would add cost to urban drayage that is currently unpriced.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

Analytical inference: The IDB Framework's emphasis on PPP financing and private-sector participation creates a structural advantage for logistics operators and freight forwarders who can demonstrate climate, gender, and governance criteria alignment. IDB Invest explicitly states it "consider[s] environmental and social aspects, gender equality, climate change, and corporate governance" in project financing decisions. *Source: IDB Invest Transport Sector Page, IDB Invest. https://www.idbinvest.org/en/sectors/transport. Operators who have formalised sustainability reporting are better positioned to participate in, or win contracts from, PPP-financed infrastructure projects in the region.

Analytical inference: The Regional Transport Network's annual dialogue creates a structured stakeholder channel. Logistics operators and freight forwarders who participate in or monitor the dialogue gain early visibility into corridor investment decisions, regulatory harmonisation moves, and energy transition priorities — ahead of competitors who engage only when decisions are finalised.

Analytical inference: The 2050 decarbonisation pathway, if it converts to NDC targets across more than the current two LAC countries that set emission reduction targets, would create compliance obligations on freight operators serving those markets. Workspaces that have already built Scope 3 emissions measurement and reporting infrastructure are positioned earlier in that compliance cycle.

No named competitor positioning is available from current sources. Competitor-specific activity requires trade reporting not present in the source pool.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The IDB Group has identified a doubling of freight transport demand by 2050 across LAC, with a parallel need to double infrastructure investment over the next 30 years. *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025; IDB Invest Transport Sector Page.*
- The IDB is actively financing bi-oceanic corridor development — the $200 million Paraguay Bi-Oceanic Corridor loan approved February 2026 is the most recent concrete signal. *Source: IDB Transport Topic Hub. https://www.iadb.org/en/who-we-are/topics/transport.
- Transport accounts for 40% of LAC CO2 emissions, with road at 92% of that figure, creating a clear decarbonisation imperative that is beginning to flow into NDC policy architecture across the region. *Source: Transportation 2050, IDB / Green Finance LAC, October 2023.*
- The IDB–ITF Regional Transport Network is now a standing annual forum — the structured nature of the dialogue means policy signals will be visible and forward-dateable, not ad hoc.

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not claim specific Framework compliance obligations on private freight operators — the Framework governs IDB Group lending to governments, not private-sector regulatory mandates.
- Do not cite specific cost savings from border modernisation or corridor upgrades — quantified savings are not available from current sources.
- Do not cite the Transport Policy Lab's university partners by name — the Moviliblog source was access-denied and partner details could not be verified.
- Do not assert that most LAC countries have set binding transport emission reduction targets — only two of 26 NDC-submitting countries had done so as of the October 2023 report date.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Monitor the Regional Transport Network's next annual dialogue (expected Q4 2026):**
The Network's stated cadence is one high-level policy dialogue per year. *Source: Transport as a Driver of Development, IDB Blog, 17 December 2025.* The workspace should set a monitoring trigger for Q3 2026 to identify the next dialogue date, agenda, and corridor investment announcements before they become public-domain news.

**Track Bi-Oceanic Corridor development on South American lanes:**
The $200 million Paraguay corridor loan (February 2026) and South Connection's 11-country commitment signal that bi-oceanic corridor development is in active financing phase, not planning phase. *Source: IDB Transport Topic Hub; IDB South Connection page.* For workspaces with road or multimodal cargo moving cross-border in South America, tracking construction timelines and border-crossing modernisation milestones on these corridors should become a standing operational intelligence task.

**Assess emissions reporting readiness against LAC trajectory:**
The gap between NDC mention (19 countries) and target-setting (2 countries) means the emissions regulatory environment is loose today but directionally tightening. *Source: Transportation 2050, IDB / Green Finance LAC, October 2023.* Workspaces that build Scope 3 emissions measurement for LAC lanes now — before national mandates formalise — will face lower compliance transition cost when targets are set.

**Re-check window:** Set a monitoring review for Q4 2026, aligned with the expected next Regional Transport Network annual dialogue and the anticipated next round of LAC NDC submissions.

---

# 8. Sources

prior.title: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation: Industry Alignment for EV Charging and Refueling Infrastructure
prior.summary: The Joint Office of Energy and Transportation provides comprehensive guidance and resources for private sector engagement in federal EV charging and refueling infrastructure projects. The page outlines funding opportunities (NEVI Formula Program, Charging and Fueling Infrastructure Program), technical standards (23 CFR Part 680), compliance requirements (Davis-Bacon Act, cybersecurity clauses), and coordination mechanisms (ChargeX Consortium, EV Working Group) designed to facilitate public-private partnerships and standardized infrastructure deployment.
prior.full_brief:
# Market Signal Brief: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation — Industry Alignment for EV Charging and Refueling Infrastructure

---

## Section 1: What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

The Joint Office of Energy and Transportation — a federal body created through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to facilitate collaboration between the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) — has established an active industry-alignment framework designed to coordinate private-sector investment in electric vehicle (EV) charging and refueling infrastructure across the United States. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry

The framework operates across four channels simultaneously: (1) technical standards development; (2) public-private technical assistance; (3) industry convening bodies; and (4) strategic corridor planning. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry

Three events have sharpened the signal in 2024–2026:

**The EVPKI / Universal Plug & Charge announcement (December 4, 2024):** SAE Industry Technologies Consortia (SAE ITC) and the Joint Office announced "a common EVPKI framework that will enable secure automatic authentication as soon as drivers plug in." The announcement stated that "throughout 2025, the industry will continue to move toward the universal Plug & Charge model," encompassing PKI user onboarding and testing, a secure process to exchange technology among vendors, and "a new competitive EV Sector PKI supply market." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, SAE EVPKI / Universal Plug & Charge Announcement, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge

**The GAO performance audit (July 22, 2025):** The U.S. Government Accountability Office published GAO-25-106992, finding that "the Joint Office has taken some steps to assess its activities, but GAO found it generally has not defined performance goals with measurable targets and time frames for its activities." The audit also recorded that "as of April 2025, 384 NEVI- and CFI-funded chargers were open to the public." GAO made three open recommendations, including that the Joint Office and DOT "fully implement a performance management framework." As of the audit's publication, "the Joint Office and DOT did not take a position on GAO's recommendations." *Source: GAO-25-106992, Electric Vehicle Infrastructure: Improved Performance Management, U.S. GAO, Jul. 22, 2025. https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-106992

**The Washington v. U.S. DOT final judgment (January 2026):** District Judge Tana Lin issued a final judgment finding the February–August 2025 freeze of NEVI formula funds to be a violation of the Administrative Procedure Act — "outside of the spending authorities given to the agency by Congress" and "arbitrary and capricious." The ruling restored "the pre-February 2025 legal status approved State Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Deployment Plans for any states that have not fully obligated FY22-25 funds." The case was initially brought by 17 states and later joined by environmental NGOs. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Washington v. U.S. Department of Transportation and NEVI Progress Updates, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

*Analytical inference:* Taken together, these three events — a major interoperability standard going into testing, a critical federal audit identifying accountability gaps, and a court-restored funding pipeline — constitute the compound trigger that moves EV charging infrastructure alignment from background program administration to an active commercial signal. The private-sector buildout window is reopening under legal clarity, but with regulatory and institutional uncertainty still present.

---

## Section 2: Who's Driving It and What They Want

The Joint Office of Energy and Transportation is the convening authority. It was created through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and "facilitates collaboration between the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, SAE EVPKI announcement, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge

**Standards and interoperability layer — SAE ITC and the EVPKI Consortium:** SAE Industry Technologies Consortia leads the Electric Vehicle Public Key Infrastructure (EVPKI) Consortium, whose "members span the EV charging ecosystem, including leading global automakers, EV charger manufacturers, charge point operators, eMobility service providers, and PKI suppliers." Their stated interest is establishing a "common security solution that will enable vehicles, chargers and charging networks to securely communicate with each other, achieving true interoperability across the entire charging ecosystem." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, SAE EVPKI / Universal Plug & Charge Announcement, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge

**States:** Seventeen states initially sued to unfreeze NEVI funds, and the court found that "before February 6, about $1.4 billion was available for the States to obligate for NEVI projects." States have "obligated $1.4 billion in funds for future charging stations through 2028." Some states, such as Ohio, "plan for an uptick in NEVI spending in 2026, with the state planning to construct 12 new charging stations in the spring." *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

**Federal program administration — FHWA:** The Federal Highway Administration administers both the NEVI Formula Program and the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure (CFI) Discretionary Grant Program, which are the primary public funding vehicles into which private-sector companies are invited to bid. *Source: GAO-25-106992, U.S. GAO, Jul. 22, 2025. https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-106992

**Private sector:** According to Paren Q3 2025 data cited in the Eno Center report, the U.S. has "a total of 12,375 charging stations with 64,486 ports" — a figure representing private-sector buildout running substantially ahead of NEVI-funded infrastructure (approximately 121 NEVI-funded open stations as of the same period). *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

*Analytical inference:* The private sector's stated interest is interoperability standards that remove barriers to scale, access to federal co-investment where private returns are sub-threshold, and procurement clarity on requirements for federally funded projects. The federal government's stated interest is network reliability, data openness, workforce development, and achieving corridor coverage that private investment alone would not deliver.

**Industry convening bodies — ChargeX and EVWG:** The Joint Office "regularly convened the National Charging Experience (ChargeX) Consortium and Electric Vehicle Working Group (EVWG)." The ChargeX Consortium "was a collaboration between national labs and organizations representing a cross section of the EV industry to address three EV charging challenges: payment processing and user interface, vehicle-charger communication, and diagnostic data sharing." The EVWG "brought together public and private sector representatives to provide recommendations regarding the development, adoption, and integration of light-, medium-, and heavy-duty EVs into the transportation and energy systems of the United States." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry

*Analytical inference:* The past-tense phrasing of the ChargeX and EVWG descriptions on the Joint Office's own site — "regularly convened" and "brought together" — is a signal worth monitoring: it may reflect the staff reductions at the Joint Office noted in the GAO report ("related actions starting in 2025 are ongoing. These include... staff reductions at the Joint Office"). The GAO noted these actions but its work was "completed by January 2025 and does not assess changes since then," leaving the current operational status of these bodies a research gap.

---

## Section 3: Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**MONITORING — re-check window: Q4 2026**

The signal has three identifiable trajectory paths depending on how the following conversion triggers resolve:

**Trigger 1 — EVPKI testing completion and PKI supplier onboarding (2025 timeline, ongoing):** The December 2024 announcement stated the industry would complete "PKI user onboarding and testing" and establish "a new competitive EV Sector PKI supply market" throughout 2025. SAE ITC anticipated "an operational EVPKI solution in 2025." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, SAE EVPKI announcement, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge Whether this timeline was met is a research gap as of this brief's generation date; no post-announcement status update was available in the source pool.

*Analytical inference:* If EVPKI testing completes and PKI suppliers are onboarded, the signal converts from interoperability standard-in-development to interoperability standard-in-deployment — a procurement-relevant event for any operator equipping fleets or facilities with EV charging infrastructure.

**Trigger 2 — NEVI funding pipeline reopening and FY2026 appropriations resolution:** The January 2026 court ruling restored the pre-February 2025 legal status for NEVI funds. However, the Eno Center noted that "states must now consider the potential impact of the pending FY 2026 appropriations bill, which transfers $879 million in NEVI funds into other Federal Highway Administration programs." *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/ Resolution of this appropriations question is the primary near-term funding trigger.

*Analytical inference:* The $879 million transfer, if enacted, would reduce the available NEVI pool materially. For private-sector operators bidding into NEVI-funded opportunities, this represents a potential contraction in the co-investment window — narrowing the number of sites where public funding closes the financial gap.

**Trigger 3 — Joint Office performance management framework implementation:** GAO's three recommendations remain open as of July 2025. "The Joint Office and DOT did not take a position on GAO's recommendations." DOT affirmatively did not concur with Recommendations 2 and 3 regarding FHWA performance management. *Source: GAO-25-106992, U.S. GAO, Jul. 22, 2025. https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-106992 The absence of a performance management framework affects the measurability of program outcomes and the federal government's ability to demonstrate results to Congress — relevant to future appropriations and program continuity.

*Analytical inference:* If the Joint Office implements the GAO performance framework, program accountability improves and the case for sustained or expanded appropriations is strengthened. If it does not, the program operates with what the GAO characterized as an inability to "better understand the outcomes of these efforts" — a condition that historically weakens the political case for funding continuity.

---

## Section 4: Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

*This section is filtered to workspace operations in freight forwarding, transport logistics, and adjacent supply chain roles. No cost figures from primary sources are available in the source pool for charger procurement, installation, or operating costs. Effect on specific cost lines requires operator-specific and site-specific data.*

**For workspaces operating road freight or drayage fleets:**

*Analytical inference:* The National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy, which the Joint Office describes as "an all-of-government approach to align, unlock, and unleash medium- and heavy-duty vehicle infrastructure," sets the planning context for electrification of long-haul and drayage routes. Workspaces with road-fleet operations on U.S. corridors will need to track which corridors are prioritized for charging infrastructure deployment, as this directly affects the feasibility and timing of fleet electrification decisions. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry

**Interoperability standard as a procurement filter:**

The NEVI program standard specifies that "charging stations under NEVI must have a minimum power of 150 kilowatts and be interoperable for all vehicle types." *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/ The emerging SAE EVPKI universal Plug & Charge standard adds an authentication and payment interoperability layer on top of connector interoperability.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces procuring EV charging equipment for facilities — warehouse operations, depot charging, logistics hub installations — equipment specified to NEVI and EVPKI standards provides the broadest future compatibility. Equipment procured to proprietary authentication standards may require retrofitting or replacement as the universal standard deploys.

**Workforce requirements:**

The NEVI standard includes workforce requirements codified at 23 CFR 680.106(j)(1) for "electricians installing, operating, or maintaining EVSE." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry Workspaces managing their own depot or facility charging installations will need to verify that installation contractors meet these requirements for any federally co-funded installations.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the 23 CFR 680.106(j)(1) workforce requirements apply to privately funded installations on non-federally-funded premises, or only to NEVI/CFI-funded stations, requires legal confirmation.

**Financial model:**

The Joint Office's stated position is that "public funding can support charging projects before the financial break-even point so companies can make investments now that will pay off in the long term." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/industry With only 2 percent of the $4.4 billion NEVI program disbursed as of the filing period, and $1.4 billion obligated through 2028, the financial gap-filling mechanism is operational but slow-moving. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

*Analytical inference:* The gap between obligated funds ($1.4 billion) and disbursed funds ($94 million) indicates that the reimbursement pipeline is building but that capital commitment precedes cash recovery by a meaningful lag — a cash-flow consideration for any operator executing federally co-funded charging projects.

---

## Section 5: Competitive Implications

*No named competitors or specific competitor positioning data was available in the source pool for the freight forwarding or logistics vertical. The following analysis is drawn from structural observations in the sourced material.*

**Who has access to the technical assistance pipeline:**

The Joint Office provides "unbiased, free, and customizable technical assistance to help states, communities, tribal governments, transit agencies, and school districts." *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Technical Assistance page, driveelectric.gov. https://driveelectric.gov/technical-assistance

*Analytical inference:* The TA program is structured primarily for public-sector recipients. Private-sector companies access the system indirectly — through bidding into state and community NEVI/CFI opportunities rather than through direct TA engagement. The Joint Office states it "has handled hundreds of technical assistance inquiries from the private sector," but the scope of private-sector TA relative to public-sector TA is not specified in the source material. Workspaces that have not engaged with the Joint Office Concierge as a technical resource are potentially behind peers who have.

**Who benefits from the EVPKI standard:**

*Analytical inference:* Charge point operators, eMobility service providers, and EV charging network operators who participate in the SAE EVPKI Consortium are positioned ahead of those who have not, as EVPKI compliance will eventually be a precondition for interoperating with the universal Plug & Charge system. For workspaces that own or operate charging networks as part of their logistics infrastructure, early EVPKI alignment is a competitive positioning decision.

**Who is exposed by the NEVI pause aftermath:**

States that have not fully obligated FY22-25 NEVI funds remain in the active buildout phase. The court ruling provides legal clarity, but the pending $879 million appropriations transfer creates continued uncertainty. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

*Analytical inference:* Private-sector operators who built business cases around NEVI co-investment in specific corridors or sites affected by the pause may face revised project timelines and financial assumptions. Operators who identified fallback structures — wholly private financing or alternative grant sources — during the pause period are better positioned for the reopened pipeline.

---

## Section 6: Client Conversation Talking Points

**What workspaces in logistics and freight roles can credibly say:**

- The U.S. federal government has an active, law-backed infrastructure program (NEVI) funding EV charging stations every 50 miles on national highway corridors. A January 2026 federal court ruling confirmed the program's legal standing after a 2025 freeze. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/
- As of Q3 2025, the total U.S. EV charging network (public and private) comprises approximately 12,375 stations with 64,486 ports. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026, citing Paren Q3 2025 data. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/
- A universal Plug & Charge authentication standard (SAE EVPKI) was announced in December 2024, with testing beginning in 2025. Once implemented, any EV can authenticate and pay at any public charging station without proprietary apps. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge
- The federal government's own auditor (GAO) has found that the Joint Office and FHWA have not yet fully defined measurable performance goals for the NEVI and CFI programs — an accountability gap that is material to assessing program delivery confidence. *Source: GAO-25-106992, U.S. GAO, Jul. 22, 2025. https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-106992

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not claim the U.S. EV charging network is "complete" or "on track" for specific corridor coverage targets — the GAO audit documents the absence of measurable progress goals, and only approximately 121 NEVI-funded stations were open as of the data period.
- Do not claim the EVPKI system is operational — it was announced as entering testing in 2025; operational status is a research gap as of this brief.
- Do not cite NEVI funding figures without noting the distinction between obligated ($1.4 billion) and disbursed ($94 million) — the gap is large and operationally significant.

**Questions to pose to clients evaluating U.S. fleet electrification:**

- Which specific corridors are relevant to your routes, and what is the current NEVI obligation status for those corridors?
- Does your EV procurement specification require EVPKI-compliant authentication, and does your charging network partner have a timeline for EVPKI onboarding?
- Have you modeled the cash-flow lag between capital commitment and NEVI reimbursement in your financial case?

---

## Section 7: What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Engage the Joint Office Concierge for technical assistance on federally funded project requirements.**
The Joint Office offers "unbiased, free, and customizable technical assistance" accessible via the Joint Office Concierge, with a three-step process: request submission, rapid response, and in-depth consult. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Technical Assistance page. https://driveelectric.gov/technical-assistance For workspaces considering any federally co-funded charging installation at logistics facilities, this is a zero-cost information channel that should be accessed before procurement decisions are made.

**Action 2 — Specify NEVI-standard and EVPKI-compatible equipment in any facility charging procurement now.**
NEVI-funded stations are required to meet interoperability standards. The SAE EVPKI framework is moving toward becoming the authentication baseline. Specifying equipment that meets both the 150 kW minimum power threshold and emerging EVPKI compatibility avoids stranded-asset risk from proprietary authentication lock-in. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Dec. 4, 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge; Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/

**Action 3 — Track the FY2026 appropriations bill outcome for the $879 million NEVI transfer.**
The pending transfer of $879 million from NEVI into other FHWA programs is the single largest near-term variable in the public co-investment pipeline. *Source: Eno Center for Transportation, Jan. 30, 2026. https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/ Any workspace with a business case that depends on NEVI co-investment at specific sites should model both the full-NEVI and reduced-NEVI scenarios before committing capital.

**Action 4 — Monitor the National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy GIS shapefiles for corridor prioritization affecting relevant routes.**
The Joint Office publishes "GIS shapefiles for National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy," which map which freight corridors are prioritized for zero-emission infrastructure deployment. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, Industry Alignment page. https://driveelectric.gov/industry Workspaces with road freight operations on U.S. routes should overlay their route maps against these shapefiles to identify where charging infrastructure investment is most likely to materialize — and where gaps remain.

**Action 5 — Set a re-check for Q4 2026.**
The EVPKI testing and PKI supplier onboarding timeline (2025), the FY2026 appropriations outcome, and the GAO recommendation response status are all pending as of this brief. The operational picture will be materially clearer by Q4 2026. Set a structured review at that point to assess whether the signal has converted to active commercial pressure or regulatory mandate.

---

## Section 8: Sources

| Source | Type | URL |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Office of Energy and Transportation — Industry Alignment for EV Charging and Refueling Infrastructure | Government agency page (Tier 2) | https://driveelectric.gov/industry |
| Joint Office of Energy and Transportation — Technical Assistance | Government agency page (Tier 2) | https://driveelectric.gov/technical-assistance |
| Joint Office of Energy and Transportation — SAE EVPKI / Universal Plug & Charge Announcement | Government agency press release (Tier 2) | https://driveelectric.gov/news/universal-plug-and-charge |
| GAO-25-106992 — Electric Vehicle Infrastructure: Improved Performance Management | Federal audit report (Tier 1) | https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-106992 |
| Eno Center for Transportation — Washington v. U.S. Department of Transportation and NEVI Progress Updates | Transport policy think tank (Tier 4) | https://enotrans.org/article/washington-v-u-s-department-of-transportation-and-nevi-progress-updates/ |

*Note: The Federal Register source (https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/02/28/2023-03500/national-electric-vehicle-infrastructure-standards-and-requirements) and the Congressional Research Service source (https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/IN12556) were blocked during retrieval and yielded no verbatim content. No FACT claims are grounded to these URLs. Both are listed under New Sources Identified as leads for future retrieval.*

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|

prior.title: Carbon Trust: Global Organization Overview and Net Zero Transition Services
prior.summary: The Carbon Trust is a global organization providing comprehensive decarbonization and net zero transition services across multiple sectors. Services include transition strategy, avoided emissions measurement, decarbonization strategies, carbon footprinting, net zero planning, supply chain engagement, sustainable finance, and market transformation initiatives across agriculture, energy, buildings, and industry.
prior.full_brief:
# Carbon Trust: Global Organization Overview and Net Zero Transition Services

MONITORING

## What's Moving and What Triggered It

The Carbon Trust — a not-for-profit private company limited by guarantee, established in March 2001 and originally funded by UK tax revenue generated from the Climate Change Levy — has evolved over 25 years from a UK government energy efficiency body into a financially independent global decarbonisation consultancy. *Source: Carbon Trust, Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Trust.

The organisation now operates with a stated mission "to accelerate the move to a decarbonised future," partnering with "leading businesses, governments and financial institutions to accelerate their route to Net Zero." *Source: Carbon Trust, Who We Are, https://www.carbontrust.com/who-we-are.

The 2024 Impact Report confirms the organisation's current footprint: experts on the ground in the UK, the Netherlands, Germany, South Africa, Singapore and Mexico, operating across 75 countries, with 2,893+ organisations supported to date and no shareholders — financially independent, with profit reinvested. *Source: Carbon Trust, Impact Report 2024, https://publications.carbontrust.com/impact-report/.

The trigger for this signal's current relevance is convergence of three forces:

- **Regulatory acceleration**: Expanding mandatory disclosure frameworks (CSRD, TCFD-aligned national regimes, CBAM) are creating compliance-driven demand for precisely the services the Carbon Trust provides — footprinting, target setting, Scope 3 measurement, and disclosure alignment — across private and public sector clients on every major trade lane.

- **Leadership transition**: The Wikipedia source records that founding CEO Tom Delay retired in July 2023 after a 22-year tenure; Chris Stark (former head of the UK Climate Change Committee) was appointed in January 2024 but resigned after three months; COO Michael Rea assumed the CEO role. *Source: Carbon Trust, Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Trust. *Analytical inference:* A leadership transition of this speed — two CEOs within months — is an organisational signal worth tracking; it may affect strategic continuity, client relationship management, and the pace of service expansion into freight-adjacent sectors.

- **Geographic expansion**: The Carbon Trust opened a German office in Berlin in 2024 "to provide on-site support for the DACH region and German-speaking customers to assist key businesses in achieving their climate targets." *Source: Carbon Trust, Our History, https://www.carbontrust.com/who-we-are/our-history. *Analytical inference:* The DACH expansion is operationally relevant for workspaces with European trade lanes and clients headquartered in Germany, Austria, or Switzerland who are now subject to CSRD and who may approach their logistics partners asking for Scope 3 data that meets Carbon Trust methodology standards.

---

## Who's Driving It and What They Want

The Carbon Trust positions itself as an independent third-party authority in a market where self-reported corporate net zero claims are under increasing scrutiny. Its stated offering spans the full transition lifecycle: measurement, strategy, financing, reporting, and disclosure alignment. *Source: Carbon Trust, Net Zero Transition Planning and Delivery, https://www.carbontrust.com/what-we-do/net-zero-transition-planning-and-delivery.

Key service lines that intersect directly with freight and logistics clients include:

- **Footprinting and reporting**: Organisational carbon footprint measurement, product carbon footprint labelling, Scope 3 supply chain emissions measurement. The Carbon Trust advised the GHG Protocol on guidance for companies to measure Scope 3 emissions, now a widely used international standard. *Source: Carbon Trust, Our History, https://www.carbontrust.com/who-we-are/our-history.

- **Supply chain engagement**: The Carbon Trust launched what it describes as "the world's first verification to recognise supply chain emissions reduction" in 2015 and "the world's first independent certification for organisations that are measuring, managing and reducing greenhouse gas (CO2e) emissions in their supply chains." *Source: Carbon Trust, Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Trust.

- **Route to Net Zero Standard**: A three-tier certification framework (Taking Action / Advancing / Leading) providing independent verification of emissions reductions. The standard "helps organisations measure and manage their emissions, inform carbon reduction strategies, and align targets for the future." *Source: Carbon Trust, Route to Net Zero Standard, https://www.carbontrust.com/what-we-do/net-zero-transition-planning-and-delivery/route-to-net-zero-standard.

- **Sustainable finance**: Green finance advisory — the Carbon Trust accredited the UK's first green bond for Barclays in 2017. *Source: Carbon Trust, Our History, https://www.carbontrust.com/who-we-are/our-history.

The parties driving demand are corporate sustainability and legal functions at multinational shippers who face mandatory CSRD reporting from 2025 onward, financial institutions requiring net zero transition plans as a lending condition, and governments procuring decarbonisation advisory. *Analytical inference:* For a global freight forwarding operation, the Carbon Trust's clients are often the same organisations that procure logistics services — meaning Carbon Trust methodology standards propagate downstream as supplier data requests.

---

## Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

The Carbon Trust's service portfolio is expanding in direct correlation with the regulatory calendar:

- CSRD reporting obligations began applying to large EU companies for financial years starting 1 January 2024 (first reports due 2025), expanding to more companies in subsequent years. *Analytical inference:* As mandatory CSRD reporting requires Scope 3 disclosure — including transport and distribution emissions — corporate clients engaging the Carbon Trust for transition planning will increasingly pass Scope 3 data requests to their freight forwarders.

- The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) transitional phase is already in force, with full implementation from 2026. The Carbon Trust's 2024 Impact Report notes staff published commentary on CBAM, signalling active engagement with this regulatory development. *Source: Carbon Trust, Impact Report 2024, https://publications.carbontrust.com/impact-report/.

- The Carbon Trust's Route to Net Zero Standard's three-tier certification framework is already commercially available and being applied to named clients including Siemens, Safaricom, and Vodafone, as listed in the Route to Net Zero Standard page. *Source: Carbon Trust, Route to Net Zero Standard, https://www.carbontrust.com/what-we-do/net-zero-transition-planning-and-delivery/route-to-net-zero-standard.

Conversion trigger: This signal converts from monitoring to active commercial pressure when freight-forwarding clients begin embedding Carbon Trust certification requirements — or equivalent Scope 3 verification standards — into logistics procurement contracts. *Analytical inference:* That conversion is most likely first visible in European trade lanes, driven by CSRD-obligated corporate clients, and is expected to intensify through 2025–2026 as first-wave CSRD reporting cycles complete.

**Re-check window**: Q4 2025 — to assess whether first-wave CSRD reporters are generating downstream Scope 3 data demands on logistics suppliers, and whether Carbon Trust methodology (or ISO 14083) is being specified as the required measurement standard in client RFPs. MONITORING severity confirmed; no immediate action required.

---

## Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating as freight forwarders across air, road, and ocean modes on Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes, the primary operational consequence of Carbon Trust methodology becoming a market standard is the need to produce verified, methodology-aligned Scope 3 transport emissions data at shipment level.

- **Air (primary mode)**: SAF-related emissions calculation is the most complex and contested element of air freight carbon reporting. The Carbon Trust's existing work on transport decarbonisation and footprinting methodology — and its advisory role to the GHG Protocol — means any Carbon Trust-endorsed transport emissions methodology would carry significant credibility with corporate clients. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces that cannot produce SAF-adjusted, methodology-compliant air freight emissions data are exposed to contract risk as CSRD-obligated shippers tighten supplier requirements.

- **Road (secondary mode)**: The Carbon Trust's DACH-region expansion is directly relevant for European road transport legs on cross-border shipments. *Analytical inference:* Corporate clients in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland engaging the Carbon Trust locally may use Carbon Trust methodology as the benchmark for road transport emissions data requests from logistics providers.

- **Ocean (tertiary mode)**: The Carbon Trust's involvement in offshore wind and marine energy, combined with its GHG Protocol advisory role, positions it to influence methodology development for ocean freight decarbonisation. The IMO's Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) regime and EU ETS for shipping create a regulatory backdrop where methodology alignment is commercially significant. *Analytical inference:* Effect on specific ocean freight Scope 3 calculations requires carrier-specific data and methodology comparison.

Current rate for Carbon Trust certification engagement: *Specific cost figure not available from primary sources as of 2025-05-27.* The Route to Net Zero Standard page offers a free initial consultation; engagement costs are not publicly listed.

---

## Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* The Carbon Trust's 3,000+ client base — spanning businesses, governments, and financial institutions across 50 countries — represents a large share of the global corporate entities that also procure air freight, road transport, and international shipping services. Its position as an independent third-party verifier of Scope 3 supply chain emissions means it is upstream of freight forwarders in the sustainability credibility chain: Carbon Trust-endorsed methodology becomes the standard against which a logistics provider's emissions data is assessed.

- Freight forwarders whose emissions reporting methodology is already aligned with GHG Protocol Scope 3 — which the Carbon Trust helped establish — are better positioned when client audits reference Carbon Trust-standard calculations.

- Freight forwarders whose Scope 3 data is produced manually, without alignment to a recognised third-party methodology, face an increasing credibility gap as Carbon Trust-verified corporate clients raise their supplier data standards.

- The Carbon Trust's supply chain engagement service specifically targets "the world's first verification to recognise supply chain emissions reduction," meaning it may approach logistics providers directly as part of a client engagement — creating both a potential partnership opportunity and a compliance audit risk. *Source: Carbon Trust, Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Trust.

Named competitor positioning: *No sourced reporting on named freight-forwarding competitors' specific Carbon Trust engagement status is available from the provided sources. This is a research gap.*

---

## Client Conversation Talking Points

What the workspace can credibly say in a client meeting:

- "The Carbon Trust — which advised the GHG Protocol on Scope 3 emissions measurement — operates a three-tier Route to Net Zero Standard that an increasing number of corporate clients are using. Our emissions reporting methodology is aligned with the GHG Protocol Scope 3 framework that underlies that standard." (Only say this if the methodology alignment is verified internally first.)

- "As your logistics partner, we can provide shipment-level emissions data. We recommend aligning on the measurement methodology your sustainability team is using — whether Carbon Trust, ISO 14083, or GHG Protocol Scope 3 — before we generate your quarterly transport emissions report."

- "The Carbon Trust has confirmed that nearly 60% of the technologies crucial for reaching Net Zero by 2050 haven't achieved commercial scale. We are tracking SAF availability and pricing on your key corridors as part of our forward planning." *Source: Carbon Trust, Impact Report 2024, https://publications.carbontrust.com/impact-report/

Pitfalls to avoid:

- Do not claim that your emissions data "meets Carbon Trust standards" unless it has been independently verified by the Carbon Trust or a body using their methodology — this is an overclaim that creates legal and commercial risk.

- Do not characterise the Carbon Trust as a regulatory body. It is a not-for-profit consultancy providing voluntary certification services; its standards are market standards, not legally mandated compliance requirements (though they may be referenced in client contracts). *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any specific Carbon Trust standard has been incorporated by reference into a client contract or procurement framework the workspace operates under requires counsel review.

- Do not represent that Carbon Trust certification of a shipper client extends to their logistics suppliers — it does not automatically do so; supply chain engagement is a separate service.

---

## What the Workspace Should Do Now

These are positioning actions, not compliance actions:

1. **Conduct an internal methodology audit**: Confirm which Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transport) and Category 9 (downstream transport) methodology the workspace currently uses to calculate and report freight emissions on behalf of clients. Map against GHG Protocol Scope 3 guidance (which the Carbon Trust helped establish) and ISO 14083 (the transport-specific standard) to identify alignment gaps before a client audit surfaces them.

2. **Add a supplier data clause to client onboarding**: Add language to RFQ responses and MSAs stating the emissions methodology used and the data fields available. This pre-empts the downstream Scope 3 data request that CSRD-obligated clients will begin generating through 2025–2026.

3. **Brief commercial leads on DACH exposure**: For trade lanes touching Germany, Austria, or Switzerland — particularly road and air — brief commercial leads that the Carbon Trust's new Berlin office is actively engaging DACH corporate clients on transition planning, and that those clients may begin inserting Scope 3 verification requirements into logistics contracts within 12–18 months.

4. **Track the Route to Net Zero Standard's transport-sector applicability**: The Carbon Trust's Route to Net Zero Standard is currently applied to corporate organisations broadly. Monitor whether a transport-operator-specific tier or methodology is developed, which would directly affect how the workspace's own net zero commitments are externally assessed. Re-check: Q4 2025.

5. **Evaluate the Carbon Trust Supply Chain Standard as a supplier positioning tool**: The Supply Chain Standard is described as "the world's first independent certification for organisations that are measuring, managing and reducing greenhouse gas (CO2e) emissions in their supply chains." *Source: Carbon Trust, Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Trust. *Analytical inference:* Achieving this certification would allow the workspace to present verified supply chain emissions credentials to clients who are themselves Carbon Trust advisory clients — a direct competitive positioning move.

---
