prior.title: Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection - Agency Programs and Services Overview
prior.summary: Pennsylvania DEP agency homepage providing comprehensive directory of environmental protection programs including air quality, water management, waste, energy, mining, oil and gas operations, land remediation, radiation protection, and climate action initiatives.
prior.full_brief:
# Pennsylvania DEP Programs and Services — Freight Sustainability Intelligence Brief

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

Pennsylvania's Department of Environmental Protection (PA DEP) operates an active suite of freight-relevant grant, rebate, and incentive programs targeting diesel emission reduction across road, rail, and marine transport modes. Three distinct funding streams are currently active or recently concluded, each with different eligibility windows, funding sources, and operational coverage relevant to workspaces operating road-primary freight in Pennsylvania.

**PA-FIT Grant Program (Pennsylvania Freight Innovations in Transportation) — $23 million, application period closed November 2025**

FACT: "The Department of Environmental Protection (Department) announces an opportunity to apply for $23 million in grants offered through the Federal Fiscal Year 2023-2024 Pennsylvania Freight Innovations in Transportation (PA-FIT) Grant Program (Program)." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

FACT: "Most of the funding for this offering, approximately $21.6 million, is from the settlement of a lawsuit with Volkswagen Group—USA, relating to diesel vehicle emission violations, and is being used as the Commonwealth's voluntary match funding under the EPA's DERA State grant program." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

FACT: "A portion of funding has been made available for this opportunity through the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the Federal Diesel Emission Reduction Act (DERA) of 2010 (Pub.L. No. 111-364)." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

FACT: "Applications must be received by 11:59 p.m. on Friday, November 14, 2025." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

*Analytical inference:* The PA-FIT application window closed November 14, 2025. The DEP review and scoring phase is in progress as of the date of this brief. Award announcements have not been publicly confirmed in the sourced content. Workspaces that did not apply in this cycle should monitor for the next PA-FIT funding notice; the program's dual federal/VW settlement funding structure suggests future cycles are likely if EPA DERA appropriations and VW trust balances permit, but no future cycle has been announced in the source content.

**Alternative Fuels Incentive Grant (AFIG) — ~$5 million, 2026 Round 2 open through October 7, 2026**

FACT: "AFIG has approximately $5 million in funding available to school districts, municipalities, nonprofit organizations, and businesses in Pennsylvania that want to transition to cleaner fuel transportation." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

FACT: "The second acceptance period will end on October 7, 2026, at 12:00 PM." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

FACT: "Applicants will be eligible for a maximum grant award across all applications of $500,000. Individual application awards are still capped at $300,000." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

FACT: "Supported alternative fuels include electricity, compressed natural gas, liquefied natural gas, propane, hydrogen, hythane, biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, and other advanced biofuels." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

FACT: "Class 1 and 2 vehicle projects are eligible for a maximum award of $7,500 per vehicle. Class 1 and 2 vehicle projects must have a minimum of two vehicles." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

WINDOW CLOSING — October 7, 2026 is the AFIG Round 2 deadline. Workspaces operating qualifying Pennsylvania-based vehicle fleets have an active, funded application window now.

**Medium- and Heavy-Duty Vehicle Rebates (Driving PA Forward / VW Settlement)**

FACT: "The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) offers rebates for the replacement or repower of Class 4-8 local freight trucks and port drayage trucks, school buses, transit buses, and shuttle buses with new diesel, electric, or alternative fuel vehicles or technologies. Vehicles or engines being replaced or repowered must be scrapped in accordance with program guidelines. Two funding options are available based on fleet size." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

FACT: "This rebate program is funded by Pennsylvania's portion of the Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

*Specific rebate dollar amounts for Class 4-8 vehicles not available from primary sources as of June 2026.*

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

PA DEP is the administering agency across all three freight-relevant programs. Funding originates from two federal/legal sources:

1. **Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust** — the settlement fund from Volkswagen's diesel emission violations, allocated to Pennsylvania for clean transportation transitions.
2. **EPA Diesel Emission Reduction Act (DERA) Program** — federal program under Pub.L. No. 111-364, which PA DEP draws on as a state grantee and uses VW settlement funds as matching contribution.

FACT: "The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) administers the Pennsylvania State Clean Diesel Grant Program for diesel emission reduction projects. Projects are funded by Pennsylvania's portion of the Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Diesel Emission Reduction Act (DERA) Program." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

FACT: DEP's stated goal for the Driving PA Forward initiative: "Reducing emissions from diesel engines is one of the most significant air quality challenges facing the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. To help Pennsylvania meet its diesel emissions reduction goals, the Wolf Administration has developed new grant and rebate programs to improve air quality in Pennsylvania and drive transformation from older, polluting diesel engines to clean technologies." *Source: PA DEP – Driving PA Forward, PA DEP, current. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/air/driving-pa-forward

*Analytical inference:* DEP's program design favors applicants who can demonstrate Pennsylvania-domiciled operations, Pennsylvania-registered entities, and vehicles operating primarily within Pennsylvania. Workspaces with multi-state road operations should confirm what fraction of fleet operating time occurs in-state before investing application effort.

**Rail and Marine Freight — Additional Programs**

FACT: "The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) offers grants for the repower or replacement of ferries, tugboats, and freight switcher locomotives with any new U.S. Environmental Protection Agency or California Air and Resource Board-certified diesel, alternative fuel, or all-electric equivalent." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

FACT: "The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) offers grants for the replacement of airport ground support equipment, forklifts, and port cargo handling equipment with an all-electric equivalent." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with ground support, cargo handling, or intermodal transfer operations at Pennsylvania facilities, the off-road electric equipment and marine/rail programs represent additional cost-offset opportunities beyond road fleet programs. Current funding levels and open application windows for these specific sub-programs are not confirmed in the sourced content — direct DEP contact is the fastest path to status.

**AFIG Priority Hierarchy**

FACT: The AFIG program states "Priority will be given to businesses located in Pennsylvania; zero emission vehicle projects; light- and medium-duty fleet refueling infrastructure projects; renewable natural gas and infrastructure projects; projects located in environmental justice areas; and minority-, veteran-, or woman-owned businesses." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces whose PA operations are in designated environmental justice areas, or who qualify as minority-, veteran-, or woman-owned businesses, receive priority scoring. This is a structural competitive advantage in AFIG applications and should be documented in any application submission.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**PA-FIT (closed cycle — forward signal):**
The PA-FIT application window closed November 14, 2025. DEP is in the review and scoring phase.

FACT: "The Department will review and score applications after the application period closes." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

*Analytical inference:* Award timing for PA-FIT is not confirmed in the sourced content. Industry-standard DERA state grant cycles typically produce awards within 3–6 months of application close; an award announcement in Q1–Q2 2026 is consistent with that pattern, but this is an estimate only. The DEP contact for questions is ra-epvwmitigation@pa.gov or (717) 787-9495.

*Analytical inference:* Whether a future PA-FIT cycle (FFY 2025 or later) will be announced depends on remaining VW trust balances and EPA DERA appropriations. Workspaces should monitor the Driving PA Forward page at https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/air/driving-pa-forward for the next notice. No future cycle has been announced in the sourced content.

**AFIG (active window — hard deadline):**
The October 7, 2026 deadline is the active conversion trigger for AFIG Round 2. AFIG is an annual program with two rounds per year; Round 1 2026 closed April 1, 2026. The program has been running under Act 166 of 1992, suggesting it is a standing annual program rather than a time-limited trust-funded vehicle.

*Specific remaining balance available in AFIG Round 2 (after Round 1 awards) not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* Direct DEP inquiry to the AFIG program contacts is needed to confirm available Round 2 balance.

**VW Mitigation Trust depletion:**
*Analytical inference:* VW settlement trust funds are finite. As Pennsylvania deploys these funds across PA-FIT, Class 4-8 vehicle rebates, and other Driving PA Forward sub-programs, available balances diminish. Workspaces operating older diesel fleets in Pennsylvania should treat current program availability as a time-limited window rather than a permanent standing program. The trust's remaining balance and depletion timeline are not confirmed in the sourced content.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**For road-primary workspaces operating Class 4-8 diesel fleets in Pennsylvania:**

*Operational implication:* The AFIG and Class 4-8 rebate programs together create a co-funding pathway for fleet electrification or CNG/LNG transition. AFIG covers incremental vehicle purchase cost and refueling infrastructure; the Class 4-8 rebate covers replacement or repower costs. A workspace applying to both programs for the same fleet transition could stack funding, subject to DEP eligibility review.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether a single fleet transition project can simultaneously receive AFIG grant funds and VW-settlement-funded Class 4-8 rebates requires direct DEP confirmation — program stacking rules are not addressed in the sourced content.

**Weight exemption operational benefit:**

FACT: "A vehicle primarily powered by natural gas or electricity may exceed the state's gross vehicle weight limits by a weight equal to the difference between the weight of the vehicle with the natural gas tank and fueling system, or EV battery, and the weight of a comparable vehicle with a diesel tank and fueling system. Any NGV and EV may exceed the limits by up to 2,000 lbs." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA (Reference Title 35 Pennsylvania Statutes, Chapter 23B, Section 4604 and Title 75 Pennsylvania Statutes, Part IV, Chapter 49, Subchapter C, Section 4941)*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces moving high-density or time-sensitive freight (live events equipment, film and TV production loads, high-value automotive cargo) on Pennsylvania roads, the 2,000 lb. weight exemption for EV or NGV trucks directly offsets the payload penalty typically associated with battery weight, improving the commercial case for electric truck adoption beyond the grant cost offset alone.

**Alternative Fuel Tax Exemption — EV fleet cost structure:**

FACT: "Beginning January 1, 2025, EVs that are subject to the annual EV registration fee are exempt from the Alternative Fuels Tax." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA (Reference Senate Bill 656, 2024)*

*Operational implication:* For workspaces that have already transitioned or are transitioning Pennsylvania road assets to electric vehicles, the Alternative Fuels Tax exemption effective January 1, 2025 reduces ongoing operational cost. The magnitude of the saving depends on fleet size and mileage; current Alternative Fuels Tax rate not confirmed in the sourced content — confirm with DEP or Pennsylvania Department of Revenue.

**Hydrogen and Natural Gas Tax Credit — procurement consideration:**

FACT: "Businesses may receive a tax credit for the purchase of hydrogen or natural gas from a Regional Clean Hydrogen Hub in Pennsylvania. Tax credits may be equal to $0.81 per kilogram of hydrogen and $0.47 per one thousand cubic feet of natural gas purchased for use in manufacturing at a facility that is part of a Regional Clean Hydrogen Hub." *Source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center – Pennsylvania Laws & Incentives, U.S. DOE AFDC, current. https://afdc.energy.gov/laws/all?state=PA (Reference Title 72 Pennsylvania Statutes, Chapter 5, Article 17-L, Subarticle D)*

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with Pennsylvania facility operations that could qualify as part of a Regional Clean Hydrogen Hub supply chain, the $0.81/kg hydrogen tax credit is materially relevant to hydrogen fleet economics. Whether a freight forwarder or logistics operator qualifies as a "facility that is part of a Regional Clean Hydrogen Hub" requires Pennsylvania Department of Revenue and DEP confirmation.

**AFIG eligible cost categories — operational translation:**

FACT: AFIG grant funding covers: "Incremental costs related to retrofitting vehicles to operate on alternative fuels; Incremental costs to purchase alternative fuel vehicles; Cost to purchase and install the necessary fleet-refueling or home-refueling equipment for alternative fuel vehicles; Cost to perform research, training, development, and demonstration of new applications or next-phase technology related to alternative fuel vehicles." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

*Operational implication:* The AFIG's coverage of fleet-refueling infrastructure costs is significant: workspaces that operate or co-locate at owned or leased Pennsylvania facilities can use AFIG funds to install on-site CNG, LNG, or EV charging infrastructure, not only to purchase vehicles. This expands the program's relevance to workspaces that manage their own drayage or last-mile delivery assets from a fixed PA facility.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* Pennsylvania's three-stream incentive structure (AFIG, PA-FIT/DERA, VW Class 4-8 rebates) creates a cost-of-fleet-transition differential between competitors who actively pursue these programs and those who do not. A competitor that successfully stacks AFIG infrastructure funding with Class 4-8 rebates and the EV weight exemption will carry a structurally lower fleet transition cost basis than one absorbing the full incremental cost of electrification or alternative fuel adoption.

*Analytical inference:* The AFIG priority hierarchy explicitly scores zero emission vehicle projects, Pennsylvania-located businesses, and environmental justice area projects higher. Competitors who have already shifted to ZEV projects may receive preferential scoring in future rounds, accelerating their access advantage.

COMPETITIVE EDGE — For workspaces that have not yet applied to AFIG or engaged Driving PA Forward programs, the October 7, 2026 AFIG Round 2 window is an immediate competitive positioning opportunity: it is open now, and the gap between applying and not applying converts directly to fleet transition cost disadvantage.

*No named competitor positions or specific competitor program participation are available in the sourced content.*

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "Pennsylvania DEP operates active grant and rebate programs specifically for freight fleet diesel emission reduction, covering Class 4-8 trucks, alternative fuel vehicles, and refueling infrastructure. These programs are funded through the VW settlement and EPA's DERA program and are currently accepting applications."
- "The AFIG program has approximately $5 million available in 2026 Round 2, closing October 7, 2026, with individual awards up to $300,000 and total per-applicant caps of $500,000."
- "Pennsylvania provides a statutory 2,000 lb. weight exemption for EV and NGV trucks operating in-state — this directly offsets the payload penalty of battery weight in electric freight vehicles."
- "EV fleets in Pennsylvania have been exempt from the Alternative Fuels Tax since January 1, 2025."

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not claim specific rebate dollar amounts for Class 4-8 vehicles beyond what is confirmed in the sourced content — amounts require verification from the Driving PA Forward program page directly.
- Do not represent that PA-FIT will have a future cycle — no future cycle has been announced in the sourced content.
- Do not claim that AFIG and VW rebate programs can be stacked on the same project without DEP confirmation.
- Do not present any of the above as federal compliance obligations. These are voluntary incentive programs, not mandates.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- "What fraction of your Pennsylvania-operating fleet is pre-2010 diesel? That is the typical target population for DERA and VW-funded replacement programs."
- "Do any of your Pennsylvania operations fall in DEP-designated environmental justice areas? That is a priority scoring factor in AFIG."
- "Have you evaluated whether your facility qualifies for AFIG infrastructure funding to install on-site CNG or EV charging?"

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — AFIG Round 2 application (WINDOW CLOSING: October 7, 2026)**
Evaluate whether the workspace or its clients operate qualifying Pennsylvania-registered vehicle fleets or facilities. The AFIG program is open now. Applications go through the DEP eGrants system. The Round 2 deadline is October 7, 2026 at 12:00 PM. Maximum award per applicant is $500,000 across all applications; individual project cap is $300,000.

FACT: "All applications must be submitted via this online tool [eGrants Application System]." *Source: PA DEP AFIG Program Page, PA DEP, 2026. https://www.pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/grants-loans-rebates/alternative-fuels-incentive-grant

- Eligible applicants include corporations, LLCs, and partnerships registered in Pennsylvania — confirm entity registration status before applying.
- Download the 2026 AFIG Guidelines (PDF) from the DEP AFIG program page before preparing the application.

**Action 2 — PA-FIT award monitoring (MONITORING: re-check Q2 2026)**
The PA-FIT application window closed November 14, 2025. DEP is in the review and scoring phase. Monitor the Driving PA Forward page and the Pennsylvania Bulletin for award announcements. Contact ra-epvwmitigation@pa.gov or (717) 787-9495 to confirm whether awards have been issued and when the next cycle will open.

FACT: "The PA-FIT program guidelines and application instructions are available on the Department's Driving PA Forward webpage at pa.gov/agencies/dep/programs-and-services/air/driving-pa-forward." *Source: Pennsylvania Bulletin, Vol. 55, No. 34 [55 Pa.B. 6145], PA DEP / PA Bulletin, August 23, 2025. https://www.pacodeandbulletin.gov/Display/pabull?file=/secure/pabulletin/data/vol55/55-34/1144.html

**Action 3 — Class 4-8 Rebate eligibility check (MONITORING)**
If the workspace or its clients operate Class 4-8 diesel trucks, port drayage trucks, or buses on Pennsylvania routes, contact DEP through the Driving PA Forward portal to confirm current rebate availability, amounts, and application windows. Rebate amounts are not confirmed in the sourced content and require direct DEP engagement.

**Action 4 — Weight exemption documentation**
For any Pennsylvania EV or NGV truck assets already in operation, confirm that operating permits and weigh station documentation reflect the statutory 2,000 lb. exemption. This is an in-force legal provision as of the current date.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Specific documentation requirements to claim the NGV/EV weight exemption under Title 35 Pennsylvania Statutes, Chapter 23B, Section 4604 and Title 75 Pennsylvania Statutes, Part IV, Chapter 49, Subchapter C, Section 4941 should be confirmed with Pennsylvania DOT or counsel.

**Action 5 — Rail, marine, and off-road equipment programs (MONITORING)**
For workspaces with Pennsylvania-based freight switcher, tugboat, ferry, or cargo handling equipment operations, contact DEP directly to confirm current funding availability under the marine/rail and off-road electric equipment grant programs. Current open windows and funding amounts for these sub-programs are not confirmed in the sourced content.

---

prior.title: Florida Department of Transportation Freight and Rail Office - Rail System Development and Multimodal Freight Programs
prior.summary: The Florida Department of Transportation's Freight and Rail Office (FRO) is responsible for developing and implementing Florida's rail system for both passenger and freight operations, managing rail safety, project development, and implementing the Florida Rail System Plan. The FRO also oversees the Freight Mobility and Trade Plan and Strategic Intermodal System (SIS) implementation, working with stakeholders including rail carriers, ports, and federal agencies to improve freight movement and commerce throughout the state.
prior.full_brief:
# Florida Department of Transportation Freight and Rail Office — Rail System Development and Multimodal Freight Programs

**Framework Brief | Operations Profile**
*Prepared for workspaces operating as freight forwarders and multimodal logistics providers across Florida and southeastern U.S. trade corridors*
*Generation date: 2025-07-14*

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief profiles the Florida Department of Transportation (FDOT) Freight and Rail Office (FRO) — its mandate, planning instruments, safety programs, and the federal compliance framework that governs its grant eligibility and rail plan acceptance cycle. It is anchored to workspaces operating as freight forwarders, importers, or multimodal logistics providers whose cargo moves through Florida's seaports, intermodal logistics centers, and highway-rail corridors. The document synthesises six sourced blocks: the FRO landing page, the FRO Divisions page, the Rail System Plan overview, the Freight Mobility and Trade Plan background, the Florida Rail Safety Coalition program page, and the FRA State Rail Plan Guidance page.

**Conventions:** All claims are labeled FACT, ANALYSIS, or LEGAL. Binding-obligation language appears only where verbatim-quoted from a source or under a *Legal Confirmation Required:* callout. The workspace is referenced generically by role; no company or individual is named.

---

# What This Program Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

MONITORING

The FDOT Freight and Rail Office is the Florida state agency unit responsible for freight and rail planning, safety, investment programming, and multimodal coordination across all transport modes. FACT: "The FRO is responsible for assisting in the development of Florida's rail system, both passenger and freight, and championing Florida's multimodal freight programs. The team develops and implements the Florida Rail System Plan, rail safety, rail project development/management, rail research, and Strategic Intermodal System (SIS) implementation. The FRO also develops and implements the Freight Mobility and Trade Plan, and provides leadership, policy guidance, and technical assistance to ensure efficient and effective freight movement throughout the state." *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces routing freight through Florida's Class I railroad network (CSX, Norfolk Southern, Florida East Coast Railway), through Florida seaports, or through intermodal logistics centers, FRO's investment programming and regulatory posture directly shape the infrastructure capacity, crossing safety profile, and multimodal access conditions those workspaces depend on. FRO's decisions on priority crossing improvements, last-mile truck access, and SIS investments are forward-visible through its published plans — giving workspaces lead time on network changes.

*Analytical inference:* The FRO's rail carrier network — which FACT includes "CSX, Florida East Coast Railway, Norfolk Southern, SunRail, Amtrak, Tri-Rail, Bay Line Railroad, Florida Central Railroad, Florida Gulf and Atlantic Railroad, Florida Midland Railroad, Florida Northern Railroad, Georgia and Florida Railway, Jacksonville Port Terminal Railroad, Port Manatee Railroad, Port of Palm Beach Railroad, Seminole Gulf Railway, South Central Florida Express, AN Railway, First Coast Railroad" — *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail — represents the operational landscape within which workspaces moving air-priority or road-secondary freight encounter rail crossings, intermodal transfer points, and port-rail interfaces.

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

No immediate compliance deadlines are imposed on freight forwarding workspaces by the FRO's planning programs directly. The items below are positioning and monitoring actions tied to active programs and open legislative developments.

**MONITORING — Florida Rail Safety Coalition (FRSC) engagement window is open.** FACT: The FRSC is described as "FDOT's new effort to help engage and collaborate with technical stakeholders" and consists of "rail industry partners at the local, state, and federal levels." *Source: FDOT Florida Rail Safety Coalition Program Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/programs/florida-rail-safety-coalition Membership applications are publicly available. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces with freight moving across Florida highway-rail grade crossings — particularly those managing high-frequency road-secondary drayage to port or intermodal facilities — face operational exposure at crossings; participation in the FRSC coalition provides visibility into upcoming countermeasure investments and crossing improvement prioritisation before they are finalised.

**MONITORING — CS/SB 1310 (2026 legislative session) — advanced detection technology study.** FACT: A 2026 Florida Senate bill analysis cites FDOT and the FRSC in connection with "a statewide advanced detection technology study at grade crossings." *Source: Florida Senate – Bill Analysis CS/SB 1310 (Railroad Crossing Safety Study, 2026), Florida Legislature, 2026. https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/1310/Analyses/2026s01310.tr.PDF *Analytical inference:* If enacted and study-funded, this legislation would produce a statewide assessment of grade-crossing detection technology deployment — a finding that could accelerate FDOT investment in crossing improvements on corridors used by workspaces operating drayage legs to Florida seaports and intermodal logistics centers. Re-check: monitor for bill passage or study publication, estimated window Q3–Q4 2026.

**MONITORING — Rail System Plan update cycle.** FACT: Under Section 11315 of the FAST Act, "a State-approved rail plan be submitted every four years for acceptance by FRA." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance The current Florida Rail System Plan is the 2023 FRA-approved document. *Analytical inference:* The next update cycle is expected to open approximately 2027. Workspaces with infrastructure access or investment interests — particularly those dependent on SIS corridors or port-rail connectivity — should engage FDOT outreach phases ahead of that cycle, as the plan governs which freight rail improvements and investments receive state sponsorship.

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The FRO's programs do not directly impose regulatory obligations on freight forwarding workspaces in the same manner as a shipper regulation or carrier standard. *Analytical inference:* The workspace's relevant position is as a downstream beneficiary and operational stakeholder in FRO's infrastructure investment decisions — specifically:

- **As a freight forwarder coordinating intermodal moves through Florida seaports or ILCs:** The FRO's motor carrier facilitation work — FACT: "facilitate efficient truck flows on highways, ensure access to freight hubs through the 'last mile', address intermodal access between trucks and other modes" *Source: FDOT FRO Divisions Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/divisions.shtm — directly affects drayage access and intermodal handoff efficiency on the road-secondary legs the workspace coordinates.

- **As a forwarder tendering freight to Class I and short-line rail carriers in Florida:** FRO's priority crossing signal and capacity improvement funding shapes the reliability and safety profile of those rail corridors. FACT: "With RAIL, we perform rail safety inspections, fund priority crossing signal improvements and capacity improvements, eliminate corridor hazards, conduct technology evaluation and research, provide crossing inventory and quality assurance, and consistently seek out projects to promote safety and awareness." *Source: FDOT FRO Divisions Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/divisions.shtm

- **As a stakeholder in freight plan development:** The FMTP is the vehicle through which Florida prioritises multimodal freight investments. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any FRO program participation, grant matching, or plan comment period creates compliance obligations for non-carrier logistics intermediaries requires counsel review.

---

# Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

*No content for this section as of 2025-07-14: the sourced blocks do not contain specific industrial electricity rates, diesel prices, port handling costs, drayage rates, or other operational cost benchmarks for Florida freight corridors. These figures are available from FDOT's FreightMovesFlorida dashboard (https://FreightMovesFlorida.com) and the FDOT Source Book — Rail, but those documents are referenced in the source blocks without quantitative content being reproduced. Specific [cost figures] not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

---

# Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

*No content for this section as of 2025-07-14: the sourced blocks do not contain feasibility assessments for on-site energy, equipment, or material sourcing decisions. This section is omitted under the integrity rule.*

---

# Substantive Program Requirements and Planning Architecture

## Florida Rail System Plan (2023)

FACT: "The Florida Department of Transportation (FDOT) develops the Rail System Plan to guide the state's rail freight and passenger transportation planning activities and project development plans." *Source: FDOT Rail System Plan Overview Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plans/railplan

FACT: The plan "complies with Section 341.302(3), Florida Statutes, which require an identification of priorities, programs, and funding levels required to meet statewide and regional goals." *Source: FDOT Rail System Plan Overview Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plans/railplan

FACT: The plan "meets the requirements embodied in the Federal Passenger Rail Investment and Improvement Act (PRIIA), as amended by the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act (FAST Act) of 2015, and in the Federal Railroad Administration's (FRA) State Rail Plan Guidance." *Source: FDOT Rail System Plan Overview Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plans/railplan

FACT: The plan "describes the state's existing rail network, its challenges and opportunities, and rail-related economic and socio-environmental impacts of each rail mode" and "includes Florida's Rail Vision and Supporting Goals, along with both proposed publicly sponsored short and long-range capital improvements and policy recommendations." *Source: FDOT Rail System Plan Overview Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plans/railplan

The 2023 Rail System Plan is FRA-accepted and comprises: Executive Summary, six substantive chapters (Role of Rail, Existing System, Proposed Passenger Rail Improvements, Proposed Freight Rail Improvements, State Rail Service and Investment Program, Coordination and Outreach), and ten appendices including commodity flows and forecasts, economic impacts of freight and passenger rail, seaport and waterways rail profile, and an FRA checklist. *Source: FDOT Rail System Plan Overview Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plans/railplan

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces routing cargo through Florida seaports, Appendix J (Seaport and Waterways Rail Profile) and Appendix G (Commodity Flows and Forecast) are the highest-value plan documents for understanding where rail-served port capacity investments are prioritised — and where gaps exist that affect drayage dependency and intermodal access timelines.

## Federal Compliance Framework: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance

FACT: "Under 49 U.S. Code Chapter 227, each State, at its own election, may prepare and maintain a State Rail Plan as a means for: Setting forth State policy involving freight and passenger rail transportation, including commuter rail operations, in the State; Establishing the period covered by the State Rail Plan; Presenting priorities and strategies to enhance rail service in the State that benefits the public; Serving as the basis for Federal and State rail investments within the State." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

FACT: "While there is no Federal statutory requirement that each State prepare a State Rail Plan, 49 U.S. Code Chapter 227 encourages each State to develop an FRA-accepted State Rail Plan to support the State's involvement in rail policy, planning, and development." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

FACT: "The guidance explains the process for developing State Rail Plans, including minimum content requirements, a standardized format, and FRA's review and acceptance process." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

FACT: "Section 11315 of the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act of 2015 (FAST Act) made subsequent changes to State Rail Plan requirements, including specifying that a State-approved rail plan be submitted every four years for acceptance by FRA, rather than every five years as defined under the Passenger Rail Investment and Improvement Act of 2008 (PRIIA)." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

FACT: "There is no set schedule for States to submit rail plans to FRA for review and acceptance because the preparation and maintenance of a State Rail Plan is at the discretion of each State, and if a State Rail Plan exists, four-year update cycles vary." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

FACT: "Additionally, rail projects are not required to be included in State Rail Plans to be eligible for Federal funding under most competitive discretionary grant programs." *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance

*Analytical inference:* The FRA acceptance of Florida's 2023 Rail System Plan activates Florida's eligibility for federal grant programs tied to plan compliance, including competitive FRA discretionary grants for freight rail improvements. Workspaces whose operations depend on infrastructure funded through these grants — crossing improvements, capacity additions, intermodal access projects — benefit indirectly from Florida's plan acceptance status.

## Freight Mobility and Trade Plan (FMTP)

FACT: "The Freight Mobility and Trade Plan (FMTP) is a comprehensive plan that identifies freight transportation facilities critical to the state's economic growth and guides multimodal freight investments in the state." *Source: FDOT Freight Mobility and Trade Plan Background, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plandevel/freight-mobility-and-trade-plan/background

FACT: "The 2012 HB 599 directed FDOT to develop this plan, and the federal re-authorization bill, MAP-21, also encouraged the creation of state freight plans." *Source: FDOT Freight Mobility and Trade Plan Background, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plandevel/freight-mobility-and-trade-plan/background

FACT: "Approved on April 27, 2012, by signature of Governor Scott, Florida House Bill 599 required the Florida Department of Transportation to develop the Freight Mobility and Trade Plan to assist in making freight mobility investments that contribute to the economic growth of the state." *Source: FDOT Freight Mobility and Trade Plan Background, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plandevel/freight-mobility-and-trade-plan/background

The FMTP's legislatively stated goals include: increasing the flow of domestic and international trade through seaports and airports; increasing development of intermodal logistics centers; increasing development of manufacturing industries through transport investment; and increasing implementation of CNG, LNG, and propane energy policies. *Source: FDOT Freight Mobility and Trade Plan Background, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plandevel/freight-mobility-and-trade-plan/background

FACT: The FAST Act "requires each State to develop a State freight plan" to receive funding under the National Highway Freight Program (23 U.S.C. 167), and that state freight plans "must, among other requirements: Cover a five-year forecast period, Be fiscally constrained, Include a 'freight investment plan' with a list of priority projects, and Describe how the State will invest and match its National Highway Freight Program funds." *Source: FDOT Freight Mobility and Trade Plan Background, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/plandevel/freight-mobility-and-trade-plan/background

*Analytical inference:* The FMTP's explicit goal of recapturing cargo currently shipped through out-of-state seaports and airports is a commercial signal for workspaces considering Florida routing versus Gulf Coast or East Coast alternative ports. FDOT investment decisions shaped by the FMTP — particularly those targeting intermodal logistics center development and last-mile access — will affect the competitive cost and capacity position of Florida-routed moves relative to alternatives.

*Analytical inference:* The ILC development goal (increasing intermodal logistics centers with strategies targeting "empty backhaul trucking and rail market") is operationally relevant for workspaces with imbalanced freight flows into or out of Florida — particularly those managing return-leg repositioning costs on road-secondary drayage.

## Florida Rail Safety Coalition (FRSC)

FACT: "The Florida Rail Safety Coalition is FDOT's new effort to help engage and collaborate with technical stakeholders. The FRSC consists of rail industry partners at the local, state, and federal levels, all dedicated to ensuring the safety of our communities around trains and tracks." *Source: FDOT Florida Rail Safety Coalition Program Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/programs/florida-rail-safety-coalition

FACT: FRSC representatives include "Subject Matter Experts, FDOT team members, Public and Private Rail Organizations (Freight and Passenger), Rail Advocacy Groups, Rail Safety Inspectors, Law Enforcement, Universities, Human Services Agencies, and Local Communities." *Source: FDOT Florida Rail Safety Coalition Program Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/programs/florida-rail-safety-coalition

FACT: The FDOT homepage describes the FRSC as "FDOT's First-in-the-Nation Rail Safety Coalition." *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail

*Analytical inference:* The FRSC's design as a multi-stakeholder technical coalition — spanning private freight and passenger rail operators, law enforcement, universities, and local communities — positions it as the primary forum through which Florida's grade-crossing safety investment priorities will be shaped. For workspaces with drayage operations crossing high-frequency rail corridors in Florida urban areas, early visibility into FRSC's priority crossing countermeasure discussions reduces operational risk from unplanned crossing closures or remediation-driven route changes.

The 2026 SB 1310 bill analysis connects the FRSC and FDOT rail crossing programs to a statewide study of advanced detection technology at grade crossings. *Source: Florida Senate – Bill Analysis CS/SB 1310 (Railroad Crossing Safety Study, 2026), Florida Legislature, 2026. https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/1310/Analyses/2026s01310.tr.PDF *Analytical inference:* If this study proceeds, its findings will feed directly into FRO's crossing improvement priority queue — a queue that shapes which road-rail interface points receive signal upgrades and which remain in current condition on the workspace's drayage corridors.

## FRO Mission and Operational Divisions Summary

FACT: "FRO enhances Florida's economy and communities. The FRO aims for success through teamwork and efficiency by means of removing institutional, infrastructure, and funding bottlenecks to build a well-connected, reliable, and safe freight and rail network." *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail

FACT: The FRO is "located within the Office of Modal Development in the Florida Department of Transportation (FDOT)" and "represents the RAIL and MOTOR CARRIER modes." *Source: FDOT FRO Divisions Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/divisions.shtm

FACT: "The FRO is located in FDOT's Central Office in Tallahassee. This office is primarily dedicated to policy/procedure/plan development, quality assurance, and technical assistance. Throughout the state, the FDOT District offices provide integral support with day-to-day operations, and rail inspectors perform safety inspections to ensure compliance." *Source: FDOT FRO Divisions Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/divisions.shtm

FACT: FRO guides and implements freight and rail investments through "leveraging funding opportunities and implementing effective countermeasures that improve rail and motor carrier safety; enhancing multimodal networks that drive commerce, bringing freight into your everyday life; and researching emerging technologies and forging partnerships in a quickly evolving industry, supporting innovation." *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail

---

# Cross-Regional Strategic Implications

*Analytical inference:* Florida's dual planning architecture — the Rail System Plan governing passenger and freight rail investment and the FMTP governing multimodal freight investment — creates two parallel channels through which infrastructure improvements affecting workspace operations are authorised and funded. Understanding which plan governs which corridor is operationally relevant: a crossing improvement on a Class I freight rail corridor is funded and tracked through a different plan and federal compliance mechanism than a last-mile truck access project at an intermodal logistics center.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces routing cargo on the Americas trade lane through Florida seaports, the FMTP's explicit goal of recapturing cargo from out-of-state ports — and the SIS investments that underpin it — signals that FDOT is actively working to improve the cost and capacity competitiveness of Florida-routed moves. This is a medium-term corridor positioning signal, not an immediate cost change.

*Analytical inference:* The FRA's four-year State Rail Plan update cycle (next expected approximately 2027) creates a forward window for workspaces to surface freight infrastructure needs — particularly port-rail connectivity and ILC access — through FDOT's outreach phases. Engagement ahead of the update cycle, not during it, is the effective timing for influencing investment priorities.

---

# Competitive Positioning in the Region

*Analytical inference:* Freight forwarders and logistics intermediaries that track FRO's investment pipeline through the FMTP and Rail System Plan can anticipate infrastructure improvements — and their timing — earlier than competitors relying on ad-hoc market intelligence. This is particularly material for Florida seaport access, where rail-served capacity improvements affect drayage cost, transit time, and service reliability for ocean-primary and air-primary cargo moved through the state.

*Analytical inference:* FRSC membership is open (membership applications are publicly available per the FRSC program page). *Source: FDOT Florida Rail Safety Coalition Program Page, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://www.fdot.gov/rail/programs/florida-rail-safety-coalition Participation provides early access to crossing improvement decisions before they are announced as construction projects — a routing intelligence advantage for workspaces with high-frequency drayage legs across Florida's rail network.

*No content for the named-competitor positioning sub-section as of 2025-07-14: no sourced reporting identifies specific freight forwarding or logistics competitors and their engagement status with FRO programs. This sub-section is omitted under the integrity rule.*

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- Florida maintains an FRA-accepted Rail System Plan (2023) and a legislatively mandated Freight Mobility and Trade Plan, both of which guide public investment in freight infrastructure across seaports, rail corridors, and intermodal logistics centers. The workspace monitors these plans to anticipate infrastructure changes affecting its Florida-routed moves.
- Florida has established the first-in-nation Florida Rail Safety Coalition — a multi-stakeholder technical body spanning private and public rail operators, law enforcement, and universities — focused on grade-crossing and trespassing safety. Crossing improvement investments flowing from this coalition affect road-secondary drayage reliability.
- A 2026 Florida Senate bill proposes a statewide study of advanced detection technology at grade crossings; if enacted, this study will feed into FDOT's crossing improvement priority queue over the next 1–2 years.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not characterise any FRO program as imposing direct compliance obligations on freight forwarders without legal confirmation. FRO programs primarily govern carrier, infrastructure, and state agency activities.
- Do not claim to have read the full 2023 Rail System Plan or its appendices unless the workspace has actually reviewed the specific chapters relevant to a client's corridor or cargo type.
- Do not represent FMTP investment goals as confirmed project timelines. The plan identifies priorities; project delivery depends on funding appropriation and procurement.

---

# Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus

**MONITORING — CS/SB 1310 Grade Crossing Detection Study (2026 legislative session)**
FACT: The 2026 Florida Senate bill analysis cites a requirement for FDOT to conduct a statewide advanced detection technology study at grade crossings. *Source: Florida Senate – Bill Analysis CS/SB 1310, Florida Legislature, 2026. https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2026/1310/Analyses/2026s01310.tr.PDF Status: active 2026 legislative session. *Analytical inference:* Enactment would direct FDOT resources toward a statewide technology evaluation that could accelerate crossing signal upgrade prioritisation — relevant for drayage routing on Florida corridors. Re-check window: Q3–Q4 2026 (expected bill resolution and, if enacted, study scoping).

**MONITORING — Rail System Plan four-year update cycle**
FACT: The FAST Act specifies a four-year update submission cycle for FRA-accepted State Rail Plans. *Source: FRA State Rail Plan Guidance, Federal Railroad Administration, last updated October 31, 2023. https://railroads.dot.gov/rail-network-development/planning/systems-planning/state-rail-plan-guidance The current plan is the 2023 FRA-accepted document. *Analytical inference:* The next update is expected to begin development approximately 2026–2027 and be submitted for FRA acceptance by approximately 2027. FDOT outreach phases typically precede plan completion by 12–18 months — placing the participation window at approximately 2026. Re-check window: Q1 2026 for FDOT announcement of outreach activities.

**MONITORING — FASP 2043 (Florida Aviation System Plan)**
FACT: The FDOT website references "FASP 2043 (Coming Soon)" as an upcoming planning document. *Source: FDOT Freight and Rail Office Homepage, FDOT, [accessed 2025-07-14]. https://fdot.gov/rail *Analytical inference:* For workspaces with air-primary cargo through Florida, the FASP 2043 will be the companion planning document to the Rail System Plan and FMTP — relevant to understanding FDOT's aviation freight infrastructure priorities. Status and publication date: not yet available from sourced content. Re-check window: monitor FDOT publications channel.

---

prior.title: Louisiana State Freight Plan 2024 and Commodity Flow Dashboard
prior.summary: Louisiana Department of Transportation and Development (LADOTD) provides a comprehensive state freight plan and commodity flow analysis tool designed to support economic growth through efficient multimodal goods movement. The plan offers strategic guidance on freight infrastructure, supply chain resilience, and equitable transportation solutions with detailed commodity-level data by parish and region.
prior.full_brief:
# Louisiana State Freight Plan 2024 and Commodity Flow Dashboard

## Operations Profile

---

# 1. Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

**Note on section scope:** The Louisiana State Freight Plan 2024 and its associated fact sheets are planning and policy documents, not cost-rate publications. This section reports the freight-volume and network characteristics that establish Louisiana's operational baseline. Specific unit-cost figures (port handling rates, drayage rates, diesel prices) are not contained in the source blocks provided and are noted as explicit gaps below.

**Network scale and throughput — waterborne freight:**
The state's inland waterway system handles 238.7 million tons annually across 41 active ports. *Source: LADOTD Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, LADOTD, 2025-03-21. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Ports-Waterways_2025-03-21.pdf.

**Network scale — highway freight:**
The Louisiana highway freight network includes mileage designated within the National Highway Freight Network (NHFN); specific NHFN mileage figures, top commodities by tonnage and value, and 2021–2050 truck-volume growth projections appear in the LADOTD Highway Freight Fact Sheet (Winter 2023/2024). *Source: LADOTD Highway Freight Fact Sheet, LADOTD, Winter 2023/2024. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Highway-Freight_2024.pdf.

*Specific [unit cost figures — port handling, drayage, diesel/SAF, labor rates] not available from primary sources as of 2026-07-14.*

**Federal monitoring status:**
The FHWA monitors and approves all State Freight Plan updates to ensure compliance with current federal surface transportation laws. *Source: FHWA Freight Professional Capacity Building Program — All State Freight Plans, FHWA, accessed 2026-07-14. https://ops.fhwa.dot.gov/freight/fpcb/toolkit/allplans.aspx.

---

# 2. Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

**Multimodal access — general posture:**
The Louisiana State Freight Plan provides the state with a blueprint for facilitating continued economic growth through a comprehensive, multimodal strategy for addressing freight transportation needs and moving goods efficiently and safely throughout the state. *Source: Louisiana Statewide Transportation Plan — Freight Mobility Plan Page, LADOTD, 2026. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/.

**Modal-shift feasibility (inland waterway to/from road):**
The Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet references modal-shift strategies as a component of the plan's freight action framework. *Source: LADOTD Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, LADOTD, 2025-03-21. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Ports-Waterways_2025-03-21.pdf.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces moving high-value, time-sensitive cargo (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV) predominantly by air, Louisiana's primary operational advantage lies in its waterway and port network — relevant to inbound feedstock or outbound bulk repositioning rather than to primary air movements. Modal-shift strategies identified in the plan are more likely to affect ocean and drayage sub-legs than the air-primary trunk haul.

**LA Waters Platform — commodity flow data tool:**
The Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet references development of the LA Waters Platform as part of the state's data infrastructure for waterborne freight visibility. *Source: LADOTD Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, LADOTD, 2025-03-21. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Ports-Waterways_2025-03-21.pdf.

*Specific [LA Waters Platform launch date, data fields, API or access protocol] not available from primary sources as of 2026-07-14.*

**On-site solar, BESS, and ZEV feasibility:**
*No content for this subsection as of 2026-07-14: the source blocks do not contain energy infrastructure, on-site generation, or vehicle electrification feasibility data for Louisiana freight facilities.*

---

# 3. Cost Comparison Against Alternatives

*No content for this section as of 2026-07-14: the source blocks provide planning-document and network-characterization content but do not contain unit-cost comparisons (manual vs. automated handling, on-grid vs. on-site solar, owned vs. leased capacity, in-region vs. imported). No cost breakeven or payback figures are available from these sources.*

---

# 4. Cross-Regional Strategic Implications MONITORING

**What the plan is designed to do:**
The Louisiana State Freight Plan is intended to: meet federal guidelines and support future planning, policies, and investment in Louisiana; support the business case for freight system investments; promote safe, efficient, and resilient goods mobility and supply chain support in Louisiana; develop data to support local and regional mobility planning; and reflect input from private sector freight stakeholders. *Source: Louisiana Statewide Transportation Plan — Freight Mobility Plan Page, LADOTD, 2026. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/.

**Federal compliance requirement:**
The FHWA monitors and approves all State Freight Plan updates to ensure compliance with current federal surface transportation laws. Louisiana's 2024 plan sits within this federal compliance framework. *Source: FHWA Freight Professional Capacity Building Program — All State Freight Plans, FHWA, accessed 2026-07-14. https://ops.fhwa.dot.gov/freight/fpcb/toolkit/allplans.aspx.

**FAST Act / IIJA federal guidance layer:**
A Federal Register notice dated February 24, 2026 (document 2026-03648) addresses guidance on multimodal state freight plans and state freight advisory committees under the FAST Act and IIJA framework. The content of that document was inaccessible due to automated-access restrictions on FederalRegister.gov at time of generation; the existence and date of the notice are confirmed. *Source: Federal Register Notice 2026-03648, FHWA/USDOT, 2026-02-24. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the February 2026 FHWA guidance imposes new minimum content requirements on Louisiana's already-published 2024 plan — triggering an update obligation before the next plan cycle — requires review of the full Federal Register document and legal assessment.

*Analytical inference:* Louisiana's combination of 41 active ports, 238.7 million tons of inland waterway throughput, and a federally compliant multimodal freight plan positions the state as a strategically significant node on the Gulf Coast corridor. Workspaces routing ocean freight through Gulf ports (Houston, New Orleans, Lake Charles, Baton Rouge) should monitor the commodity flow data released under the 2024 plan and the LA Waters Platform to identify capacity constraints and priority investment corridors before competitors do.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with trade lanes spanning the Americas, Louisiana's waterway network is a high-throughput alternative or feedstock lane for ocean consolidations. The plan's stated goal of developing data to support local and regional mobility planning suggests commodity-flow data granularity will increase as the plan's implementation progresses — creating a window to embed that data into routing decisions before it becomes widely commoditized.

---

# 5. Competitive Positioning in the Region

*No content for this section as of 2026-07-14: the source blocks do not contain named competitor activity, operator-level positioning data, or sourced reporting on specific freight operators in Louisiana. Emitting named competitors or their positions without sourced reporting would violate the integrity rule.*

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say, grounded in sourced facts:**

1. "Louisiana's 2024 State Freight Plan is a federally compliant, FHWA-monitored multimodal planning document covering road, port, and inland waterway infrastructure — it is a blueprint, not a binding regulatory instrument, and does not directly impose compliance obligations on freight forwarders."

2. "Louisiana handles 238.7 million tons on its inland waterways annually across 41 active ports — one of the highest-throughput waterway networks in the United States." (*Source: LADOTD Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, 2025.*)

3. "The state is developing the LA Waters Platform as a commodity-flow visibility tool. When live, it may provide data on waterborne freight movements that could inform routing and capacity-planning decisions."

4. "The 2024 plan explicitly includes private sector freight stakeholder input as a design principle — meaning the commodity flow data and investment priorities it surfaces reflect industry-identified needs, not solely government priorities."

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not represent the State Freight Plan as a compliance mandate on private operators. It is a planning document that informs state investment priorities, not a regulatory instrument imposing direct obligations on forwarders or shippers.
- Do not quote specific cost figures, growth projections, or NHFN mileage statistics in client meetings without first retrieving and reading the specific fact sheet pages directly — the source blocks confirm these figures exist in the LADOTD Highway Freight Fact Sheet (Winter 2023/2024) and Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet (2025), but the verbatim numbers were not available in the provided source content for this generation.
- Do not describe the LA Waters Platform as operational — its development status is referenced but its launch date is not confirmed in the provided sources.

**Questions worth posing to clients routing through Louisiana:**
- Which commodities are you moving through Gulf Coast ports, and does the 2024 plan's investment priority list (infrastructure needs assessment) affect your terminal access or drayage corridors?
- Have you reviewed the commodity flow data from the plan to identify whether your cargo verticals are reflected in Louisiana's priority investment categories?

---

# 7. Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus MONITORING

**Federal guidance update — February 2026:**
A Federal Register notice (2026-03648, dated 2026-02-24) was issued by FHWA addressing guidance on multimodal state freight plans and state freight advisory committees. This notice post-dates Louisiana's 2024 plan. The full content was inaccessible at generation time due to access restrictions. *Source: Federal Register Notice 2026-03648, FHWA/USDOT, 2026-02-24. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees.

*Analytical inference:* If the February 2026 federal guidance introduces new minimum content requirements for state freight plans under IIJA, Louisiana may be required to update or supplement its 2024 plan before the next scheduled plan cycle. The standard FHWA plan update cycle is typically every 5 years, suggesting the next Louisiana plan would ordinarily be due around 2029, but new federal guidance could compress that timeline.

**Re-check window:** Retrieve and review the full text of Federal Register Notice 2026-03648 within 30 days (target: August 2026) to assess whether Louisiana's 2024 plan requires supplementation and whether any new freight advisory committee obligations affect stakeholder engagement timelines.

**LA Waters Platform — development status:**
The LA Waters Platform is referenced as in development in the Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet (2025 update). No launch date is confirmed in the provided sources.

*Analytical inference:* The platform's launch will mark a step-change in publicly available commodity-flow visibility for Louisiana waterborne freight. Workspaces that establish data relationships with LADOTD before platform launch may gain earlier access to routing and capacity intelligence than competitors who wait for commercial data vendors to ingest and resell the same data.

**Re-check window:** Monitor LADOTD and the Louisiana Statewide Transportation Plan portal (https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/) quarterly for LA Waters Platform launch announcements.

---

# 8. Sources

|---|--------|------|-----|
| 1 | Louisiana Statewide Transportation Plan — Freight Mobility Plan Page, LADOTD, 2026 | Regulator planning portal (Tier 2) | https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/ |
| 2 | FHWA Freight Professional Capacity Building Program — All State Freight Plans, FHWA, accessed 2026-07-14 | Federal agency repository (Tier 2) | https://ops.fhwa.dot.gov/freight/fpcb/toolkit/allplans.aspx |
| 3 | Federal Register Notice 2026-03648 — Guidance on Multimodal State Freight Plans and State Freight Advisory Committees, FHWA/USDOT, 2026-02-24 | Federal Register notice (Tier 1 — content inaccessible at generation time; existence confirmed) | https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees |
| 4 | LADOTD Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, LADOTD, 2025-03-21 | State agency modal fact sheet (Tier 2) — cited for 238.7M ton figure, 41 ports, modal-shift strategies, LA Waters Platform; content confirmed in source block description; specific verbatim data spans not in provided source block text | https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Ports-Waterways_2025-03-21.pdf |
| 5 | LADOTD Highway Freight Fact Sheet (Winter 2023/2024), LADOTD | State agency modal fact sheet (Tier 2) — cited for NHFN mileage, truck safety stats, growth projections; content confirmed in source block description; verbatim data spans not in provided source block text | https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Highway-Freight_2024.pdf |
| 6 | 2024 Louisiana State Freight Plan (Full PDF), LADOTD | Primary state planning document (Tier 2) — primary source for the plan; PDF content not in provided source blocks | https://dotd.la.gov/media/qquhsdst/2024-louisiana-state-freight-plan.pdf |

---

prior.title: Canada Clean Fuel Regulations Phase 2
prior.summary: Expands clean fuel requirements to include marine and aviation sectors, with specific compliance obligations for freight operators. Establishes carbon intensity reduction targets of 15% by 2030 for all transportation fuels used in commercial freight operations.

prior.title: Air Cargo Market Dynamics: Elevated Rates, Geopolitical Impacts, and Capacity Adjustments (May 2026)
prior.summary: Air cargo market shows sustained elevated rates despite ceasefire announcements, driven by Middle East conflict impacts and currency pressures. Major carriers (Emirates SkyCargo, Lufthansa Cargo, Cathay Cargo) are adjusting capacity and routing strategies. Market participants are implementing risk management tools (war risk insurance) while infrastructure investments continue. Regional variations noted with different impacts on IAG Cargo versus Lufthansa Cargo.

prior.title: Florida DEP Notice of Proposed Rulemaking: Revisions to Chapter 62-210 Stationary Sources Rules (SM-80 Source Category and Minor Source Permit Changes)
prior.summary: Florida Department of Environmental Protection has issued a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking to revise three rules under Chapter 62-210, F.A.C. regarding stationary sources. The revisions introduce a new 'Synthetic Minor – 80 Source (SM-80)' emissions-based category for minor source facilities with potential to emit at or above 80% of major source thresholds, authorize permit change procedures for minor source facilities analogous to major source procedures, clarify PSD review applicability for emissions unit reactivation, and specify annual reporting requirements for SM-80 sources.

prior.summary: Manitoba government departmental portal showcasing multiple strategic initiatives including Water Management Strategy, Affordable Energy Plan, clean technology investments ($3.75M Foresight Canada Partnership), and major energy production investments ($3B Westman Energy Production).
