prior.full_brief:
# GreenBiz Supply Chain — Sustainability Intelligence Brief

## Market Signal Brief: Supply Chain Decarbonization Intelligence Landscape

---

# Purpose and Context of This Document

This brief covers the supply chain sustainability intelligence landscape as published and tracked by Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz), synthesised with corroborating sources on Scope 3 reporting obligations, AI-driven decarbonisation research, and the 2026 regulatory environment affecting supply chain emissions disclosure. The brief is anchored to the workspace's role as an international freight forwarder operating across air (primary), road (secondary), and ocean (tertiary) modes, with cargo verticals spanning high-value and time-critical freight.

**Convention notes:** All binding-verb statements in this brief are either verbatim-sourced FACTs with inline citations, labeled inferences, or routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts. No regulatory conclusions are asserted without grounding. Dates reflect the UTC generation timestamp for this brief.

---

# Section 1: What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

The supply chain sustainability intelligence space is undergoing a structural shift across three simultaneous axes, each of which creates direct operational or commercial pressure for freight-focused workspaces.

**Axis 1 — Editorial and industry agenda-setting.** Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz) publishes a dedicated supply chain topic hub tracking corporate decarbonisation, circular economy, and emissions accounting across freight-relevant sectors. Current editorial coverage as of April–May 2026 includes: the Greenhouse Gas Protocol's proposed expansion of emissions accounting scope; AI's application to supply chain waste and emissions avoidance; and next-generation textile supply chain risk. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

Specifically, Trellis editorial notes: "Greenhouse Gas Protocol mulls dramatic expansion of emissions accounting — The protocol's proposal builds on new guidelines, released this week, for how companies can claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization." *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 14, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

*Analytical inference:* If adopted, a GHG Protocol scope expansion would directly affect how freight forwarders' clients account for the emissions embedded in logistics services — potentially increasing the granularity of data that cargo principals request from carriers and forwarders for Scope 3 reporting purposes.

**Axis 2 — Regulatory fragmentation on climate disclosure.** The US SEC Climate Disclosure Rule, finalized in March 2024, has effectively stalled at the federal level following the SEC's March 2025 vote to end its legal defense. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

In this regulatory vacuum, state-level mandates are advancing. California's SB 253 now carries a firm deadline: "large enterprises doing business in California must report their Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by August 10, 2026. Scope 3 reporting is set to follow in 2027." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

At the international level, "the EU CSRD remains the most comprehensive global standard. Following the Sustainability Omnibus update in early 2026, the EU raised its reporting thresholds to capture only the largest firms, specifically those with over 1,000 employees and €450 million in net turnover." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* The combination of a stalled federal rule and active state-level and EU mandates creates an asymmetric compliance burden: clients of the workspace operating in California or EU jurisdictions face real and active Scope 3 disclosure obligations, even as US federal pressure has temporarily receded. This asymmetry is the commercial pressure point for freight forwarders — the clients who need data are still present, even if the federal enforcer has stepped back.

**Axis 3 — AI as an operational decarbonisation tool.** A peer-reviewed study published in MDPI Sustainability (October 2025) examines AI's role in "proactive supply chain emission avoidance" across logistics, agri-food, and textile sectors. The journal is indexed as an open-access peer-reviewed publication. *Source: MDPI Sustainability, "AI-Driven Supply Chain Decarbonization: Strategies for Sustainable Carbon Reduction," MDPI, October 2025. https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/21/9642

*Specific content of the MDPI study's findings not fully extractable from the available source block — the source page rendered with JavaScript-dependent content partially unavailable. The journal title and subject matter are confirmed from the source URL and page metadata; specific quantitative findings from this study are treated as a research gap pending full-text retrieval.*

---

# Section 2: Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Named parties and stated interests, sourced:**

**Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz)** operates as the primary editorial and convening body for corporate sustainability strategy across supply chains. Its supply chain topic hub aggregates coverage from corporate strategy, decarbonisation, circular economy, and technology angles. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

**The Greenhouse Gas Protocol** is actively consulting on an expansion of emissions accounting methodology. Trellis coverage specifically notes "new guidelines, released this week, for how companies can claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization." *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 14, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

*Analytical inference:* The GHG Protocol's proposed expansion is being driven by corporate demand for a credible method to claim credit for supply chain investment — suggesting that large cargo-principal clients of freight forwarders are seeking accounting frameworks that reward supplier-level decarbonisation, which would include freight decarbonisation.

**California regulators (CARB / California legislature)** are advancing SB 253 and SB 261 as state-level replacements for the stalled federal SEC rule. SB 261 (climate-related financial risk) is noted as "currently under a temporary judicial stay from the Ninth Circuit, though companies are encouraged to remain 'publish-ready' as a ruling could resume enforcement at any time." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**New York, New Jersey, Colorado, and Illinois** are identified as states "attempting to introduce laws requiring large businesses to report GHG emissions." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**The EU Commission** has proceeded with CSRD implementation, with the Sustainability Omnibus update in early 2026 raising thresholds but preserving the double materiality obligation. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**BCG** has published a 2025 report framing supply chain decarbonisation as a liability-to-advantage transition. Specific content from BCG's report was not extractable from the available source block — the BCG page rendered as a navigation/services index without article body content. BCG's general positioning on supply chain decarbonisation is confirmed by the source's title and URL; specific quantitative claims from this report are treated as a research gap pending full-text retrieval. *Source: BCG, "Liability to Advantage: Decarbonizing the Supply Chain," BCG, 2025. https://www.bcg.com/publications/2025/liability-to-advantage-decarbonizing-supply-chain

---

# Section 3: Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**California SB 253 — conversion already occurred.** The August 10, 2026 Scope 1 and Scope 2 deadline for large enterprises doing business in California is a live, active obligation. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace, this means clients in scope for California SB 253 are already requesting or will shortly request emissions data from logistics providers, including freight forwarders, to populate their Scope 3 inventory ahead of the August 10, 2026 deadline. The conversion from signal to active commercial pressure for freight-forwarder data supply has already occurred for California-connected trade flows.

**Scope 3 reporting under California SB 253** is scheduled for 2027. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* The 2027 Scope 3 deadline is the higher-stakes trigger for freight forwarders, as logistics emissions are typically classified as Scope 3 (Category 4: upstream transportation; Category 9: downstream transportation) in the GHG Protocol framework. A workspace that can provide auditable, methodology-consistent emissions data per shipment is positioned ahead of competitors who cannot.

**EU CSRD — double materiality obligation active for large firms.** "Double materiality" requires companies to report on both financial materiality (how climate change affects the company's value) and impact materiality (how the company's activities impact the environment). *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**GHG Protocol expansion — pre-conversion.** The expansion is under consultation as of April 2026. A stated schedule for finalisation is not available from the sourced content. *Analytical inference:* Based on GHG Protocol's historical consultation-to-publication cycles (typically 12–24 months after new guideline release), a finalised expanded standard could arrive 2027–2028, though this estimate is not sourced and should be confirmed when the Protocol publishes its consultation timeline.

**Ninth Circuit ruling on California SB 261** is pending. The timing of the ruling is not available from the sourced content. The workspace should monitor for any ruling that lifts the judicial stay.

---

# Section 4: Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Filtered by air (primary), road (secondary), ocean (tertiary) — and by the workspace's role as freight forwarder:**

**Air-primary operations:**
*Operational implication:* Clients shipping time-critical, high-value cargo by air who are subject to California SB 253 or EU CSRD will require per-shipment or per-lane emissions data from the workspace. Air freight generates significantly higher emissions per tonne-kilometre than road or ocean; without a documented methodology and data supply capability, the workspace is exposed to being excluded from RFPs that include emissions data requirements.

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace, the air-freight emissions data gap is the most urgent, because air lanes are where per-shipment emissions intensity is highest, making the data both hardest to estimate without tooling and most material to a client's Scope 3 inventory.

**Road operations:**
*Operational implication:* Road drayage and secondary surface moves connected to air or port operations carry embedded emissions. For clients filing under California SB 253, the Scope 3 boundary likely captures road segments operated by or on behalf of the forwarder. The methodology used for road emissions calculation (distance-based vs. spend-based) will affect reported figures significantly.

**Ocean-tertiary operations:**
*Operational implication:* EU CSRD clients with ocean shipments face the most developed regulatory emissions reporting environment (FuelEU Maritime, EU ETS for shipping). For ocean segments, carrier-provided emissions data through tools such as the IMO's Data Collection System or shipper-oriented reporting platforms is more mature than for air. The workspace should assess whether its ocean carrier partners provide per-shipment CO2 data today.

**Cost of data infrastructure:**
*Specific cost figure for emissions data platform implementation not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

*Analytical inference:* The cost of building or procuring a shipment-level emissions data capability is an investment against the risk of client attrition or RFP exclusion. Whether this is a cost centre or a margin-protective investment depends on the share of the workspace's client base subject to active disclosure obligations — which is currently highest for California-connected and EU-connected clients.

---

# Section 5: Competitive Implications

*No specific named competitor positioning for freight forwarders on supply chain emissions data capability was available from the sourced content. The following is labeled inference only.*

*Analytical inference:* Large integrated logistics providers (operating across air, road, and ocean at global scale) have invested earlier and more heavily in per-shipment CO2 reporting tools. A mid-scale freight forwarder without a documented emissions methodology is at a structural disadvantage in RFPs from California-scope or EU-CSRD-scope clients who require Scope 3 data from their logistics providers.

*Analytical inference:* The GHG Protocol's proposed expansion of emissions accounting — specifically guidelines allowing companies to "claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization" — could create a differentiation mechanism: forwarders who can document decarbonisation investments (SAF uplift, lower-emission routing, modal shift) may generate a reportable credit for their clients, converting the forwarder's sustainability investment into a client-facing commercial asset.

*Industry interpretation:* EcoVadis frames the environment for 2026 as one where "Major investors and state-level authorities have moved forward with distinct mandates, making proactive reporting critical for maintaining market access and meeting stakeholder expectations." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

---

# Section 6: Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say — sourced basis:**

- California SB 253 creates a firm August 10, 2026 deadline for Scope 1 and 2 reporting for large enterprises doing business in California, with Scope 3 following in 2027. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The EU CSRD threshold was raised in early 2026 to firms with over 1,000 employees and €450M turnover, but the double materiality obligation — covering both the company's exposure to climate risk and its own impact on the environment — remains in force for in-scope firms. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The US federal SEC rule is effectively suspended, but state-level and EU obligations remain active and create real reporting requirements for clients operating in those jurisdictions. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The GHG Protocol is actively consulting on new guidelines for how companies can claim benefits from supply chain decarbonisation investments — positioning supply chain sustainability spend as potentially reportable value, not just cost. *(Sourced fact — cite Trellis above.)*

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- Are you doing business in California, and do you meet the SB 253 threshold? If so, your Scope 3 reporting obligation begins in 2027 — what methodology are you using for logistics emissions today?
- Are you subject to EU CSRD? If so, what emissions data do you currently receive from your logistics providers, and is it auditable?
- Has your sustainability team assessed whether the GHG Protocol's proposed expansion affects how you account for supply chain decarbonisation investments?

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not claim the workspace's emissions data methodology meets any specific regulatory standard without legal and technical verification.
- Do not assert that the SEC rule is "dead" — it remains formally on the books; federal enforcement is suspended, not the rule itself. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/
- Do not cite BCG or MDPI study-specific quantitative findings in client conversations without first reading the full reports; the source blocks available for this brief did not render their article bodies.

---

# Section 7: What the Workspace Should Do Now

Listed by action urgency, filtered by transport mode and cargo vertical:

**1. Map client exposure to California SB 253 and EU CSRD — now, before August 2026.**
*Action:* For each active client account, identify whether the client is (a) doing business in California at a scale likely to meet SB 253 thresholds or (b) subject to EU CSRD. This mapping determines which clients will request Scope 3 data from the workspace in 2026–2027.
*Why now:* California SB 253 Scope 1 and 2 deadline is August 10, 2026. Scope 3 reporting follows in 2027. Clients who have not yet established their logistics emissions methodology are behind, and the workspace can be a proactive data partner rather than a reactive supplier.

**2. Assess per-shipment emissions data capability — air lanes first.**
*Action:* Determine whether the workspace can currently produce per-shipment CO2e figures for air shipments using a documented, reproducible methodology (e.g., GLEC Framework or ISO 14083-consistent approach). If not, identify the gap and the fastest path to close it.
*Why air first:* Air freight emissions per tonne-kilometre are the highest of the three modes the workspace operates, making air-lane emissions the most material line item in a client's Scope 3 logistics category and the most likely subject of client audit scrutiny.

**3. Monitor GHG Protocol consultation on emissions accounting expansion.**
*Action:* Subscribe to GHG Protocol updates and assign a team member to track the consultation on new supply chain decarbonisation accounting guidelines (noted as released April 2026). Set a re-check window: **Q3 2026**, when consultation feedback periods typically close and a draft standard may be published.
*Why:* If the proposed expansion creates a mechanism for clients to claim credit for forwarder-level decarbonisation investments, the workspace's SAF purchases, fleet electrification spend, or modal shift data become client-reportable assets — a commercial differentiator.

**4. Monitor Ninth Circuit ruling on California SB 261.**
*Action:* The temporary judicial stay on SB 261 (climate-related financial risk disclosure) could be lifted at any time. Set a monitoring flag. Re-check: **quarterly through end 2026**.
*Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**5. Track GreenBiz/Trellis Impact 26 — June 23–25, San Francisco.**
*Action:* Trellis Impact 26 is a confirmed event with sourced dates. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/ This is the primary convening moment for corporate sustainability and supply chain decarbonisation decision-makers in 2026. Client contacts who attend this event will return with updated expectations. Brief commercial and operations leads before the event; debrief clients who attend.

**6. Retrieve full text of BCG and MDPI reports before citing them.**
*Action:* The BCG "Liability to Advantage" report (2025) and the MDPI AI-driven supply chain decarbonisation study (October 2025) are cited in industry conversations but their full content was not available in the source blocks for this brief. Retrieve both before using them in client presentations or RFP responses. Adding unread research to client-facing materials is a credibility risk.

---

# Section 8: Sources

prior.title: DNV Quarterly Insights: Regulatory Updates in Maritime, Food Safety, Cybersecurity, and Net-Zero Compliance (Q2 2026)
prior.summary: DNV releases comprehensive market intelligence covering multiple regulatory domains: FSSC 22000 Version 7 food safety certification, PFOS-free fire-extinguishing foam compliance deadline (January 2026), IMO MEPC 84 Net-Zero Framework discussions, Q1 2026 Port State Control detention trends, UK CO₂ transport network certification, industrial AI regulatory sandbox, and emerging autonomous maritime capabilities. Key compliance windows and enforcement actions identified across maritime, energy, and cybersecurity sectors.
prior.full_brief:
# DNV Quarterly Insights: Regulatory Updates in Maritime, Net-Zero Compliance, and Classification Rules (Q2 2026)

## Market Signal Brief

**Severity: MONITORING**
**Format: Market Signal Brief**

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

Three converging regulatory streams define Q2 2026 maritime compliance pressure: (1) the IMO Net-Zero Framework (NZF) moving from MEPC 83 approval toward formal adoption and implementation guideline development; (2) DNV's 2026 class-rule hearing cycle, covering 77 documents with a planned publication date of 1 July 2026; and (3) accelerating alternative-fuel fleet growth that is outpacing the technical standards required to govern it.

**IMO Net-Zero Framework — regulatory approval and timeline**

The International Maritime Organization (IMO) approved draft amendments to MARPOL Annex VI during its 83rd Marine Environment Protection Committee session (MEPC 83), held 7–11 April 2025, establishing a mandatory emissions-limit and GHG-pricing framework for global shipping. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

The IMO Net-Zero Framework is described as "the first in the world to combine mandatory emissions limits and GHG pricing across an entire industry sector." *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

The draft regulations, as approved at MEPC 83, were set to be formally adopted in October 2025 before entry into force in 2027. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

The MEPC 83 roadmap identified Spring 2026 (MEPC 84) as the milestone for approval of detailed implementation guidelines. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

*Analytical inference:* MEPC 84 implementation guideline outcomes are the critical near-term gate for the NZF. Until those guidelines are finalised, the operational mechanics of the GFI compliance credit system and the IMO Net-Zero Fund disbursement rules remain unresolved, limiting the workspace's ability to model compliance cost exposure on ocean corridors.

**DNV class-rule hearing cycle — Q2 2026**

DNV submitted 77 class-rule and standards documents for industry hearing, open for comments from 1 March to 12 April 2026, with planned publication on 1 July 2026. *Source: DNV, "DNV Rules 2026 – Hearing Period Now Open," DNV, 2 March 2026. https://www.dnv.com/news/2026/dnv-rules-2026-hearing-period-now-open/

The proposed 2026 changes include new Shore Power class notations (qualifier IEC for full IEC compliance; qualifier Permanent for continuous shore power use; new Shore Power Ready notation for pre-installation readiness), a new Battery Ready qualifier, and a new Ultra Low Emission Vessel (ULEV) qualifier for NOx/SOx emission reduction systems. *Source: DNV, "DNV Rules 2026 – Hearing Period Now Open," DNV, 2 March 2026. https://www.dnv.com/news/2026/dnv-rules-2026-hearing-period-now-open/

**Alternative-fuel fleet growth — market signal**

By 2024, vessels powered by marine clean alternative fuels accounted for 43.2% of the global fleet by deadweight tonnage, a two-percentage-point increase from 2023. From January to April 2025, the share of alternative-fuel newbuild orders rose further to 63%. *Source: Frontiers in Marine Science, "Global Coordination and Challenges of Technical Standards and Application Specifications for Marine Clean Alternative Fuels in the IMO's New Emission Reduction Regulations," Frontiers, 12 December 2025. https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2025.1683017/full

*Analytical inference:* The 63% alternative-fuel share of newbuild orders by early 2025 signals a structural, not marginal, fleet transition — the signal is well past the question of whether it converts and is now a question of which fuel pathway dominates and which ports build the required bunkering infrastructure first.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

**IMO and Member States** are driving the NZF toward legally binding form. The framework targets ships over 5,000 gross tonnage, which emit 85% of the total CO₂ emissions from international shipping. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

The IMO Net-Zero Fund, once established, will collect pricing contributions from emissions and disburse revenues to reward low-emission ships, support innovation and infrastructure in developing countries, fund training and technology transfer, and mitigate negative impacts on vulnerable States including Small Island Developing States and Least Developed Countries. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

**Major maritime nations** are each advancing divergent standards strategies. A peer-reviewed Frontiers analysis identifies three major challenges in global standard harmonisation: geopolitical competition leading to fragmentation of standards, technological innovation outpacing regulatory development, and uneven infrastructure deployment. *Source: Frontiers in Marine Science, "Global Coordination and Challenges of Technical Standards and Application Specifications for Marine Clean Alternative Fuels," Frontiers, 12 December 2025. https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2025.1683017/full

The same peer-reviewed analysis identifies distinct national-bloc strategies: China's "1+N" policy system emphasising top-down coordination, the EU relying on carbon trading mechanisms and mandatory measures including FuelEU Maritime, Japan prioritising safety standards (e.g., ClassNK guidelines), and Singapore adopting pragmatic policies to establish a green fuel hub. *Source: Frontiers in Marine Science, "Global Coordination and Challenges of Technical Standards and Application Specifications for Marine Clean Alternative Fuels," Frontiers, 12 December 2025. https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2025.1683017/full

**DNV** is using the 2026 hearing cycle to advance classification support for decarbonisation infrastructure — shore power, battery-readiness, and ultra-low-emission vessel designations — positioning class notation as a commercial differentiator aligned with the NZF compliance pathway. *Source: DNV, "DNV Rules 2026 – Hearing Period Now Open," DNV, 2 March 2026. https://www.dnv.com/news/2026/dnv-rules-2026-hearing-period-now-open/

*Analytical inference:* DNV's simultaneous introduction of Shore Power Ready, Battery Ready, and ULEV notations in a single 2026 cycle — timed to precede the NZF's expected 2027 entry into force — is consistent with a strategy of making class notation the visible commercial compliance signal before the regulatory mechanism itself activates.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Conversion trigger — NZF entry into force (2027):** The IMO NZF, once formally adopted, is expected to enter into force in 2027 — 16 months after adoption, in accordance with MARPOL articles. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx

**Conversion trigger — MEPC 84 implementation guidelines (Spring 2026):** Spring 2026 (MEPC 84) is the scheduled milestone for approval of detailed implementation guidelines. *Source: IMO Press Briefing, "IMO Approves Net-Zero Regulations for Global Shipping," IMO, 11 April 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/imo-approves-netzero-regulations.aspx The NZF's adoption vote status and any timeline revision — reported as the subject of an October 2026 vote per DNV's MEPC 84 brief (corroborating source, listed below) — is a monitoring priority for Q3 2026.

**Conversion trigger — DNV rule publication (1 July 2026):** New class notations including Shore Power, Shore Power Ready, Battery Ready, and ULEV become operative on planned publication date of 1 July 2026. *Source: DNV, "DNV Rules 2026 – Hearing Period Now Open," DNV, 2 March 2026. https://www.dnv.com/news/2026/dnv-rules-2026-hearing-period-now-open/

**Conversion trigger — alternative-fuel standard harmonisation:** The Frontiers study identifies technological innovation outpacing regulatory development as a primary challenge. *Analytical inference:* Until IMO, EU, and flag-state technical standards for LNG, methanol, ammonia, and hydrogen converge, workspaces operating across multi-jurisdictional ocean corridors face heterogeneous bunkering and documentation requirements that complicate cargo planning and contract clause drafting.

**Re-check window: Q3 2026** — for MEPC 84 implementation guideline outcomes, any NZF adoption vote revision, and DNV rule publication confirmation.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating as freight forwarders or shippers on deep-sea ocean lanes involving vessels over 5,000 gross tonnage — the coverage threshold of the NZF — the entry of the GHG pricing mechanism from 2027 onward will create a new carrier-level cost that propagates through freight rates, fuel surcharges, and bunker adjustment factors. The workspace has no current sourced visibility into the specific surcharge pass-through mechanism, as implementation guidelines are pending MEPC 84.

*Operational implication:* For ocean freight moved on corridors touching EU ports, the NZF's GHG pricing layer operates alongside the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) maritime extension, which is already in force. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether and how remedial units purchased under the IMO Net-Zero Fund interact with or offset EU ETS allowance obligations on the same voyage requires carrier-specific legal review; no authoritative guidance reconciling dual obligations has been published as of Q2 2026.

*Operational implication:* The new DNV Shore Power class notations and Shore Power Ready designation — planned for publication 1 July 2026 — are directly relevant to port-call planning for workspaces moving high-value or time-sensitive cargo on vessels calling at European or North American ports where shore power mandates are active or approaching. Vessels without the Shore Power notation may face higher port-call emissions costs or berth restrictions at ports enforcing connection requirements.

*Operational implication:* The fragmentation of alternative-fuel technical standards — across China's domestic framework, EU FuelEU Maritime, Japan's ClassNK guidelines, and Singapore's hub strategy — means that bunkering availability, documentation requirements, and cost differentials will vary by corridor. This directly affects workspaces routing ocean freight across Asia-Europe and trans-Pacific lanes.

**Effect on air-priority verticals (events, fine art, luxury goods, film/TV):** The NZF's scope is expressly limited to large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage. Ocean is a tertiary or supplementary mode for high-value, air-primary verticals. *Analytical inference:* The direct cost exposure from the NZF GFI compliance mechanism is limited for cargo moved primarily by air; the indirect effect — elevated base freight rates on feeder and pre-carriage ocean legs — is real but unquantified without carrier-specific rate data.

Specific surcharge figures and carrier-level GFI compliance cost pass-through rates are not publicly available as of Q2 2026 and require carrier-specific data.

*Specific cost impact per TEU or shipment not available from primary sources as of June 2026.*

---

# Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* Freight forwarders that can demonstrate active monitoring of MEPC 84 guideline outcomes, interpret the two-tier GFI compliance structure (Base Target vs. Direct Compliance Target and surplus unit mechanics) for commercial clients, and select carrier partners with NZF-ready fleet strategies will hold a client-conversation advantage over those reacting to surcharge notifications after the fact.

*Analytical inference:* The DNV 2026 class-rule hearing's Shore Power and Shore Power Ready notations provide a visible commercial signal for vessel selection. Workspaces that build a preference for vessels carrying these notations into carrier procurement criteria — before the notations become standard market expectations — are positioned ahead of the regulatory timeline.

*Analytical inference:* The Frontiers peer-reviewed analysis identifies three fragmented regulatory blocs (China, EU, Japan/Singapore). Workspaces with ocean freight regularly transiting Asia-Europe corridors face the highest complexity from standard fragmentation, particularly on fuel documentation. Forwarders with established carrier relationships on these corridors and fluency in dual-regime compliance (IMO NZF + EU ETS) will be differentiated from generalist operators.

Named competitor-level positioning — specific named freight forwarders and their NZF readiness status — is not available from the sourced materials as of Q2 2026 and is omitted per the integrity rule.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The IMO has formally approved the first regulatory framework in any industry sector to combine mandatory emissions limits with GHG pricing, covering ships over 5,000 GT — the core of the deep-sea fleet. This is confirmed by IMO's own press briefing dated 11 April 2025.
- Detailed implementation guidelines are the subject of MEPC 84 (Spring 2026). Until those guidelines are finalised, the specific compliance cost mechanics — including how the IMO Net-Zero Fund operates and how surplus units can be transferred — are not resolved.
- DNV's 2026 classification rule changes, planned for 1 July 2026 publication, introduce Shore Power Ready and Battery Ready designations that will become visible commercial differentiation signals for vessel selection.
- The alternative-fuel fleet is growing rapidly — 43% of global fleet by DWT in 2024, 63% of newbuild orders by early 2025 — but technical standards for LNG, methanol, ammonia, and hydrogen remain fragmented across jurisdictions.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not assert a specific per-voyage or per-tonne compliance cost: implementation guidelines are not yet finalised and no authoritative cost figure is available.
- Do not assert that the NZF is already in force: it was approved at MEPC 83 in April 2025 and was scheduled for formal adoption in October 2025, with 2027 entry into force. The adoption vote status and any timeline revision should be confirmed against MEPC 84 outcomes before citing.
- Do not assert that the NZF displaces or replaces EU ETS obligations on EU-touching voyages: the interaction between the two instruments is unresolved and requires legal review before any client-facing statement.
- Do not cite the Frontiers study as a regulatory source: it is peer-reviewed academic analysis (Tier 6), providing structural insight but not authoritative interpretation of binding obligations.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**1. Set a MEPC 84 monitoring trigger — Q3 2026.**
Implementation guidelines approved at MEPC 84 will define the operational mechanics of the GFI compliance credit system and the IMO Net-Zero Fund. The workspace should designate one operations or compliance contact to pull the MEPC 84 meeting summary when published (expected Spring–Summer 2026) and translate the guidelines into surcharge-scenario modelling for ocean-corridor clients.

**2. Engage carrier partners on NZF surcharge pass-through methodology — now.**
Before surcharge mechanisms are formalised, the workspace should ask ocean carrier partners: (a) whether they are modelling a GFI-based fuel surcharge distinct from existing BAF structures; (b) which vessels in their fleet are on track for Base Target vs. Direct Compliance Target compliance; and (c) whether they hold or are accumulating surplus units. These questions position the workspace for informed rate negotiations and client cost transparency.

**3. Build DNV Shore Power and Shore Power Ready notation into vessel selection criteria — ahead of 1 July 2026 rule publication.**
For port-intensive ocean corridors where shore power mandates are active or approaching, preference for vessels carrying or being prepared for DNV Shore Power notation reduces exposure to port-call emission cost escalation. This is an operational clause recommendation, not a current contractual requirement. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether existing carrier contracts permit or require notation-based vessel substitution requires counsel review.

**4. Map ocean-corridor exposure to the 5,000 GT threshold — now.**
The NZF applies to large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage. For workspaces consolidating cargo on smaller feeder vessels, *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether feeder legs below the threshold generate any downstream GFI obligation on the main haul requires carrier-level legal review.

**5. Participate in the DNV 2026 rule hearing — comment window closed 12 April 2026.**
The comment window for DNV's 77-document hearing closed 12 April 2026. For future cycles, the workspace should designate an observer on DNV's Rules and Standards Explorer to identify proposed changes relevant to vessel notations affecting cargo handling, securing (new T-bar qualifier), and VOC emissions (new PV qualifier for VCS), which may affect cargo selection or acceptance terms.

---

prior.title: IATA Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Framework and Net Zero Roadmap
prior.summary: IATA's SAF program outlines that sustainable aviation fuel could contribute approximately 65% of emissions reductions needed for aviation to reach net zero CO2 by 2050. The framework emphasizes the critical role of harmonized government policies, technology-agnostic incentives, and production scaling. Mandates are recommended only as part of broader SAF production strategies complemented by innovation and cost reduction programs.
prior.full_brief:
# IATA Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Framework and Net Zero Roadmap

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING | COST ALERT

**The core movement:** SAF production doubled in 2025 but growth is now decelerating sharply, while mandatory blend requirements in Europe and the UK are driving cost spikes that airline cargo carriers — and their freight forwarding supply chains — are absorbing today.

**Production status (2025 baseline):** FACT — "In 2025, SAF output is expected to reach 1.9 million tonnes (Mt) (2.4 billion liters), double the 1 Mt produced in 2024. However, in 2026, SAF production growth is projected to slow down and reach 2.4 Mt." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, "SAF Production Growth Rate is Slowing Down," IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Share of total jet fuel:** FACT — "SAF production in 2025 represents only 0.6% of total jet fuel consumption, increasing to 0.8% the following year." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Cost premium at industry level:** FACT — "At current price levels, the SAF premium translates into an additional USD 3.6 billion in fuel costs for the industry in 2025." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**What triggered deceleration:** FACT — "The estimated SAF output for 2025 of 1.9 Mt is a downward revision from IATA's earlier forecasts due to lack of policy support to take full advantage of the installed SAF capacities." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**EU and UK mandate effect (price distortion):** FACT — "In Europe, ReFuelEU Aviation has sharply increased costs amid limited SAF capacity and oligopolistic supply chains. Fuel suppliers have widened their profit margins to such an extent that airlines pay up to five times more than the price of conventional jet fuel and double the market price of SAF." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Airline premium burden:** FACT — "The cumulative impact of poorly designed policy frameworks is that airlines paid a premium of USD 2.9 billion for the limited 1.9 Mt of SAF available in 2025. Of this, USD 1.4 billion reflects the standard SAF price premium over conventional fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Strategic context — what SAF is meant to do:** FACT — "sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) representing the most impactful solution in the coming decades: it could contribute up to 65% of the reduction in emissions needed by aviation to reach net zero CO2 emissions by 2050." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Lifecycle emissions reduction claim:** FACT — "SAF can significantly reduce lifecycle carbon emissions, typically by around 80% compared to conventional aviation fuel." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Drop-in compatibility:** FACT — "As a drop-in fuel, it can be blended with conventional aviation fuel and readily used without requiring any modifications to aircraft or airport infrastructure." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating air-primary freight lanes — particularly time-sensitive verticals such as live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, and high-value automotive — the SAF cost premium is already embedded in airline fuel economics and is beginning to appear in fuel surcharge structures on European routes. The 2025 figures confirm this is a present-tense cost pressure, not a future risk.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**IATA (International Air Transport Association):** Represents over 370 airlines accounting for approximately 85% of global air traffic. FACT — "IATA (International Air Transport Association) represents over 370 airlines accounting for some 85% of global air traffic." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

IATA's stated interest is scaling SAF through incentive-based policy rather than mandates, seeking harmonisation across jurisdictions to avoid fragmented compliance burdens. FACT — "Harmonization, simplification, and competition-neutrality are all essential for delivering the greatest possible emissions reductions at the lowest possible cost." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**ICAO (International Civil Aviation Organization):** The intergovernmental body setting global frameworks. The ICAO Global Framework for SAF — agreed at the Third ICAO Conference on Aviation and Alternative Fuels (CAAF/3) — is built across four building blocks: policy and planning; regulatory frameworks; implementation support; and financing. FACT — "The ICAO Global Framework for Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF), Lower Carbon Aviation Fuels (LCAF) and other Aviation Cleaner Energies includes a collective global aspirational Vision to reduce CO2 emissions in international aviation by 5 per cent by 2030, compared to zero cleaner energy use." *Source: ICAO SAF Framework Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/SAF

FACT — "The 41st ICAO Assembly adopted a long-term global aspirational goal (LTAG) for international aviation of net-zero carbon emissions by 2050 in support of the UNFCCC Paris Agreement's temperature goal." *Source: ICAO SAF Framework Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/SAF

**European regulators (ReFuelEU Aviation):** Have adopted a mandate-first approach (in force from January 2025) that IATA characterises as counterproductive. FACT — "The recent entry into force of ReFuelEU for Aviation (RFEUA) in January 2025 is already presenting significant challenges to aircraft operators in Europe." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Fuel suppliers (European market):** IATA identifies an oligopolistic supply structure as a price amplifier. Industry interpretation (IATA position, not an independent finding): FACT — "Fuel suppliers have widened their profit margins to such an extent that airlines pay up to five times more than the price of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

*Analytical inference:* IATA's public critique of EU and UK mandate design is a lobbying signal. Workspaces should read IATA's policy positions as advocacy, not as regulatory authority. The underlying production and cost data, however, are sourced from IATA's own estimates and are consistent with the direction of travel visible from ReFuelEU and CORSIA documentation.

**CORSIA framework:** ICAO's Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation embeds SAF as a compliance mechanism through which aircraft operators reduce their offsetting requirements. FACT — "CORSIA allows aircraft operators to reduce its offsetting requirements through the use of CORSIA eligible fuels, which include CORSIA sustainable aviation fuels and CORSIA lower carbon aviation fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Production trajectory — near term:** FACT — 2025 output: 1.9 Mt. 2026 projection: 2.4 Mt, representing a significant slowdown in the year-on-year growth rate from the 2024–2025 doubling. *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Production potential — long term:** FACT — "around 400 Mt of SAF is forecast to be possible to produce in 2050." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA (citing Global Feedstock Assessment for SAF Production, September 2025). https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**e-SAF mandates — the next conversion trigger:** FACT — "With e-SAF mandates approaching in the UK (2028) and EU (2030), it's essential not to repeat the policy missteps seen with SAF. Already, e-SAF faces a much higher cost base, potentially up to 12 times that of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**e-SAF compliance cost exposure:** FACT — "compliance costs could escalate to EUR 29 billion by 2032 if targets aren't met, as seems very likely with the current policy framework." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Airline target revision signal:** FACT — "many airlines that have committed to use 10% SAF by 2030 will be forced to reevaluate these commitments. SAF is not being produced in sufficient amounts to enable these airlines to achieve their ambition." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**CORSIA feedstock expansion (live process):** The ICAO Council is actively expanding the list of CORSIA-approved feedstocks. FACT — "19 November 2025 - ICAO Council approves the classification of new feedstocks and default core LCA values for CORSIA eligible fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

FACT — "27 June 2025 - ICAO Council approves CAEP/13 recommendations, including various amendments to ICAO documents related to CORSIA eligible fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

**IATA Net Zero Roadmaps — framework document update:** FACT — "In September 2024, IATA released updated Policy and Finance Net Zero Roadmaps, containing expanded and deepened analyses." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

The roadmaps cover five domains: aircraft technology, energy infrastructure, operations, finance, and policy. FACT — "IATA's Net Zero roadmaps provide step-by-step detailing of critical actions for aviation to achieve net zero CO2 by 2050. They address aircraft technology, energy infrastructure, operations, finance, and policy." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

**Peer review process:** FACT — "A peer-to-peer review, complemented by a modeling tool provided by the Air Transportation Systems Laboratory at University College London, was conducted to calculate emission reductions for each technology." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

*Analytical inference:* The combination of decelerating SAF production growth and advancing e-SAF mandate dates (UK 2028, EU 2030) creates a structural cost escalation corridor for European-route air freight between now and 2032. The EUR 29 billion compliance cost figure, if borne even partially by air carriers, is large enough to trigger structural fuel surcharge redesign by major carriers on transatlantic and intra-European corridors.

**MONITORING — re-check window:** Review this signal at Q2 2026 for European Commission STIP (Strategic Technologies for Europe Platform) timeline announcement, referenced by IATA as a step forward without a clear timeline. Re-check again at Q4 2027 ahead of the UK e-SAF mandate effective year of 2028.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

COST ALERT

**Air-primary operations — most directly exposed.** For workspaces moving high-value, time-sensitive cargo (live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production materials, high-value automotive components) predominantly by air, SAF cost passthrough is the operative mechanism:

- *Operational implication:* Carriers operating on European routes are already paying the SAF premium embedded in ReFuelEU Aviation from January 2025. That premium — sourced at up to 5× conventional jet fuel on mandated routes — feeds into airline cost bases that surcharge structures reflect. Freight forwarding workspaces quoting on European air routes should model SAF surcharge increases as a recurring line item, not a one-off.

- *Operational implication:* The SAF price multiple of 2× the open-market SAF price (and 5× conventional jet fuel) in mandated European markets is not a temporary spike. It reflects structural supply concentration and will persist until additional production capacity reaches final investment decision and commercial operation — a timeline that, based on the 2026 growth projection, extends well beyond 2026.

**e-SAF cost base — forward-planning horizon:** FACT — e-SAF faces "a much higher cost base, potentially up to 12 times that of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces with clients who have committed to scope 3 emission reduction targets linked to air freight will face increasing pressure to produce SAF-attributable emissions data as SAF mandates formalise carrier reporting requirements. The CORSIA monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) framework already sets standards for how SAF emissions reductions are accounted at the aircraft operator level — this is the upstream source of the data that freight Scope 3 reporting will eventually need to draw from.

**Non-EU/UK routes — lower immediate cost exposure but not zero:** *Analytical inference:* Routes outside the EU and UK ReFuelEU mandate zone are not currently subject to the same mandatory blending requirements. However, CORSIA Phase 1 (2021–2026 baseline) applies globally to participating international operators, and the ICAO aspirational 5% CO2 reduction by 2030 sets the direction of travel for non-EU jurisdictions as well. Workspaces with significant transpacific or transatlantic non-EU air freight should monitor US SAF policy developments separately, as US production incentive structures differ materially from EU mandate structures.

**Cargo vertical — live events and film/TV:** *Operational implication:* These verticals move equipment on tight charter and ad-hoc air freight schedules that cannot easily absorb last-minute fuel surcharge changes. SAF surcharge transparency (what the carrier is charging, what it reflects, how it is calculated) should be a standard clause in spot-rate negotiations and master rate agreements with air carriers operating EU-origin or EU-destination flights.

**Cargo vertical — fine art and luxury goods:** *Operational implication:* Clients in these verticals increasingly require sustainability credentials as part of carrier selection and shipment documentation. SAF certificate provenance (which sustainability certification scheme, which feedstock category, which lifecycle value under CORSIA) is becoming a legitimate client-facing data point. Workspaces without access to this data from their carrier partners are exposed to being unable to substantiate emissions claims in client sustainability reporting.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

COMPETITIVE EDGE

**No sourced data is available in the provided source blocks on named competitor freight forwarder positions regarding SAF procurement, SAF surcharge strategies, or client-facing SAF certificate programmes.** Specific competitor positioning requires carrier-specific and forwarder-specific data not available in the provided sources.

*Analytical inference:* The competitive dynamic is structural rather than competitor-specific at this stage. Freight forwarders who have established SAF certificate procurement relationships — either through direct airline SAF offtake agreements or through IATA's CADO (Civil Aviation Decarbonization Organization) registry — are positioned to provide clients with verifiable, CORSIA-compliant SAF attribution data. Those without such relationships cannot credibly make SAF-linked emissions claims in client reporting.

*Analytical inference:* The IATA SAF Matchmaker tool (referenced in source content as a CADO-operated platform) is the established mechanism for buyers and sellers to connect in the SAF certificate market. Workspaces not tracking this tool are operating with a gap in their procurement intelligence.

*Analytical inference:* The deceleration in SAF production growth, combined with mandate-driven price spikes, means that early SAF offtake agreement holders are relatively better positioned than spot buyers. The workspaces most exposed are those relying entirely on spot-rate air freight without any long-term carrier relationships that include SAF transparency provisions.

**European route concentration risk:** Workspaces with a high proportion of EU-origin or EU-destination air freight are more directly exposed to the ReFuelEU Aviation cost dynamic than those operating primarily on non-European corridors. This is a differentiator in competitive quoting: a workspace that can explain the SAF surcharge origin and trajectory is more credible than one that cannot.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

1. SAF production reached 1.9 million tonnes in 2025 — double the 2024 level — but remains under 1% of total jet fuel use. This is a verified IATA figure (December 2025).

2. On European routes, SAF mandate costs (ReFuelEU Aviation, in force January 2025) are already flowing through airline cost bases and appearing in surcharge structures. The premium on mandated European routes has reached up to 5× the conventional jet fuel price — this is the source of the fuel surcharge increases your clients are seeing on EU air freight movements.

3. SAF is a drop-in fuel — it does not require changes to aircraft or airport infrastructure. The barrier is cost and production volume, not technical compatibility.

4. SAF offers up to approximately 80% lifecycle carbon reduction versus conventional jet fuel. This is the IATA figure; it requires sustainability certification scheme validation to be attributable to a specific shipment.

5. ICAO's CORSIA framework sets the global standard for SAF sustainability criteria and lifecycle accounting. SAF certificates that are CORSIA-compliant have a defined, audited emissions reduction value.

**Questions to pose to carrier partners:**
- Which sustainability certification scheme certifies your SAF supply?
- What feedstock category and lifecycle emissions value applies to the SAF on this route?
- Is the SAF surcharge a passthrough of your actual SAF purchase cost, or a fixed programme fee?
- Can you provide CORSIA-eligible fuel batch documentation for Scope 3 reporting purposes?

**Claims to avoid:**
- Do not characterise SAF as a near-term solution to aviation's full emissions footprint. At 0.6% of jet fuel use in 2025, it is a trajectory, not a solution.
- Do not represent a specific lifecycle emissions reduction figure for a client's shipments without the underlying certification documentation from the carrier.
- Do not state that SAF "offsets" emissions — the CORSIA framework credits SAF as an emissions reduction, not an offset. The distinction matters for Scope 3 reporting integrity.
- Do not quote the IATA 65% contribution figure as a current fact — it is a modelled 2050 projection, not a present-day operational figure.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Request SAF transparency data from air carrier partners on European routes.**
European routes are under the ReFuelEU Aviation mandate (in force January 2025). Carriers on these routes are blending SAF and passing costs through. The workspace should, as a standard account management action, request from each carrier: the sustainability certification scheme name, feedstock category, and CORSIA lifecycle emissions value for SAF used on routes where surcharges are applied. This data is needed for any client Scope 3 reporting that references air freight emissions. Timeline: immediate. Effect on verticals: fine art, luxury goods, live events, film and TV — all of which have clients with sustainability disclosure expectations.

**Action 2 — Track the IATA CADO registry and SAF Matchmaker for certificate market access.**
The Civil Aviation Decarbonization Organization (CADO), launched by IATA, operates the SAF certificate registry and Matchmaker tool. Workspaces without a working knowledge of this mechanism cannot engage credibly in SAF certificate procurement discussions with clients or carriers. Assign a sustainability-designated staff member to review CADO registry activity on a quarterly basis.

**Action 3 — Add SAF surcharge transparency language to carrier rate agreements.**
Current rate agreements with air carriers on EU-origin and EU-destination routes should be reviewed for fuel surcharge definitions. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether existing agreement language adequately captures SAF-specific surcharge passthrough, how disputes over SAF surcharge quantum are resolved, and what documentation rights the workspace holds requires counsel review against specific agreement terms.

**Action 4 — Monitor UK e-SAF mandate timeline (2028 effective date).**
The UK e-SAF mandate takes effect in 2028. For workspaces with significant UK-origin or UK-destination air freight, this is a 24-month planning horizon. The cost base for e-SAF is sourced at up to 12× conventional jet fuel — materially higher than current SAF premiums. Begin scenario modelling now for what a UK e-SAF surcharge would do to landed costs on the workspace's highest-volume UK air lanes.

**Action 5 — Do not commit to SAF-linked client sustainability claims without supply chain documentation.**
Multiple airlines have indicated they will need to revise their own 10% SAF by 2030 commitments. Workspaces should not make forward SAF availability commitments to clients that depend on airline SAF commitments that are themselves under review.

---

# Sources

| # | Title | Type | Date | URL |
|---|-------|------|------|-----|
| 1 | IATA SAF Programme Page | Industry body (Tier 4) | Current | https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/ |
| 2 | IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page | Industry body (Tier 4) | Current (updated Sept 2024) | https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/ |
| 3 | IATA Press Release No. 57 — SAF Production Growth Rate is Slowing Down | News/industry release (Tier 5) | 9 December 2025 | https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/ |
| 4 | ICAO Global Framework for SAF, LCAF and Aviation Cleaner Energies | Intergovernmental body (Tier 3) | Current | https://www.icao.int/SAF |
| 5 | ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page | Intergovernmental body (Tier 3) | Last updated November 2025 | https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels |

*Note: The IATA SAF Fact Sheet (June 2026) at https://www.iata.org/en/iata-repository/pressroom/fact-sheets/fact-sheet-sustainable-aviation-fuels/ was blocked by a security verification page and yielded no groundable content. Its headline figure (1.9 Mt in 2025) is corroborated verbatim by the December 2025 press release. No content from the fact sheet URL has been used as a FACT source.*

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------------|------------------------|

| IATA Net Zero Roadmaps (five-domain) | https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/ | 4 | Contains the only available peer-reviewed (UCL-modelled) step-by-step roadmap to 2050 net zero across aircraft technology, energy, operations, finance, and policy — foundational for any forward planning brief on aviation decarbonisation trajectory. |

| ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page | https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels | 3 | The authoritative ICAO source for CORSIA SAF sustainability criteria, feedstock classifications, and lifecycle emissions values — directly relevant to any Scope 3 SAF attribution claim a workspace or its carrier partners make. |
