prior.title: ITF-OECD Automated and Autonomous Driving Resource
prior.summary: Source URL points to OECD International Transport Forum content on automated and autonomous driving, but content is inaccessible due to security verification page. Actual content cannot be determined from excerpt.
prior.full_brief:
# ITF-OECD Automated and Autonomous Driving: Policy Landscape, Regulatory Uncertainty, and Freight Relevance

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises the ITF-OECD Automated and Autonomous Driving resource and corroborating published materials across the ITF/OECD policy corpus and peer-reviewed literature. The primary source is the ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report published 30 April 2015. Corroborating sources span a 2021 peer-reviewed environmental review and OECD infrastructure and AI-regulation titles identified as candidates for the source registry. The Cloudflare-blocked ITF AI/ML report (https://www.itf-oecd.org/ai-machine-learning-and-regulation-case-automated-vehicles) and the NHTSA and Eno Center pages could not be content-verified from the provided source blocks; their substance is excluded from FACT claims and listed under New Sources Identified for retrieval. The OECD Regulation under Uncertainty and Preparing Infrastructure OECD pages returned only navigation HTML with no substantive claim content; they are similarly listed under New Sources Identified.

The workspace operates as a global freight forwarder with road transport as a secondary mode and a client base that includes high-value, time-sensitive cargo verticals. The brief is anchored to that profile throughout.

Convention: dates marked "(30 Apr 2015)" refer to the ITF CPB primary report. "Annual Reviews 2021" refers to Nikitas, Thomopoulos & Milakis (2021).

---

## What the Research Found

**Headline finding.** The ITF Corporate Partnership Board report — drafted through working groups of ITF researchers, CPB member companies, and external experts — identifies automated and autonomous driving as a set of **distinct emerging concepts that must be understood individually and as part of broader trends toward automation and connectivity**, not as a single arrival event.

*Source: "Automated driving comprises a diverse set of emerging concepts that must be understood individually and as part of broader trends toward automation and connectivity," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Publication scope and methodology.** The report is 33 pages, produced under the ITF Corporate Partnership Board (CPB) structure. The CPB model is designed to enrich policy discussion with a business perspective, conducted through working groups of CPB member companies, external experts, and ITF researchers.

*Source: "The work for this report was carried out in the context of a project initiated and funded by the International Transport Forum's Corporate Partnership Board (CPB). CPB projects are designed to enrich policy discussion with a business perspective. Led by the ITF, work is carried out in a collaborative fashion in working groups consisting of CPB member companies, external experts and ITF researchers," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Deployment status at time of publication.** The report notes that prototype cars capable of driving autonomously had been tested on public roads in Europe, Japan, and the United States, and that future deployment was expected to accelerate.

*Source: "prototype cars capable of driving autonomously have been and continue to be tested on public roads in Europe, Japan and the United States. These technologies have arrived rapidly on the market and their future deployment is expected to accelerate," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Core policy finding — regulatory uncertainty.** The report centres on the principle that **uncertainty on market deployment strategies and pathways to automation complicates the regulatory task**.

*Source: "Uncertainty on market deployment strategies and pathways to automation complicates the regulatory task," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Insurance.** The report finds that incrementally shifting the driving task from humans to machines will require changes in insurance.

*Source: "Incrementally shifting the driving task from humans to machines will require changes in insurance," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Information sharing.** The shift from human to machine may affect what product information developers and manufacturers share, and with whom.

*Source: "The shift from human to machine may have an impact on what product information developers and manufacturers of autonomous vehicles share, and with whom," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Legacy risk.** The report recommends that regulators and developers actively plan to minimise legacy risks.

*Source: "Regulators and developers should actively plan to minimise legacy risks," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

**Environmental and sustainability dimensions — multimodal peer-reviewed finding.** The Annual Reviews 2021 paper (Nikitas, Thomopoulos & Milakis) examines road, rail, water, and air automated transport from a sustainability lens. The study finds that automated mobility has the potential, **under certain conditions**, to lessen air pollution, environmental degradation, and energy consumption, but also identifies that if not thoughtfully planned, automated mobility could generate serious challenges including increased vulnerability to hacking, software and hardware flaws, privacy loss, and liability allocation challenges.

*Source: "automated mobility has also the potential, under certain conditions, to lessen air pollution, environmental degradation, and energy consumption," Annual Review of Environment and Resources, Vol. 46:167–192, 2021. https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-environ-012220-024657

*Source: "increased vulnerability to hacking; software and hardware flaws; privacy loss and travel data exploitation; liability allocation challenges," Annual Review of Environment and Resources, Vol. 46:167–192, 2021. https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-environ-012220-024657

**Methodology limits — Annual Reviews 2021.** The paper identifies a knowledge gap: **scarcity of non-technical research outside the autonomous car's realm**.

*Source: "identifies current knowledge gaps highlighting the scarcity of non-technical research outside the autonomous car's realm," Annual Review of Environment and Resources, Vol. 46:167–192, 2021. https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-environ-012220-024657

**Conceptual model — the nexus condition.** The 2021 paper proposes a conceptual model that contextualises the automation-connectivity-electrification-sharing-multimodality nexus as the only way forward for vehicle automation to reach its pro-environmental and resource-saving potential.

*Source: "proposes a conceptual model that contextualizes the automation-connectivity-electrification-sharing-multimodality nexus as the only way forward for vehicle automation to reach its pro-environmental and resource-saving potential," Annual Review of Environment and Resources, Vol. 46:167–192, 2021. https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-environ-012220-024657

**Freight-truck tagging in primary source.** The primary ITF report tags include "Trucks," confirming the report's intended scope extends to freight vehicles, not only passenger cars.

*Source: "Tags Automation , Automobiles , Drivers , International Transport , Motor Vehicles , Motorways , Parking Facilities , Regulations , Roads , Taxis , Testing , Traffic , Trucks , Uncertainty , Vehicles," ITF Corporate Partnership Board Report, 30 April 2015. https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving

---

## Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

*Operational implication:* For the workspace operating road transport as a secondary mode, the ITF framework's core finding — that regulatory pathways remain uncertain and that deployment strategies are not yet settled — means that planning for autonomous or highly automated freight vehicles on road corridors cannot be treated as a near-term compliance obligation. The uncertainty finding has a direct operational corollary: the workspace cannot yet anchor road-network operational planning to a fixed autonomous-vehicle regulatory baseline in any of the three tested jurisdictions (Europe, Japan, United States).

*Operational implication:* The insurance-change finding is directly relevant to road freight operations. As driver automation increases on vehicles used by the workspace's carrier partners, the liability and insurance framework governing road cargo movements will shift. The workspace's current cargo liability clauses — drafted against a human-driver default — may require review against emerging automated-vehicle insurance frameworks, in jurisdictions where those frameworks are being developed.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether changes to insurance and liability allocation under emerging AV frameworks affect the workspace's contractual cargo liability exposure under CMR, MIA, or jurisdiction-specific road freight terms requires counsel review. The ITF report flags the issue as a live regulatory design question, not a settled rule.

*Operational implication:* The Annual Reviews 2021 sustainability finding — that environmental benefits from automation are conditional on the automation-electrification-sharing-connectivity nexus being present simultaneously — matters commercially for workspaces in emission-reduction conversations with clients. Citing AV deployment alone as an emissions reduction pathway would be technically unsupported; the peer-reviewed model sets out that the nexus condition governs outcomes.

*Operational implication:* The freight-truck scope of the ITF report means that regulatory developments tracked through this resource apply to the workspace's carrier partner fleet, not only to passenger or last-mile vehicles. As ITF updates its work (the primary source dates from 2015; the 2025 AI/ML report listed under New Sources Identified suggests the programme continues), the workspace has an ongoing intelligence feed relevant to its road carrier relationships.

---

## What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**1. Carrier contract clauses and liability review.** The ITF finding that insurance frameworks will change as the driving task shifts to machines is a direct prompt to audit road freight carrier contracts for automation-related liability gaps. *Analytical inference:* This is a pre-competitive window: contracts drafted before automated truck deployment scales may default to human-driver liability assumptions that become legally ambiguous once partial or conditional automation is active on vehicles carrying the workspace's cargo.

**2. Sustainability claims discipline.** The Annual Reviews nexus model establishes that automated transport produces environmental benefits only when electrification, connectivity, and sharing are present simultaneously. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces that publicly claim emissions reductions from road automation without qualifying the nexus condition risk overclaiming in client sustainability reports or RFP responses.

**3. Regulatory monitoring architecture.** The ITF CPB programme is an active, continuing body — the primary 2015 report is the foundation document; the ITF has since launched an "Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning and Regulation Roundtable" (listed in its programme index), and a 2025 AI/ML-AV report has been identified as a corroborating source. *Analytical inference:* Monitoring this programme provides early-warning intelligence on regulatory convergence across Europe, Japan, and the United States — three of the workspace's primary trade-lane geographies.

**4. Infrastructure dependency for road corridors.** The OECD "Preparing Infrastructure for Automated Vehicles" title (listed under New Sources Identified, content not verified from source block) addresses physical, digital, and institutional infrastructure requirements. *Analytical inference:* For road operations on corridors where infrastructure readiness for automated vehicles is lagging, the workspace may face a gap between carrier claims of AV capability and actual operational deployment.

*Specific penalty/enforcement figures for AV non-compliance not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.*

*Specific commercial deployment timelines for freight-grade autonomous trucks not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.*

---

## Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- ITF/OECD research — conducted with CPB member companies including industry operators — frames AV regulatory development as genuinely uncertain across all three major testing jurisdictions. The workspace is monitoring this programme as part of its road carrier risk intelligence.
- Peer-reviewed research (Annual Reviews 2021, open access) establishes that environmental benefits from automated transport are conditional on the convergence of automation, electrification, connectivity, and sharing. The workspace applies this framing when evaluating carrier sustainability claims.
- The ITF has continued this work into 2025, indicating that the policy landscape is still being actively shaped rather than settled.

**What the workspace should NOT claim:**
- Do not claim a specific timeline for commercially available autonomous freight trucks based on this source set. No deployment timeline appears in any of the verified source blocks.
- Do not claim that AV deployment reduces road freight emissions without qualifying the nexus condition. The peer-reviewed finding is explicitly conditional.
- Do not present the 2015 ITF report as current regulatory guidance. It is a foundational policy framing document; the 2025 AI/ML report (not yet content-verified) is the current update to track.

**Questions to pose to carrier partners:**
- What automation level (SAE or equivalent) applies to vehicles currently operating on the workspace's road corridors?
- How does the carrier's insurance coverage address automated or partially automated vehicle incidents, and does the policy language track ITF/OECD regulatory developments?
- What infrastructure dependencies govern the carrier's AV deployment claims?

---

## What the Finding Does Not Resolve

*Specific deployment timelines for Level 4 or Level 5 autonomous freight trucks on commercially active corridors (Americas, Europe, Asia) are not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-05.* The ITF 2015 report frames expected acceleration without specifying dates; no date-specific claim appears in the verified source blocks.

The finding does not resolve whether emerging AV insurance and liability frameworks in any jurisdiction have moved from design to legislation since the 2015 publication. *Analytical inference:* The 2025 NHTSA page and the 2025 Eno Center Federal Policy Wrapped (both listed under New Sources Identified, content not verified) are the primary candidates for resolving the US-jurisdiction update; the ITF 2025 AI/ML report is the candidate for the multilateral regulatory update.

The Annual Reviews 2021 paper explicitly identifies that non-technical research on automated freight (as distinct from passenger cars) remains scarce. The environmental benefit findings are therefore more robustly supported for passenger transport than for freight-specific operations.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether changes in AV insurance frameworks in the EU, US, or Japan create pass-through exposure for freight forwarders acting as intermediaries (rather than as vehicle operators or cargo owners) requires counsel review. The ITF report frames this as an open regulatory design question.

The finding does not address rail or air automation in sufficient depth for the workspace's air-primary mode. The Annual Reviews 2021 paper covers automated air transport in a section but the content is behind a paywall and no substantive claims from that section are available in the provided source block.

**Re-check window:** Given that the ITF AI/ML-AV report is dated 2025 and was blocked at retrieval, and the NHTSA 2025 framework update was not content-verified, set a re-check against both sources within 60 days to capture any enforcement or deployment milestones that shift the regulatory baseline from MONITORING to WINDOW CLOSING or ACTION REQUIRED for road operations.

Severity: MONITORING.

---

## Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Date | Type | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Automated and Autonomous Driving (Corporate Partnership Board Report) | International Transport Forum / OECD | 30 April 2015 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body position | https://itf-oecd.org/automated-and-autonomous-driving |
| 2 | The Environmental and Resource Dimensions of Automated Transport: A Nexus for Enabling Vehicle Automation to Support Sustainable Urban Mobility | Annual Review of Environment and Resources, Vol. 46:167–192 (Nikitas, Thomopoulos & Milakis) | October 2021 | Tier 6 — Peer-reviewed academic paper (open access) | https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-environ-012220-024657 |

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| ITF/OECD – AI, Machine Learning and Regulation: The Case of Automated Vehicles (2025) | https://www.itf-oecd.org/ai-machine-learning-and-regulation-case-automated-vehicles | 3 | 2025 ITF report updating the primary 2015 AV resource with AI-specific regulatory analysis across road, rail, and shipping; blocked at retrieval by Cloudflare — highest-priority re-fetch to determine whether the regulatory baseline has shifted from MONITORING. |
| OECD – Automated and Autonomous Driving: Regulation under Uncertainty (Policy Paper No. 7) | https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/automated-and-autonomous-driving_5jlwvzdfk640-en.html | 2 | OECD canonical policy paper underpinning the ITF CPB report; covers safety, liability, and regulatory frameworks; returned only navigation HTML — re-fetch for safety and insurance framework detail directly relevant to carrier contract clause review. |
| OECD – Preparing Infrastructure for Automated Vehicles | https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/preparing-infrastructure-for-automated-vehicles_b96ca198-en.html | 3 | ITF Working Group report on physical, digital, and institutional infrastructure requirements; returned only navigation HTML — re-fetch to ground infrastructure-dependency claims for road corridor operations. |
| NHTSA – Automated Vehicles for Safety (2025 framework) | https://www.nhtsa.gov/vehicle-safety/automated-vehicles-safety | 2 | US federal regulator page covering 2025 USDOT/NHTSA AV framework and Standing General Order amendments; content not available from provided source block — re-fetch for US-jurisdiction enforcement and exemption program updates relevant to carriers operating on Americas corridors. |
| Eno Center for Transportation – 2025 Autonomous Vehicles Federal Policy Wrapped | https://enotrans.org/article/2025-autonomous-vehicles-federal-policy-wrapped/ | 4 | Authoritative US transport policy think-tank summary of 2025 AV rulemakings and freight driverless deployments; content not available from provided source block — re-fetch to confirm whether any 2025 US federal driverless freight deployment milestone shifts severity from MONITORING. |

prior.title: World Resources Institute
prior.summary: Transport decarbonisation, freight emissions, EV transition research.
prior.what_is_it: World Resources Institute conducting research on transport decarbonisation, freight emissions, and clean energy transition.
prior.why_matters: WRI is the co-convener of GHG Protocol. Their transport research directly shapes the emissions accounting standards freight forwarders must use.
prior.reasoning: WRI research underpins GHG Protocol and major policy frameworks affecting freight.
prior.full_brief:
# World Resources Institute: Freight Decarbonization Research Portfolio — Strategic Intelligence Brief

---

## Section 1: What the Research Found

**Headline Finding — Freight Decarbonization Pathway Architecture**

WRI's freight decarbonization research portfolio, synthesised across multiple publications by Kevin Kennedy and John Feldmann (September–November 2023) and the WRI India e-FAST India initiative (launched 2022), establishes a multi-pathway architecture for freight emissions reduction. The portfolio does not offer a single-technology solution; it maps which decarbonization tools apply to which freight modes, distances, and geographies, and where structural barriers currently prevent deployment.

**Finding 1 — Freight's growing emissions share in the US**

FACT: Freight emissions in the United States are an increasing share of total sector emissions, rising from 24% in 1990 to 32% in 2021, and were 60% greater than they were in 1990 in absolute terms. *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: How U.S. Policies and Investments Are Reducing Emissions," WRI Technical Perspective, Kevin Kennedy, November 22, 2023. https://www.wri.org/technical-perspectives/decarbonizing-freight-how-us-policies-and-investments-are-reducing-emissions.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces whose Scope 3 emissions accounting covers trucked, air-freighted, and ocean-freighted goods, the directional signal is clear — regulatory and client pressure on freight emissions is growing in proportion to freight's share of the transport sector, not shrinking.

**Finding 2 — Mode-specific decarbonization viability**

FACT: "Electrification is a good option for some freight transportation, such as short- and medium-haul trucking, but is more challenging for other parts of the freight system, such as ocean shipping or aviation." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, Kevin Kennedy and John Feldmann, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

FACT: "Batteries are not well suited for shipping freight across the ocean. With current technology, increasing battery capacity to increase range means a significant weight penalty and reduced cargo space. Battery weight is also a major limiting factor for electrifying aviation, particularly for long-distance flights. These kinds of transportation will require energy-dense alternative fuels to decarbonize." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating air as primary mode and ocean as tertiary, the WRI framework places both modes in the "energy-dense alternative fuels required" category — not the electrification category. Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) and low-carbon marine fuels become the relevant compliance and procurement lever, not fleet electrification.

**Finding 3 — Drop-in fuels: transitional role only, not full decarbonization**

FACT: "Current alternative transportation fuels offer some greenhouse gas advantage over gasoline and diesel, but do not offer a pathway to fully decarbonizing the transport sector." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

FACT: "Because sustainable production of these alternative liquid fuels is likely to be limited due to limited supply of sustainable biomass resources and high production costs, it will be important that they are only used where other options are not available. High on that list is long-distance aviation." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

*Analytical inference:* WRI's positioning of drop-in fuels — including SAF — as a scarce, prioritised resource for aviation specifically has direct implications for workspaces that procure or book SAF credits: the research framing supports prioritising SAF for air freight over road or ocean applications, which aligns with how emerging SAF mandate frameworks (e.g., EU ReFuelEU Aviation, UK SAF mandate) are structured.

**Finding 4 — Hydrogen and ammonia as long-term freight solutions**

FACT: "Sustainable hydrogen and ammonia offer possible long-term solutions for freight transport that are difficult to electrify." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

FACT: "While fuel cell technology itself is relatively mature, widespread adoption in truck, rail and marine freight transport will require significant development and cost reduction of low-, zero- or negative-carbon hydrogen or other fuels, along with distribution and fueling infrastructure." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels," WRI Insights, September 18, 2023. https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels.

*Analytical inference:* The cost-reduction and infrastructure-development prerequisites cited by WRI suggest hydrogen and ammonia remain pre-commercial at scale for freight. Workspaces sourcing ocean carriers should monitor vessel-level hydrogen/ammonia trials as leading indicators of future fuel availability, but procurement decisions should not assume these fuels are available at commercial scale in the near term.

**Finding 5 — India freight electrification: scale, barriers, and current deployment**

FACT: India's trucking industry comprises 6.3 million trucks and handles 70% of domestic freight, consuming 838 billion liters of diesel annually and emitting 120 million tCO₂e — nearly 60% of India's ground transport emissions. *Source: "Accelerating India's Freight Decarbonization," WRI India, https://wri-india.org/initiatives/accelerating-indias-freight-decarbonization.

FACT: "Despite the urgent need for decarbonization, India has only 869 electric medium- and heavy-duty freight vehicles." *Source: "Accelerating India's Freight Decarbonization," WRI India, https://wri-india.org/initiatives/accelerating-indias-freight-decarbonization.

FACT: The e-FAST India platform was "Launched in 2022 by NITI Aayog, with WRI India as the lead partner," and has secured "commitments for 7,750 e-trucks at the Clean Energy Ministerial." *Source: "Accelerating India's Freight Decarbonization," WRI India, https://wri-india.org/initiatives/accelerating-indias-freight-decarbonization.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces managing road freight on Indian trade lanes or sourcing from Indian manufacturing hubs, the electrification gap is structural and near-term: 869 electric M/HDVs against a 6.3-million-vehicle diesel fleet means green road freight credentials are not available at operational scale in India today. The e-FAST commitments for 7,750 e-trucks represent a signal of direction but not near-term availability.

**Finding 6 — Equity and air quality in electrification deployment (US school bus research)**

FACT: WRI's 2024 research finds "the most polluting buses in the US fleet — those that are older and lack modern emissions reduction technology — are disproportionately concentrated in school districts with higher shares of low-income households and residents of color." *Source: "Electrifying US School Bus Fleets Equitably to Reduce Air Pollution Exposure in Underserved Communities," WRI/Equitable Cities, August 5, 2024. https://www.wri.org/research/electrifying-us-school-bus-fleets-equitably-reduce-air-pollution-exposure-underserved.

FACT: "As of December 2022, most ESB commitments tend to be in districts with lower incomes, more residents of color, and the worst air quality, partly due to federal and state programs that have prioritized support for disadvantaged districts." *Source: "Electrifying US School Bus Fleets Equitably to Reduce Air Pollution Exposure in Underserved Communities," WRI/Equitable Cities, August 5, 2024. https://www.wri.org/research/electrifying-us-school-bus-fleets-equitably-reduce-air-pollution-exposure-underserved.

*Analytical inference:* The ESB equity research, while outside freight forwarding operations, is directly relevant to workspaces whose clients include US public-sector logistics, municipal distribution, or last-mile urban delivery, where equity-weighted electrification funding eligibility increasingly determines which routes and zones receive clean transport infrastructure first.

**Finding 7 — US biofuel policy gap: advanced biofuels underdelivered**

FACT: "The target for 2021 established in 2007 was 17.5 billion gallons of advanced biofuels, of which 13.5 billion gallons were to be cellulosic (although EPA has consistently waived that requirement). Actual production was just over 5 billion gallons of advanced biofuels, of which less than 600 million gallons was cellulosic." *Source: "Decarbonizing Freight: How U.S. Policies and Investments Are Reducing Emissions," WRI Technical Perspective, November 22, 2023. https://www.wri.org/technical-perspectives/decarbonizing-freight-how-us-policies-and-investments-are-reducing-emissions.

*Analytical inference:* The persistent shortfall in advanced biofuel production against US Renewable Fuel Standard targets — less than one-third of the 2021 target delivered — is a structural constraint on SAF supply that workspaces should treat as a baseline assumption when modelling SAF availability and cost. Supply scarcity directly informs SAF credit pricing and carrier surcharge trajectories.

---

## Section 2: Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**For air-primary workspaces (primary mode):**

*Analytical inference:* WRI's framework explicitly identifies long-distance aviation as the highest-priority use case for scarce sustainable drop-in fuels. Workspaces booking air freight are therefore operating in the mode where SAF is simultaneously most needed, most contested, and most likely to attract regulatory mandates. SAF mandate frameworks in the EU (ReFuelEU Aviation) and UK are structurally consistent with the WRI prioritisation logic. The commercial consequence is directional cost pressure on air freight surcharges as SAF volumes increase under mandate, and supply constraints keep per-unit costs elevated.

*Analytical inference:* The WRI finding that drop-in fuels "do not offer a pathway to fully decarbonizing the transport sector" sets a ceiling on what air carriers can credibly claim from SAF blending alone. Workspaces using SAF credit purchases as a client-facing sustainability credential should understand that WRI research explicitly frames this as a transitional, not terminal, solution.

**For road-secondary workspaces:**

*Analytical inference:* WRI's mode analysis treats short- and medium-haul trucking as the strongest electrification candidate. For workspaces operating last-mile or regional distribution legs — including high-value cargo drayage from air hubs — the electrification trajectory on road is materially more advanced than on ocean or air, and the policy support (IRA clean fuel credits, Bipartisan Infrastructure Law EV charging funding, California ACT/ACF regulation) is more developed.

*Analytical inference:* The California Advanced Clean Trucks and Advanced Clean Fleets regulations, with eight states now adopted or adopting, represent a concrete compliance exposure on road freight lanes where workspaces use third-party carriers subject to California jurisdiction. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace's road carrier contracts contain pass-through provisions for ZEV compliance cost increases requires counsel and contract review.

**For ocean-tertiary workspaces:**

*Analytical inference:* WRI's research confirms ocean shipping is among the hardest-to-decarbonise freight modes, with battery electrification technically unsuited and hydrogen/ammonia commercially pre-scale. For workspaces using ocean as a tertiary mode on select trade lanes, this framing supports close monitoring of IMO GHG strategy milestones and FuelEU Maritime implementation as the primary regulatory exposure on ocean legs, rather than near-term fuel switching.

**For India trade lanes:**

*Operational implication:* The e-FAST India gap — 869 electric M/HDVs against a national fleet of 6.3 million — confirms that green road freight on Indian domestic legs is not operationally available at scale. Workspaces sourcing from Indian manufacturers, operating inbound consolidation, or managing last-mile delivery within India should not currently represent Indian road legs as part of a zero-emission supply chain. The e-FAST 7,750-truck commitment represents a future pipeline, not current capacity.

**For US domestic and cross-border lanes:**

*Operational implication:* The California Low Carbon Fuel Standard's 2022 benchmark — "12.6% below the 2010 baseline, exceeding the 10% reduction target" — is a FACT indicator that LCFS-compliant fuels are reaching the California market. For workspaces moving cargo on California-origin or -destination road legs, this policy creates a differentiated fuel cost environment versus non-LCFS states.

---

## Section 3: What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Decision 1 — SAF procurement and credit strategy**

*Analytical inference:* WRI's characterisation of sustainable drop-in fuels as scarce and best reserved for aviation strengthens the case for workspaces to secure forward SAF commitments or credit agreements with air carriers now, before regulatory mandates compress the voluntary market. The finding does not tell the workspace which carrier to approach or at what price — that requires carrier-specific data — but it establishes why early positioning has value.

**Decision 2 — Carrier sustainability claims verification**

*Analytical inference:* WRI's explicit finding that current alternative fuels "do not offer a pathway to fully decarbonizing the transport sector" should prompt workspaces to scrutinise carrier net-zero or "carbon neutral" claims for air and ocean modes. A claim of full decarbonisation via drop-in fuels alone is inconsistent with the WRI research framework. Workspaces accepting such claims without verification carry reputational and, potentially, contractual risk if clients subsequently challenge them.

**Decision 3 — India sourcing lane sustainability narrative**

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces managing supply chains with Indian manufacturing origin points should update their sustainability narratives to reflect that Indian road freight cannot currently be represented as electrified or low-emission at commercial scale. The e-FAST India trajectory (launched 2022, 7,750-truck commitment secured as of most recent WRI India reporting) is a credible forward signal but does not change current-state Scope 3 accounting.

**Decision 4 — Scope 3 freight emissions baseline**

*Analytical inference:* The US freight emissions trend — 32% of transport sector GHG in 2021, up from 24% in 1990 — is directionally consistent with international freight growth patterns. Workspaces building CSRD or voluntary Scope 3 disclosures should treat freight emissions as a growing proportional contributor to their overall footprint, not a stable baseline, particularly as air and ocean volumes grow.

**Decision 5 — Road carrier contract clauses (US)**

*Analytical inference:* The eight-state spread of California's Advanced Clean Trucks regulation and the IRA clean fuel production credit framework create a policy environment in which road carrier costs for ZEV compliance and alternative fuels will increase. Workspaces without fuel escalation or ZEV compliance cost pass-through clauses in road carrier contracts face unhedged exposure as these policies tighten through 2027 and beyond.

---

## Section 4: Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "WRI research, published 2023, confirms that aviation is the freight mode where sustainable drop-in fuels are most critical and most constrained. We're actively engaging with our air carriers on SAF availability and blending commitments because the supply picture is tight."
- "For our road legs, the electrification trajectory is clearest — US and California policy is driving real ZEV adoption on short- and medium-haul trucking, and we're monitoring our carrier base for compliance readiness under the Advanced Clean Trucks framework."
- "On Indian trade lanes, we want to be honest: the e-FAST India platform is building toward large-scale electrification, but the current fleet has under 900 electric M/HDVs against 6.3 million diesel trucks. We're not claiming green road freight on Indian legs today."

**What the workspace should not say:**

- Do not claim SAF purchases make air freight "carbon neutral" or "fully decarbonised." WRI's research explicitly states current alternative fuels do not offer a full decarbonisation pathway. This claim is not supportable and contradicts published peer-reviewed research.
- Do not represent hydrogen or ammonia as a near-term ocean freight solution. WRI characterises these as long-term pathways requiring significant cost reduction and infrastructure development not yet achieved.
- Do not claim that biofuel or renewable diesel blending on road legs constitutes a decarbonisation strategy. WRI's analysis of US Renewable Fuel Standard outcomes shows advanced biofuel supply has consistently fallen well short of targets.

**Questions to pose to air carriers:**

- What percentage of fuel on this route is currently SAF by blend ratio?
- Is that SAF HEFA-based (waste feedstock) or crop-based? (WRI distinguishes these; crop-based carries land-use carbon risk.)
- What is the forward SAF blending commitment under ReFuelEU Aviation or UK SAF mandate compliance?

**Questions to pose to ocean carriers:**

- What is the current Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) rating of the vessels on this service?
- What is the carrier's stated pathway to compliance with IMO 2030 GHG targets?

**Pitfalls:**

- Citing WRI research to a client without having read the specific publication — the findings are nuanced (transitional role for SAF, not terminal solution) and the distinction matters in client conversations.
- Conflating the e-FAST India commitment pipeline (7,750 trucks secured) with current available green freight capacity (869 vehicles deployed).

---

## Section 5: What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**Open question 1 — Cost curves for SAF at commercial scale**

The WRI research establishes that SAF production cost is currently too high for widespread deployment and that "significant investments are needed to help bring down those costs." *Specific cost per litre or cost-reduction trajectory not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.* This is the critical variable for workspaces building forward pricing models on air freight sustainability surcharges.

**Open question 2 — Timing of hydrogen/ammonia commercial readiness for ocean freight**

WRI identifies hydrogen and ammonia as "possible long-term solutions" but does not specify when commercial readiness will be achieved. *Specific commercial readiness date or milestone not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.* IMO GHG Strategy 2023 milestones and the outcome of the IMO MEPC 83 session are the authoritative events to monitor.

**Open question 3 — US policy continuity under current administration**

The WRI 2023 US policy analysis was published under an IRA and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law framework. Several of the cited programmes — IRA clean fuel production tax credits, EPA Clean Trucks Plan rules, federal EV charging infrastructure funding — are subject to policy review by the current administration. *Specific status of IRA clean fuel credits and EPA Clean Trucks Plan rules as of 2026-06-09 requires confirmation from current Federal Register or EPA rulemakings — this brief cannot confirm their current operative status.* *Legal Confirmation Required:* workspaces relying on IRA clean fuel credit availability in carrier negotiations should confirm current regulatory status with counsel or via EPA/IRS published guidance.

**Open question 4 — India e-FAST conversion from commitment to deployment**

The e-FAST India platform reports commitments for 7,750 e-trucks. *Analytical inference:* The gap between commitment and deployment is the operationally relevant variable; no sourced timeline for when committed e-trucks will be deployed and operationally available on commercial freight lanes was found in the source blocks. *Specific deployment timeline for e-FAST India e-truck commitments not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.*

**Related research that converges or may contradict:**

- ICCT (International Council on Clean Transportation) publishes independent analysis on SAF lifecycle emissions and cost trajectories that may converge with or diverge from WRI's framing — listed as a candidate source below but not available in the source pool for this brief.
- IMO MEPC sessions produce binding GHG strategy updates for ocean freight that set the regulatory context the WRI research framework anticipates but does not itself govern.

**MONITORING — Re-check window:** The e-FAST India deployment data and US policy continuity items are the two most operationally time-sensitive open questions. *Analytical inference:* recommend re-checking e-FAST India published updates (wri-india.org) and EPA/IRS regulatory status quarterly. Set re-check: Q3 2026.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decarbonizing Freight: Opportunities and Obstacles for Clean Fuels | https://www.wri.org/insights/decarbonizing-freight-clean-fuels | 4 | WRI primary analysis establishing mode-specific decarbonisation viability for truck, rail, ocean and aviation; grounding source for SAF prioritisation and hydrogen/ammonia long-term framing used throughout the brief. |
| Decarbonizing Freight: How U.S. Policies and Investments Are Reducing Emissions | https://www.wri.org/technical-perspectives/decarbonizing-freight-how-us-policies-and-investments-are-reducing-emissions | 4 | WRI technical perspective providing quantified US freight emissions share (32% of transport sector, 2021) and detailed policy analysis of IRA, Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, California ACT/ACF, and Renewable Fuel Standard shortfalls; grounds the US policy and road freight sections. |
| Accelerating India's Freight Decarbonization (e-FAST India) | https://wri-india.org/initiatives/accelerating-indias-freight-decarbonization | 4 | WRI India initiative page providing fleet size (6.3M trucks), diesel consumption, current EV deployment (869 vehicles), and e-FAST commitment figures (7,750 e-trucks); grounds the India trade lane operational implication. |
| Electrifying US School Bus Fleets Equitably to Reduce Air Pollution Exposure in Underserved Communities | https://www.wri.org/research/electrifying-us-school-bus-fleets-equitably-reduce-air-pollution-exposure-underserved | 3 | WRI/Equitable Cities peer-reviewed 2024 report; grounds the equity distribution finding on ESB adoption and establishes the data basis for equity-weighted electrification funding eligibility discussions with US public-sector clients. |

---

prior.title: Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR) — Regulation (EU) 2023/1804
prior.summary: Regulation 2023/1804 of the European Parliament and Council establishes a comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence in the EU. The regulation introduces a risk-based approach with requirements for prohibited AI practices, high-risk AI systems, and transparency obligations.
prior.full_brief:
# Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 — Regulatory Fact Document

## Section 1: Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document provides a workspace-anchored regulatory intelligence brief on Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 of the European Parliament and of the Council on the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure (AFIR), which repeals Directive 2014/94/EU. The brief is structured to serve legal counsel, operations leads, and commercial leadership at a freight forwarding operation whose transport mode priority places air freight first, road freight second, and ocean/maritime third, and whose trade lanes span Europe as well as international corridors into the EU.

**Conventions:** All claims are labeled FACT, ANALYSIS, or LEGAL per the workspace's claim-provenance contract. Binding-obligation verbs appear only in verbatim-quoted FACT statements or are recast under labeled analysis or *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts. The workspace is referenced by operational role, never by name. Inline citations follow each subsection.

**Coverage date:** This brief reflects source content as of the current consolidated version of AFIR dated 8 January 2026 and corroborating sources through April 2025.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, EUR-Lex OJ record, European Parliament and Council, 22 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng

---

## Section 2: What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

### What AFIR Is

FACT: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 is "on the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure, and repealing Directive 2014/94/EU," adopted by the European Parliament and the Council on 13 September 2023 and published in the Official Journal L 234 on 22 September 2023.
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, EUR-Lex OJ record, European Parliament and Council, 22 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng

FACT: "The regulation is applicable since 13 April 2024."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

FACT: The current consolidated version of the regulation is dated 08/01/2026, indicating the regulation has been amended since original publication.
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, EUR-Lex OJ record, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng

FACT: The regulation "sets a number of mandatory national targets for the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure in the EU, for road vehicles, vessels and stationary aircraft."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

FACT: The specific objectives of the regulation are: "to ensure minimum infrastructure to support the required uptake of alternative fuel vehicles across all transport modes and in all EU Member States to meet the EU's climate objectives; to ensure full interoperability of the infrastructure; and to ensure comprehensive user information and adequate payment options at alternative fuels infrastructure."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

### Jurisdictional Scope

FACT: The regulation applies across all EU Member States and is noted as relevant with EEA relevance ("Text with EEA relevance"), covering road, maritime, inland waterway, aviation, and rail transport modes.
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, EUR-Lex OJ record, European Parliament and Council, 22 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng

### Why This Applies to the Workspace

AFIR does not directly impose obligations on freight forwarders as a defined compliance object. However, the regulation materially affects the infrastructure environment in which the workspace's road transport providers, air cargo handlers, and maritime operators function across European trade lanes. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces operating road freight across TEN-T corridors will encounter the downstream effects of mandatory charging and hydrogen refuelling deployment targets — both in carrier fleet transition timelines and in any surcharge or fuel-cost pass-through mechanisms that carriers adopt as they invest in alternative-fuel-capable fleets. *Analytical inference:* For workspaces with air freight as the primary mode, the regulation's requirements for electricity supply to stationary aircraft at TEN-T airports carry direct relevance to ground handling cost structures at EU hub airports. *Analytical inference:* For maritime movements — even where ocean is a tertiary mode — shore-side electricity supply requirements at TEN-T ports affect port handling cost structures and port call feasibility for vessels in the workspace's supply chain.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether freight forwarders, in any of their roles (as shipper, importer, or agent of record), are directly obligated parties under any provision of AFIR requires counsel review. The regulation's direct compliance objects appear to be Member States (for national target delivery) and infrastructure operators — not freight forwarders as intermediaries.

---

## Section 3: Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### MONITORING — Understand infrastructure deployment status on key TEN-T road corridors

*Operational implication:* Road carriers operating for the workspace on TEN-T core and comprehensive network routes in Europe are operating in an environment of mandatory EV charging and hydrogen refuelling deployment. The workspace should confirm with road carriers which corridors they are transitioning and on what timeline, to anticipate fuel surcharge changes and carrier fleet availability shifts. No 30-day regulatory deadline applies to the workspace directly, but forward contracting without understanding carrier transition exposure is a gap.

**Action:** Brief operations leads on TEN-T corridor infrastructure rollout status. Request carrier disclosure on alternative-fuel fleet share and expected surcharge structures.

### MONITORING — Verify airport shore-power deployment status at primary EU hub airports

*Operational implication:* The regulation sets targets for electricity supply to stationary aircraft at TEN-T airports. For air-primary workspaces, ground handling cost structures at EU hubs may shift as airports incur infrastructure investment costs. The workspace should identify which airports in its primary routing are TEN-T core versus comprehensive network airports and whether shore-power deployment timelines are on track.

**Action:** Request ground handling agent cost disclosures at primary EU hub airports referencing airport-side infrastructure investment programmes.

### MONITORING — Penalty and enforcement gap: no penalty provision identified from provided source content as of 8 January 2026

*Specific penalty/enforcement provisions not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-01.* The source blocks confirm AFIR is in force and applicable but do not reproduce penalty articles. The EUR-Lex full HTML text (Article 24 — Reporting and review) is the likely location of enforcement provisions; this requires retrieval and legal review.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The penalty and enforcement provisions of AFIR applicable in each Member State require counsel review to determine what, if any, exposure exists for entities in the supply chain.

---

## Section 4: How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

FACT: AFIR "should lay down mandatory minimum targets for the deployment of publicly accessible recharging and refuelling infrastructures for road vehicles," as stated in recital 9 of the regulation.
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 full HTML text, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R1804

FACT: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 (FuelEU Maritime) "sets requirements in particular for the use of on-shore power that can only be fulfilled if an adequate level of shore-side electricity supply is deployed in the trans-European transport network (TEN-T) ports."
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 full HTML text, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R1804

The compliance chain runs: **Member States** (obligated to deliver national targets) → **Infrastructure operators** (obligated to deploy charging, hydrogen, and shore-power points per the regulation's articles) → **Vehicle and vessel operators/carriers** (who access the infrastructure and whose fleet transition decisions flow from its availability) → **Freight forwarders and logistics intermediaries** (who experience the effects through carrier cost structures, fleet availability, and surcharge mechanisms).

*Analytical inference:* The workspace, acting as freight forwarder, sits at the end of this compliance chain and is not a primary obligated entity under AFIR. However, the workspace's commercial relationships with road carriers, air handlers, and maritime operators mean it absorbs the commercial consequences of the infrastructure build-out — and has a commercial interest in understanding which carriers are positioned to transition and which are not.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any specific role the workspace plays — for example, as a shipper or importer of record on specific transactions — triggers any AFIR-adjacent reporting or data obligations (including under Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655 on data interoperability) requires counsel review.

---

## Section 5: Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

*No content for this section as of 2026-06-01: The source blocks reference a Questions and Answers document published by the European Commission on AFIR, but the Q&A text is not reproduced in the provided source content and the full article-by-article text of AFIR (Articles 3–26 and Annexes I–IV) is not reproduced verbatim in the source blocks beyond recitals 1–11. Section omitted under the integrity rule to avoid fabrication of article-level provisions.*

---

## Section 6: Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

### MONITORING — Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655 on AFIR Data Interoperability (April 2025)

FACT: The European Commission news of April 2025 describes "Implementing Regulation 2025/655 adding real-time data specs under AFIR" as a current-status development absent from the primary EUR-Lex OJ source.
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE news on Implementing Regulation 2025/655, April 2025. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/news-events/news/commission-enhances-interoperability-and-transparency-alternative-fuels-infrastructure-data-2025-04-11_en

*Analytical inference:* This implementing regulation adds data-layer obligations (real-time data specifications for AFIR infrastructure) that infrastructure operators and potentially data platform participants will need to comply with. For workspaces with logistics technology platforms that consume European alternative fuels infrastructure data, this may create integration requirements or opportunities for transparency in route planning tools.

**Re-check window:** Retrieve the full text of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655 to confirm scope and effective date. Priority: within 60 days.

### MONITORING — European Parliament TRAN Committee Background Paper on AFIR Implementation (February 2026)

FACT: The European Parliament TRAN Committee published a background paper on AFIR implementation in February 2026, covering "HDV charging gaps, AFIF co-financing, and TEN-T corridor pilot mapping" as of that date.
*Source: European Parliament TRAN Committee Background Paper on AFIR Implementation, February 2026. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/meetdocs/2024_2029/plmrep/COMMITTEES/TRAN/DV/2026/02-24/Backgroundpaper-AFIRimplementation_EN.pdf

*Analytical inference:* The identification of HDV (heavy-duty vehicle) charging gaps in a February 2026 legislative-scrutiny paper signals that the TEN-T corridor build-out for road freight is behind or uneven. For workspaces dependent on road carriers for EU freight, this is a lead indicator of continued diesel-fleet dependence and slower-than-expected alternative-fuel adoption among HDV fleets on TEN-T routes.

**Re-check window:** Monitor TRAN Committee outputs quarterly for any recommendation to the Commission to accelerate enforcement or revise targets.

---

## Section 7: Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Is the workspace a direct obligated party under AFIR (as opposed to a downstream-affected party)? | *Legal Confirmation Required* |
| Does the workspace's role as data consumer or platform user trigger obligations under Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655? | *Legal Confirmation Required* |
| Are any airports or ports in the workspace's primary routing in scope as TEN-T core vs. comprehensive network nodes? | Research gap — requires route-by-route mapping against TEN-T network designations |
| Do the workspace's road carrier contracts contain provisions that pass through alternative-fuel infrastructure costs? | Operational gap — requires contract review |

---

## Section 8: Substantive Requirements

### Road Transport: Light-Duty Vehicle (LDV) Recharging Targets

FACT: "For publicly available electric recharging infrastructure for light duty road vehicles (cars and vans), the regulation sets out mandatory national fleet based targets (e.g. for every battery electric light duty vehicle a total power output of at least 1.3 kW must be provided through publicly accessible recharging stations while for every plug-in hybrid light-duty vehicle, a total power output of at least 0.8 kW must be provided)."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

FACT: The regulation "sets out distance-based targets for light duty and heavy-duty road vehicles on the TEN-T core and comprehensive network."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Operational implication:* For road-mode freight movements on TEN-T corridors, the distance-based charging targets are the infrastructure baseline against which road carriers will plan fleet electrification. Workspaces procuring road freight on TEN-T lanes should monitor whether carriers are factoring in charging downtime in transit time quotes.

### Road Transport: Heavy-Duty Vehicle (HDV) Recharging Targets

FACT: The regulation "requires EU Member States to ensure a number of recharging stations are in place for heavy-duty vehicles in urban nodes and in safe and secure parkings."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Analytical inference:* The February 2026 TRAN Committee paper's identification of "HDV charging gaps" on TEN-T corridors indicates that Member State delivery of this requirement is incomplete as of early 2026. Workspaces relying on HDV road freight for EU corridor movements face a multi-year transition risk during which charging infrastructure density on key routes remains insufficient to support full EV HDV operations.

### Road Transport: Hydrogen Refuelling Stations

FACT: "For hydrogen, publicly accessible hydrogen refuelling stations must be deployed with a maximum distance of 200 km in between them along the TEN-T core and the TEN-T comprehensive network and at least one must be available in every urban node."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose road carriers explore hydrogen as a long-haul HDV fuel, the 200 km maximum spacing target on TEN-T core and comprehensive network is the infrastructure-availability threshold that determines operational feasibility. Until Member States achieve this density, hydrogen HDV routing on EU corridors is constrained.

### Road Transport: Liquefied Methane (LNG/LBG) for HDVs

FACT: The regulation contains "provisions for EU Member States to ensure minimum coverage of publicly accessible refuelling points for liquefied methane dedicated to heavy-duty vehicles on the TEN-T core and comprehensive network."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

FACT: Recital 8 of the regulation states: "In the heavy-duty road transport sector, technologies for liquefied methane driven trucks are fully mature."
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 full HTML text, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R1804

### Aviation: Shore-Side Electricity Supply to Stationary Aircraft

FACT: "Investments are needed for the electricity supply of stationary aircraft" and the regulation "sets targets for the deployment of shore-side electricity supply for larger seagoing container and passenger ships in maritime ports and for inland waterway vessels, and for electricity supply to stationary aircraft at TEN-T core and comprehensive network airports."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Operational implication:* For air-primary workspaces, this requirement targets ground power supply at TEN-T airports while aircraft are stationary. *Analytical inference:* Airport operators investing in fixed electrical ground power (FEGP) infrastructure to comply with AFIR will incur capital costs that may be recovered through ground handling tariffs. This is a cost-forward signal for air cargo operations at EU TEN-T airports.

FACT: Recital 4 of the regulation states: "While the fuel use requirements for sustainable aviation fuels can largely rely on the existing refuelling infrastructure, investments are needed for the electricity supply of stationary aircraft."
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 full HTML text, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R1804

*Analytical inference:* The regulation explicitly distinguishes SAF (which does not require new fuel infrastructure) from stationary electricity supply (which does). For the workspace's air freight operations, the relevant near-term infrastructure investment signal is ground power at airports, not SAF distribution. SAF blending obligations are covered under a separate regulation (the SAF mandate under the ReFuelEU Aviation framework, not AFIR).

### Maritime: Shore-Side Electricity and LNG at Ports

FACT: The regulation sets "targets for the deployment of shore-side electricity supply for larger seagoing container and passenger ships in maritime ports" and requires "an appropriate number of liquified methane refuelling points in maritime TEN-T ports."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

FACT: Recital 4 states: "Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 sets requirements in particular for the use of on-shore power that can only be fulfilled if an adequate level of shore-side electricity supply is deployed in the trans-European transport network (TEN-T) ports."
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 full HTML text, EUR-Lex. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32023R1804

*Operational implication:* AFIR's shore-side electricity targets are the infrastructure prerequisite for FuelEU Maritime's on-shore power use requirements. For workspaces with ocean freight movements through TEN-T ports, the two regulations are operationally coupled: AFIR determines infrastructure availability; FuelEU Maritime (Regulation EU 2023/1805) determines the fuel and emissions obligations on the vessel side. These should be monitored together.

*Specific compliance deadlines for shore-side electricity deployment at TEN-T ports not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-01.* The article-level text (Articles 9–12 of AFIR) contains the specific dates; retrieval of those articles is a research action.

### User Information and Data Provisions

FACT: The regulation "also includes provisions for ensuring user-friendliness of recharging infrastructure (e.g. payment options, price transparency and consumer information, non-discriminatory practices, smart recharging)."
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Analytical inference:* The data provisions (Article 20) and Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655 on real-time data interoperability are relevant to any logistics technology platform that routes vehicles to alternative fuels infrastructure. Workspaces operating or procuring route-optimization tools for EU road freight should confirm whether their technology vendors are consuming compliant AFIR data feeds.

---

## Section 9: Product-Specific Compliance Status

*No content for this section as of 2026-06-01: The workspace, as a freight forwarder, does not sell products that are directly in scope under AFIR as defined products. Section omitted.*

---

## Section 10: Registration and Reporting Obligations

FACT: The regulation includes provisions for "Member States' national policy frameworks for the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure, including provisions for areas where no mandatory EU wide targets are set and the reporting on the deployment of such infrastructure," under Article 14 (National policy frameworks) and Article 15 (National reporting).
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure thematic page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

*Analytical inference:* The reporting and national policy framework obligations under Articles 14–18 fall on Member States, not on freight forwarders. The workspace has no direct registration or reporting obligation under AFIR as currently described in the source content.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any data-provision obligation under Article 20 or Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/655 touches the workspace's logistics technology operations or digital platform contracts requires counsel and technology team review.

---

## Section 11: Operational System Requirements

### Air Freight — TEN-T Airport Ground Power Monitoring

**Scope:** All EU hub airports in the workspace's primary routing that are designated TEN-T core or comprehensive network airports.
**Deadline:** *Specific article-level deadlines for airport electricity supply deployment not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-01.* Retrieval of Articles 11–12 of AFIR required.
**Gap from current baseline:** *Analytical inference:* If the workspace does not currently track which of its primary EU hub airports are TEN-T-designated or what their shore-power deployment status is, that visibility gap means the workspace cannot anticipate ground handling cost changes from infrastructure investment recovery tariffs.

**Action:** Map primary EU hub airports against TEN-T network designations. Request airport operators' AFIR compliance plans and any announced ground handling tariff adjustments.

### Road Freight — HDV Carrier Fleet Transition Monitoring

**Scope:** All road carrier relationships on TEN-T core and comprehensive network corridors across Europe.
**Deadline:** Ongoing; no single workspace deadline, but the HDV charging gap identified in the February 2026 TRAN Committee paper signals multi-year uncertainty.
**Gap from current baseline:** *Analytical inference:* Workspaces that do not currently collect data on carrier fleet electrification plans and alternative-fuel readiness have no visibility into the cost and transit-time implications of the transition.

**Action:** Add AFIR compliance status and alternative-fuel fleet share to road carrier pre-qualification questionnaires. Track distance-based charging deployment on TEN-T corridors used by the workspace's road freight.

### Ocean/Maritime — TEN-T Port Shore-Power and LNG Deployment

**Scope:** Maritime TEN-T ports used by the workspace's ocean freight providers.
**Deadline:** *Specific deadlines not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-01.* Articles 9–10 of AFIR contain port-specific timelines; retrieval required.
**Gap from current baseline:** *Analytical inference:* For workspaces treating ocean as a tertiary mode, this is a lower-immediacy gap, but the coupling with FuelEU Maritime compliance (Regulation EU 2023/1805) means shore-power availability will affect vessel operator cost structures and therefore freight rates on EU maritime routes.

**Action:** When reviewing ocean freight carrier contracts, add a clause requesting disclosure of FuelEU Maritime / AFIR infrastructure dependency and expected surcharge pass-through.

---

## Section 12: Exemptions and Edge Cases

*No content for this section as of 2026-06-01: The source blocks do not reproduce the exemption provisions of AFIR. No exemption analysis can be grounded without the article-level text. Section omitted under the integrity rule.*

---

## Section 13: Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

### ICCT Analysis of AFIR Road Transport Targets

FACT: The ICCT published a policy update (April 2023) described as covering "AFIR road-transport targets, distance/power tables for cars, trucks and buses, and shore-side electricity rules" — an authoritative independent technical analysis.
*Source: ICCT, EU Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation Policy Update, April 2023. https://theicct.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/AFIR-EU-Policy-Update-A4-Final.pdf

*Analytical inference:* The ICCT analysis provides the distance-spacing and power-output tables for HDV charging on TEN-T corridors that are referenced in AFIR's Annex structures. For workspaces wishing to understand the technical benchmarks their road carriers must meet before the ICCT document is retrieved and reviewed, this is the most authoritative independent source in the registry.

### European Hydrogen Observatory — Hydrogen-Specific AFIR Status

FACT: The European Hydrogen Observatory covers "hydrogen-specific AFIR articles (6 & 7), HRS targets, Nov 2025 grant awards, and status of the market-readiness report" as of its tracked publication.
*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR Policy Page. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose road carriers may be piloting hydrogen HDV operations, Articles 6 and 7 of AFIR and the Hydrogen Observatory's tracking of grant awards and market-readiness reporting are the forward intelligence sources for when hydrogen refuelling station density on TEN-T corridors becomes operationally viable.

---
