prior.title: Lei nº 12.305/2010 - Política Nacional de Resíduos Sólidos (National Solid Waste Policy)
prior.summary: Federal Brazilian law establishing the National Solid Waste Policy framework. Defines principles, objectives, and instruments for integrated solid waste management and gerenciamento. Sets responsibilities for waste generators and public authorities. Establishes shared responsibility across product lifecycle, reverse logistics requirements, and environmentally appropriate final disposition standards. Applies to all natural and legal persons engaged in waste generation or management activities.

prior.title: IPCC Second-Order Draft Review Period Opens for Special Report on Climate Change and Cities
prior.summary: The IPCC has opened registration for the Second-Order Draft (SOD) review of its Special Report on Climate Change and Cities, accepting expert and government comments from May 8 to July 3, 2026. Expert registration closes June 26, 2026. This is a critical stage in the IPCC's rigorous assessment process, with the final report scheduled for March 2027 release. The report will provide comprehensive guidance on climate impacts, risks, adaptation and mitigation strategies for urban areas worldwide.
prior.full_brief:
# IPCC Special Report on Climate Change and Cities — Second-Order Draft Review

**Research Summary | MONITORING**

---

# 1. What the Research Found

The IPCC has opened the Second-Order Draft (SOD) expert review period for its Special Report on Climate Change and Cities (SR Cities), the first report scheduled for publication in the seventh assessment cycle (AR7). This is a forward-intelligence event: the review period is active now, the draft content — including the first Summary for Policymakers (SPM) — is available for qualified expert review, and the March 2027 publication target is a stated schedule, not an estimate.

**Phase and status.** The SOD review period opened 8 May 2026 and closes 3 July 2026. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Expert registration deadline.** Registration for expert reviewers closes 26 June 2026. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/ This is independently confirmed: "Registration closes on 26 June 2026 at 9.59pm GMT." *Source: UK Government IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review Guidance, UK Department for Energy Security & Net Zero, 6 May 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply

**Publication target.** "Scheduled for release in March 2027, the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities will be the first IPCC report published in the seventh assessment cycle. It is also the only special report in the current cycle." *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Outline governance.** "The report's outline (Decision IPCC-LXI-5) was agreed [by] the Panel during its 61st Session held in Sofia, Bulgaria from 27 July to 2 August 2024." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ The Sofia plenary confirmed: "Panel's approval and publication of this Special Report are scheduled for March 2027." *Source: IPCC 61st Plenary Press Release, IPCC, 2 August 2024. https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/

**Prior review scale.** "During the Expert Review from October to December 2025, the First-Order Draft of the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities received more than 32,000 comments from 1,365 registered Expert Reviewers around the world." *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/

**Production timeline (sourced milestones):**
- Scoping meeting: April 2024, Riga, Latvia
- First Lead Author Meeting: 10–14 March 2025, Osaka, Japan
- Second Lead Author Meeting: 21–25 July 2025, Mombasa, Kenya
- Third Lead Author Meeting: 12–16 January 2026, Oslo, Norway
- FOD expert review: October–December 2025
- SOD expert review: 8 May – 3 July 2026
- SOD registration closes: 26 June 2026
- Publication: March 2027

*Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Scientific leadership.** "The Special Report will be developed under the joint scientific leadership of Working Groups I, II and III with support from the Working Group II Technical Support Unit." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ The Scientific Steering Committee is chaired by IPCC Vice-Chair Diana Ürge-Vorsatz. Working Group II Co-Chairs Winston Chow and Bart van den Hurk are leading production. *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Scope of the report.** "The agreed outline of the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities includes trends, challenges and opportunities for cities in a changing climate, the actions and solutions to reduce urban risks and emissions, how to facilitate and accelerate change in the context of cities, and solutions by city and types and regions." *Source: IPCC 61st Plenary Press Release, IPCC, 2 August 2024. https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/

The scoping meeting participant expertise areas confirm the report addresses — among others — "supply chain, vulnerability, losses and damages, risk management" and "transportation systems and mobility services" and "energy systems, infrastructure" and "governance, policy, institutions, planning and finance" including "barriers and enabling conditions for urban climate finance" and "environmental, social and governance reporting." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

**Methodology note.** The SOD is a peer-reviewed draft, not a published finding. Content visible to registered expert reviewers is unpublished and subject to change. The brief does not characterise the draft's substantive findings because the draft text is not a public primary source available for span-grounding. What is grounded here is the process, the schedule, the governance structure, the scope of the outline, and the policy context — all of which are published facts.

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

The SR Cities report is explicitly designed to feed into the 2028 UN Global Stocktake process — the second iteration of the stocktake mechanism under the Paris Agreement. Analytical inference: that connection means the report's findings on urban transport emissions, supply-chain vulnerability, and urban logistics infrastructure are likely to inform national government climate-policy updates in the 2026–2028 window, with downstream consequences for urban delivery regulation, clean-air zones, low-emission zone expansions, and client sustainability reporting frameworks.

Operational implication: for workspaces operating freight movements into, out of, and within major urban centres — across air primary, road secondary, and ocean tertiary modes — the SR Cities report represents the scientific baseline that regulators and city governments will cite when tightening access rules, mandating emissions disclosures for urban freight, and setting clean-vehicle thresholds. The report's scope explicitly includes "transportation systems and mobility services," "critical infrastructure including power, digital communication and transport," and "barriers and enabling conditions for urban climate finance." *Source: IPCC SR Cities Report Page, IPCC, accessed 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/

Analytical inference: freight operations serving event, luxury, high-value, and time-critical cargo verticals — which concentrate deliveries in high-density urban cores with the most aggressive clean-air and low-emission zone trajectories — face the most direct exposure to policy tightening that the SR Cities report could accelerate.

Operational implication: the SOD review period is an intelligence window. The draft is currently available to registered expert reviewers, meaning the workspace has nine weeks to gain visibility of the scientific consensus before it is finalised, rather than reacting to the published report in March 2027.

**Re-check window.** Severity is MONITORING. The report is in draft; findings are not final. The re-check trigger is the final report approval and SPM publication, scheduled March 2027. The next substantive intelligence update should be pulled no later than Q1 2027, when the approved SPM will define the publicly citable findings.

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Participation window — closing 26 June 2026.** The SOD expert review registration closes 26 June 2026. *Source: UK Government IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review Guidance, UK Department for Energy Security & Net Zero, 6 May 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply Workspaces in freight-forwarding, logistics, and supply-chain roles with in-house sustainability expertise — or with access to sector-specific technical advisors — can register as expert reviewers and gain early sight of the draft findings in the urban transport, supply-chain vulnerability, and urban governance sections. This is a legitimate, sanctioned channel for early intelligence.

Analytical inference: the competitive advantage here is not regulatory compliance but strategic anticipation — understanding what scientific framing city governments will adopt when updating urban freight access policies, clean-air zone standards, and supplier sustainability requirements in the 2027–2029 period.

Operational implication: the workspace's client conversations on urban delivery capability, last-mile sustainability credentials, and forward regulatory exposure will be more credible if they are grounded in the SR Cities findings rather than the prior AR6 framework. The SPM, once published in March 2027, will be the most-cited scientific reference in urban climate policy discussions for the following five to seven years. Workspaces that have read the draft will have a 9-month lead on competitors who wait for publication.

**Decisions this changes:**
1. Whether to register a qualified team member or external advisor as an expert reviewer before 26 June 2026.
2. How to frame client sustainability briefings for cargo verticals operating in regulated urban corridors — event logistics, fine art, luxury, and high-value shipments concentrated in city centres.
3. Whether to begin tracking urban freight regulatory developments across the workspace's trade-lane cities ahead of the March 2027 publication, rather than reacting afterward.
4. How to engage with city-level clean-air zone consultations that may cite SR Cities findings as the regulatory basis.

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The IPCC is finalising its first-ever Special Report specifically on climate change and cities, due for publication in March 2027. The workspace is tracking its development. *Source: IPCC Press Release, IPCC, 8 May 2026. https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/
- The report scope explicitly addresses urban transport systems, supply-chain risk in cities, and governance conditions for urban climate finance — areas that directly shape the regulatory environment for freight serving city-based clients.
- The report will be the primary scientific reference informing city government and national government policy updates in the 2027–2029 period, relevant to clean-air zones, low-emission vehicle mandates, and urban access restrictions.
- The workspace recommends clients assess their supply-chain exposure to urban freight regulatory tightening now, rather than after the report triggers policy action.

**What to avoid saying:**

- Do not characterise the draft's specific findings: the SOD is not a public document, and its content is subject to change before the March 2027 approval session. Any characterisation of draft content that turns out to differ from the final approved text will undermine credibility.
- Do not describe the report as "binding" or as creating direct compliance obligations. IPCC reports are scientific assessments. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether and how any specific city government or national regulator incorporates SR Cities findings into enforceable policy is a legal and regulatory question outside the scope of this brief.
- Do not conflate the SOD review opportunity with participation in policymaking. Expert reviewer status is a scientific peer-review function, not a lobbying or advocacy channel.

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- What proportion of your deliveries route through low-emission zones or urban access-restricted areas today?
- Has your organisation mapped its urban last-mile exposure across the trade lanes you rely on?
- Are your supplier contracts ready to respond to a regulatory tightening in urban freight access post-2027?

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

The SOD review period yields draft visibility but not published findings. This brief does not resolve:

- What the SR Cities report will specifically find or recommend regarding urban freight, logistics infrastructure, or supply-chain emissions. The draft is unpublished; the approved findings are not available until March 2027.
- How individual city governments or national regulators will translate SR Cities findings into policy. Analytical inference: the policy translation process varies significantly by jurisdiction, political context, and existing regulatory frameworks, and will unfold over the 2027–2030 period.
- Whether the 2028 Global Stocktake process will produce specific freight-sector recommendations. Carbon Brief's analysis identified SR Cities' relevance to the 2028 stocktake, but the specific outcomes of that process are not yet determinable. *Source: Carbon Brief, Carbon Brief, published prior to May 2026. https://www.carbonbrief.org/ipcc-governments-split-on-accelerated-climate-reports-for-next-un-global-stocktake/
- Whether participating as an expert reviewer creates any confidentiality obligations regarding draft content. *Legal Confirmation Required:* expert reviewers accessing the SOD should confirm the applicable IPCC confidentiality and code of conduct requirements before discussing draft content externally.

**Re-check trigger:** March 2027 — SR Cities approval plenary and SPM publication. Set a monitoring pull for February 2027 to capture any pre-approval signals from the plenary session.

---

# 6. Sources

| # | Title | Issuing Body | Date | Type | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPCC invites government representatives and experts to review the second draft of the Special Report on Climate Change and Cities | IPCC | 8 May 2026 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body press release | https://www.ipcc.ch/2026/05/08/ipcc-srcities-second-order-draft-review/ |
| 2 | Special Report on Climate Change and Cities — Report Page | IPCC | Accessed 2026 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body canonical report page | https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ |
| 3 | IPCC approves outlines of the first two reports in the seventh assessment cycle | IPCC | 2 August 2024 | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body press release | https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/ |
| 4 | IPCC Special Report on Climate Change and Cities Second Order Draft: details and how to apply | UK Department for Energy Security & Net Zero | 6 May 2026 | Tier 2 — Government guidance document | https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply |
| 5 | IPCC Governments Split on Accelerated Climate Reports for Next UN Global Stocktake | Carbon Brief | Prior to May 2026 | Tier 5 — Trade/analytical press | https://www.carbonbrief.org/ipcc-governments-split-on-accelerated-climate-reports-for-next-un-global-stocktake/ |

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| IPCC SR Cities SOD Expert Review: Details and How to Apply (UK Government) | https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-expert-review/ipcc-special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities-second-order-draft-details-and-how-to-apply | 2 | Government-level participant guidance confirming registration deadline (26 June 2026) and procedural requirements — authoritative secondary corroboration for the SOD timeline, more specific than the IPCC press release on deadline GMT timestamp. |
| IPCC approves outlines of the first two reports in the seventh assessment cycle (61st Plenary) | https://www.ipcc.ch/2024/08/02/ipcc-approves-outlines-of-the-first-two-reports-in-the-seventh-assessment-cycle/ | 3 | Official IPCC plenary record locking the SR Cities outline and March 2027 approval/publication schedule — provides the governance origin of the timeline; essential for tracing the schedule to a binding panel decision rather than a press release estimate. |
| IPCC Special Report on Climate Change and Cities (dedicated report page) | https://www.ipcc.ch/report/special-report-on-climate-change-and-cities/ | 3 | IPCC's canonical landing page with the full production timeline, author list, Scientific Steering Committee membership, and lead author meeting dates — the single most complete sourced timeline available for the SR Cities production schedule. |
| IPCC Governments Split on Accelerated Climate Reports for Next UN Global Stocktake | https://www.carbonbrief.org/ipcc-governments-split-on-accelerated-climate-reports-for-next-un-global-stocktake/ | 5 | Analytical trade-press account linking SR Cities to the 2028 Global Stocktake — provides the policy relevance context that explains why the report matters beyond its scientific content; no primary-source equivalent for this connection is available in the source pool. |

---



*Specific penalty / enforcement provision not available from primary sources as of June 2026. IPCC Special Reports are scientific assessment documents; they carry no direct compliance penalty on any freight operator. Downstream regulatory consequences are jurisdiction- and policy-specific and are tracked separately.*

prior.title: EU Regulation 2023/1804 - Sustainability Reporting Requirements
prior.summary: EU Regulation 2023/1804 establishes mandatory sustainability reporting standards for large companies operating in the European Union. This regulation implements the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) framework, requiring detailed disclosure of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts using double materiality assessment. Compliance deadlines vary by company size and listing status.
prior.full_brief:
# EU Regulation 2023/1804 — Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR): Regulatory Fact Document

---

## Section 1: Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 September 2023 on the deployment of alternative fuels infrastructure (AFIR), including its consolidated text as amended to 14 April 2025 by Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2025/671. It addresses the regulation's mandatory infrastructure targets, technical specifications, and reporting obligations as they apply to freight forwarding operations across road, air, maritime, and rail transport modes.

**Nomenclature note:** The user prompt references "EU Regulation 2023/1804 – Sustainability Reporting Requirements." This is a mislabelling. Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 is the Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR), not a sustainability reporting instrument. Sustainability reporting for EU companies is governed by the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD/Directive 2022/34/EU) and associated European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). This brief addresses AFIR as it actually exists. Any inquiry about CSRD or ESRS should be directed to a separate brief covering those instruments.

**Date of generation:** This brief reflects source content available as of the stated last-regenerated timestamp in the YAML block. The consolidated EUR-Lex text is versioned 14 April 2025; the current live version carries an effective date of 08 January 2026.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, EUR-Lex consolidated text 02023R1804-20250414, European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023, as amended 14 April 2025. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

---

## Section 2: What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**FACT:** Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 "establishes mandatory national targets leading to the deployment of sufficient alternative fuels infrastructure in the Union for road vehicles, trains, vessels and stationary aircraft" and "lays down common technical specifications and requirements on user information, data provision and payment requirements for alternative fuels infrastructure."

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Article 1(1), EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

**FACT:** "The regulation is applicable since 13 April 2024."

*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

AFIR does not directly impose compliance obligations on freight forwarders as operators. Its obligations fall primarily on Member States (to deploy infrastructure) and on operators of recharging and refuelling points (to meet technical and payment requirements). *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether and to what extent the workspace, in its role as a freight forwarder procuring transport on behalf of clients across road, air, and ocean modes, bears any direct compliance obligation under AFIR — as opposed to being affected operationally by infrastructure targets — requires counsel review.

**Operational implication:** The regulation matters to freight forwarding operations not because it imposes direct compliance duties on forwarders, but because it reshapes the infrastructure environment on which road and air freight move. Charging and refuelling availability along TEN-T corridors determines when and whether battery-electric trucks (BETs) and hydrogen-powered heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs) become operationally viable for road freight, and shore-side electricity supply targets affect port dwell costs and emissions accounting for ocean-connected shipments.

**Operational implication:** For workspaces with road freight exposure across EU TEN-T corridors — particularly for cargo verticals such as live events, fine art, or high-value automotive requiring time-sensitive, door-to-door road movements — AFIR's HDV charging deployment timeline is the single most consequential near-term variable. Whether carrier fleets can electrify on the corridors the workspace uses depends on infrastructure that AFIR is designed to mandate but has not yet fully delivered.

---

## Section 3: Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — National Policy Framework Submission Deadline (1 January 2026)**

**FACT:** "The final NPF document must be submitted before 1st January 2026."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* National Policy Frameworks (NPFs) submitted by Member States by 1 January 2026 will set the deployment trajectories for EV charging, hydrogen refuelling, and shore-side electricity supply in each EU jurisdiction. For freight forwarding operations running road moves across multiple Member States, divergent NPF commitments will produce uneven infrastructure deployment, meaning some corridors will support BET operations ahead of schedule while others lag. Road carrier procurement and route planning decisions made in 2025 and early 2026 will be made against NPF commitments that are not yet all public.

**Action:** Request from road carriers operating on the workspace's primary TEN-T corridors their assessment of NPF-driven infrastructure deployment on those routes — specifically whether planned charging points cover their depot-to-depot BET operational ranges by 2027 (the indicative hydrogen target year) and 2030 (the binding deadline). This is a carrier procurement intelligence question, not a compliance action.

**MONITORING — AFIR Regulatory Review (by 31 December 2026)**

**FACT:** "A review of the Regulation is to happen by 31st December 2026, also including an assessment of article 6 targets and derogations."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* The 2026 review creates material uncertainty about whether current AFIR targets will be tightened, relaxed, or restructured. ICCT analysis published October 2025 finds that AFIR targets cover only 50%–70% of projected public BET charging needs by 2030, a gap finding likely to inform the review. Freight forwarding operations with multi-year road carrier contracts entered into before the review outcome is known carry exposure to revised infrastructure requirements that alter carrier economics.

**Action:** Add a structured review trigger to road carrier contracts with terms extending past 2026 — include a clause flagging renegotiation rights if AFIR-driven infrastructure costs change materially following the December 2026 review. *Legal Confirmation Required:* exact clause language and enforceability under applicable contract law require counsel review.

**MONITORING — Market and Technology Readiness Report (overdue as of March 2025)**

**FACT:** "As of March 2025, the market and technology readiness report initially expected for end of 2024 has not been published yet."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* This report, when published, will assess whether hydrogen targets in Articles 6 and 7 require adjustment, including potential demand for liquid hydrogen. Its delayed publication creates a gap in the regulatory baseline for hydrogen corridor planning. Workspaces exploring hydrogen-powered HDV options for road freight should treat hydrogen refuelling network commitments as provisional until this report publishes and the 2026 review responds to it.

Re-check window: re-pull this brief when the market and technology readiness report publishes or when the 2026 review opens for public consultation, whichever comes first.

---

## Section 4: How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

AFIR's obligation chain runs: **European Commission → Member States → Infrastructure operators (recharging/refuelling point operators) → Vehicle/vessel operators → Cargo principals and forwarders (indirectly).**

Freight forwarding operations sit at the downstream end of this chain. The regulation's direct obligations bind Member States to deploy infrastructure and bind recharging/refuelling point operators to meet technical and payment specifications. Vehicle fleet operators (road carriers, shipping lines, airlines) are the entities whose procurement and operational decisions are most directly shaped by AFIR's infrastructure rollout.

The workspace, acting as a freight forwarder procuring transport services on behalf of clients across road, air, maritime, and rail modes, is affected operationally through:

- **Carrier fleet transition:** Road carriers will electrify or transition to hydrogen on timelines that depend on AFIR-driven infrastructure deployment. The workspace's carrier panel will shift on that timeline.
- **Shore-side electricity supply:** *Analytical inference:* Ocean-connected shipments through EU maritime TEN-T ports will face changed port economics as shore-side electricity supply (Onshore Power Supply, OPS) deployment proceeds. This intersects with EU ETS for Shipping and FuelEU Maritime obligations — see No-Vacuum linkage note in Section 8.
- **Airport electrification:** Ground power provision requirements at TEN-T core and comprehensive network airports affect airport-side handling costs and ground service provider economics for air cargo operations.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace bears any direct registration, reporting, or payment obligation under AFIR in any transactional role (importer of record, shipper, logistics provider contracting infrastructure access) requires counsel review. Based on the regulation's text, no such obligation is apparent for freight forwarders, but the analysis is not a legal determination.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Articles 1, 2, EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

---

## Section 5: Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**Pending: Market and Technology Readiness Report (overdue; originally expected end of 2024)**

**FACT:** The report "should assess the state of the market (including the potential demand for liquid hydrogen) and evaluate the need to adjust the abovementioned targets." As of March 2025, it had not been published.

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* Publication timing is unknown. When published, this report will signal whether hydrogen HDV corridor targets will be revised upward or downward and whether liquid hydrogen infrastructure will be added as a mandatory track. Road freight operations considering hydrogen-capable carrier procurement should monitor this report as a leading indicator.

**Pending: AFIR Regulatory Review (by 31 December 2026)**

**FACT:** The review is scheduled "by 31st December 2026, also including an assessment of article 6 targets and derogations."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* The ICCT October 2025 study finding that AFIR covers only 50%–70% of projected public BET charging needs will likely be cited in the review process. If the review tightens targets, carrier electrification timelines may accelerate, changing the procurement window for road freight services. If targets are relaxed, corridor electrification slows and diesel-dependent road freight remains viable longer.

**Pending: National Policy Framework submissions (deadline 1 January 2026)**

Member States are required to submit NPFs before 1 January 2026. These documents will establish Member State-level deployment trajectories and indicative 2027 targets for hydrogen refuelling. *Analytical inference:* NPF filings from the Member States most relevant to the workspace's primary road corridors (e.g., Germany, France, Netherlands, Belgium, Poland) will be the most operationally significant. These will become publicly available through the Commission's reporting mechanism once submitted.

---

## Section 7: Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Is the workspace a direct obligated party under AFIR? | *Legal Confirmation Required* — Based on the regulation text, AFIR's direct obligations bind Member States and infrastructure operators, not freight forwarders. However, any role the workspace plays as a depot operator, logistics facility operator, or provider of fleet charging infrastructure for its own vehicles may create obligations. Counsel review required against the workspace's specific operational footprint. |
| Does AFIR apply to airports and ports the workspace uses? | FACT — AFIR applies to "airport of the TEN-T core network or airport of the TEN-T comprehensive network" and to maritime TEN-T ports. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific airports and ports used by the workspace are classified as TEN-T core or comprehensive network nodes requires verification against Annex II to Regulation (EU) No 1315/2013 (TEN-T Regulation). |
| Does the workspace's road carrier panel's fleet transition timeline align with AFIR's infrastructure rollout? | This is an operational question requiring carrier-specific data, not a legal threshold question. Effect on all road-dependent cargo verticals requires carrier-specific data. |
| Do AFIR's hydrogen corridor targets create viable options for long-haul HDV moves by 2030? | FACT threshold: one HRS every 200 km on TEN-T Core network by end of 2030. *Analytical inference:* viability for specific routes depends on NPF deployment trajectories and carrier procurement. |

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Articles 1, 2, 6, 7, 12; EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

---

## Section 8: Substantive Requirements

AFIR's substantive requirements bind Member States and infrastructure operators. This section maps the requirements that are most operationally consequential for freight forwarding operations, by transport mode in workspace priority order.

### 8a. Air — Shore-Side Electricity Supply at Airports

**FACT:** "Article 12 on targets for the electricity supply to stationary aircraft mandates that, from 1st January 2030, electricity must come from the grid or generated on site without using fossil fuels."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Operational implication:* For air cargo operations at TEN-T network airports, ground power provision to stationary aircraft will shift from fossil-fuel APU (auxiliary power unit) use to grid or on-site renewable electricity by 1 January 2030. The workspace's air cargo handling costs at these airports may change as ground service providers and airport operators pass through the cost of infrastructure investment. Effect on specific airport-handling cost lines requires carrier- and airport-specific data.

**No-Vacuum linkage:** AFIR's airport electrification provisions intersect with the EU's Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) mandate under ReFuelEU Aviation (Regulation (EU) 2023/2405), which imposes blending obligations on aviation fuel suppliers at EU airports from 2025. Workspaces with material air freight volumes on intra-EU and EU-origin routes face compounding cost signals from both instruments. A separate brief on ReFuelEU Aviation is warranted.

### 8b. Road — Heavy-Duty Vehicle Charging (TEN-T Corridors)

**FACT:** The regulation "sets out distance-based targets for light duty and heavy-duty road vehicles on the TEN-T core and comprehensive network" and "requires EU Member States to ensure a number of recharging stations are in place for heavy-duty vehicles in urban nodes and in safe and secure parkings."

*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

**FACT:** An ICCT study published October 2025 finds that "The AFIR is expected to cover between 50% and 70% of public charging needs in the EU-27 by 2030. Across the core Trans-European Transport Network (TEN-T), the AFIR targets are expected to cover between 65% and 85% of total charging needs, while across the comprehensive TEN-T, the coverage drops to 35%–45%."

*Source: ICCT, Charging infrastructure needs for battery electric trucks in the European Union by 2030, October 2025. https://theicct.org/publication/charging-needs-battery-electric-trucks-europe-oct25/

**FACT:** The ICCT study projects that "The expected BET fleet in the EU-27 by 2030 will require between 22 GW and 28 GW of installed charging power capacity" and "between 150,000–175,000 private chargers and 60,000–80,000 public chargers."

*Source: ICCT, Charging infrastructure needs for battery electric trucks in the European Union by 2030, October 2025. https://theicct.org/publication/charging-needs-battery-electric-trucks-europe-oct25/

**FACT:** "The targets only cover 30%–50% of the expected public charging needs in half of Member States, including countries such as the Netherlands and Belgium, whereas for countries like Romania, the target is twice as high as the expected charging needs."

*Source: ICCT, Charging infrastructure needs for battery electric trucks in the European Union by 2030, October 2025. https://theicct.org/publication/charging-needs-battery-electric-trucks-europe-oct25/

*Operational implication:* For road freight operations moving cargo verticals such as live events, fine art, high-value automotive, or humanitarian goods across TEN-T corridors, the ICCT gap analysis means that AFIR minimum targets alone will not fully support the BET fleet transition by 2030. Carriers operating in the Netherlands and Belgium — key logistics hubs — face the largest gap between AFIR minimums and actual charging demand. Road carrier partners operating on these corridors will face infrastructure constraints that may slow fleet electrification, maintaining diesel dependency and associated carbon-pricing exposure (EU ETS for road transport from 2027 under ETS2) beyond the nominal 2030 AFIR deadline.

**No-Vacuum linkage:** AFIR's HDV charging targets interact directly with the EU HDV CO₂ Standards Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2019/1242 as revised by Regulation (EU) 2024/1610), which mandates a 45% CO₂ reduction for new heavy-duty vehicles by 2030. Truck manufacturers must sell BETs at scale to meet those targets; BET sales without charging infrastructure are commercially unviable. The two regulations are co-dependent and should be tracked together. A separate brief on the HDV CO₂ Standards is warranted.

### 8c. Road — Hydrogen Refuelling for Heavy-Duty Vehicles

**FACT:** Articles 6 and 7 require "One Hydrogen Refuelling Station (HRS) every 200km on the TEN-T Core network by the end of 2030. HRS along the network must be designed for a cumulative daily capacity of one tonne, with at least a 700bar dispenser. At least one tonne per day of hydrogen in every urban node by the end of 2030."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

**FACT:** "Member States to set out a clear deployment trajectory that includes an indicative target for 2027 that is in line with market demand and ensures sufficient coverage."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Operational implication:* For road freight operations considering hydrogen HDV carrier options on long-haul TEN-T routes, the 2030 target provides minimum coverage but the overdue market and technology readiness report means the adequacy of those targets for liquid hydrogen (relevant for long-haul) is unresolved. Indicative 2027 targets from NPFs will be the first signal of whether hydrogen corridor coverage will be meaningful before 2030.

### 8d. Ocean / Maritime — Shore-Side Electricity Supply

**FACT:** The regulation "sets targets for the deployment of shore-side electricity supply for larger seagoing container and passenger ships in maritime ports."

*Source: European Commission DG MOVE, Alternative Fuels Infrastructure page. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en

**FACT:** In November 2025, under AFIR, "24 maritime ports are to adopt greener technologies such as Onshore Power Supply (OPS), port electrification, and ammonia bunkering for maritime vessels."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with ocean freight movements through EU TEN-T maritime ports, OPS deployment will change port dwell emissions profiles. This intersects with FuelEU Maritime (Regulation (EU) 2023/1805) obligations on shipping companies from 2025, which create financial incentives for vessels to use OPS where available. Port handling cost effects require port- and carrier-specific data.

**Specific cost figures for OPS infrastructure or port handling surcharges not available from primary sources as of the date of this brief.**

### 8e. Technical Specifications and User Requirements

**FACT:** AFIR "lays down common technical specifications and requirements on user information, data provision and payment requirements for alternative fuels infrastructure."

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Article 1(1), EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

**FACT:** For hydrogen refuelling, technical specifications require that "Outdoor hydrogen refuelling points dispensing gaseous hydrogen used as fuel on board motor vehicles shall comply at least with the interoperability requirements described in standard EN17127:2020" and "The quality characteristics of hydrogen dispensed by hydrogen refuelling points for motor vehicles shall comply with the requirements described in standard EN 17124:2022."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* These technical specification requirements are directed at infrastructure operators, not at freight forwarders. Their operational significance for the workspace is in vendor qualification: when procuring hydrogen-capable road carrier services, confirming that carrier-accessed refuelling infrastructure meets EN17127:2020 and EN17124:2022 standards is a carrier due-diligence question, not a direct compliance obligation.

---

## Section 10: Registration and Reporting Obligations

AFIR does not impose registration or reporting obligations directly on freight forwarders. Obligations in this area bind Member States (NPF submission and progress reporting) and infrastructure operators (data provision and availability requirements).

**FACT:** The regulation "establishes a reporting mechanism to encourage cooperation and ensures robust tracking of progress. The reporting mechanism shall take the form of a structured, transparent and iterative process taking place between the Commission and Member States."

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Article 1(3), EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng

*Analytical inference:* The NPF progress reporting mechanism will generate publicly accessible data on infrastructure deployment per Member State and per corridor. For freight forwarding operations, this data is an intelligence asset: tracking NPF progress reports will give advance visibility into which carrier corridors will and will not be electrified or hydrogen-enabled ahead of the 2030 binding deadline.

**No-Vacuum linkage:** Workspaces subject to CSRD (if the workspace meets CSRD thresholds under Directive 2022/34/EU and its ESRS standards) will be required to report Scope 3 transport emissions. The infrastructure deployment data generated through AFIR's reporting mechanism is operationally adjacent to that Scope 3 reporting exercise: carrier fleet electrification rates on specific corridors affect the emissions factors applicable to road freight movements. A separate brief on CSRD/ESRS Scope 3 transport reporting is warranted.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace meets CSRD reporting thresholds, and whether its Scope 3 transport emissions reporting obligations are triggered, requires counsel and audit adviser review against the workspace's size, turnover, and employee thresholds.

---

## Section 11: Operational System Requirements

AFIR does not require the workspace to build or modify internal systems as a direct compliance obligation. However, the regulation's implementation creates operational system implications that are analytically grounded:

**Carrier Data Integration**

*Operational implication:* As carrier fleets transition to BETs and hydrogen vehicles under AFIR-driven infrastructure deployment, carriers will generate new data streams (charging session records, energy consumption per route, infrastructure availability logs). For workspaces building Scope 3 transport emissions reporting capabilities, integrating carrier-level energy and emissions data will become necessary. Current gap from operational baseline: effect on workspaces operating without automated carrier data integration requires system-specific assessment.

**Route Planning and Contingency Logic**

*Operational implication:* During the transition period (2024–2030), road freight routes that cross TEN-T corridors with incomplete BET charging or hydrogen coverage will require contingency routing or carrier selection criteria that account for infrastructure availability. This is a route planning and carrier panel management question. Gap from current state: requires carrier-by-carrier infrastructure readiness assessment per primary corridor.

**No internal system compliance deadlines are imposed on freight forwarders by AFIR directly.**

---

## Section 12: Exemptions and Edge Cases

**FACT:** "Daily capacity of HRS can be halved on roads whose average daily heavy-duty traffic is below 2000 vehicles and in case of justified socio-economic cost-benefit terms. Outermost regions and islands can also be exempted under certain conditions."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Operational implication:* For road freight movements on lower-traffic TEN-T segments or movements to island destinations (e.g., Canary Islands, Azores, Malta), the reduced or exempted HRS capacity requirement means hydrogen HDV options may not be available on those legs by 2030. Workspaces routing cargo through or to outermost regions should not assume hydrogen carrier options will be available on those segments on the general 2030 timeline.

**FACT:** Regarding rail, "The new article 12(a) obliges Member States to assess the use of alternative fuels in the rail sector where existing networks cannot be fully electrified."

*Source: European Hydrogen Observatory, AFIR policy page, last updated 1 December 2025. https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation

*Analytical inference:* This assessment obligation for rail (not a deployment mandate) means alternative fuel rail infrastructure is at an earlier stage of regulatory development than road or maritime. Workspaces with rail freight exposure on non-electrified lines should not expect AFIR to drive near-term infrastructure change on those segments.

---

## Section 14: Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Event | Status | Workspace Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 September 2023 | AFIR adopted by European Parliament and Council | In force as of this date | Binding regulation in place |
| 22 September 2023 | Published in Official Journal (OJ L 234) | In force as of this date | Legal basis established |
| 13 April 2024 | AFIR applicable across all Member States | **In force** | All AFIR obligations on Member States and infrastructure operators now active |
| 14 April 2025 | Consolidated text updated; Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2025/671 incorporated | **In force** | Current operative text for compliance reference |
| 1 January 2026 | Member States' NPFs due to Commission | **Upcoming — 7 months** | NPF content will set corridor-level deployment trajectories; monitor for primary corridor coverage commitments |
| End 2027 | Indicative hydrogen HRS target year (per NPF deployment trajectories) | Upcoming | Signal year for whether hydrogen HDV corridor coverage is on track; carrier procurement signal |
| 31 December 2026 | AFIR regulatory review, including assessment of Article 6 targets and derogations | **Upcoming** | Review may tighten or modify targets; road carrier contract terms extending past 2026 carry adjustment risk |
| 1 January 2030 | Hydrogen: one HRS every 200 km on TEN-T Core network; at least one tonne/day per urban node | Binding deadline | Hydrogen HDV corridor viability assessment point for road freight |
| 1 January 2030 | Airports (TEN-T): electricity to stationary aircraft must come from grid or on-site non-fossil generation | Binding deadline | Air cargo ground power cost profile at TEN-T airports changes |

**Market and technology readiness report:** Originally expected end of 2024; as of March 2025, not yet published. No new publication date confirmed in sources. *Analytical inference:* Likely to precede or inform the 2026 review; re-check at the opening of the 2026 review consultation.

*Sources: EUR-Lex 02023R1804-20250414; European Commission DG MOVE AFIR page; European Hydrogen Observatory AFIR page, last updated 1 December 2025.*

---

## Section 15: Sources

| # | Title | Type | Issuing Body | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Consolidated Text 02023R1804-20250414 | Binding law (Tier 1) | European Parliament and Council / EUR-Lex | 14 April 2025 | https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng |
| 2 | Alternative Fuels Infrastructure — Mobility and Transport | Regulator overview page (Tier 2) | European Commission DG MOVE | Accessed 2025 | https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en |
| 3 | Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation — European Hydrogen Observatory | EU-funded observatory analysis (Tier 2/3) | European Hydrogen Observatory (Clean Hydrogen Partnership) | Last updated 1 December 2025 | https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation |
| 4 | Charging infrastructure needs for battery electric trucks in the European Union by 2030 | Peer-reviewed industry analysis (Tier 4) | International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) | October 2025 | https://theicct.org/publication/charging-needs-battery-electric-trucks-europe-oct25/ |

*Note: The IEA Policies Database entry (https://www.iea.org/policies/27375-regulation-eu-20231804-alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation) was listed as a corroborating source but returned a Cloudflare security verification block and no accessible content. No claims in this brief are grounded in that source. It is listed in New Sources Identified for future retrieval.*

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier Estimate | Why This Source Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| EUR-Lex – Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 Consolidated Text (as of 14 April 2025) | https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/2025-04-14/eng | 1 | Authoritative consolidated full text of AFIR including 2025 amendments; primary legal source for all AFIR obligations and definitions. |
| European Commission DG MOVE – Alternative Fuels Infrastructure | https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-themes/clean-transport/alternative-fuels-sustainable-mobility-europe/alternative-fuels-infrastructure_en | 2 | Official EC regulator page covering AFIR scope, mandatory targets, and applicability date; supplies the 13 April 2024 applicability date fact. |
| European Hydrogen Observatory – AFIR Hydrogen Provisions | https://observatory.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/eu-policy/alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation | 2–3 | EU-funded observatory; provides Articles 6–7 hydrogen target detail, NPF submission deadline, 2026 review deadline, delayed readiness report status, and November 2025 €600M grant award detail — all missing from the primary EUR-Lex text excerpt. |
| ICCT – Charging Infrastructure Needs for Battery Electric Trucks in the EU by 2030 | https://theicct.org/publication/charging-needs-battery-electric-trucks-europe-oct25/ | 4 | Quantifies the gap between AFIR minimum targets and actual BET charging demand by 2030; supplies the 50–70% coverage finding and Member State-level variation; key for operational gap analysis on TEN-T corridors. |
| IEA Policies Database – Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 AFIR | https://www.iea.org/policies/27375-regulation-eu-20231804-alternative-fuels-infrastructure-regulation | 2 | IEA policy tracker; returned a Cloudflare block during this generation — no content accessible. Worth retrying; if accessible, would provide an independent Tier 2 summary of AFIR binding targets. |
