prior.title: California Energy Commission Business Meeting Materials and RPS Compliance Period 4 Summary
prior.summary: California Energy Commission publishes business meeting supporting materials and presentations from April-May 2026, including Summary Claims Details for Compliance Period 4 related to Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) Track requirements. Contact information provided through RPSTrack@energy.ca.gov.
prior.full_brief:
# California RPS Compliance Period 4 — CEC Verification and Business Meeting Status

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**Severity: MONITORING**

California's Renewables Portfolio Standard (RPS) Compliance Period 4 (CP4), covering reporting years 2021–2024, has now entered the formal CEC verification and adoption phase. This is the phase that converts four years of procurement claims into official compliance determinations — and it is moving now.

The California Energy Commission (CEC) has released three simultaneous deliverables that mark the opening of the CP4 verification window:

- **CP4 Summary Claims Reports** — now available.
- **Notice of Availability (NOA)** — issued and available.
- **25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports** — now available for public comment.

*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

CP4 covers the reporting years 2021–2024. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

The CP4 NOA and the 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports for POUs are listed and linked from the CEC's POU Verification page. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables

A parallel deliverable — the **RPS Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition** — has also been released simultaneously with its own Notice of Availability. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

The trigger for this release is structural: CP4 ends with the 2024 reporting year, and California statute organizes RPS compliance on a multiyear compliance period basis rather than annual basis. The close of CP4 requires CEC to initiate formal verification before CPUC and CEC can issue compliance determinations to retail sellers and publicly owned utilities respectively.

The California Energy Commission conducts business meetings on a regular 2025 schedule, with meetings documented through December 2025, at which CP4 adoption items are expected to appear as agenda items. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings

*Analytical inference:* The simultaneous release of the NOA, 25 Staff Draft Reports, and the Methodology Report Fourth Edition signals that CEC staff have completed internal verification analysis and the programme is entering its public-comment-and-adoption sequence, which — based on the CP3 pattern documented in the source materials — typically proceeds through staff draft, comment period, business meeting consideration, and final adoption.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

The CP4 process involves two California state agencies and four categories of load-serving entity (LSE), each with distinct roles and interests.

**California Energy Commission (CEC):**
The CEC verifies the eligibility of renewable energy procured by load-serving entities, which include retail sellers, publicly owned utilities (POUs), and all other entities serving retail sales of electricity in California that are obligated to participate and report to the RPS. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

For POUs specifically, the Energy Commission determines the Portfolio Content Category classification of procurement claims, calculates procurement requirements, and determines RPS compliance. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

The Energy Commission is authorized to issue violation notices for noncompliance and refer the POU to the California Air Resources Board to assess penalties. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

**California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC):**
The CPUC implements and administers RPS compliance rules for California's retail sellers of electricity, which include investor-owned utilities (IOUs), electric service providers (ESPs), and community choice aggregators (CCAs). *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

After the California Energy Commission verifies RPS procurement claims for each compliance period, retail sellers submit Verified Compliance Reports from which the CPUC bases its compliance determination. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

**Four LSE Categories Subject to CP4 Verification:**
Load-serving entities (LSEs) include POUs, investor-owned utilities (IOUs), electricity service providers (ESPs), and community choice aggregators (CCAs). *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

**The CP4 Target:**
California's load-serving entities are required to procure electricity from eligible renewable resources to meet 44 percent by the end of 2024. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The 44% procurement target for CP4 (end of 2024) is the headline compliance threshold against which all LSE claims in the 25 Staff Draft Reports are being measured. Whether individual LSEs meet, exceed, or fall short of this threshold is what the draft reports disclose — and those reports are now open for comment.

**WREGIS — Infrastructure Dependency:**
California's Renewables Portfolio Standard program relies on WREGIS to support program administration. WREGIS is an independent renewable energy certificate tracking system. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

WREGIS announced its agreement with its current software provider ends on December 31, 2027, and will not be extended. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The WREGIS software transition is a background risk factor running parallel to the CP4 adoption cycle. It does not affect CP4 determinations directly, but it is a forward operational risk for CP5 (2025–2027) and CP6 (2028–2030) tracking infrastructure.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Phase sequence now underway:**

The CP4 verification process follows the same sequential structure documented for CP3 in the source materials:

1. **Staff Draft Reports issued** — the 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports for CP4 are now available. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables
2. **Notice of Availability and Request for Comments** — issued for CP4. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables
3. **Business meeting consideration** — CEC holds scheduled business meetings through the 2025 calendar year where CP4 adoption items appear. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings
4. **Commission Final Reports adoption** — following business meeting vote.
5. **Compliance determination process** — after verification reports are adopted, the compliance determination begins according to the RPS POU regulations. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Specific date for CP4 final adoption not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-29.*

*Analytical inference:* Based on the CP3 pattern in the source materials — where the CP3 NOA and Staff Draft Reports were followed by a business meeting adoption cycle — the CP4 adoption sequence is likely to proceed through a CEC business meeting in the second half of 2025 or early 2026. The 2025 business meeting calendar runs through December 19, 2025. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings

**Conversion triggers — when "draft" becomes "determination":**
- CEC Commissioners vote to adopt individual POU verification results reports at a business meeting.
- Once adopted, CPUC uses the CEC Verification Report to confirm each retail seller's annual REC claims and issues formal compliance determinations.
- For non-compliant POUs, the Energy Commission may issue violation notices and refer to CARB for penalty assessment.

**Compliance Period 5 has already opened:**
Compliance Period 5 covers 2025–2027. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

Annual reporting instructions for CP5 are already available, and the RPS reporting due date is July 1 every year. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Operational implication:* CP4 verification and CP5 annual reporting are running in parallel. LSEs simultaneously respond to CP4 draft verification comments while submitting their first CP5 annual report by July 1 of each year.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Re-check window: MONITORING — re-check at CEC business meeting adoption of CP4 Final Reports (expected 2025 H2 – 2026 Q1; exact date not yet published).**

**Penalty exposure — documented from prior periods:**
The CPUC's published compliance determination table documents penalty amounts for non-compliant retail sellers across CP1, CP2, and CP3. Examples from CP3 (2017–2020): EDF Industrial Power Services paid $460,185.00 for failing to meet the Portfolio Quality Requirement (PQR); Liberty Utilities paid $778,745.00 for the same. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

From CP2 (2014–2016): Gexa Energy paid $3,704,675.00 for failing to meet the long-term contracting requirement and PQR. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

*Specific CP4 penalty figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-29 — CP4 compliance determinations are pending adoption.*

*Analytical inference:* The penalty history across CP1–CP3 establishes the order of magnitude of financial exposure for non-compliant LSEs: from low hundreds of thousands to multi-million dollar figures, depending on the nature and scale of the shortfall. CP4's 44% target is higher than CP3's 33% target, and the LSE landscape now includes a larger share of CCAs alongside IOUs and ESPs, making the compliance picture more complex.

**Methodology Report Fourth Edition — new methodology implications:**
The Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition is now available. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The release of a new edition of the Methodology Report concurrent with CP4 draft reports suggests that the analytical framework used to assess CP4 claims may have been updated. LSEs or their advisors reviewing CP4 draft results should compare the Fourth Edition methodology against prior editions to identify any changed eligibility determinations, Portfolio Content Category classification updates, or verification formula changes that affect their specific claims.

**WREGIS transition risk for CP5 and CP6:**
WREGIS's software provider agreement ends December 31, 2027 — which falls within Compliance Period 5 (2025–2027). *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Operational implication:* Any LSE with CP5 procurement claims tracking through WREGIS faces a mid-period software transition risk. The CEC has directed stakeholders to follow WREGIS development updates directly. No continuity plan details are available from these source blocks as of 2026-05-29.

**For workspaces procuring renewable energy in California for Scope 2 or Scope 3 emissions accounting:**
*Operational implication:* RPS-certified RECs, tracked through WREGIS and verified by CEC, are the foundational instrument for renewable energy claims in California. CP4 verification outcomes — once adopted — will determine the official REC eligibility position of every facility whose generation a workspace may have relied on for emissions reporting claims covering 2021–2024. If a facility's RPS certification or generation claim is revised in the CP4 final reports, downstream REC-based claims in sustainability disclosures may require correction.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-29: The source blocks do not contain sourced reporting on named competitor positions or named operator responses to CP4 that would allow competitive positioning analysis without invention.*

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- California's RPS program has entered the formal verification phase for Compliance Period 4 (2021–2024). The CEC has issued 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports and opened a public comment period. This is the administrative step that precedes formal compliance determinations.
- The CP4 target was 44% renewable procurement by end of 2024. Whether individual utilities and CCAs met that target will be determined once the CEC adopts the final verification reports, which is still pending.
- Compliance determinations for retail sellers (IOUs, ESPs, CCAs) are made by the CPUC using the CEC's verification report as the authoritative input.
- Annual RPS reporting for Compliance Period 5 (2025–2027) continues in parallel, with a July 1 annual reporting deadline.

**Questions to pose to clients or counterparties:**

- Has your energy supplier confirmed their CP4 RPS compliance position under the CEC Staff Draft Reports? Has your REC-based procurement been verified as eligible?
- Are your renewable energy claims for 2021–2024 sustainability disclosures traceable to WREGIS-registered, CEC-certified facilities? If a facility's claim is revised in the CP4 final report, do you have a process to update your disclosures?
- Does your CP5 procurement strategy account for the WREGIS software transition risk by end of 2027?

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert that any specific LSE is CP4 compliant or non-compliant — the final verification reports have not yet been adopted and compliance determinations are pending.
- Do not assert penalty figures for CP4 — no CP4 enforcement data is published as of the date of this brief.
- Do not assert that the Fourth Edition Methodology Report changes any specific eligibility outcome without reviewing that document directly.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Track the CP4 Business Meeting Adoption Calendar**
Monitor the CEC 2025 Business Meeting agenda releases (agendas typically available approximately 10 days before each meeting) for CP4 Final Verification Results Report adoption items. Subscribe to CEC Commission Business Meeting Agendas at: https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings
- *Why now:* The adoption vote is the trigger for CPUC compliance determinations. Once adopted, enforcement timelines begin for non-compliant LSEs.
- *Re-check window:* Prior to each 2025 H2 CEC business meeting through December 19, 2025, and into 2026 if adoption does not conclude in 2025.

**Action 2 — Review the Methodology Report Fourth Edition**
Obtain and review the RPS Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition, now available at the CEC RPS programme page. For workspaces whose renewable energy procurement claims (2021–2024) are tied to RPS-certified facilities, compare the Fourth Edition methodology against prior editions for any classification or eligibility changes affecting those claims.
*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

**Action 3 — File or Monitor CP4 Staff Draft Report Comments**
The CP4 NOA and Staff Draft Reports are open for public comment. Workspaces with direct LSE relationships, PPA counterparties, or REC-based reporting obligations tied to facilities in the 25 draft reports should review the relevant draft and determine whether to submit comments or respond to any findings that affect their supply chain.
- Comment filing: CEC's E-Filing and E-Commenting system (note: the system has been temporarily unavailable; CEC has stated that comment and filing deadlines will be extended while the system is unavailable and new deadlines will be posted). *Source: California Energy Commission, CEC Home Page. https://energy.ca.gov/about/divisions-and-offices/fuels-and-transportation-division

**Action 4 — Assess CP5 WREGIS Continuity Risk**
For workspaces procuring renewable energy or managing REC-based sustainability reporting in California through 2027: note that WREGIS's current software provider agreement ends December 31, 2027, coinciding with the close of CP5. Track WREGIS governance and software transition announcements at the WREGIS/WECC page referenced by CEC. Contact: rpstrack@energy.ca.gov.
*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

**Action 5 — Confirm CP5 Annual Reporting Obligations**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether a specific workspace entity — depending on its supply chain role (importer of electricity, buyer under a PPA, or REC purchaser without retail seller status) — has direct annual reporting obligations to CEC or CPUC under CP5 requires legal confirmation against the specific entity's California load-serving status and CPUC/CEC jurisdictional classification.

Retail sellers and POUs are required to report their RPS procurement claims annually to the Energy Commission on or before July 1 of each year. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

---

# 8. Sources

| # | Title | Type | URL |
|---|-------|------|-----|
| 1 | Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS (CEC Main Programme Page) | Tier 2 — Regulator programme page | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard |
| 2 | Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance (CEC) | Tier 2 — Regulator programme page | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard |
| 3 | Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities (CEC) | Tier 2 — Regulator programme page | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables |
| 4 | 2025 Business Meetings (CEC) | Tier 2 — Regulator proceedings page | https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings |
| 5 | RPS Compliance and Reporting (CPUC) | Tier 2 — Regulator programme page | https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting |
| 6 | CEC Home / Fuels and Transportation Division Page | Tier 2 — Regulator home page (used for E-Filing system notice) | https://energy.ca.gov/about/divisions-and-offices/fuels-and-transportation-division |

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------------|------------------------|
| CEC Renewables Portfolio Standard – Main Program Page | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard | 2 | Primary CEC RPS hub; announces CP4 Summary Claims Reports, NOA, and 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports as now available — direct primary regulatory source for CP4 status. |
| CEC RPS Verification and Compliance – Retail Sellers & LSEs | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard | 2 | Details CEC's verification process for all LSEs across CP4 (2021-2024), RPS targets by compliance period, POU enforcement regulations, and Methodology Report Fourth Edition reference. |
| CEC RPS Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities – CP4 Draft & Final Reports | https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables | 2 | Lists CP4 (2021-2024) POU-specific draft and final verification results reports; directly links NOA and request-for-comments documents; confirms 25 Staff Draft Reports in public comment phase. |
| CEC Business Meetings – 2025 | https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings | 2 | Official repository of 2025 CEC business meeting agendas and schedule through December 2025; the venue at which CP4 adoption items will appear for Commissioner vote. |
| CPUC RPS Compliance and Reporting | https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting | 2 | CPUC counterpart to CEC's RPS role; provides documented compliance determination history (CP1–CP3) with penalty tables; explains the two-agency compliance architecture and REC verification chain. |

prior.title: First Movers Coalition - WEF Global Sustainability Initiative
prior.summary: The First Movers Coalition is a World Economic Forum initiative focused on mobilizing corporate commitments to climate action and sustainable business practices. The page excerpt primarily contains cookie consent and technical implementation details rather than substantive initiative content.
prior.full_brief:
# First Movers Coalition — WEF Global Sustainability Initiative

**Market Signal Brief | Initiative**
*Prepared for workspaces in freight forwarding operating across air, road, and ocean modes with cargo verticals spanning high-value, time-sensitive, and industrial supply chains*

---

## 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

The First Movers Coalition (FMC) is a World Economic Forum-convened initiative that uses the collective purchasing power of corporate members to create early commercial markets for breakthrough decarbonization technologies in hard-to-abate sectors — including aviation, shipping, road freight, steel, aluminium, cement and concrete, and carbon dioxide removal (CDR).

**FACT:** "The First Movers Coalition was launched by U.S. President Biden at the 2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference in Glasgow, Scotland."
*Source: Canada joins First Movers Coalition, Government of Canada / Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada, January 18, 2023. https://www.canada.ca/en/innovation-science-economic-development/news/2023/01/canada-joins-first-movers-coalition-aimed-at-commercializing-zero-carbon-technologies-globally.html

**FACT:** "Today, the FMC represents 101 companies that have pledged a total of $19 billion in purchasing commitments by 2030 across hard-to-abate mobility industries including aviation, shipping and trucking and materials sectors including cement and concrete, steel, aluminium as well as carbon dioxide removal (CDR)."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, World Economic Forum Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** "These commitments would help reduce 26 million tonnes of carbon dioxide (CO₂) through purchasing alone."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, World Economic Forum Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** "Five years ahead of the commitment target, members are already moving towards implementing their pledges and have collectively signed more than 130 offtake agreements."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, World Economic Forum Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

The trigger for FMC's original formation was an explicit technology-readiness gap: **FACT:** "In 2021, half of the emissions reductions needed to reach net zero depended on technologies that were not yet commercially available." **FACT:** "By 2023, the International Energy Agency (IEA) estimated that the share of required emissions reductions dependent on early-stage technologies had fallen to 35%."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, World Economic Forum Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

*Analytical inference:* The decline from 50% to 35% early-stage technology dependency since 2021 is the clearest available metric of FMC's directional impact. A 15-percentage-point shift in four years, while a substantial gap remains, indicates the demand-signal mechanism is producing measurable commercialization acceleration.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces whose clients procure freight services for aviation, maritime, and road cargo — where FMC members are the cargo principals — the 130+ offtake agreements already signed represent commercial deals that embed low-carbon fuel and vehicle expectations into supply-chain partner selection criteria. Freight forwarders serving FMC-member shippers are increasingly proximate to those criteria.

---

## 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Coalition membership and structure**

**FACT:** The FMC "represents 101 companies" as of January 2026, making purchasing commitments by 2030.
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** The joint COP29 Call to Action references "125+ commitments that FMC's members have made, supported by 13 FMC Government Partners."
*Source: First Movers Coalition, Industrial Deep Decarbonization Initiative, Net-Zero Government Initiative Joint Call to Action, UNIDO/CEM-IDDI/NZGI, November 20, 2024. https://decarbonization.unido.org/news/first-movers-coalition-industrial-deep-decarbonization-initiative-net-zero-government-initiative-joint-call-to-action/

**Government partners confirmed in source materials**

**FACT:** The following governments are named as signatories of the November 2024 Joint Call to Action: "Government of Canada, Government of Germany, Government of Lithuania, Government of the United Kingdom, Government of the United States of America."
*Source: First Movers Coalition, IDDI, NZGI Joint Call to Action, UNIDO, November 20, 2024. https://decarbonization.unido.org/news/first-movers-coalition-industrial-deep-decarbonization-initiative-net-zero-government-initiative-joint-call-to-action/

**FACT:** Canada joined FMC as a government partner, announced January 18, 2023, via Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry François-Philippe Champagne: "Canada's participation in the FMC is aligned with the government's commitment to advance the adoption of clean technologies and to forge ahead on the path to industrial decarbonization."
*Source: Canada joins First Movers Coalition, Government of Canada, January 18, 2023. https://www.canada.ca/en/innovation-science-economic-development/news/2023/01/canada-joins-first-movers-coalition-aimed-at-commercializing-zero-carbon-technologies-globally.html

**Named corporate actors in mobility sectors (sourced)**

**FACT:** "Freight company Toll Group is addressing this challenge through an investment of $43 million in charging infrastructure, including 28 electric trucks and 30 dedicated charging hubs."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** "American Express Global Business Travel, together with a consortium of partners, helped establish Avelia, one of the first large-scale book-and-claim platforms for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF)." "By June 2025, the platform had enabled more than 155 million litres of SAF to enter the global network and avoided more than 370,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** "Mitsui O S K Lines plans to deploy nine ammonia dual fuel ships beginning in 2026, while also investing in carbon transformation company Twelve to support the development of clean marine fuels."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**What they want:** *Analytical inference:* FMC members' stated mechanism is using committed purchasing power to send demand signals to technology suppliers, reducing the risk premium on capital deployment by guaranteeing early revenue. Government partners add procurement mandates and policy alignment for steel, cement, and concrete in public infrastructure. The combined goal is cost convergence — bringing green premiums down to parity with conventional production — across sectors where current economics are structurally negative for low-carbon alternatives.

---

## 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Current phase and status**

**FACT:** As of January 2026, FMC members "have collectively signed more than 130 offtake agreements" ahead of the 2030 commitment target.
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** "Further in 2026, the First Movers Coalition will continue to explore the following questions: What are the mechanisms that have demonstrated they can work in addressing the green premium? How can we leverage new markets and key geographies to ensure the availability of supply and further grow the demand signal and global purchasing power for near-zero products? And who are the new actors and what are the new engagement models that need to be pursued to continue to remain relevant and impactful in a troubled world?"
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**FACT:** A "newly released First Movers Coalition's impact brief" was referenced as published, assessing "members' pathways towards fulfilling their commitments as part of a wider assessment of best practices to catalyse early markets," published in connection with Davos 2026 (Annual Meeting).
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

**Regulatory coupling — what converts signals to obligations**

*Analytical inference:* FMC commitments are voluntary corporate pledges, not binding regulation. However, the initiative directly intersects with binding regulatory frameworks already in force or approaching enforcement:

- **EU ETS aviation extension and CORSIA** — SAF mandates (ReFuelEU Aviation) impose blending obligations on fuel suppliers at EU airports from 2025. FMC aviation commitments by carriers using Avelia-type book-and-claim platforms generate Scope 3 emissions accounting entries that interact with corporate CSRD disclosure obligations now in effect for large EU entities.
- **IMO GHG Strategy and FuelEU Maritime** — FMC maritime commitments (e.g., Mitsui O.S.K. Lines ammonia vessels from 2026) align with IMO's 2030 target to reduce GHG intensity by at least 20% against 2008 levels. FuelEU Maritime's fuel intensity standards apply to vessels calling EU ports from January 2025.
- **EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)** — FMC corporate members subject to CSRD face mandatory Scope 3 transport emissions disclosure. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether freight forwarders acting as service providers to FMC member shippers face downstream disclosure obligations under CSRD or supply-chain due-diligence clauses depends on contract structure and counsel review.

*Analytical inference:* The conversion trigger for the workspace is not FMC membership itself — it is FMC member clients inserting green-procurement clauses into freight contracts, or carriers (airlines, ocean lines, trucking companies) within the FMC ecosystem re-pricing services to reflect their own commitment costs. The 130+ offtake agreements already signed represent the leading edge of that commercial pressure.

**MONITORING — Re-check window: Q4 2026.** The FMC's 2026 annual workplan explicitly names Q4 2026 as the period for assessing green premium reduction mechanisms and new engagement models. The next material update will arrive with the WEF Annual Meeting (Davos) in January 2027 or a mid-year impact publication. Pull this brief for update at that point.

---

## 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Aviation (primary transport mode for the workspace)**

**FACT:** "In aviation, sustainable aviation fuel remains expensive and unevenly distributed." Avelia's book-and-claim model "had enabled more than 155 million litres of SAF to enter the global network and avoided more than 370,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide" by June 2025.
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with air as primary transport mode, the Avelia book-and-claim model is the current operational mechanism by which airline partners can allocate SAF emissions benefits to specific cargo customers. If the workspace's airline partners participate in Avelia or equivalent platforms, this creates both a cost exposure (SAF surcharges passed through on air waybills) and a commercial opportunity (credible, third-party-verified SAF attribution for client sustainability reporting). Current SAF surcharge rates vary by carrier and route — *Specific current SAF surcharge rates by carrier not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-10.*

**Road freight (secondary transport mode)**

**FACT:** Toll Group committed "$43 million in charging infrastructure, including 28 electric trucks and 30 dedicated charging hubs."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces routing road freight in markets where FMC-aligned carriers (such as Toll Group) are subcontractors or co-loaders, the green freight corridor infrastructure being built represents a medium-term transition in available equipment mix. In the near term (2024–2027), zero-emission trucking remains infrastructure-constrained outside specific corridors. The workspace's drayage cost model is not yet materially affected at scale, but selective lanes served by Toll-type operators may carry green-premium pricing.

**Ocean freight (tertiary transport mode)**

**FACT:** "Early movers are now operating vessels powered by cleaner fuels such as methanol, sending demand signals to shipyards and fuel producers." Mitsui O.S.K. Lines "plans to deploy nine ammonia dual fuel ships beginning in 2026."
*Source: How First Movers are helping drive decarbonization through demand, WEF Stories, January 19, 2026. https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/01/first-movers-coalition-decarbonization-through-demand/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with ocean freight as tertiary mode, ammonia and methanol dual-fuel vessel deployment by named FMC member carriers creates the prospect of tiered ocean freight pricing by the late 2020s — conventional vs. low-carbon vessel — similar to the SAF surcharge model now emerging in aviation. Green ocean freight premiums are not yet standardized across booking platforms; *Specific low-carbon ocean freight premium rates not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-10.*

**Green premium compression timeline**

*Analytical inference:* The FMC's 2030 commitment horizon and the IEA's 35% residual early-stage technology dependency figure (down from 50% in 2021) together suggest that green premiums in aviation fuel, maritime fuel, and electric road freight will compress through the late 2020s as supply scales. Workspaces locking multi-year rates before 2027 without green-premium adjustment clauses may benefit if premiums fall; workspaces without such clauses may face cost exposure if premiums remain elevated through 2028–2030.

---

## 5. Competitive Implications

**Who is positioned ahead**

*Analytical inference:* Freight forwarders who have already established SAF book-and-claim attribution mechanisms with airline partners are positioned to offer clients verified low-carbon air freight options. The Avelia platform — established by American Express Global Business Travel in consortium — represents the model that cargo-oriented forwarders would need to replicate or access. Workspaces without a SAF attribution product lack the commercial language FMC-member clients expect.

*Analytical inference:* Freight operators with formal FMC membership or supplier-hub listings (the "First Suppliers Hub" referenced in source materials) gain explicit visibility with FMC corporate buyers. Named examples in the source materials are infrastructure and fleet operators (Toll Group in road) and fuel-technology platforms (Avelia in aviation) — not freight forwarders per se, but the adjacency is commercially relevant.

**Who is exposed**

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that cannot demonstrate Scope 3 transport emissions accounting to clients facing CSRD reporting obligations are competitively exposed as those clients are forced to report. The FMC's demand-signal mechanism accelerates the timeline by which clients need verifiable emissions data from service providers.

**Named competitor activity**

*No sourced reporting on specific named freight forwarder competitors and their FMC-related positioning is available from the source materials provided. Omitted under the integrity rule.*

---

## 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say**

- The FMC has 101 corporate members with $19 billion in purchasing commitments by 2030, signed by companies in aviation, shipping, trucking, steel, aluminium, cement, and CDR — these are sourced, verifiable figures from WEF's January 2026 impact narrative.
- More than 130 offtake agreements have been signed ahead of the 2030 target — the commercial transition is already underway, not aspirational.
- The Avelia SAF book-and-claim platform had enabled over 155 million litres of SAF and 370,000 tonnes of CO₂ avoidance by June 2025 — a specific, operational proof point for aviation clients.
- Mitsui O.S.K. Lines' deployment of nine ammonia dual-fuel vessels from 2026 is a named, sourced maritime signal clients in ocean-touched supply chains can reference.

**What to avoid claiming**

- Do not claim FMC membership creates binding compliance obligations on freight forwarders — the FMC is a voluntary commitment platform, not a regulator.
- Do not cite green premium compression timelines as certainties — these are directional inference from IEA and FMC data, not fixed price forecasts.
- Do not claim FMC commitments satisfy CSRD or EU ETS compliance — *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether FMC-aligned offtake agreements count as verified Scope 3 emissions reductions under CSRD reporting methodology requires counsel and accountant review.

**Questions to pose in client conversations**

- Does your organization have FMC-type purchasing commitments or supplier selection criteria tied to low-carbon freight?
- Are your Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) emissions currently being reported, and if so, at what granularity?
- Have your airline or ocean carrier partners quoted SAF surcharges or green freight premiums, and are these being tracked against sustainability budgets?

---

## 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING — No immediate compliance obligation. The following are positioning actions with a 12-month window.**

**Action 1 — Map FMC membership against the client base.**
Determine which current clients are among FMC's 101 members or are direct counterparties to FMC-member supply chains. This is a commercial intelligence exercise: FMC-member clients will be the first to insert low-carbon service clauses into freight contracts or RFQ scoring criteria. The workspace should know which accounts face this pressure before the next tender cycle.
*Deadline: internal Q3 2026 recommended.*

**Action 2 — Establish SAF book-and-claim capability for air freight.**
The Avelia platform or equivalent SAF book-and-claim mechanisms are the current operational standard for verified SAF attribution in aviation. For workspaces with air as primary transport mode, the absence of a SAF attribution product is a gap that FMC-member clients and CSRD-reporting clients will increasingly surface. Steps: identify which airline partners participate in Avelia or equivalent platforms; determine whether current air waybill data infrastructure can capture and pass through SAF attribution certificates; assess cost exposure at current SAF surcharge levels.
*Deadline: commercial decision before next annual client contract renewals.*

**Action 3 — Add green-freight data tracking to subcontractor onboarding.**
As FMC-aligned carriers (road and ocean) begin tiering services by fuel type, the workspace needs subcontractor-level fuel and emissions data to construct client-facing Scope 3 reports. This is an operational systems gap. Steps: review current subcontractor data fields; add fuel-type and emissions-intensity fields to carrier and drayage onboarding questionnaires; align with ISO 14083 (transport emissions calculation standard) where applicable to ensure data is reportable to clients.
*Deadline: operational system update within 6 months.*

**Action 4 — Monitor the FMC 2026 Impact Brief and Davos 2026 outputs.**
The WEF's January 2026 narrative references a "newly released First Movers Coalition's impact brief" and names 2026 workplan questions around green premium mechanisms and new geographies. These outputs will carry updated commitment figures, new member names, and sector-specific findings that change the competitive picture. Assign a specific team member to pull the FMC Impact Brief 2026 (URL: https://www.weforum.org/publications/first-movers-coalition-impact-brief-2026/) and brief commercial leadership before Q4 2026.
*Re-check window: Q4 2026 / Davos 2027 at latest.*

**Action 5 — Add a sustainability-clause review to new freight contracts.**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether and how FMC-aligned client purchasing commitments translate into downstream freight contract obligations — including audit rights, emissions reporting requirements, or carrier selection restrictions — requires counsel review before the next major contract cycle.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Movers Coalition Impact Brief 2026 – World Economic Forum | https://www.weforum.org/publications/first-movers-coalition-impact-brief-2026/ | 2 | Most current WEF-issued quantitative assessment of FMC member commitment delivery across all seven sectors; provides updated member counts, offtake volumes, and sector-specific findings not available in the narrative piece. |
| First Movers Coalition: 120 Commitments Press Release – WEF Davos 2024 | https://www.weforum.org/press/2024/01/wef24-first-movers-coalition-commitments/ | 2 | Official WEF press release from Davos 2024 naming 96 members and 120+ commitments; provides a dated reference point for membership trajectory between 2024 and the 101-member figure in January 2026. |
| First Movers Coalition: Introduction and Overview of Commitments – WTO/WEF Presentation, March 2025 | https://www.wto.org/english/tratop_e/tessd_e/11_subsidies_presentation_by_wef.pdf | 2 | WEF formal presentation to WTO (March 2025) detailing FMC's eight sectors and ~25% of global GHG scope coverage; provides the technology-adoption phase model and policy-subsidy interaction analysis not in the narrative sources. |

---

FMC purchase commitments create guaranteed demand for green freight, enabling carriers to invest in decarbonisation with revenue certainty. Members include Amazon, Apple, Maersk, and major shippers.

For freight forwarders: (1) FMC member companies will specifically request green freight options in procurement, (2) carriers serving FMC members will offer premium green services, (3) forwarders who can connect FMC demand with carrier green supply have a brokerage opportunity.
