MCS chargers: deployment accelerating on TEN-T and US interstates
TCO parity: expected 2027-2028 for regional haul applications
prior.full_brief:
# Battery & Electric Vehicle Technology

## Technology Profile | MONITORING

---

## Section 1 — Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises five sourced blocks covering EV battery demand trajectories, chemistry evolution, supply chain vulnerability, lifecycle sustainability, and 2025–2026 technology trends. It is written for a global freight forwarding operation whose primary exposure to EV battery technology is threefold: (1) the electrification of the road-fleet segment of its own and clients' supply chains, (2) battery-as-cargo across air, road, and ocean modes, and (3) emerging regulatory obligations tied to battery passports and supply chain sustainability disclosures. Sources are the IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 (electric vehicle batteries chapter), the IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability report (December 2024), the IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability overview, the Carnegie Mellon University Engineering & Public Policy study (March 2024), and the CALSTART EV Battery Trends briefing (February 2026).

All claims are labeled FACT, ANALYSIS, or LEGAL. Inline source citations follow each subsection. Source spans are verbatim substrings of the source blocks provided.

---

## Section 2 — What's Being Tested or Deployed and By Whom

MONITORING | Re-check window: Q4 2026 (next IEA Global EV Outlook cycle expected; CALSTART 2026 trends update anticipated mid-2026)

**Global battery demand milestone**

FACT: "Battery demand in the energy sector, for both EV batteries and storage applications, reached the historical milestone of 1 TWh in 2024."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "demand for EV batteries grew to over 950 GWh – 25% more than in 2023."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

**Electric truck battery demand — the segment most relevant to freight operations**

FACT: "the sector whose demand grew the most was electric trucks, growing over 75% in 2024 to reach nearly 3% of global EV battery demand."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "Electric truck battery demand was driven by growth in China, but demand also ramped up in Europe (about 25%), which accounted for about 10% of the global total."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating drayage and road-haulage networks, the 75% growth in electric truck battery demand confirms that fleet electrification is no longer a future planning scenario — it is an active procurement cycle in Europe and China today. The re-check window for European fleet clients is now.

**Battery chemistry in active deployment**

FACT: "In 2025, the deployment of LFP batteries surpassed nickel-based chemistries for the first time, with a [surge] in demand globally, with China and Europe leading the way."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries – the main battery chemistry used in China – being almost 30% cheaper per kilowatt-hour (kWh) than lithium nickel cobalt manganese oxide (NMC) batteries, which remain the most widely used batteries in the United States and Europe."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "NMC batteries are typically limited to 80% to preserve long-term performance."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

**Sodium-ion: first commercial passenger EV deployment**

FACT: "battery manufacturer CATL, in partnership with Changan Automobile, unveiled the first passenger EV featuring a sodium-ion battery."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "The battery, named the 'Naxtra' battery, was announced last April and immediately sparked discussion over the benefits of sodium-ion technology, namely the increased battery safety and energy efficiency allowed by the technology."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

**Sodium-ion: setback in North America**

FACT: "Natron Energy ceased operations, underscoring the difficulty of scaling novel battery chemistries in a competitive market environment."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

**Solid-state batteries: signalled but not yet deployed at commercial freight-relevant scale**

FACT: "A number of companies have announced new vehicle offerings with SSBs, including Dodge and Mercedes-Benz signaling continued industry investment in the technology."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "Although there remain many critical engineering challenges to scalable manufacturing, steady progress in material science and engineering suggests that these barriers may eventually be overcome, bringing commercial viability incrementally closer."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

*Analytical inference:* Solid-state batteries remain a pre-commercial technology for freight-relevant heavy-duty applications. No sourced deployment data exists for commercial freight trucks. The workspace should monitor OEM announcements but not build procurement plans around solid-state availability before 2028 at earliest. CALSTART's language — "incrementally closer" — does not support a nearer date.

**800V architecture: fleet relevance emerging**

FACT: "higher voltage enables faster charging time, decreased weight, and increased thermal efficiency."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "The appeal of 800V is no longer a dream and may become a reality for fleets across the industry craving more power for their vehicles."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

**Battery price trajectory**

FACT: "Prices for lithium-ion battery packs fell 20% in 2024 – the largest drop since 2017."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "Battery pack prices fell in all markets, but the extent of the drop varied significantly, with the fastest declines seen in China, where prices fell nearly 30% in 2024, compared to 10-15% in Europe and the United States."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "lithium-ion battery pack pricing had dropped 8% since then [2024], costing $108 per kilowatt-hour."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "Goldman Sachs previously reporting in 2024 that they expected EV battery prices to drop by almost 50% in 2026, as compared to their 2023 price levels."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

*Analytical inference:* At $108/kWh (2025 BloombergNEF figure cited by CALSTART), battery pack cost is approaching — but has not yet crossed — the threshold typically cited in industry analysis as the point where electric trucks achieve total-cost-of-ownership parity with diesel without subsidy. The workspace should track the Goldman Sachs ~50% reduction projection against actual 2026 figures as a fleet-procurement trigger signal.

---

## Section 3 — What This Tells Us About Industry Trajectory

**Demand growth is confirmed; geographic diversification is underway**

FACT: "EV battery demand continues to grow, and is expected to reach more than 3 TWh in 2030 in the STEPS, up from about 1 TWh in 2024."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "the contribution of electric trucks to EV battery demand triples by 2030 to reach more than 8%, up from nearly 3% in 2024."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "Battery demand is set to continue growing fast based on current policy settings, increasing four-and-a-half times by 2030 and more than seven times by 2035."
*Source: EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

**China's structural advantage is widening, not converging**

FACT: "The faster pace of battery cost reduction and innovation in China has been enabled by fierce competition that has driven down profit margins for most producers (though not all), at the same time as driving up manufacturing efficiency and yields, as well as access to a large skilled workforce, and battery supply chain integration."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

FACT: "production at multiple stages of the supply chain across battery technologies occurs in China, primarily due to its role in materials refining and cathode production."
*Source: Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions, CMU Engineering & Public Policy, March 2024. https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html

*Analytical inference:* The combination of widening China price advantage and concentrated refining creates a structural dependency that is not resolved by cell-assembly diversification alone. For workspaces advising clients on EV fleet procurement in Europe or North America, this means procurement cost will differ materially from China-market benchmarks for the foreseeable medium term.

**Recycling: critical for 2035+, limited near-term impact**

FACT: "due to feedstock limitations, it will take about a decade before recycling has a significant impact on reducing primary mineral demand."
*Source: EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

FACT: "If recycling is scaled effectively, recycling can reduce lithium and nickel demand by 25%, and cobalt demand by 40% in 2050, in a scenario that meets national climate targets."
*Source: EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

*Analytical inference:* Near-term supply security planning for workspaces managing battery-as-cargo or battery-related freight flows cannot rely on recycling to buffer mineral price volatility before approximately 2035. This is a sourced IEA finding, not an estimate.

**Lifecycle emissions: electric already outperforms ICE at global average**

FACT: "the emissions of a medium-size battery electric car are half the emissions of an equivalent internal combustion engine (ICE) car as a global average."
*Source: EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

FACT: "This difference in emissions is similar to the global average in China, larger in the United Kingdom and Chile (over 60%), and smaller in India (20%)."
*Source: EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability, IEA, December 2024. https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability

*Operational implication:* For workspaces reporting Scope 3 emissions for road-freight segments, the IEA lifecycle finding is a sourced reference point for client sustainability disclosures. The 50% global-average reduction claim is supportable; claims above 60% are only supportable in UK and Chile grid-mix contexts and require grid-specific justification.

---

## Section 4 — Supplier Access and Procurement Reality

**Price access by geography**

*Analytical inference:* Chinese-manufactured LFP packs are approximately 30% cheaper per kWh than NMC packs dominant in Europe and North America (IEA sourced). Workspaces procuring or advising on fleet electrification outside China face a structural cost premium relative to Chinese-market benchmarks. The gap is expected to persist given manufacturing integration advantages documented by IEA.

**Supply chain concentration risk — not resolved by current diversification efforts**

FACT: "The primary lithium ion battery chemistries used in EVs today are lithium iron phosphate (LFP), nickel manganese cobalt (NMC), and nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA), which each depend on varying amounts of these minerals."
*Source: Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions, CMU Engineering & Public Policy, March 2024. https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html

FACT: "the LFP chemistry may currently be more vulnerable to supply disruptions in China, but NMC chemistries depend on more critical materials, resulting in more pathways for disruptions from other countries to affect the supply chain, such as the Democratic Republic of Congo or South Africa."
*Source: Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions, CMU Engineering & Public Policy, March 2024. https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html

FACT: "effectively reducing vulnerabilities to disruption related to any particular country may require addressing multiple layers of upstream supply relationships."
*Source: Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions, CMU Engineering & Public Policy, March 2024. https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html

FACT: "Last year, China restricted exports of graphite, another key material used in batteries."
*Source: Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions, CMU Engineering & Public Policy, March 2024. https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose clients operate or procure European or North American electric truck fleets, the CMU finding means that a disruption in China's refining sector — regardless of where the battery cells are assembled — propagates to the entire supply chain. This is a scenario-planning input, not a prediction.

**Mineral price outlook: surplus now, potential shortage by 2030**

FACT: "Low critical mineral prices are primarily driven by supply surplus, which is making it difficult for some mining companies to compete, thus increasing the level of supply chain concentration among established players. This surplus is expected to persist over the next few years, but low prices could discourage future investments and might cause supply shortages for lithium and nickel by 2030."
*Source: Global EV Outlook 2025 – Electric Vehicle Batteries, IEA, 2025. https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries

*Operational implication:* This is the most operationally important cost signal in this brief: battery prices are falling now, but the IEA flags a potential reversal for lithium and nickel by 2030. Workspaces advising clients on multi-year fleet electrification capex should flag this window explicitly.

**Battery passport and EU regulatory compliance — emerging procurement condition**

*Analytical inference:* The IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability report (December 2024) analyses battery passport requirements and EU Battery Regulation compliance as part of its supply chain sustainability framework. The report is published but its specific regulatory obligations under the EU Battery Regulation are not fully reproduced in the source blocks provided. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the specific obligations on battery importers and freight intermediaries under the EU Battery Regulation's due-diligence and passport provisions require counsel and regulatory review before the workspace advises clients on compliance posture.

**Second-life and recycling as emerging cargo and logistics streams**

FACT: "In 2025, researchers broke barriers by discovering new ways to extract critical metals from EV batteries."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

FACT: "we will begin to see a wave of older EV models reaching end-of-life and we are already beginning to see emerging policies develop to promote the repurposing and recycling of these batteries."
*Source: CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026, CALSTART, February 27, 2026. https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/

*Operational implication:* End-of-life battery logistics — collection, classification, and transport of used battery packs — is an emerging freight vertical. Workspaces with automotive or industrial cargo handling capability should evaluate whether dangerous goods certification for used lithium-ion battery transport is in scope for their service offering. Specific dangerous goods classification requirements for used batteries are not reproduced in the source blocks; this requires separate regulatory research (IATA, ADR, IMDG).

---

## Section 5 — Operational Fit by Transport Mode and Cargo Vertical

**Air (primary mode)**

*Analytical inference:* Battery-as-cargo classifications for lithium-ion batteries are the primary air-mode exposure. EV battery chemistry evolution — particularly the rise of LFP packs and the emergence of sodium-ion — changes the hazard profile of batteries transported as air freight. LFP batteries are characterised by different thermal runaway properties than NMC, which is operationally relevant for UN 3480/3481 classification and packaging compliance. No sourced dangerous goods air-mode figures appear in the current source blocks; this is a research gap requiring IATA DGR review.

*Analytical inference:* The ultra-fast charging and 800V architecture trends documented by CALSTART are road-mode infrastructure developments and do not directly affect air-mode freight operations. They are relevant only insofar as electrification of ground-support equipment at airport operations might eventually reduce ground-handling emissions exposure.

**Road (secondary mode)**

*Analytical inference:* Road is the mode most directly affected by EV battery technology deployment. The 75% growth in electric truck battery demand (IEA sourced), the emergence of 800V architecture enabling faster fleet charging (CALSTART sourced), and the cost trajectory toward $108/kWh (CALSTART/BloombergNEF sourced) together indicate that electric truck procurement is entering a commercially viable phase in Europe and China. For workspaces operating or contracting road haulage, the decision horizon for fleet electrification planning is 2025–2028.

*Analytical inference:* V2G (vehicle-to-grid) technology, documented by CALSTART as an emerging 2025 trend for grid integration, is operationally relevant to workspaces managing depot charging infrastructure. If the workspace operates owned or leased charging infrastructure, V2G capability may represent both a cost-management and a grid-compliance asset in European markets where grid demand-response programmes are active.

**Ocean (tertiary mode)**

*Analytical inference:* Ocean-mode exposure to EV battery technology is primarily as cargo (battery packs for vehicle manufacturers, consumer electronics batteries, and end-of-life battery return flows). The IEA documentation of supply chain concentration in China is directly relevant to ocean trade lane risk on Asia–Europe and Asia–Americas routes: a disruption in Chinese battery production or mineral refining propagates to export volumes on those corridors. No sourced ocean freight rate or surcharge data for battery cargo appears in the provided source blocks.

---

## Section 6 — Competitive Positioning Implications

*No sourced competitor-specific positioning data for freight forwarders or logistics operators on EV battery technology appears in the provided source blocks. The following is inference from the technology trajectory, labeled accordingly.*

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that develop operational competency in battery-as-cargo handling — including dangerous goods certification, temperature-controlled transport for battery packs, and second-life battery logistics — are positioned ahead of competitors that treat batteries as undifferentiated general freight. The CALSTART-documented wave of end-of-life EV batteries reaching recycling streams is a near-term volume signal, not a distant one.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces managing road fleet operations for clients in Europe should treat the electric truck battery demand growth figure (75%, IEA 2025) as a client-conversation opening. Clients actively replacing diesel fleets are making procurement and route-planning decisions now that affect which logistics providers they retain. A workspace that can advise on charging infrastructure logistics, battery supply lead times, and LFP vs NMC chemistry trade-offs is differentiated.

*Analytical inference:* The widening China price advantage in battery manufacturing (IEA: prices fell ~30% in China vs 10–15% in Europe and the US in 2024) creates a structural split in fleet electrification economics by geography. Clients procuring fleets in Europe face higher capex for equivalent battery capacity than Chinese-market competitors. This is an input to cross-regional cost advisory conversations.

---

## Section 7 — Conversational and Strategic Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say to clients:**

- FACT-grounded: EV battery demand hit 1 TWh globally in 2024, a decade-scale milestone. Electric truck demand grew 75% year-on-year. These are IEA figures, defensible in any client briefing. *Source: IEA Global EV Outlook 2025.*
- FACT-grounded: Battery pack prices fell 20% in 2024 globally and ~30% in China. At $108/kWh (BloombergNEF, cited by CALSTART), packs are approaching total-cost-of-ownership thresholds for fleet electrification. Goldman Sachs projected ~50% reduction vs 2023 levels by 2026.
- FACT-grounded: LFP surpassed nickel-based chemistries in 2025 deployment. LFP is ~30% cheaper per kWh than NMC (IEA). The technology is in active fleet deployment, not pilot phase.
- FACT-grounded: The IEA finds that electric cars produce half the lifecycle emissions of equivalent ICE vehicles at the global average — a defensible Scope 3 reference for road segments.

**What the workspace should not claim:**

- Do not claim solid-state batteries are commercially available for freight trucks. No sourced deployment data supports this; the CALSTART source explicitly notes ongoing engineering challenges.
- Do not claim recycling resolves near-term mineral supply risk. IEA explicitly states recycling will not significantly affect primary mineral demand for approximately a decade.
- Do not assert specific battery-as-air-cargo compliance parameters from this brief. IATA DGR governs those; they are not reproduced in the source blocks.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- The Goldman Sachs ~50% price reduction figure is a 2024 projection, not a confirmed 2026 outcome. Label it as such when using it.
- The 50% lifecycle emissions reduction is a global average; the figure is smaller in high-coal-grid markets (India: ~20%). Grid mix matters. Do not apply the global average figure in market-specific conversations without checking grid context.

---

## Section 8 — Time-to-Market, Procurement Window, and Action

MONITORING — Re-check Q4 2026

**Confirmed near-term signals with operational implications:**

| Signal | Evidence | Action window |
|---|---|---|
| Electric truck battery demand tripling by 2030 | IEA STEPS projection (sourced) | Fleet electrification decisions for European road ops: 2025–2028 |
| Battery pack price ~$108/kWh (2025) | BloombergNEF via CALSTART (sourced) | TCO parity assessment for road fleet: now |
| LFP surpassing NMC in deployment | CALSTART (sourced) | Chemistry-specific cargo handling protocols: review now |
| Potential Li/Ni supply shortage by 2030 | IEA (sourced) | Multi-year fleet procurement: build in price-risk buffer from 2027 |
| End-of-life battery logistics wave emerging | CALSTART (sourced) | Dangerous goods capability assessment: 2026 |

**Specific actions:**

1. *Road fleet electrification advisory:* Brief road-haulage clients on the IEA demand trajectory and LFP cost advantage data. Frame the 2025–2028 window as the actionable procurement period before potential mineral price reversal (IEA: possible Li/Ni shortage by 2030).
2. *Battery-as-cargo dangerous goods review:* Commission a review of current IATA DGR, ADR, and IMDG classifications for LFP and sodium-ion battery chemistries, as chemistry shift (LFP now dominant) may change packaging and handling requirements. This is a research gap in the current source pool — flagged as a research action, not a compliance claim.
3. *EU Battery Regulation monitoring:* The IEA EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability report identifies battery passport requirements as an emerging compliance layer. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the workspace's role (as freight forwarder handling battery cargo, or as importer of battery-containing equipment) under the EU Battery Regulation's due-diligence provisions requires counsel confirmation before any compliance posture is communicated to clients.
4. *Second-life battery logistics scoping:* Evaluate whether the workspace's dangerous goods and temperature-controlled handling capabilities position it to service the emerging end-of-life battery return and recycling logistics stream documented by CALSTART.
5. *Re-check trigger:* Set Q4 2026 re-check for (a) IEA Global EV Outlook 2026 update on electric truck demand and mineral prices, (b) CALSTART 2026 end-of-year trends update, and (c) Goldman Sachs ~50% price reduction projection outcome vs actual 2026 battery pack pricing.

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| IEA – Global EV Outlook 2025: Electric Vehicle Batteries | https://www.iea.org/reports/global-ev-outlook-2025/electric-vehicle-batteries | 2 | IEA regulator-adjacent intergovernmental body; 2025 edition; carries the 1 TWh milestone, 75% truck demand growth, price trends, and 2030 demand projections used throughout this brief |
| IEA – EV Battery Supply Chain Sustainability (December 2024) | https://www.iea.org/reports/ev-battery-supply-chain-sustainability | 2 | IEA companion report; provides the lifecycle emissions findings (50% ICE reduction), recycling timeline (decade lag), mineral demand projections, and battery passport regulatory context |
| Carnegie Mellon University – Anticipating and Reducing EV Battery Supply Disruptions | https://engineering.cmu.edu/news-events/news/2024/03/19-ev-supply-chain.html | 4 | Peer-reviewed Nature Communications study (CMU EPP team); provides the sourced chemistry-specific supply chain vulnerability index (LFP/NMC/NCA) and multi-layer upstream concentration finding |
| CALSTART – Top 10 EV Battery Trends in 2025 and What We Can Expect in 2026 | https://calstart.org/top-10-ev-battery-trends-in-2025-and-what-we-can-expect-in-2026-february-27-2026/ | 4 | Authoritative clean-transport non-profit; February 2026 publication; carries 800V architecture, V2G, LFP deployment milestone, $108/kWh pricing, sodium-ion CATL deployment, solid-state status, and second-life battery trends |

---
prior.intersection_summary: IRENA's Critical Materials: Batteries for Electric Vehicles (id: 4703866f) directly conditions the IEA battery cost trajectory: if lithium and cobalt supply chains remain concentrated, the $100/kWh threshold and the 2027–2028 TCO parity projection may slip, changing fleet procurement timing. IEA Global Supply Chains of EV Batteries (id: 681a4d52) provides upstream supply chain context that reinforces the same risk. The IEA Data and Statistics Hub (id: 7b0bb91d) is the live tracking resource for charging infrastructure deployment on TEN-T and US corridors, which is the gating infrastructure factor for EV carrier viability on the workspace's European and Americas road lanes.

prior.title: IPCC Opens Second-Order Draft Review for Special Report on Climate Change and Cities (March 2027 Release)
prior.summary: The IPCC has launched the Second-Order Draft (SOD) review phase for its Special Report on Climate Change and Cities, inviting government representatives and experts to submit comments from May 8 to July 3, 2026. Expert registration closes June 26, 2026. This report, scheduled for release in March 2027, will be the first IPCC publication in the seventh assessment cycle and the only special report in this cycle. The SOD includes the first draft of the Summary for Policymakers (SPM), a critical tool for informing global climate policy decisions in urban areas.
prior.full_brief:
# Special Report on Climate Change and Cities — IPCC Second-Order Draft Review (March 2027 Release)

## Research Summary

---

# 1. What the Research Found

**FACT:** The IPCC is producing a *Special Report on Climate Change and Cities*, listed on the IPCC Reports page with a scheduled release of March 2027.
*Source: Reports — IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2026. https://ipcc.ch/reports.*

The source content confirms the report's existence and scheduled release date. It does not describe the report's methodology, scope, chapter structure, findings, or which working group is leading it. The item title references a "Second-Order Draft Review" phase; however, the source content does not contain the phrase "second-order draft," does not describe the review phase, and does not confirm that a second-order draft review is currently open or its duration.

*Specific methodology, chapter scope, lead authors, and review-phase status not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.*

*Specific [second-order draft review period details] not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.*

**FACT:** Two additional IPCC reports are listed for December 2027 release alongside this special report: the *2027 IPCC Methodology Report on Inventories for Short-lived Climate Forcers* and the *2027 IPCC Methodology Report on Carbon Dioxide Removal Technologies, Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories (Additional guidance)*.
*Source: Reports — IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2026. https://ipcc.ch/reports.*

*Analytical inference:* The clustering of the Cities Special Report (March 2027) ahead of the two December 2027 methodology reports suggests it is on a separate, earlier production timeline — consistent with IPCC practice of issuing Special Reports between full Assessment Report cycles. The AR6 cycle closed with the Synthesis Report in March 2023; the AR7 Working Group reports are listed but undated in the source. The Cities report therefore sits in the gap between cycles, not as a Working Group product.

*Analytical inference:* IPCC Special Reports typically follow a process that includes scoping, first-order draft expert review, second-order draft government and expert review, and final government approval of the Summary for Policymakers before publication. If the report is on track for March 2027, a second-order draft government review would be consistent with where the report would be in its cycle at this point. However, the source does not confirm this, and the review-phase status must be treated as unconfirmed.

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating freight forwarding and logistics in or serving major urban centers — including road drayage, last-mile delivery, air cargo hubs embedded in metropolitan regions, and port-adjacent warehousing — this report is a direct upstream driver of urban freight regulation. IPCC Special Reports do not themselves impose obligations, but they establish the scientific baseline that national and city-level regulators cite when designing clean-air zones, urban truck-access restrictions, zero-emission vehicle mandates, and port decarbonisation requirements.

*Analytical inference:* Cargo verticals with urban-dense delivery profiles — events, fine art, luxury goods, film and television production — are disproportionately affected by urban freight policies because their shipments concentrate in city centres, airports, and culturally significant urban districts. Regulations that tighten urban truck-access windows, require zero-emission last-mile vehicles, or impose congestion-pricing on freight vehicles in city cores would directly affect routing, dwell time, and cost for these verticals.

*Analytical inference:* The report's findings are likely to inform post-AR6 municipal and national policy debates on urban mobility, logistics zoning, and freight emission standards. Workspaces that understand the scientific framing before it becomes policy language are better positioned to engage with clients facing those policy pressures and with regulators who will shape them.

The source does not contain the report's findings, so no finding-specific operational analysis is possible at this stage. The operational materiality is structural — this report is a leading indicator for urban freight regulation — not based on published conclusions.

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

*Analytical inference:* The primary strategic shift is timing awareness: the March 2027 publication will mark the point at which city-level regulators, national transport ministries, and corporate sustainability functions begin citing this report in policy consultations, RFP requirements, and client ESG due diligence questionnaires. Workspaces with urban freight exposure should treat March 2027 as the moment their urban-operation sustainability positioning will be tested against a new authoritative baseline.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces that communicate publicly on urban sustainability — in client proposals, on ESG disclosure statements, or in media engagement — the approach between now and March 2027 should be forward-referenced rather than backward-referenced. Citing AR6 findings as current best practice remains valid until the Cities report publishes; citing the Cities report before it is approved and released risks overstating what the science says.

*Analytical inference:* Operationally, the report's release window (March 2027) aligns with a period when several urban-freight-adjacent regulatory processes are expected to be active across the EU and UK — including post-2025 review of Euro 7 implementation, urban access regulation (UAR) frameworks under discussion in major European cities, and CSRD supply-chain reporting cycles that will capture Scope 3 urban-distribution emissions. The convergence of the IPCC scientific baseline with active regulatory review cycles is the mechanism through which this report converts into operational pressure.

*Specific [Euro 7 post-2025 review schedule and city UAR consultation timelines] not confirmed from primary sources as of 2026-05-27; labeled here as analytical context, not sourced fact.*

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The IPCC is scheduled to publish a Special Report specifically on Climate Change and Cities in March 2027. This is the first IPCC special report focused on urban systems and will become the authoritative scientific reference for city-level climate policy globally.
- Workspaces managing freight in major urban centres — including time-critical, high-value cargo — are in the regulatory front line of whatever policy frameworks this report informs. Understanding the report's direction before it finalises is part of intelligent supply chain risk management.
- The workspace is monitoring the report's progress and will brief commercial and operations leads on material policy implications as the Summary for Policymakers is approved.

**What to avoid:**

- Do not characterise the report's findings. The source does not contain them and they have not been approved by IPCC member governments. Citing draft or pre-publication language as settled science misrepresents the IPCC process and risks reputational harm if the approved text differs.
- Do not state that the second-order draft review is open or describe its content. The source does not confirm this and the item title should not be treated as sourced fact.
- Do not link this report directly to specific cost increases or regulatory changes without a sourced causal chain. The report is an upstream driver; the downstream policy decisions are not yet made.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- How does your organisation currently account for urban delivery emissions in your Scope 3 reporting, and is your logistics provider able to separate urban-centre movements for disclosure purposes?
- Are your warehousing or last-mile delivery operations in any cities that have active clean-air zone or urban vehicle-access consultations running in 2025–2026?

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

The source confirms the report exists and is scheduled for March 2027. It does not resolve:

- **What the report finds.** No findings, chapter structure, key conclusions, or Summary for Policymakers language is available from the source or any cited document.
- **Whether the second-order draft review is currently open.** The item title asserts this but the source content does not confirm it.
- **Which working group or cross-working-group team is responsible.** The source lists the report under "Special and Methodology Reports" but does not assign it to WG I, II, or III.
- **Which urban freight or logistics topics the report covers.** Urban freight, logistics emissions, last-mile delivery, and cargo-specific urban policies may or may not be in scope. Not sourceable until the approved report or an outline from the IPCC is published.
- **The regulatory translation pathway.** How municipal and national regulators will use the report's findings is not determinable from the source. The connection between the IPCC finding and specific regulation is speculative until policy bodies cite it.

*Does NOT resolve what the Cities Special Report finds or which urban freight policies it will inform — see the approved Summary for Policymakers (expected March 2027) for the binding scientific basis.*

---

# 6. Sources

| Source | Type | Date | URL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reports — IPCC | Tier 3 — Intergovernmental Body Position (IPCC official reports registry) | 2026 | https://ipcc.ch/reports |

---

# New Sources Identified

*No external sources beyond the input source were cited in this brief. Section omitted.*
