prior.title: USGBC Projects and 2026 LEED Initiative Updates
prior.summary: USGBC projects page aggregates multiple 2026 initiatives including LEED committee volunteer opportunities, Greenbuild 2026 conference activities, regional clean energy business events, and sustainability certifications. Notable items include volunteer applications for LEED committees, workforce scholarship programs, Maryland's 2026 policy landscape analysis, and articles on zero waste economics and resilience in LEED projects.
prior.full_brief:
# USGBC LEED v5 Initiative: 2026 Transition Timeline and Certification Framework

**Format:** Market Signal Brief | **Severity:** MONITORING | **Re-check window:** July 2026 (v4/v4.1 registration extended deadline confirmation) and Q4 2026 (final LEED v5 exams publication)

---

## Context Note

This brief covers the USGBC's LEED v5 initiative as it stands in May–June 2026, synthesised across five sourced blocks. The signal is directly relevant to workspaces operating in sectors where tenant fit-out, logistics facility certification, or client sustainability requirements reference LEED status — including warehousing, distribution centres, hospitality facilities, data centres, and commercial interiors. The transition timeline has commercial and procurement implications for workspaces that occupy, lease, commission, or advise on certified facilities.

---

# Section 1 — What's Moving and What Triggered It

**LEED v5 is now the active rating system, and a one-year registration extension for v4/v4.1 has just been issued.**

USGBC has extended the LEED v4 and LEED v4.1 commercial registration close date from June 30, 2026 to June 30, 2027. *Source: "LEED v4 and v4.1 commercial registration close extended to June 30, 2027," USGBC, May 27, 2026. https://www.usgbc.org/articles/leed-v4-and-v41-commercial-registration-close-extended-june-30-2027

The extension was triggered by direct market feedback. USGBC states the decision "reflects direct feedback from you, our global LEED community. USGBC members and project teams have shared that market conditions—including supply chain constraints, financing challenges and regulatory uncertainty—are affecting project timelines." *Source: USGBC extension article, May 27, 2026. https://www.usgbc.org/articles/leed-v4-and-v41-commercial-registration-close-extended-june-30-2027

*Analytical inference:* This extension reveals that the LEED v5 uptake rate has encountered real-market friction — supply chain disruption, financing conditions, and regulatory uncertainty are slowing new project initiation at a scale sufficient to prompt a policy change from the governing body. For workspaces assessing whether facility partners or landlords will achieve or maintain LEED status on planned timelines, this is a signal that certification schedules may slip, and that v4/v4.1 documentation processes remain the operative default for a larger share of the active market than USGBC's v5 launch communications implied.

LEED v5 was ratified by members on March 28, 2025, and is currently available for registration via the Arc platform for BD+C (New Construction, Core and Shell), ID+C (Commercial Interiors), and O+M (Existing Buildings). *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

Starting on July 1, 2027, LEED v5 will be the only version available for new registrations for the commercial LEED BD+C, ID+C, and O+M rating systems, with limited exceptions. *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

---

# Section 2 — Who's Driving It and What They Want

**USGBC** is the governing body setting the rating system and transition schedule. Its stated interest is accelerating decarbonisation, resilience, and occupant wellbeing through measurable building performance, while maintaining market adoption rates that sustain the certification ecosystem's commercial viability. The one-year extension is evidence that market pace, not only technical ambition, shapes USGBC's sequencing. *Source: USGBC extension article, May 27, 2026. https://www.usgbc.org/articles/leed-v4-and-v41-commercial-registration-close-extended-june-30-2027

**GBCI (Green Business Certification Inc.)** is the certification body administering the LEED credential exams and project certification reviews. GBCI has announced LEED v5 beta credentialing exams for 2026, signalling a parallel professional-transition programme alongside the project-registration transition. *Source: "LEED v5 beta exams to launch in 2026," GBCI, January 29, 2026. https://www.gbci.org/leed-v5-beta-exams-launch-2026

**Canada Green Building Council (CAGBC)** is active as a consortium partner, having communicated the Arc platform certification launch, new calculators, and the spring 2026 credential exam transition to its network. *Source: "LEED v5 expands: New Arc certification tools and resources for users," CAGBC, November 3, 2025. https://www.cagbc.org/news-resources/cagbc-news/leed-v5-expands-new-arc-certification-tools-and-resources-for-users/

*Analytical inference:* CAGBC's active communication of the v5 transition to the Canadian market is relevant for workspaces with Canadian facility footprints or Canadian client relationships where LEED certification appears in lease, procurement, or ESG-reporting clauses.

**Project teams and building sector operators** are the primary commercial drivers of the transition pace. Their feedback — specifically on supply chain constraints, financing challenges, and regulatory uncertainty — directly produced the registration extension. *Source: USGBC extension article, May 27, 2026. https://www.usgbc.org/articles/leed-v4-and-v41-commercial-registration-close-extended-june-30-2027

---

# Section 3 — Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Confirmed milestones (FACTs with sourced dates):**

The following dates are confirmed on the official USGBC deadlines page as of the current source content:

- LEED v4 and v4.1 registration close (all commercial BD+C, ID+C, O+M): **June 30, 2027**
- LEED v4 and v4.1 certification sunset: **June 30, 2033**
- LEED v4.1 O+M: Interiors registration close: **June 30, 2027**; certification sunset: **June 30, 2033**
- LEED v4/v4.1 Campus/Group/Volume new prototype registration close: **June 30, 2027**
- LEED v4/v4.1 volume projects referencing an existing prototype: **June 30, 2030** (exception to the 2027 close date)
- LEED v5 becomes the only option for new commercial registrations: **July 1, 2027**

*Source: LEED Certification Deadlines, USGBC. https://www.usgbc.org/tools/leed-certification/deadlines
*Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

For split-review BD+C and ID+C projects, LEED notes that "LEED BD+C and ID+C projects using the split review timeline must submit for design review by June 30, 2033, but will have an additional 18 months (until December 31, 2034) to submit for construction review." *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

**LEED v5 for Cities public comment:**
- First public comment: November 3, 2025 – January 9, 2026 (closed)
- Second public comment: opened May 14, 2026, closes June 16, 2026
*Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

**Professional credentialing transition (GBCI):**
- LEED Green Associate beta exam window: **April 28 – June 30, 2026**
- LEED AP with Specialty beta exam window: **June 30 – August 31, 2026**
- Beta exam registration opened: mid-February 2026
- Final LEED v5 exams: toward the end of 2026 (stated but not precise-dated)
- Beta exam participants receive results approximately three to four months after close of the beta period

*Source: "LEED v5 beta exams to launch in 2026," GBCI, January 29, 2026. https://www.gbci.org/leed-v5-beta-exams-launch-2026

*Analytical inference:* The professional credential transition running in parallel with the project-registration transition creates a near-term window (mid-2026) where qualified LEED v5 credentialed professionals are still scarce — the beta exam data collection phase is still open as of this brief's generation date. Workspaces commissioning LEED v5-certified facilities should factor in consultant availability risk: the pool of credentialed LEED v5 APs will be limited until Q4 2026 at earliest.

**Arc platform expansion (2026 roadmap):**
The CAGBC source — reporting USGBC content from November 2025 — states that in 2026, project teams can expect: additional Project Priority credits; expanded international guidance and alternative compliance paths; regular addenda updates; and project impact reports summarising certifications using metrics on emissions, energy, water, waste, IAQ, transportation and resilience. *Source: CAGBC, November 3, 2025. https://www.cagbc.org/news-resources/cagbc-news/leed-v5-expands-new-arc-certification-tools-and-resources-for-users/

*Analytical inference:* The 2026 Arc roadmap items listed above are stated intent, not confirmed deployed features. The re-check window for Arc platform expansion should be Q3–Q4 2026 when these features are expected to be published.

**Conversion trigger — signal to commercial pressure:**
*Analytical inference:* The signal converts from "transition in progress" to "active commercial pressure" at two points: (1) July 1, 2027, when v5 becomes mandatory for new commercial registrations, creating a hard cliff for any project team not yet registered under v4/v4.1; and (2) when the first wave of LEED v5-certified facilities enters the leasing and logistics market, embedding v5 performance standards into lease covenants and client procurement criteria.

---

# Section 4 — Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**For workspaces occupying or procuring space in LEED-certified or LEED-targeting facilities** (warehouses and distribution centres, data centres, hospitality properties, commercial interiors — all of which appear explicitly in the LEED v4/v4.1 deadline tables):

The June 30, 2033 certification sunset for v4/v4.1 is the operative backstop. Any facility for which LEED certification status supports a lease clause, client contract, or ESG disclosure has until June 2033 to complete certification under those versions if registered by June 2027. *Source: LEED Certification Deadlines, USGBC. https://www.usgbc.org/tools/leed-certification/deadlines

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating out of leased warehousing or distribution facilities — a rating system category explicitly covered by LEED v4/v4.1 BD+C and O+M — lease agreements that reference LEED certification status may need to specify which version is the contractual standard, since v4/v4.1 and v5 have different credit structures and performance thresholds.

*Analytical inference:* LEED v5 introduces three stated impact areas (decarbonisation, resilience, occupant wellbeing — referenced in USGBC communications but the detailed credit structure is in the LEED credit library, not in the source blocks provided). Workspaces whose facility partners are transitioning from v4.1 to v5 should request documentation of which rating system version applies to any certification claim used in client-facing ESG reporting.

**Cost dimension:** USGBC states that fees for LEED v5 projects are the same as earlier versions, and all LEED registration and certification fees are available on the LEED certification fees page. *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5 

*Specific fee schedule figures not available from primary sources as of May 29, 2026 — the source blocks reference the fees page but do not reproduce the fee table.*

*Analytical inference:* The near-term cost implication for workspaces is not registration fees (which are unchanged) but the professional services cost associated with recertification documentation — particularly for O+M recertification, which now has a June 30, 2027 registration deadline and a June 30, 2033 review deadline under v4/v4.1. *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

**Campus, group, and volume approaches:** LEED campus, group, and volume approaches are not yet available for LEED v5. For workspaces or facility partners operating multi-site or portfolio certification strategies, this is a functional gap: the volume approach deadline under v4/v4.1 is June 30, 2027 for new prototypes, with an exception allowing volume projects referencing existing prototypes to register until June 30, 2030. *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026; LEED Certification Deadlines, USGBC. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5; https://www.usgbc.org/tools/leed-certification/deadlines

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with multi-site logistics or warehousing footprints where facility partners are pursuing volume LEED certification, the exception to the 2027 close date (June 30, 2030 for volume projects referencing an existing prototype) provides additional runway — but only if a qualifying prototype is already registered.

---

# Section 5 — Competitive Implications

**No named competitor positioning is available from the source blocks provided.** The source materials are USGBC, GBCI, and CAGBC communications; they do not contain comparative operator-level data on which freight forwarders, logistics operators, or facility providers hold LEED certification or are transitioning to v5.

*Specific competitor/operator LEED certification data not available from primary sources as of May 29, 2026. This is a research gap — see New Sources Identified section for candidate sources.*

*Analytical inference:* In sectors where logistics facility certification (particularly warehouses and distribution centres, which are explicitly named in the LEED v4/v4.1 and expected v5 rating system tables) forms part of client sustainability credentials, workspaces that can document their facilities' LEED version, registration date, and expected certification timeline are positioned ahead of competitors who cannot. The shift to v5 creates a near-term information asymmetry: clients who understand the difference between v4.1 Platinum and v5 certification — and the higher performance thresholds the latter implies — will begin asking the question before most service providers have a rehearsed answer.

*Analytical inference:* The credential gap is also competitive. With LEED v5 AP beta exams only running April–August 2026 and final exams not published until late 2026, workspaces whose sustainability, operations, or commercial teams hold or are pursuing LEED credentials have a window to become early LEED v5-credentialed advisors in client conversations — before the credential becomes commoditised.

---

# Section 6 — Client Conversation Talking Points

**What workspaces can credibly say now:**

- USGBC has officially extended the LEED v4/v4.1 commercial registration deadline by one year, to June 30, 2027, citing supply chain constraints, financing challenges, and regulatory uncertainty in the market. Sourced — safe to cite in client meetings. *Source: USGBC, May 27, 2026.*
- LEED v5 is now active and available for registration in Arc for BD+C, ID+C, and O+M. It is the recommended path for new projects from USGBC's perspective.
- The certification sunset for v4/v4.1 is June 30, 2033. Any facility registered before June 30, 2027 has until that date to complete certification — this is a long operational runway.
- For workspaces in sectors where warehouses and distribution centres are the operative facility type: both versions explicitly cover that asset class.

**Questions to pose to facility partners, landlords, and logistics property providers:**

- Which LEED version is your facility registered or certifying under?
- If registered under v4 or v4.1, what is your target certification submission date against the June 2033 sunset?
- If your facility is pursuing O+M recertification, have you registered by or before the June 30, 2027 deadline?
- Is your certification consultant or project team LEED v5-credentialed, or v4.1-credentialed?

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not present LEED v5 certification as already widespread — the source materials indicate that v5 campus, group, and volume approaches are not yet available, and the credential transition is still in beta. Overclaiming on market penetration of v5 is a credibility risk.
- Do not conflate LEED v4.1 Platinum with LEED v5 certification in client-facing ESG materials — they are different rating systems with different credit structures.
- Do not assert that the registration extension reflects a LEED v5 quality or credibility issue — USGBC is explicit that the extension is a market-flexibility response, not a rating system revision.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether LEED certification status constitutes a binding contractual representation in any specific lease, logistics services agreement, or ESG reporting covenant, and which version of the standard satisfies that representation, requires counsel review against the specific contract language.

---

# Section 7 — What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Audit facility LEED registration status before June 30, 2027**
For workspaces occupying or procuring warehousing, distribution, or commercial interior space under leases or services agreements that reference LEED certification, confirm: (a) whether each facility is registered under v4, v4.1, or v5; (b) the registration date and target certification submission date; (c) whether O+M recertification is required and registered. The June 30, 2027 deadline is 13 months from this brief's generation date.

**Action 2 — Flag LEED version in client-facing ESG materials**
Where LEED certification status is cited in sustainability reports, RFP responses, or client presentations, specify the version (v4, v4.1, or v5). The market is mid-transition; version specificity is increasingly material to informed clients.

**Action 3 — Track LEED v5 credential transition for team members**
GBCI's LEED v5 beta credentialing exams are running now (LEED Green Associate: through June 30, 2026; LEED AP with Specialty: June 30 – August 31, 2026). For team members whose roles involve facility sustainability oversight or client advisory work on building sustainability, the beta window offers a 30% fee discount and early-access advantages. *Source: GBCI, January 29, 2026. https://www.gbci.org/leed-v5-beta-exams-launch-2026

**Action 4 — Monitor v5 campus/volume availability for multi-site footprints**
LEED v5 campus, group, and volume approaches are not yet available. For workspaces with multi-facility logistics networks, watch for USGBC's announcement of v5 volume availability — the current deadline extension to 2030 for volume projects referencing existing v4/v4.1 prototypes provides interim runway.

**Action 5 — Participate in LEED v5 for Cities second public comment (if relevant to urban logistics operations)**
The second public comment period for LEED v5 for Cities is open May 14 – June 16, 2026, and is open to the public. Workspaces with interests in urban freight, last-mile facilities, or city-level sustainability commitments may find the Cities rating system relevant to their operations. *Source: LEED v5 FAQ, USGBC Help Center, updated May 28, 2026. https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5

**Re-check window:** July 2026 — confirm whether any further LEED v5 transition deadline changes were announced following the May 27, 2026 extension; Q4 2026 — check whether final LEED v5 exams have been published and whether v5 campus/volume approaches have been announced.

---

# New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| LEED v5 FAQ – USGBC Help Center | https://support.usgbc.org/hc/en-us/articles/25316160948755-LEED-v5 | 2 | Authoritative USGBC guidance document; primary source for all transition deadlines, registration rules, and eligibility conditions cited in this brief |
| LEED Certification Deadlines – USGBC | https://www.usgbc.org/tools/leed-certification/deadlines | 1 | Official USGBC deadline table; single authoritative source for all registration close and certification sunset dates across all versions and pathways |
| LEED v4 & v4.1 Registration Extension – USGBC Article | https://www.usgbc.org/articles/leed-v4-and-v41-commercial-registration-close-extended-june-30-2027 | 1 | Official USGBC announcement of the one-year extension; provides the market-rationale language (supply chain, financing, regulatory uncertainty) that contextualises the signal |
| LEED v5 Expands: New Arc Certification Tools – CAGBC | https://www.cagbc.org/news-resources/cagbc-news/leed-v5-expands-new-arc-certification-tools-and-resources-for-users/ | 3 | CAGBC as consortium partner documents Arc platform certification launch and 2026 roadmap commitments; relevant for Canadian market and multi-national facility footprints |
| LEED v5 Beta Exams to Launch in 2026 – GBCI | https://www.gbci.org/leed-v5-beta-exams-launch-2026 | 2 | GBCI official announcement; provides specific exam windows, discount structure, and timeline for professional credential transition — directly actionable for workspace team members |

---

prior.title: Wisconsin 2023 State Freight Plan - Updated Multimodal Transportation Framework
prior.summary: Wisconsin Department of Transportation (WisDOT) has adopted the 2023 State Freight Plan, a long-range eight-year multimodal plan addressing the state's freight transportation needs. The plan covers highways, local roads, freight rail, aviation, harbors, and intermodal connections. Updates are scheduled every four years, with the next update cycle beginning in 2025-2026 for completion in 2027.
prior.full_brief:
# Wisconsin 2023 State Freight Plan — Updated Multimodal Transportation Framework

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

MONITORING

This document covers the Wisconsin Department of Transportation (WisDOT) multimodal freight planning framework, anchored on the adopted 2023 State Freight Plan (SFP) and the active 2027 SFP update cycle. It is produced for operations leads, commercial leadership, and logistics professionals whose freight movements transit Wisconsin or connect to the Wisconsin multimodal network — including highway, rail, harbor, aviation, and intermodal segments.

**Convention notes:** All dated milestones are drawn verbatim from sourced WisDOT planning documents. Forward phases labeled "(planned)" reflect WisDOT's stated schedule; they are cited as facts where sourced and labeled analytical inferences where estimated. The Federal Register source block was access-restricted at time of generation; content from that source is not used for FACT claims. The 2023 SFP Full Plan PDF was listed as a corroborating source but was not available as a populated source block; specific plan text and investment figures from that document are noted as research gaps where they would otherwise be required. Inline citations follow each subsection.

---

## What This Plan Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**What it is:** WisDOT updates its State Freight Plan every four years. The currently operative plan is the 2023 SFP, adopted July 2023. A successor 2027 SFP update is in active development with a stated completion target of summer 2027.

The plan covers the full Wisconsin multimodal freight network: state highways and local roads, freight rail, regional airports, harbors, pipelines, and intermodal connections. *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces routing freight through Wisconsin — whether by road, air freight connection through regional airports, intermodal rail, or Great Lakes harbor — the SFP is the document that sets investment priorities, corridor designations, and policy recommendations that WisDOT uses to allocate project funding. Changes in the 2027 update will reshape which routes and intermodal facilities receive capital investment over the following eight-year horizon.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces handling time-sensitive or high-value cargo by air whose movements connect to Wisconsin regional airports are affected by the aviation component of this plan; workspaces moving cargo by over-the-road truck through Wisconsin are most directly affected by the highway and intermodal sections; those using rail or harbor connections are affected by the rail and port investment priorities.

The plan "aligns policies and recommendations with Connect 2050, Wisconsin's statewide long-range transportation plan." *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

*Effective date of 2023 SFP:* The 2023 SFP was adopted July 2023 and is currently in force. *Source: WisDOT Intermodal Freight page, which confirms the 2025 intermodal report "supports the policies found in WisDOT's 2023 State Freight Plan." https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/inter-sub.aspx.

*Jurisdictional scope:* The plan applies statewide across Wisconsin, covering state highways, local roads, railroads, regional airports, ports, and pipeline infrastructure within Wisconsin's borders, with regional coordination extending to the ten-state Mid-America Freight Coalition (MAFC) network. *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan; MAFC About page, Mid-America Freight Coalition. https://midamericafreight.org/index.php/about/.

*Specific penalty/enforcement provision:* Specific [penalty / enforcement provision] not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27. State freight plans are planning and funding-guidance instruments; non-compliance with plan priorities is not an enforcement matter against private operators. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific NHFP funding eligibility conditions impose binding obligations on sub-recipients requires review of the applicable FAST Act / IIJA federal funding terms.

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Attention

**MONITORING — 2027 SFP Public Comment Window: Fall 2026**

The draft 2027 SFP is scheduled to be released for public comment in Fall 2026. *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

*Operational implication:* The public comment period is the primary formal window for freight operators, logistics providers, and shippers to influence corridor prioritization, intermodal investment decisions, and policy recommendations that will govern WisDOT freight investment for 2027–2035. Missing this window means accepting the plan as finalized without sector input.

**Action:** Identify which Wisconsin corridors and intermodal facilities are operationally material to the workspace's freight flows. Prepare comment documentation before Fall 2026. Contact WisDOT's freight team at freight@dot.wi.gov to request advance notice of the comment period opening. *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

**MONITORING — FAC Engagement: April 21, 2026 Meeting**

The next scheduled Freight Advisory Committee (FAC) meeting is April 21, 2026. *Source: WisDOT FAC page, WisDOT. https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/fac.aspx.

*Operational implication:* FAC meetings are the earliest point at which draft plan direction, corridor priorities, and intermodal investment framing are aired before public release. Stakeholders with industry representation on the FAC receive earlier visibility into plan direction than those relying solely on the public comment period.

**Action:** Determine whether a trade association or sector body relevant to the workspace's freight verticals holds FAC membership. FAC members represent sectors including "industry, agriculture, logistics, warehousing, economic development and transportation." *Source: WisDOT FAC page, WisDOT. https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/fac.aspx.

---

## How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The Wisconsin SFP is a planning and investment-guidance instrument, not a direct regulatory compliance obligation on private freight operators. Its compliance chain runs from federal funding requirements (NHFP/IIJA) → WisDOT → public investment allocation → corridor and facility conditions that affect private operator costs and access.

**Role positions relevant to the workspace:**

- *As a shipper or importer using Wisconsin highway corridors:* The workspace is a beneficiary (or victim) of WisDOT's highway investment priorities as set by the SFP. Corridor investment gaps translate into operating cost differentials — slower transit times, weight-restriction detours, higher drayage costs on under-invested routes.
- *As a freight forwarder arranging intermodal movements through Wisconsin:* The workspace's intermodal routing options, intermodal facility capacity, and competitive pricing depend on investments the SFP prioritizes for rail-truck interchange and harbor-road connections.
- *As an air cargo operator or shipper using Wisconsin regional airports:* The SFP covers regional airport freight infrastructure; investment level determines facility adequacy for air cargo operations.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any NHFP sub-grant conditions passed through WisDOT to freight facility operators impose direct compliance obligations on the workspace's freight arrangements requires review of specific grant agreement terms.

The FAC's role is described explicitly: "its role is strictly advisory." *Source: WisDOT FAC page, WisDOT. https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/fac.aspx. The FAC does not issue binding requirements on private operators.

---

## Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**2027 SFP — Full Development Schedule (Sourced, Stated by WisDOT)**

The following milestones are stated by WisDOT as the 2027 SFP development timeline. Phases labeled "(planned)" by WisDOT are cited as the department's stated schedule:

| Phase | Timing | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Review 2023 SFP; identify update areas; begin FAC engagement | Spring 2025 | Completed |
| Data collection; continued FAC and freight rail engagement | Fall 2025 | Completed |
| Data collection; begin draft creation | Winter 2025/2026 | Completed |
| Continued FAC engagement; continued drafting with stakeholder feedback incorporated | Spring 2026 | In progress |
| Continued drafting with stakeholder feedback | Summer 2026 (planned) | Upcoming |
| Finalize draft plan; release for public comment | Fall 2026 (planned) | Upcoming |
| Incorporate public comment; finalize plan; submit to U.S. DOT | Winter 2026/2027 (planned) | Upcoming |
| Edits based on U.S. DOT feedback; U.S. DOT approval and WisDOT adoption | Spring–Summer 2027 (planned) | Upcoming |

*Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

**Re-check window:** Re-assess this brief when the Fall 2026 public comment draft is released. At that point, corridor priorities, investment allocations, and intermodal facility designations will be available for operational review. Monitor FAC meeting minutes (next meeting: April 21, 2026) for early signals of draft plan direction. *Source: WisDOT FAC page, WisDOT. https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/fac.aspx.

**Federal Guidance on Multimodal State Freight Plans and FACs (Feb 2026)**

USDOT published guidance in the Federal Register on February 24, 2026 (document 2026-03648) covering NHFP compliance, four-year update requirements, and FAC consultation rules. *Source: Federal Register, USDOT, February 24, 2026. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees. **Note:** The source block for this document returned an access-restricted page; no verbatim content was available. The existence and date of the document are cited as fact from the corroborator list; its specific provisions are not quoted and no FACT claims are grounded in its text. Specific [NHFP compliance requirements and FAC consultation obligations from the Feb 2026 federal guidance] not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.

*Analytical inference:* The publication of updated federal guidance concurrent with Wisconsin's active 2027 SFP drafting cycle suggests the 2027 SFP will need to align with the February 2026 federal guidance on plan content and FAC process requirements. Workspaces engaging in the public comment process should expect the 2027 SFP to reflect these updated federal standards.

---

## Substantive Requirements

*No direct regulatory compliance obligations fall on private freight operators under the SFP. This section documents the plan's substantive investment scope and policy framework as it affects workspace operations.*

**Multimodal network coverage**

The 2027 SFP update "continues to focus on the needs of state highways and local roads, freight rail, aviation, harbors, and intermodal connections." *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

The plan: "Includes new and updated information since the previous statewide freight plan was released, including an overview of the current multimodal freight system; Assesses statewide freight needs and issues through data analysis and stakeholder engagement; Guides statewide investments to improve the multimodal freight system, including roadways and roadside facilities, railroads, regional airports, ports and pipelines; Engages and reflects the interests of a wide array of freight stakeholders." *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

**Intermodal freight policy support**

The 2025 Intermodal Freight in Wisconsin report "supports the policies found in WisDOT's 2023 State Freight Plan." The report is described as serving as "an update to the 2019 Overview of Intermodal Freight in Wisconsin" and includes "data and operational updates that have occurred since the 2019 report" and "leverages two regional studies that have been published since then." *Source: WisDOT Intermodal Freight page, WisDOT, 2025. https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/inter-sub.aspx.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces using containerized or intermodal movements through Wisconsin, the 2025 intermodal report is the current technical companion document to the 2023 SFP and contains the most recent facility data and supply-chain trend analysis supporting those SFP policies. The full report is available at https://wisconsindot.gov/Pages/doing-bus/freight/inter-sub.aspx.

**Specific [intermodal facility investment figures, port volume data, and supply-chain trend metrics from the 2025 intermodal report]** not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27 (the source block for the intermodal page did not contain report body content).

**Regional coordination — Mid-America Freight Coalition**

Wisconsin is one of ten states participating in the MAFC, described as "a regional organization that cooperates in the planning, operation, preservation, and improvement of transportation infrastructure in the Midwest." The ten participating states are "Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Michigan, Minnesota, Missouri, Ohio, and Wisconsin" and "share key interstate corridors, inland waterways, and the Great Lakes." These states "signed a Memorandum of Understanding in October 2006." *Source: MAFC About page, Mid-America Freight Coalition. https://midamericafreight.org/index.php/about/.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces moving freight on cross-state corridors connecting Wisconsin to Illinois, Michigan, Minnesota, or the broader Midwest, the MAFC regional planning context means Wisconsin's corridor investment decisions are shaped partly by multistate freight flow priorities, not solely by intrastate needs. Corridor funding priorities that emerge from the 2027 SFP will therefore reflect both Wisconsin-specific and MAFC regional freight planning frameworks.

**Four-year update cycle**

"WisDOT updates our State's Freight Plan every four years so that it accurately reflects, considers, and addresses our state's current and future freight needs." *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

*Analytical inference:* The four-year cycle — 2018 → 2023 → 2027 — means that investment frameworks adopted in the 2027 SFP will govern WisDOT freight project prioritization through approximately 2031, with the subsequent update cycle beginning around 2027–2028.

**Investment objectives**

The plan's findings "help WisDOT integrate freight improvements in future construction projects and prioritize future freight-related projects for funding. These future investments position Wisconsin to remain competitive in the global marketplace." *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT, 2025–2026. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

**Specific [investment dollar amounts, project lists, and funding allocations from the 2023 SFP or the 2027 SFP draft]** not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.

---

## Registration and Reporting Obligations

No registration or reporting obligations fall on private freight operators under the Wisconsin SFP itself. The plan is a public investment-planning instrument.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces seeking to participate formally in the 2027 SFP process have two available channels: (1) submitting comments during the Fall 2026 public comment period, and (2) securing representation through a FAC member organization. Direct engagement inquiries can be directed to freight@dot.wi.gov. *Source: WisDOT State Freight Plan planning page, WisDOT. https://wisdotplans.gov/plan/state-freight-plan.

---

## Operational System Requirements

No direct operational system requirements fall on private freight operators under the SFP. Requirements are institutional (WisDOT's internal planning and project programming processes).

*Operational implication:* Workspaces that depend on specific Wisconsin corridors, intermodal terminals, or airport freight facilities for regular freight flows should maintain an internal record of which facilities and routes are material to their operations. This record is the prerequisite for preparing an effective comment submission during the Fall 2026 public comment window.

---

## Exemptions and Edge Cases

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-27: The SFP is an investment-planning framework, not a regulatory instrument with exemptions applicable to private operators.*

prior.title: International Roadcheck 2026: ELD and Cargo Securement Focus Expected to Impact Spot Rates and Capacity
prior.summary: International Roadcheck enforcement period begins May 12, 2026, with heightened inspections focusing on electronic logging devices (ELDs) and cargo securement. Historical data shows spot rate increases of 6-8% and tender rejection rates rising 117 basis points during the inspection week. The CVSA expects to inspect approximately 15 vehicles per minute across North America's 72-hour period. Market impact varies by carrier size and equipment type, with reefer experiencing strongest rate response (9%) last year.

prior.title: MIT Climate Machine: First Comprehensive Assessment of Live Music Emissions in US and UK (2025)
prior.summary: MIT Climate Machine published the first comprehensive assessment of live music greenhouse gas emissions, finding that UK live music generated 4.0 MtCO₂e (1% of national emissions) and US live music generated 14.3 MtCO₂e (0.2% of national emissions) in 2023. The analysis identified fan travel as the dominant emission source (77.2% UK, 62.2% US), followed by air freight, food & beverage, and venue operations. The research employs three complementary frameworks (decision-based, activities-based, and event-type) and sophisticated mathematical models including touring optimization, building performance, and transportation modeling.
prior.full_brief:
# MIT Climate Machine: First Comprehensive Assessment of Live Music Emissions in US and UK (2025)

**Research Summary | Published: December 11, 2025 | Issuing Body: MIT Climate Machine (formerly MIT Environmental Solutions Initiative)**

---

## Context Note

This brief synthesises five source blocks: the authoritative PR Newswire wire release (primary), Music Week trade coverage, Billboard Pro analysis, and two Nature editorial/news pieces (September 2024 and March 2025). The Nature (March 2025) source block on the Massive Attack concert is paywalled beyond its abstract in the provided content; only its headline finding (98% energy-emissions reduction, battery-powered outdoor concert, 32,000+ attendees) is available as a span-grounded FACT. All other claims are drawn from verbatim spans in the remaining source blocks. Where a finding is attributed to the study but no verbatim span exists in the source blocks, the claim is either omitted or labeled as an analysis inference.

For workspaces operating in the live events cargo vertical — moving production equipment, staging, artist and crew freight, and consumables by road, air, and ocean — this is the first peer-reviewed-grounded, industry-scale dataset quantifying where live music's emissions actually concentrate. The findings directly reprioritise where the workspace's own operational choices (freight modal shift, supplier selection, venue logistics) sit in the client's emission story.

---

## 1. What the Research Found

**Study identity and institutional lineage.** "The Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Climate Machine (formerly part of the MIT Environmental Solutions Initiative) with support from Coldplay, Warner Music Group (WMG), Live Nation, and Hope Solutions have released the first comprehensive annual carbon emissions calculation of the live music industry in the U.S. and U.K." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Dataset scale.** "The study analyzes data from over 80,000 events across the U.S. and the U.K., capturing greenhouse gas emissions across all major impact areas including trucking, energy, food and beverage consumption, water, waste, fan travel, artist and crew travel, accommodation, and freight." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Sector share of national emissions.** "Though the live music sector accounts for just 0.2% of the U.S.'s and 1.1% of the U.K.'s total emissions, its cultural reach is vast." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Advisory structure.** "An Advisory Committee of more than 50 senior leaders, sustainability experts, and music industry veterans" contributed to the report's methodology and recommendations. *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Methodology characterisation.** "Grounded in rigorous peer-reviewed research, industry reports, and advanced analysis, the findings offer an unprecedented, data-driven view of live music's environmental impact." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Headline finding — fan travel dominance.** "Fan travel is the largest driver of live music emissions, accounting for 77% in the U.K. and 62% in the U.S. across nearly all event types." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Food and beverage ranking.** "Food and beverage ranks next, contributing to 16.9% in the U.S. and 7.6% in the U.K., driven largely by animal-based products. A shift toward plant-based menus could reduce these emissions by 40% or more." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Trucking and air freight when fan travel is excluded.** "When fan travel is excluded, trucking and freight emerge as major contributors — trucking makes up 14% of U.S. emissions, while air freight accounts for nearly 35% in the U.K." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Large-format events as leverage point.** "Large-format shows, though fewer in number, generate a disproportionate share of total emissions, making festivals and stadium tours powerful catalysts for innovation and scalable climate solutions." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

**Project timeline context — pre-publication phase.** The Nature editorial (September 2024) recorded that the Climate Machine research group "has undertaken a project co-funded by the Warner Music Group, Live Nation and Coldplay to analyse the carbon footprint of the live music industry, initially in the United Kingdom and United States," with co-leader Norhan Bayomi indicating "that should be published in an academic report later this year, with a public report following in 2025." *Source: Nature editorial, September 4, 2024. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-02828-1 The December 2025 wire release confirms the public report's delivery, fulfilling this schedule.

**Prior UK baseline context.** "In 2010, researchers used figures from 2007 to estimate that the UK music industry produced some 540,000 tonnes of greenhouse-gas emissions annually, around 0.1% of the country's total energy-related carbon dioxide emissions. Live music accounted for 74% of that." *Source: Nature editorial, September 4, 2024. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-02828-1 *Analytical inference:* The MIT Climate Machine study now supersedes this 2007-data baseline as the most current publicly available industry-scale emission estimate for both the US and UK.

**Massive Attack battery-powered concert finding.** "A battery-powered live outdoor concert headlined by the UK trip-hop band Massive Attack, and attended by more than 32,000 fans, slashed energy-related greenhouse-gas emissions by 98%, according to a report by climate researchers." *Source: Nature career news, March 25, 2025. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-00753-5 *Analytical inference:* This Tyndall Centre / Massive Attack result, published three months before the MIT Climate Machine study's December release, provides a supply-side complement: the MIT dataset establishes where emissions concentrate; the Tyndall finding demonstrates what energy-system intervention achieves for the venue energy component.

**Scope and study limitations note.** The press release and corroborating sources confirm the study covers the US and UK only. Extension to other territories is not announced in any source block. *Specific scope of peer-review journal submission, underlying data granularity, and any forthcoming academic publication peer-review status not available from primary sources as of December 11, 2025.*

---

## 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

*Operational implication:* For workspaces whose live events cargo vertical includes production freight, staging equipment, artist and crew baggage, and consumable logistics, the study's freight taxonomy is directly actionable: when fan travel is excluded — the category the workspace does not control — "trucking and freight emerge as major contributors," with air freight at nearly 35% of UK emissions. This places the workspace's own modal decisions (air vs. road for equipment moves, air freight for last-minute production components) at the top of the controllable emission stack for UK touring operations. *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

*Operational implication:* The 14% US trucking share, measured against all US live music emissions including fan travel, means road freight for production logistics is a proportionally smaller but still named category. For workspaces operating road-primary domestic US touring runs (equipment artics, overnight moves between stadium dates), the study provides the first nationally-scaled reference figure for benchmarking a touring client's road freight emission contribution.

*Operational implication:* The finding that "large-format shows generate a disproportionate share of total emissions" concentrates the workspace's commercial attention on festival and stadium-tour clients as the highest-leverage accounts for emission reduction conversations. These are also the accounts most likely to face client-side sustainability scrutiny from artists and labels, given the consortium membership (Live Nation, Warner Music Group, Coldplay). *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025. https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/mit-climate-machine-with-support-from-coldplay-warner-music-group-live-nation-and-hope-solutions-release-first-ever-total-emissions-of-live-music-in-us-and-uk-302639267.html

*Analytical inference:* The consortium structure — Live Nation as the world's largest live events promoter, Warner Music Group as a major label, Coldplay as a high-profile touring act — signals that this dataset is intended to drive industry-wide procurement and contracting decisions, not merely academic publication. Workspaces that serve these organisations or their touring clients should anticipate the dataset being used to score vendors against freight emission performance.

*Analytical inference:* The UK air freight figure (nearly 35% of emissions when fan travel is excluded) intersects with the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) extension to aviation and with SAF blending mandates under ReFuelEU Aviation. Touring air freight into or transiting through EU airports is exposed to carbon pricing on those routes. The MIT study provides the category weight; the EU ETS and ReFuelEU framework determine the cost implication per tonne. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific touring air freight movements (e.g. equipment shipped as cargo on commercial aircraft vs. charter aircraft) fall within the current EU ETS aviation scope requires route-specific and carrier-specific confirmation.

---

## 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Modal strategy for UK touring clients.** The near-35% air freight share of UK live music emissions (fan travel excluded) creates a direct commercial argument for road-over-air substitution on UK-Europe touring legs. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces that can demonstrate a documented modal shift from air to road for production equipment — and quantify the emission reduction against the MIT Climate Machine category baseline — have a differentiated offer for clients whose label or artist sustainability team is benchmarking against this study.

**Emission reporting baseline for client proposals.** The study provides the first publicly citable, MIT-authored, 80,000-event dataset against which a workspace's freight emission contribution can be contextualised. *Analytical inference:* In RFP responses and client sustainability reports, workspaces can now reference the study's freight category weights to explain what share of total tour emissions their logistics scope represents — previously, no such publicly available anchor existed for US and UK tours.

**Scope 3 supplier data requests.** *Analytical inference:* Where live events clients operate under CSRD reporting obligations (EU-listed or EU-turnover-threshold companies) or voluntary SBTi commitments, the study's freight emission categories map directly onto Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation). Workspaces should anticipate requests for carrier-level emission intensity data aligned to these categories. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether a specific client's CSRD reporting obligation extends to their UK and US touring logistics requires entity-level confirmation.

**Artist-driven procurement pressure.** The study's citation of Billie Eilish's team working with venues to ensure plant-based meal options on the 2024–25 tour demonstrates that artist sustainability commitments are already translating into supply-chain requirements at venue level. *Source: Billboard Pro, December 11, 2025. https://www.billboard.com/pro/fan-travel-concerts-most-us-live-music-carbon-emissions/ *Analytical inference:* The same dynamic — artist team sets emission-reduction targets, production and logistics vendors are expected to align — is foreseeable for freight and trucking as the MIT baseline makes those categories numerically visible.

**Fan-travel category is not the workspace's lever.** The 77% UK / 62% US fan travel dominance means the majority of a tour's emissions sit outside freight logistics entirely. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces should be precise in client conversations: they address the freight and trucking category, not the dominant category. Overclaiming a logistics provider's role in overall tour decarbonisation, when fan travel is the primary driver, undermines credibility with sustainability-literate clients.

---

## 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "The first MIT-led study of 80,000 US and UK live music events finds that when fan travel is excluded, air freight accounts for nearly 35% of UK live music emissions and trucking accounts for 14% of US emissions. That is the category our logistics decisions sit in." *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025.*
- "This study gives us a shared baseline. We can now show, against publicly citable MIT data, how your tour's freight emission intensity compares to the category average — and where modal substitution or routing optimisation delivers verified reductions."
- "The consortium behind this study — Live Nation, Warner Music Group, and Coldplay — are the same organisations your artist and label sustainability teams likely report into. The dataset they funded is now the reference your buyers will use."

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- "Does your artist or label sustainability commitment reference this study or set a freight emission target against it? If so, we should align our reporting format to that framework now."
- "Are your UK-Europe touring equipment moves currently air-freighted? We can quantify the Scope 3 reduction from shifting to road — it maps directly to the category the MIT study isolates."
- "Who in your organisation is responsible for Scope 3 Category 4/9 reporting for touring? We should give them carrier-level emission data in the format they need."

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not claim that logistics optimisation reduces the overall tour's carbon footprint by a large percentage. Fan travel is 62–77% of total emissions; freight is a secondary category. Clients with sustainability expertise will flag the overclaim immediately.
- Do not cite the MIT study as a compliance standard or regulatory requirement. It is a research finding and industry baseline, not a regulatory instrument. *Analytical inference:* Its influence on procurement criteria and contractual sustainability clauses is foreseeable but not yet documented in any source block.
- Do not characterise the 40% food-and-beverage reduction figure as applying to logistics. "A shift toward plant-based menus could reduce these emissions by 40% or more" applies to catering, not to freight. *Source: PR Newswire / Warner Music Group Corp., December 11, 2025.*
- Do not assert that the 98% energy-emission reduction from Massive Attack's battery-powered concert transfers to standard touring logistics. That finding is venue energy-specific, derived from a different research programme (Tyndall Centre), and covers only the energy-related component of a single outdoor event. *Source: Nature career news, March 25, 2025. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-00753-5

---

## 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**No per-route or per-event freight emission intensity figure.** The study provides category-level shares (14% trucking in US, 35% air freight in UK when fan travel excluded) but does not — in the source blocks available — publish per-tonne-km emission intensity figures or per-event freight baselines. Workspaces cannot directly derive a kg CO₂e per event figure from the published top-line percentages without the underlying absolute emission totals. *Specific absolute total emission figures for US and UK live music not available from primary sources as of December 11, 2025 in the source blocks provided.* The full report is linked in the wire release; the workspace should obtain and review it directly.

**No confirmed peer-reviewed academic publication.** The Nature editorial (September 2024) anticipated "an academic report later this year, with a public report following in 2025." *Source: Nature editorial, September 4, 2024. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-02828-1 The December 2025 release is the public report. Whether the accompanying academic publication has been submitted to or accepted by a peer-reviewed journal is not confirmed in any source block as of December 11, 2025. *Analytical inference:* Until the academic version is published, the study's methodology cannot be formally peer-evaluated by the research community, which limits its use as a regulatory reference or legal defence.

**No guidance on territorial extension.** The study covers the US and UK only. No source block confirms plans or timelines for extending the methodology to EU touring markets, Asia-Pacific, or other territories material to the workspace's trade lanes.

**No verified causal link between study publication and procurement mandate.** *Analytical inference:* The consortium's commercial weight (Live Nation, WMG) makes it plausible that the study will inform procurement criteria and tour sustainability reporting requirements. However, no source block documents any specific contractual or tendering requirement tied to the study's publication. This remains an inference, not a confirmed signal.

**The Massive Attack 98% energy-emission reduction does not resolve venue-energy generalisability.** That finding is drawn from a single battery-powered outdoor concert in Bristol, UK, produced in collaboration with the Tyndall Centre. Replicability across indoor arenas, multi-day festivals, and international touring configurations is not addressed in the available source content. *Source: Nature career news, March 25, 2025. https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-00753-5

**MONITORING — re-check window:** The academic publication version of the MIT Climate Machine study is pending confirmation. Re-check by Q2 2026 for journal submission status, and for any follow-on procurement criteria issued by Live Nation or Warner Music Group referencing the study's emission categories. *Analytical inference:* Industry standard for translating a consortium-backed research publication into procurement language is 6–18 months post-public release.

---

## 6. Sources

| 3 | How a scientist–pop industry partnership slashed a live gig's carbon emissions by 98% | Nature (Career News) | March 25, 2025 | Tier 6 — News/analysis (Nature; Tyndall Centre battery-concert finding) | https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-00753-5 |
| 4 | Backed by Coldplay, Warner Music and Live Nation, MIT's Climate Machine reports on US-UK emissions | Music Week | December 12, 2025 | Tier 5 — Trade press (leading UK music industry trade publication) | https://www.musicweek.com/live/read/backed-by-coldplay-warner-music-and-live-nation-mit-s-climate-machine-reports-on-us-uk-emissions/093247 |
| 5 | Fan Travel Accounts for 62% of Live Music-Related Carbon Emissions in the U.S.: Study | Billboard Pro | December 11, 2025 | Tier 5 — Trade press (leading US music industry trade publication) | https://www.billboard.com/pro/fan-travel-concerts-most-us-live-music-carbon-emissions/ |

prior.title: NYC Benchmarking and Energy Efficiency Rating Compliance Requirements (Local Law 84, 133, 33, and 95)
prior.summary: NYC requires building owners meeting specified criteria to annually benchmark energy and water consumption using EPA's Energy Star Portfolio Manager, with submission by May 1st. Buildings receive energy efficiency grades (A-F) based on EPA ratings, with grades publicly disclosed. Compliance involves account setup, data entry of building characteristics, collection of utility data, and submission of benchmarking information. Local Law 33 (amended by LL95 of 2019) establishes grading system where scores ≥85=A, 70-84=B, 55-69=C, <55=D, non-submission=F, exempt/unsupported=N. Exemptions include buildings with ≤3 stories with individually owned HVAC/heating systems ('garden style' apartments).
